42 Topics
01
Two Pointers
Foundation Technique
54 problems · sorted array, linked list, string
Why It Matters: Tests ability to eliminate search space using invariants. O(n) solutions to O(n²) problems demonstrate insight, not brute force.
Core Idea: Maintain lo/hi (or slow/fast) pointers. Decide which to move based on a monotone condition. The invariant — what you prove about the space between pointers — IS the algorithm.
Sub-Variants
Opposite-end sortedOpposite-end maximizeOpposite-end countThree-pointer (3Sum)Four-pointer (4Sum)Dutch National FlagFast/slow — cycle detectionFast/slow — cycle entryFast/slow — middle findingFast/slow — kth from endFast/slow — palindromeFast/slow — implicit graphTwo pointers on two arraysString two-pointerFill from endsTwo-pointer + sortingGreedy + opposite-endMulti-pointer + heap
Problem Set
#LayerLinkProblemDiffPSub-VariantKey Insight
1FoundationLC 167Two Sum IIMedOpposite-end sortedlo+hi pointers; move lo up if sum too small, hi down if too large
2FoundationLC 153SumMedThree-pointer (3Sum)Sort; fix i, run opposite-end on [i+1,n-1]; skip duplicates at every level
3FoundationLC 11Container With Most WaterMedOpposite-end maximizeAlways move the shorter side — moving the taller can only shrink width without gain
4FoundationLC 125Valid PalindromeEasyString two-pointerSkip non-alphanumeric; compare case-insensitive; lo < hi
5FoundationLC 141Linked List CycleEasyFast/slow — cycle detectionFloyd's: if fast meets slow → cycle. fast=head.next.next, slow=head.next
6FoundationLC 142Linked List Cycle IIMedFast/slow — cycle entryAfter meeting, reset one to head; advance both at speed 1; second meeting = entry
7FoundationLC 876Middle of the Linked ListEasyFast/slow — middle findingfast=head.next.next, slow=head.next; when fast null → slow is middle
8FoundationLC 19Remove Nth Node From End of ListMedFast/slow — kth from endAdvance fast by n+1; move both until fast null; slow.next = slow.next.next
9FoundationLC 75Sort ColorsMedDutch National FlagThree pointers lo/mid/hi; loop while mid≤hi; 3-way partition in single pass
10FoundationLC 977Squares of a Sorted ArrayEasyFill from endsFill result array from right; compare abs(lo) vs abs(hi); advance smaller inward
11VariantsLC 184SumMedFour-pointer (4Sum)Sort; fix i,j; run opposite-end on remainder; skip all duplicates; watch overflow
12VariantsLC 163Sum ClosestMedThree-pointer (3Sum variant)Track min |diff|; update bestSum when closer; same skip pattern as 3Sum
13VariantsLC 1498Number of Subsequences Sum ≤ TargetMedOpposite-end countSort; for each lo, find max hi where nums[lo]+nums[hi]≤target; add 2^(hi-lo)
14VariantsLC 42Trapping Rain WaterHardFill from endsTrack maxLeft, maxRight; water at i = min(maxL,maxR)-height[i]; move smaller side
15VariantsLC 283Move ZeroesEasyTwo-pointer + sortingslow tracks next non-zero position; fast scans; swap when nums[fast]≠0
16VariantsLC 344Reverse StringEasyOpposite-end sortedSwap s[lo] and s[hi]; advance lo++, hi--; until lo>=hi
17VariantsLC 234Palindrome Linked ListEasyFast/slow — palindromeFind middle; reverse second half; compare; restore if required
18VariantsLC 160Intersection of Two Linked ListsEasyTwo pointers on two arraysPointers traverse both lists; at end switch; meet at intersection after len(A)+len(B)
19VariantsLC 88Merge Sorted ArrayEasyTwo pointers on two arraysFill from end; compare m1[i] vs m2[j]; place larger at end of m1; no extra space
20VariantsLC 392Is SubsequenceEasyTwo pointers on two arraysi on s, j on t; advance i only when s[i]==t[j]; return i==len(s)
21VariantsLC 202Happy NumberEasyFast/slow — implicit graphImplicit linked list; fast=next(next(n)), slow=next(n); cycle → unhappy; 1 → happy
22VariantsLC 287Find the Duplicate NumberMedFast/slow — cycle entryArray as implicit linked list; Floyd's finds cycle entry = duplicate
23ComboLC 76Minimum Window SubstringHardTwo-pointer + HashMapVariable window; two freq maps; formed counter avoids O(26) scan each step
24ComboLC 713Subarray Product Less Than KMedOpposite-end countProduct window; count subarrays ending at right = right-left+1 when valid
25ComboLC 763Partition LabelsMedGreedy + opposite-endPre-compute last occurrence; expand end=max(end,last[c]); cut when i==end
26ComboLC 881Boats to Save PeopleMedGreedy + opposite-endSort; pair heaviest with lightest if possible; always take heaviest alone if not
27ComboLC 845Longest Mountain in ArrayMedTwo-pointer + sortingFind peak; expand left and right while strictly increasing; length=r-l+1
28ComboLC 189Rotate ArrayMedFill from endsReverse all; reverse [0,k-1]; reverse [k,n-1]; three reversal trick
29ComboLC 31Next PermutationMedFill from endsFind rightmost ascent; swap with smallest greater element to right; reverse suffix
30ComboLC 9233Sum With MultiplicityMedThree-pointer (3Sum variant)Count-based 3Sum; multiply counts when lo==hi==target/3 etc; careful modular
31HardLC 1658Min Operations to Reduce X to ZeroMedOpposite-end countReframe: find max subarray with sum = total-x; prefix sum + HashMap alternative
32HardLC 2824Count Pairs Whose Sum is Less Than TargetEasyOpposite-end countSort; lo+hi < target → count += hi-lo; lo++; else hi--
33HardLC 4544Sum IIMedTwo pointers on two arraysHashMap on A+B sums; count complements -(C+D) in second pass; O(n²)
34HardLC 135CandyHardTwo-pointer + sortingTwo-pass greedy: L→R then R→L; max of two passes at each index
35HardLC 2037Min Moves to Seat EveryoneEasyTwo-pointer + sortingSort both; sum |seat[i]-student[i]|; sorting aligns pairs optimally
36HardLC 1099Two Sum Less Than KEasy✅Opposite-end sortedSort; track max valid sum; move lo up when sum < k, hi down otherwise
37HardLC 2593Sum SmallerMed✅Three-pointer countFix i; opposite-end on rest; if sum < target → count += hi-lo (all pairs with lo valid)
38HardLC 633Sum of Square NumbersMedOpposite-end sortedlo=0, hi=sqrt(c); check lo²+hi²; move pointers by comparison; stop when lo>hi
39HardLC 2563Count the Number of Fair PairsMedOpposite-end countSort; answer=countLess(upper+1)-countLess(lower); opposite-end for each bound
40HardLC 143Reorder ListMedFast/slow + reverseFind middle; reverse second half; merge two halves alternately
41HardLC 457Circular Array LoopMedFast/slow — implicit graphFast/slow on circular index; detect cycle; must be same direction and length > 1
42HardLC 516Longest Palindromic SubsequenceMed⚠️ DP not two-pointerSubsequences need DP; two-pointer only works for contiguous substrings
43⚠️TrapLC 560⚠️ Subarray Sum Equals KMed⚠️ Trap — negatives break monotonicityArray has negatives; shrink logic breaks; must use prefix sum + HashMap
44⚠️TrapLC 325⚠️ Max Size Subarray Sum = kMed✅⚠️ Trap — prefix + first-seen indexCan have negatives; sliding window invariant breaks; HashMap of first-seen prefix
45⚠️TrapLC 1⚠️ Two Sum (unsorted)Easy⚠️ Trap — unsorted → HashMapUnsorted array; two-pointer requires O(n log n) sort + breaks index requirement
46⚠️TrapLC 532⚠️ K-diff Pairs in ArrayMed⚠️ Trap — k=0 special casek=0: count freq>1 elements; k<0: 0 pairs; only standard two-pointer for k>0
47⚠️TrapLC 5⚠️ Longest Palindromic SubstringMed⚠️ Trap — expand around centerTwo-pointer expands from center; NOT opposite-end; odd and even length both
48⚠️TrapLC 680⚠️ Valid Palindrome IIEasy⚠️ Trap — one deletion forkOn mismatch, check isPalin(lo+1,hi) OR isPalin(lo,hi-1); both must be checked
49⚠️TrapLC 454⚠️ 4Sum II (4 separate arrays)Med⚠️ Trap — 4 arrays needs HashMap4 separate arrays not sortable together; O(n²) HashMap on A+B sums
50⚠️TrapLC 2023⚠️ Pairs of Strings Concat = TargetMed⚠️ Trap — string lengths varyVariable string lengths; two-pointer inapplicable; HashMap on prefix/suffix splits
Pattern Triggers
Trigger PhraseSub-VariantWhen It BREAKS
Find pair/triplet summing to X in sorted arrayOpposite-end sortedArray has negatives + subarray sum — monotone breaks
Maximize value using two elements at endsOpposite-end maximizeValue is not monotone w.r.t. pointer movement
Count all pairs with sum < / > targetOpposite-end countNeed all pairs enumerated (not just count)
Sort array of 0s, 1s, 2s in-placeDutch National FlagMore than 3 distinct values → counting sort
Detect cycle in linked listFast/slow — cycle detectionNeed cycle length, not just detection
Find where cycle beginsFast/slow — cycle entryGraph is not functional (multiple nexts)
Merge two sorted arrays / find intersectionTwo pointers on two arraysArrays unsorted → sort first or use HashMap
80% Coverage Set
Minimum 16 problems: LC 167LC 15LC 11LC 141LC 142LC 75LC 42LC 76LC 287LC 881LC 19LC 876LC 763LC 1498LC 713LC 143
Red Flags
🚩 Moving wrong pointer — always move based on invariant, not arbitrarily
🚩 Forgetting duplicate skip in 3Sum — after finding valid triplet, advance BOTH lo AND hi past duplicates
🚩 Fast/slow meeting ≠ cycle start — after meeting, reset one to head; second meeting = cycle entry
🚩 Dutch flag loop termination — loop while mid ≤ hi, not mid < hi
🚩 Two-pointer on negatives — sum not monotone; use HashMap instead
🚩 Fast/slow palindrome modifies list — if cannot modify, use stack instead
02
HashMap
Foundation Data Structure
52 problems · complement lookup, frequency, grouping, prefix+map
Why It Matters: Tests ability to trade space for time. O(1) lookup transforms O(n²) brute-force into O(n). The skill is choosing the RIGHT hash key.
Core Idea: Map a computed key → value/index/count. Hardest part: identifying WHAT to hash and ensuring mapping is bijective when needed (bidirectional maps for isomorphism).
Sub-Variants
Complement lookupFrequency countingGrouping by canonical keyPrefix sum + HashMapPrefix modulo HashMapFirst-seen index mapBidirectional HashMapSliding window + freq mapHashMap + DLL (LRU)Array as implicit HashMapComposite/tuple keyReverse/inverted mapContent-based groupingFrequency stackMax-frequency aggregation
Problem Set
#LayerLinkProblemDiffPSub-VariantKey Insight
1FoundationLC 1Two SumEasyComplement lookupmap[target-num] → index; single pass; check before inserting
2FoundationLC 242Valid AnagramEasyFrequency countingint[26] counts; increment for s, decrement for t; all zero → anagram
3FoundationLC 49Group AnagramsMedGrouping by canonical keyKey = sorted string or int[26] freq; group all strings with same key
4FoundationLC 560Subarray Sum Equals KMedPrefix sum + HashMapcount += map[prefix-k]; init map[0]=1 BEFORE loop; handles negatives
5FoundationLC 347Top K Frequent ElementsMedFrequency countingBucket sort by freq O(n); freq map → bucket array; scan buckets from end
6FoundationLC 217Contains DuplicateEasyFrequency countingHashSet add; return false if already present
7FoundationLC 290Word PatternEasyBidirectional HashMapMap char→word AND word→char; both directions required for bijection check
8FoundationLC 205Isomorphic StringsEasyBidirectional HashMapMap s[i]→t[i] and t[i]→s[i]; mismatch on either direction → false
9VariantsLC 128Longest Consecutive SequenceMedHashSet membershipOnly start chain from n where n-1 not in set; O(n) total chain walking
10VariantsLC 4544Sum IIMedComplement lookupHashMap on A+B sums; count -(C+D) complements in second O(n²) pass
11VariantsLC 383Ransom NoteEasyFrequency countingint[26] counts from magazine; decrement for ransom; negative → false
12VariantsLC 299Bulls and CowsMedFrequency countingCount bulls first; count cows by overlap of remaining freq maps
13VariantsLC 438Find All Anagrams in a StringMedSliding window + freq mapFixed window of len(p); diff counter; record start when diff==0
14VariantsLC 525Contiguous ArrayMedFirst-seen index mapReplace 0→-1; map[prefix_sum]=first_index; longest = i - map[prefix]
15VariantsLC 974Subarray Sums Divisible by KMedPrefix modulo HashMapSame remainder → divisible subarray; negative mod fix: ((p%k)+k)%k
16VariantsLC 2023Pairs Concat Equal to TargetMedComplement lookupStore all words in map; for each word check if target - word exists
17VariantsLC 387First Unique Character in StringEasyFrequency countingTwo passes: build freq map; scan for first char with freq 1
18VariantsLC 3Longest Substring Without RepeatingMedSliding window + freq mapMap char→last index; jump lo = max(lo, map[c]+1); track max window length
19VariantsLC 170Two Sum III — Data Structure DesignEasy✅Complement lookupFreq map; find handles num==target-num with freq>1 check
20VariantsLC 1189Max Number of BalloonsEasyFrequency countingCount b,a,l,l,o,o,n frequencies; min(b,a,l//2,o//2,n)
21ComboLC 146LRU CacheMedHashMap + DLL (LRU)DLL for O(1) evict; map key→node for O(1) access; move to head on get/put
22ComboLC 460LFU CacheHardHashMap + DLL (LFU)Three maps: key→val, key→freq, freq→DLL; track minFreq; O(1) all ops
23ComboLC 1396Design Underground SystemMedComposite/tuple keyMap id→(station,time) for checkin; map (s1,s2)→(total,count) for average
24ComboLC 895Maximum Frequency StackHardFrequency stack (bucket stacks)Map freq→stack and val→freq; pop from maxFreq bucket; decrement freq
25ComboLC 710Random Pick with BlacklistHardReverse/inverted mapMap blacklisted low→safe high; rand in [0, M-|B|); remap via map
26ComboLC 432All O(1) Data StructureHardHashMap + DLLDLL of count buckets; each bucket has set of keys; map key→bucket node
27ComboLC 535Encode and Decode TinyURLMedBidirectional HashMapTwo maps: code→url and url→code; random hash or counter as code
28ComboLC 554Brick WallMedFrequency countingCount edge positions; answer = rows - maxEdgeCount; never cut through edge
29ComboLC 726Number of AtomsHardRecursive parsing + freq mapStack of maps; on ')' pop and multiply by count; merge into parent map
30ComboLC 819Most Common WordEasyFrequency countingNormalize; skip banned words; max freq scan
31HardLC 149Max Points on a LineHardComposite/tuple key (slope)Fix one point; map slope (dy/gcd, dx/gcd) → count; handle vertical/duplicate
32HardLC 1711Count Good MealsMedComplement lookupFor each num, check all powers of 2 as target; count += map[target-num]
33HardLC 2266Count Number of TextsMedDP + HashMapDP on runs of same character; 2 or 3 consecutive → multiply ways
34HardLC 355Design TwitterMedHashMap + heapMap user→tweets, user→followees; k-way merge via minHeap for news feed
35HardLC 123Best Time Buy/Sell Stock IIIHardDP states (not HashMap)4 states: hold1,sold1,hold2,sold2; update each day; state machine DP
36HardLC 472Concatenated WordsHardHashSet + DP/DFSBuild word set; DP/DFS for each word checking if constructible from shorter words
37HardLC 447Number of BoomerangsMedFrequency counting (distance)For each point, map dist²→count; sum k*(k-1) for each distance group
38HardLC 273Integer to English WordsHardArray as implicit HashMapArrays for units/tens/thousands; recursively process 3-digit chunks
39HardLC 981Time Based Key-Value StoreMedHashMap + binary searchMap key→list of (timestamp, value); BS for largest timestamp ≤ query
40HardLC 138Copy List with Random PointerMedOriginal→clone mapMap old→new node; first pass clone nodes; second pass link next and random
41HardLC 133Clone GraphMedOriginal→clone map (DFS)Map old→new; DFS; create clone when not in map; recurse on neighbors
42HardLC 269Alien DictionaryHard✅HashMap (adjacency) + topological sortDerive edges from adjacent words; Kahn's BFS; cycle → empty string
43⚠️TrapLC 1⚠️ Two Sum (sorted → use 2ptr)Easy⚠️ Trap — sorted → two-pointerSorted array + no index requirement → two-pointer O(1) space; HashMap wasteful
44⚠️TrapLC 560⚠️ Missing map[0]=1Med⚠️ Trap — prefix initWithout map[0]=1, subarrays from index 0 are missed entirely
45⚠️TrapLC 523⚠️ Continuous Subarray SumMed⚠️ Trap — must store index not countNeed length ≥ 2; store FIRST index of prefix%k; check i-map[r] ≥ 2
46⚠️TrapLC 128⚠️ Sorting solutionMed⚠️ Trap — sort is O(n log n)Sorting works but violates O(n) constraint; HashSet required for O(n)
47⚠️TrapLC 290⚠️ One-direction mapEasy⚠️ Trap — surjection not bijectionOne map allows a→dog, b→dog; second reverse map catches this failure
48⚠️TrapLC 146⚠️ LinkedHashMap shortcutMed⚠️ Trap — interviewer wants DLLJava LinkedHashMap is valid but interviewer expects manual DLL implementation
80% Coverage Set
Minimum 14 problems: LC 1LC 242LC 49LC 560LC 347LC 128LC 290LC 525LC 974LC 146LC 460LC 895LC 1396LC 149
Red Flags
🚩 Single-direction map for isomorphism — always use bidirectional maps; surjection violations silently pass
🚩 Missing map.put(0,1) — prefix sum subarray problems break without this initialisation
🚩 HashMap vs int[26] — for char-only freq counting use int[26]; 10× faster in Java
🚩 Forgetting equals/hashCode — custom key objects in Java MUST override both
🚩 HashMap on sorted data — sorted array + complement → two-pointer is O(1) space
🚩 LRU via HashMap alone — HashMap cannot maintain order; needs DLL or LinkedHashMap
🚩 Negative modulo in Java — (-1)%k = -1; always add k and re-mod: ((p%k)+k)%k
03
Binary Search
Foundation Technique
46 problems · classic search, rotated, answer space, matrix
Why It Matters: Binary search is the canonical O(log n) technique. Every FAANG interview expects it on sorted arrays, rotated arrays, and — critically — on the answer space itself (binary search on value, not index).
Core Idea: Maintain invariant: answer is always in [lo, hi]. Choose mid; decide which half to discard based on a monotone predicate. Template: lo=0, hi=n-1; while(lo≤hi); always shrink the search space.
Sub-Variants
Classic sorted arrayLeftmost / first occurrenceRightmost / last occurrenceRotated sorted arrayRotated with duplicatesBS on answer space (minimize)BS on answer space (maximize)BS on floating point2D matrix searchPeak findingBS + greedy validationExponential searchUnbounded/infinite arrayK-th element (two arrays)
Problem Set
#LayerLinkProblemDiffPSub-VariantKey Insight
1FoundationLC 704Binary SearchEasyClassic sorted arrayTemplate: lo=0,hi=n-1; mid=(lo+hi)>>>1; move lo=mid+1 or hi=mid-1
2FoundationLC 34Find First and Last PositionMedLeftmost / rightmostTwo separate BS calls; left: hi=mid when found; right: lo=mid when found
3FoundationLC 33Search in Rotated Sorted ArrayMedRotated sorted arrayDetermine which half is sorted; check if target in that half; discard other
4FoundationLC 153Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted ArrayMedRotated sorted arrayIf nums[mid] > nums[hi] → min is in right half; else left half including mid
5FoundationLC 162Find Peak ElementMedPeak findingIf nums[mid] < nums[mid+1] → peak is right; else peak is left (including mid)
6FoundationLC 74Search a 2D MatrixMed2D matrix searchTreat as 1D; index = mid; row = mid/cols, col = mid%cols
7FoundationLC 374Guess Number Higher or LowerEasyClassic sorted arrayPure BS template; use API result to move lo or hi
8VariantsLC 278First Bad VersionEasyLeftmost / first occurrenceFind first true in boolean array; hi=mid when bad; lo=mid+1 when good
9VariantsLC 81Search in Rotated Array II (duplicates)MedRotated with duplicatesWhen nums[lo]==nums[mid]==nums[hi] → can't determine sorted half; lo++,hi--
10VariantsLC 154Find Minimum in Rotated II (duplicates)HardRotated with duplicatesWhen nums[mid]==nums[hi], can't decide; hi-- to shrink safely
11VariantsLC 69Sqrt(x)EasyBS on answer space (maximize)BS on [0,x]; find largest mid where mid*mid ≤ x; use long to avoid overflow
12VariantsLC 875Koko Eating BananasMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on speed [1, max(piles)]; check if feasible in h hours; minimize valid speed
13VariantsLC 1870Minimum Speed to Arrive on TimeMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on speed; compute ceil division for each train; only last leg is exact
14VariantsLC 1011Capacity to Ship Packages in D DaysMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on [max(weights), sum(weights)]; greedy check: simulate days for given capacity
15VariantsLC 410Split Array Largest SumHardBS on answer space (minimize)BS on [max, sum]; check if k splits achievable with given max sum; classic
16VariantsLC 1283Smallest Divisor Given a ThresholdMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on divisor [1, max]; check ceil(num/div) sum ≤ threshold
17VariantsLC 4Median of Two Sorted ArraysHardK-th element (two arrays)BS on partition of shorter array; ensure left halves balanced; O(log min(m,n))
18VariantsLC 719Find K-th Smallest Pair DistanceHardBS on answer space + sliding windowBS on distance; count pairs with dist ≤ mid via sliding window; O(n log n)
19ComboLC 240Search a 2D Matrix IIMed2D matrix searchStart at top-right; move left if > target, down if < target; O(m+n)
20ComboLC 1095Find in Mountain ArrayHardPeak finding + two-phase BSFind peak; BS ascending side; if not found BS descending side
21ComboCustom GFGAggressive CowsMedBS + greedy validationBS on min distance; greedily place cows; check if k cows placed with min dist
22ComboCustom GFGBook AllocationMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on max pages; check if k students can each read ≤ mid pages; greedy sim
23ComboLC 774Minimize Max Distance to Gas StationHard✅BS on floating pointBS on distance; count stations needed to achieve max dist ≤ mid; O(n log W/eps)
24ComboLC 1552Magnetic Force Between Two BallsMedBS + greedy validationBS on min force; greedily place balls; check if m balls placed with min force
25ComboLC 378Kth Smallest in Sorted MatrixMedBS on answer space + 2D countBS on value; count elements ≤ mid in O(n) via staircase; find smallest mid with count ≥ k
26ComboLC 367Valid Perfect SquareEasyBS on answer space (maximize)BS on [1,num]; check if mid*mid == num; use long to avoid overflow
27ComboLC 1268Search Suggestions SystemMedLeftmost / first occurrenceSort; for each prefix, find leftmost match; take next 3 elements
28ComboLC 1802Maximum Value at a Given IndexMedBS on answer space (maximize)BS on value at index; calculate sum of array for given peak; check sum ≤ maxSum
29ComboLC 1760Minimum Limit of Balls in a BagMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on penalty; count ops needed to achieve penalty ≤ mid; (ball-1)/mid
30HardLC 668Kth Smallest in Multiplication TableHardBS on answer space + row-wise countBS on value; count elements ≤ mid in O(m) via min(mid/i, n); find smallest mid with count ≥ k
31HardLC 862Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least KHard⚠️ Deque not BSNegatives break BS on answer space; use prefix sums + monotonic deque
32HardLC 702Search in Array of Unknown SizeMed✅Exponential searchFind bounds with hi=1, hi*=2; then classic BS within [lo,hi]
33HardLC 287Find the Duplicate NumberMedBS on answer space (Pigeonhole)BS on value [1,n]; count numbers ≤ mid; if count>mid → duplicate in [1,mid]
34HardLC 1231Divide ChocolateHard✅BS on answer space (maximize)BS on min sweetness; check if k+1 chunks achievable; maximize valid sweetness
35HardLC 2141Maximum Running Time of N ComputersHardBS on answer space (maximize)BS on time; check if n computers can run for time T; extra power can be distributed
36HardLC 1482Minimum Number of Days to Make m BouquetsMedBS on answer space (minimize)BS on days; check if m bouquets of k flowers can be made on day D; greedy count
37HardLC 1964Longest Valid Obstacle CourseHardBS on LIS arrayPatience sorting (LIS); BS to find position to replace/extend in LIS array
38HardLC 354Russian Doll EnvelopesHardBS on LIS arraySort by width asc, height desc; find LIS on heights via patience sorting/BS
39HardLC 300Longest Increasing SubsequenceMedBS on LIS arrayPatience sorting: maintain smallest tail of all increasing subsequences; O(n log n)
40⚠️TrapLC 704⚠️ Integer overflowEasy⚠️ Trap — mid = lo+(hi-lo)/2mid=(lo+hi)/2 can overflow; use mid=lo+(hi-lo)/2 or mid=(lo+hi)>>>1
41⚠️TrapLC 704⚠️ Off-by-one in loopEasy⚠️ Trap — lo≤hi vs lo<hilo≤hi is robust; lo<hi can miss single-element case; adjust lo/hi updates
42⚠️TrapLC 34⚠️ Infinite loop in leftmostMed⚠️ Trap — hi=mid needs lo=mid+1If lo=mid, hi=mid, can infinite loop; ensure search space always shrinks
43⚠️TrapLC 33⚠️ Wrong half checkMed⚠️ Trap — target bounds checkMust check target against nums[lo] and nums[mid] to decide which half is valid
44⚠️TrapLC 875⚠️ Floating point divisionMed⚠️ Trap — integer ceil divisionTime = (pile+k-1)/k for integer ceil; floating point is slow and imprecise
80% Coverage Set
Minimum 12 problems: LC 704LC 34LC 33LC 153LC 162LC 74LC 875LC 1011LC 410LC 4LC 240LC 378
Red Flags
🚩 mid = (lo+hi)/2 — classic overflow bug; use mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2 or mid = (lo+hi)>>>1
🚩 lo < hi vs lo ≤ hi — lo ≤ hi is generally safer and easier to reason about
🚩 BS on answer space without monotone predicate — if check(mid) doesn't imply check(mid+1), BS is invalid
🚩 Rotated array duplicate handling — if nums[lo]==nums[mid]==nums[hi], must do lo++, hi-- to shrink
🚩 Forgetting long for mid*mid — when checking for sqrt, mid*mid can overflow standard integer types
🚩 Incorrectly identifying the sorted half — in rotated arrays, the check is crucial: if nums[lo] <= nums[mid]...
04
Sliding Window
Core DSA Technique
45 problems · subarray, substring, frequency maps, deque variants
Why It Matters: Converts O(n²) brute-force subarray enumeration into O(n) single-pass solutions by maintaining a live window [left, right] updated incrementally. The "aha" of recognizing when the window should shrink is a genuine test of algorithmic intuition.
Core Idea: The window always represents a valid/optimal range. Instead of recomputing from scratch, add one element at right and remove one at left. The invariant is what makes the window valid — if you can't define it, the technique doesn't apply.
Sub-Variants
Fixed-size window (size K)Variable window: maximize lengthVariable window: minimize lengthWindow with frequency mapExact-K constraint (atMost trick)At-most-K constraint countingReplacement allowed windowMonotonic deque (max/min)2D matrix windowWindow + binary searchWindow + prefix sumCircular/wrap-around windowString permutation matchingMulti-pointer windowCounting subarrays (not just find)
Problem Set
#LayerLinkProblemDiffPSub-VariantKey Insight
1FoundationLC 209Minimum Size Subarray SumMedVariable window: minimizeShrink left as soon as sum ≥ target; track minimum window length
2FoundationLC 3Longest Substring Without RepeatingMedVariable window + freq mapMove left to max(left, last_seen[char] + 1) when duplicate found
3FoundationLC 643Maximum Average Subarray IEasyFixed-size windowSlide fixed window of size k; subtract outgoing, add incoming element
4FoundationLC 567Permutation in StringMedFixed window + freq arrayFixed size = len(p); compare freq arrays after each slide
5VariantsLC 424Longest Repeating Character ReplacementMedReplacement trickWindow valid if size - maxFreq ≤ k; never shrink maxFreq (key insight)
6VariantsLC 438Find All Anagrams in a StringMedFixed window + all positionsCollect all left indices where frequency maps match
7VariantsLC 1004Max Consecutive Ones IIIMedFlip-k-zeros windowCount zeros in window; shrink when zeros > k
8VariantsLC 1493Longest Subarray of 1s After Deleting OneMedAt-most-1-zero windowEquivalent to at-most-1 zero window; track last zero position
9VariantsLC 904Fruit Into BasketsMedAt-most-2 distinctHashMap to count fruit types; shrink when distinct > 2
10VariantsLC 340Longest Substring with At Most K DistinctMed✅At-most-K distinctGeneralization of #904; evict least-recently-seen when > K
11VariantsLC 992Subarrays with K Different IntegersHardExact-K (atMost trick)exactly(K) = atMost(K) − atMost(K−1); non-obvious decomposition
12VariantsLC 930Binary Subarrays With SumMedExact-sum atMost trickcount(sum=k) = atMost(k) − atMost(k−1) applied to binary arrays
13VariantsLC 1695Maximum Erasure ValueMedMax sum, no duplicatesSame as longest unique substring but maximize sum, not length
14VariantsLC 1438Longest Subarray With Abs Diff ≤ LimitMedWindow + monotonic dequeMaintain two deques — one for max, one for min simultaneously
15VariantsLC 239Sliding Window MaximumHardFixed window + monotonic dequeDeque stores indices in decreasing order; front is always window max
16ComboLC 76Minimum Window SubstringHardMinimize + multi-char freq mapTrack have vs need counters; shrink only when have == need
17ComboLC 1234Replace Substring for Balanced StringMedInvert: window is part to replaceShrink when outside counts are all ≤ n/4 (inverted thinking)
18ComboLC 1358Substrings Containing All Three CharactersMedCount valid extensionsWhen window valid, all extensions right are valid; add n - right per left
19ComboLC 2302Count Subarrays With Score < KHardScore = sum × lengthNon-linear score; expand right, shrink left; count = right - left + 1
20ComboLC 1248Count Number of Nice SubarraysMedExact-K odds (atMost trick)Transform: odd→1, even→0; exactly(k) = atMost(k) − atMost(k−1)
21ComboLC 1838Frequency of Most Frequent ElementMedSorted + windowSort first; window valid if k ≥ right_val × size − window_sum
22ComboLC 2401Longest Nice SubarrayMedBitwise AND windowWindow invalid when any two elements share a bit; use OR of window
23HardLC 480Sliding Window MedianHardFixed window + two heapsTwo heaps for lower/upper half; lazy deletion with HashMap
24HardLC 862Shortest Subarray with Sum ≥ KHardNegatives: prefix + dequeNegatives break standard shrink; use prefix sum + monotonic deque
25HardLC 2398Maximum Number of Robots Within BudgetHardDeque max + running sumMax charge = deque max + sum of running costs; both must satisfy budget
26⚠️TrapLC 713⚠️ Subarray Product Less Than KMed⚠️ Looks like prefix product but IS windowProduct can't use prefix-sum safely (zeros, overflow); direct window is correct
27⚠️TrapLC 560⚠️ Subarray Sum Equals KMed⚠️ Negatives → prefix + HashMapNegatives prevent monotonic shrink; MUST use prefix-sum + hashmap
28⚠️TrapLC 53⚠️ Maximum SubarrayEasy⚠️ NOT sliding window, use Kadane'sWindow shrink doesn't map cleanly; Kadane's is cleaner and correct
29VariantsLC 395Longest Substring with At Least K RepeatingMedSliding window on unique countFix number of unique chars (1..26); for each unique count, shrink when any char repeats < k
30VariantsLC 2962Count Subarrays Where Max Elem Appears ≥ K TimesMedSliding + max count trackingFind array max; slide window counting max occurrences; each valid right contributes left+1 subarrays
31VariantsLC 1052Grumpy Bookstore OwnerMedFixed window: maximize benefitFixed window of size minutes; find window where extra satisfied customers is max; add always-satisfied base
32VariantsLC 2134Minimum Swaps to Group All 1s Together IIMedCircular fixed windowCircular array: window size = count(1s); find window with max ones; answer = window_size - max_ones
33VariantsLC 2461Maximum Sum of Distinct Subarrays Length KMedFixed window + distinct checkFixed window of size k; HashSet to track distinct elements; update sum on slide; skip if duplicates
34VariantsLC 1456Maximum Number of Vowels in SubstringMedFixed window: count vowelsFixed window of size k; subtract outgoing, add incoming; track max vowel count seen
35ComboLC 30Substring with Concatenation of All WordsHardFixed window + word freq mapTry each offset 0..wordLen-1; slide word-by-word window; compare word freq maps using HashMap
36ComboLC 2958Longest Subarray with At Most K FrequencyMedAt-most-K frequency variable windowStandard shrink when freq[num] > k; HashMap for counts; track max length
37ComboLC 2799Count Complete SubarraysMedVariable window: count subarraysComplete = all distinct elements present; when window valid each right adds left+1 subarrays
38ComboLC 995Minimum Number of K Consecutive Bit FlipsHardSliding window + difference arrayTrack cumulative flips in window with diff array; avoid recomputing flip parity at each position
39HardLC 1499Max Value of EquationHardDeque + window constraintyi+yj+|xi-xj| = (yi-xi)+(yj+xj); maintain deque of (yi-xi) dropping expired; maximize as j advances
40HardLC 2444Count Subarrays with Fixed BoundsHardTwo-pointer with bad-element resetTrack last bad index, last minVal index, last maxVal index; count = max(0, min(lastMin,lastMax) - lastBad)
41HardLC 2972Count Incremovable Subarrays IIHardTwo-pointer binary searchFind longest non-decreasing prefix and suffix; two-pointer to count valid middle removal ranges
42⚠️TrapLC 121⚠️ Best Time to Buy and Sell StockEasy⚠️ Trap — not SW, use running minNo sliding window; track min price seen; profit = price - min_so_far at each step
43⚠️TrapLC 487⚠️ Max Consecutive Ones IIMed✅⚠️ Trap — SW works but DP needed for streamSliding window with 1-flip allowed works for array; follow-up (stream input) requires DP
44⚠️TrapLC 325⚠️ Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals kMed✅⚠️ Trap — prefix sum + HashMapNegatives present; sliding window loses monotone property; use prefix sum + earliest-index HashMap
45⚠️TrapLC 523⚠️ Continuous Subarray SumMed⚠️ Trap — prefix sum mod + HashMapUse prefix sum modulo k; if same remainder seen before, subarray sum is multiple of k
Pattern Triggers
Trigger PhraseSub-VariantWhen It BREAKS
Find longest/shortest subarray/substring satisfying conditionVariable-size windowNegatives in sum problem; non-monotonic condition
Find all subarrays of size K with property XFixed-size windowNeed elements, not just length/sum
Count subarrays where exactly K elements satisfy XExact-K = atMost(K) − atMost(K−1)Direct single-window approach (always wrong)
Maximum/minimum of all windows of size KMonotonic dequeUsing nested scan O(nk) instead of deque
Replace at most K elements to maximize run of XReplacement: size − maxFreq ≤ kDecreasing maxFreq when shrinking (wrong)
80% Coverage Set
Minimum 10 problems: LC 209LC 3LC 424LC 1004LC 992LC 239LC 76LC 1838LC 862LC 560
Red Flags
🚩 Negatives in sum problem — The shrink invariant breaks; negative values destroy monotonic property. Use prefix-sum + HashMap instead.
🚩 Not updating maxFreq correctly — In #424, maxFreq is intentionally NOT decreased when shrinking. Decreasing it leads to wrong answer.
🚩 Off-by-one in fixed window — After init loop, right should be k-1, not k. Always verify first window indices.
🚩 Forgetting to remove outgoing element — When left advances, decrement count of nums[left] and delete key if count reaches 0.
🚩 Directly solving "exactly K" with single window — Writing if distinct == k: shrink is wrong. Always use exactly(K) = atMost(K) − atMost(K-1).
05
Kadane's Algorithm
Algorithms Technique · DP
28 problems · max subarray, circular, product, deletion, 2D, tree variants
Why It Matters: Kadane's Algorithm is a linear-time DP technique that finds the maximum-sum contiguous subarray by maintaining one invariant: at each index i, compute the best subarray sum ending exactly at i — either extend the previous best or start fresh. The recurrence maxEndingHere = max(nums[i], maxEndingHere + nums[i]) is deceptively simple, yet it underpins problems spanning circular arrays, products, 2D matrices, absolute values, and DP-on-tree variants. It appears consistently in top-tier interviews because (a) naive O(n²) is trivially wrong, (b) the optimal is O(n), and (c) extensions like circular arrays and product subarrays test whether a candidate truly understands the invariant or has merely memorised the code.
Core Invariant: maxEndingHere = max(nums[i], maxEndingHere + nums[i]) — start fresh when maxEndingHere < 0 because a negative prefix can never help. Global answer = max over all maxEndingHere values.

State: dp[i] = max subarray sum ending at i  ·  Recurrence: dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i−1] + nums[i])  ·  Preferred: Bottom-up, O(1) space with rolling variables (maxEndingHere, maxSoFar). Top-down adds call-stack overhead for zero benefit.
Sub-Variants to Master
Standard max-sum subarray Return indices (start, end) All-negative array handling Minimum sum subarray (inverse Kadane's) Maximum product subarray Circular array maximum sum Max sum with size constraint (≥k or ≤k) At-most-one deletion Max sum no two adjacent (⚠️ NOT Kadane's) 2D matrix max-sum rectangle Max subarray divisible by K Maximum absolute sum subarray Count subarrays with maximum sum Max sum two non-overlapping subarrays Maximum alternating sum subarray Kadane's on a tree / path in tree (DFS) Kadane's DP + monotonic deque (gap ≤ k) Character-pair variance
DP State Definitions
Classic (LC 53): dp[i] = max subarray sum ending at i
Recurrence: dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i−1] + nums[i])  ·  Preferred: bottom-up, O(1) space

Deletion variant (LC 1186):
dp_noDelete[i] = max sum ending at i, no deletion used
dp_oneDelete[i] = max sum ending at i, exactly one element deleted
dp_noDelete[i] = max(nums[i], dp_noDelete[i−1] + nums[i])
dp_oneDelete[i] = max(dp_noDelete[i−1], dp_oneDelete[i−1] + nums[i])  ← delete nums[i] OR continue after a prior deletion
Preferred: bottom-up, O(1) rolling variables
Problem Set
#LayerLinkProblemDiffPSub-VariantKey Insight
1FoundationLC 53Maximum SubarrayMedStandard Kadane'smaxEndingHere = max(nums[i], maxEndingHere + nums[i]); init maxSoFar = nums[0] for all-negative support; O(n)/O(1)
2FoundationLC 152Maximum Product SubarrayMedProduct: track max AND minNegative × negative = positive; propagate both maxProd and minProd and swap on each negative element
3FoundationLC 918Maximum Sum Circular SubarrayMedCircular arrayAnswer = max(normalKadane, totalSum − minKadane); edge case: all-negative array → return normalKadane only (circular gives 0)
4VariantsLC 1749Maximum Absolute Sum of Any SubarrayMedMax absolute summax(maxSubarraySum, |minSubarraySum|); run both max and min Kadane's in one pass
5VariantsLC 1186Maximum Subarray Sum with One DeletionMedAt-most-one deletiondp_noDelete[i] and dp_oneDelete[i] = max(dp_noDelete[i−1], dp_oneDelete[i−1] + nums[i]); must stay non-empty
6VariantsLC 1191K-Concatenation Maximum SumMedRepeated / concatenated arrayk=1: plain Kadane's; k=2: Kadane on doubled array; k>2: k=2 answer + (k−2)×max(0, totalSum)
7VariantsLC 2321Maximum Score of Spliced ArrayMedDual-array max gain via Kadane'sSwapping [l,r] of nums1 with nums2 gains sum(nums2−nums1)[l..r]; run Kadane's on the diff array for both directions
8VariantsLC 2393Count Strictly Increasing SubarraysMedCounting subarrays (Kadane's counting variant)Track current run length; each run of length L contributes L×(L+1)/2 subarrays; subtract previous run's contribution
9Variants🔧 CustomMinimum Sum SubarrayMedMin Kadane's (inverted comparisons)Replace max with min in every comparison; init minEndingHere = nums[0]; isolates all-positive edge case; see custom statement below
10Variants🔧 CustomMax Subarray Sum with Length ≥ KMedKadane's + prefix sum (size-constrained)prefix[i] − min(prefix[0..i−k]); track running minimum of prefix sums as i advances; see custom statement below
11ComboLC 363Max Sum of Rectangle No Larger Than KHard2D Kadane + sorted-set binary searchFix column boundaries; compress rows to 1D; TreeSet finds largest prefix sum ≤ K; Hard: 4-layer nested solution (2D→1D→prefix→BST) non-trivial to implement correctly
12ComboLC 1074Number of Submatrices That Sum to TargetMed2D Kadane + HashMap prefix sumFix row boundaries; compress to 1D column prefix sums; count subarrays summing to target using HashMap
13ComboLC 689Maximum Sum of 3 Non-Overlapping SubarraysHardK non-overlapping subarrays via prefix Kadane arraysPrecompute left-best and right-best window arrays; scan middle window; Hard: off-by-one index tracking and lexicographic tie-breaking across three windows
14ComboLC 1031Maximum Sum of Two Non-Overlapping SubarraysMedTwo non-overlapping fixed-size windowsBuild maxL[i] (best L-window ending at/before i) and maxM[i] (best M-window ending at/before i); scan once to combine
15ComboLC 1537Get the Maximum ScoreHardTwo-path Kadane's + sync at common valuesWalk both sorted arrays; at shared values take max accumulated sum; Hard: race between two paths syncing only at common nodes requires mod arithmetic
16ComboLC 2414Longest Alphabetical Continuous SubstringMedKadane's on char-score differencesTrack run of consecutive alphabet chars; max-run Kadane's with check c == prev + 1
17ComboLC 238⚠️ Product of Array Except SelfMed⚠️ Looks like product Kadane's — it is NOTNo division allowed; product Kadane's doesn't apply; use prefix-product × suffix-product arrays
18HardLC 124Binary Tree Maximum Path SumHardKadane's on tree (DFS post-order)gain = max(0,L)+max(0,R)+val is global candidate; return only max(L,R)+val upward; Hard: local return differs from global update
19HardLC 1696Jump Game VIMedKadane's DP + monotonic dequedp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) for j ∈ [i−k, i−1]; deque holds sliding window max of dp values
20HardLC 2272Substring With Largest VarianceHardKadane's on character frequency differencesFor each (a,b) pair: +1 for a, −1 for b; Kadane's with "≥1 b seen" guard; Hard: O(26²) pairs × state dimension
21HardLC 1425Constrained Subsequence SumHardKadane's + deque (gap ≤ k)dp[i] = nums[i] + max(0, windowMax); Hard: gap-constrained subsequence → sliding-window-max DP, not O(nk)
22HardLC 2770Max Jumps to Reach Last IndexMedKadane-style DP with value-range constraintdp[i] = max(dp[j]+1) for j where |nums[i]−nums[j]| ≤ target; linear scan with running max
23Hard🔧 CustomMax Subarray Sum Divisible by KHardKadane's + modular arithmetic + HashMapPrefix sums mod k; same remainder at j and i → sum divisible by k; maximize via earliest HashMap; Hard: modular prefix + Kadane spirit is non-obvious
24⚠️TrapLC 198⚠️ House RobberMed⚠️ Non-adjacent DP — NOT Kadane'sCannot take adjacent elements; use dp[i] = max(dp[i−1], dp[i−2] + nums[i])
25⚠️TrapLC 300⚠️ Longest Increasing SubsequenceMed⚠️ Non-contiguous — Kadane's is wrongLIS skips elements; contiguous invariant violated; use patience sort / O(n log n) DP
26⚠️TrapLC 560⚠️ Subarray Sum Equals KMed⚠️ Exact-sum counting; shrink fails with negativesExact sum + negatives → no monotonic shrink; use prefix sum + HashMap
27⚠️TrapLC 56⚠️ Merge IntervalsMed⚠️ Interval merging — nothing to do with subarraysVisual similarity to "running max" fools some; this is sort + greedy, not Kadane's
28⚠️TrapLC 862⚠️ Shortest Subarray with Sum ≥ KHard⚠️ Negatives + minimise lengthNegatives destroy monotonic shrink; use prefix sums + monotonic deque
Custom Problem Statements
🔧 #9 — Minimum Sum Subarray: Given integer array nums, find the minimum sum of any non-empty contiguous subarray. Constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10⁵, −10⁴ ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10⁴. Example: [2,−3,4,−1,−2] → −3. Key Insight: Replace max with min in every comparison; init minEndingHere = nums[0]; start loop at index 1.
🔧 #10 — Max Subarray Sum with Length ≥ K: Find max sum of any contiguous subarray of length ≥ k. Constraints: 1 ≤ k ≤ n ≤ 10⁵, −10⁴ ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10⁴. Example: n=6, k=3, [1,−1,2,3,−2,4] → 7 ([2,3,−2,4]). Key Insight: prefix[i] − min(prefix[0..i−k]); track rolling minimum of prefix sums as i advances.
🔧 #23 — Max Subarray Sum Divisible by K: Find max sum of non-empty contiguous subarray whose sum % k == 0. Constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10⁵, 1 ≤ k ≤ 100. Example: n=5, k=3, [2,7,−4,3,2] → 9 ([2,7]). Key Insight: HashMap of remainder → earliest prefix index; prefix[j]−prefix[i] with same mod gives the largest valid subarray.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Trigger PhraseSub-VariantWhen It BREAKS
"Find maximum/minimum sum of contiguous subarray"Standard Kadane's; single pass, two variablesNever breaks for plain max/min sum
"...in a circular array / array can wrap around"Circular: max(normalMax, total − minSubarray)All-negative: circular gives 0 (empty) → fall back to normalMax
"Maximum product of contiguous subarray"Track both maxProd AND minProdZeros reset both; treat zeros as natural reset points
"Maximum sum path through any node in a binary tree"Tree Kadane's (DFS post-order)Can't return both branches to parent — return only one branch upward
"You can delete at most one element / skip one"Two-state DP: withDeletion + withoutDeletionMore than one deletion → generalised deletion-count DP state
"Max sum of exactly/at least/at most K length subarray"Kadane's + prefix sums with rolling minimumExact length K only → fixed sliding window, not Kadane's
"Substring with largest variance / frequency difference"26² pair Kadane's; map char → +1/−1More than two types still requires pair enumeration
"Max score jumping with gap limit k"Kadane's DP + monotonic deque for sliding-window maxNo gap limit → plain DP or greedy
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 Non-adjacent / non-contiguous requirement — "No two adjacent elements" (House Robber) changes the recurrence to dp[i] = max(dp[i−1], dp[i−2] + nums[i]); Kadane's "extend or restart" is invalid.
🚩 Exact sum required — "Find subarray with sum = k" with negatives: Kadane's shrink logic breaks. Use prefix sum + HashMap.
🚩 Count of all subarrays satisfying a condition — Kadane's finds one optimal subarray; counting all requires prefix map or at-most decomposition.
🚩 Maximum subarray of exact fixed length — Use a fixed sliding window of size k; Kadane's variable-length nature doesn't apply.
🚩 Empty subarray allowed when all values are negative — If empty subarray is permitted the answer is 0; standard Kadane's (init to nums[0]) returns negative. Clarify the empty-subarray contract.
🚩 Product with zeros — Max product subarray needs special handling: zeros reset both maxProd and minProd. Failing to handle zeros gives incorrect results.
🚩 2D problem without column-compression — Running Kadane's row-by-row misses cross-row rectangles. Fix column boundaries → compress rows → apply 1D Kadane's.
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (in order): #1 → #2 → #3 → #4 → #5 → #9 → #10 → #6 → #7 → #14 → #12 → #18 → #20 → #21 → #11 → #13
Problems 1–3 nail the three canonical forms (sum, product, circular). Problems 4–5 expand the state space. Custom #9–#10 force first-principles derivation, not memorisation. #18 (tree path sum) appears directly in Google interviews. #20 (largest variance) is the hardest recognition problem; do it after all Medium variants. #11 closes the 2D chapter.

Phase 2 — Drill: Before coding each problem, explicitly write the Kadane's state variables: "What does maxEndingHere mean here? When do I reset? When do I update global?" Practise the 1D→2D transition: given any 2D sum problem, immediately compress to 1D using fixed column boundaries — this should become automatic. Always init to nums[0], never 0, unless empty subarray is explicitly allowed.

Phase 3 — Validate: AtCoder ABC/ARC — search "maximum subarray" (several problems disguise Kadane's in scoring games). USACO Silver "Hoof, Paper, Scissors" — multi-state transitions extending Kadane's DP. HackerRank DP set: "Max Array Sum" and "The Maximum Subarray" test all-negative and non-adjacent variants back-to-back.
80% Coverage Set
Minimum 10 problems: LC 53LC 152LC 918LC 1749LC 1186Custom #10LC 1074LC 1031LC 124LC 2272
Red Flags
🚩 Initialising maxSoFar = 0 instead of nums[0] — Returns 0 for all-negative arrays, implying the empty subarray was chosen. Always init both variables to nums[0] and start the loop at index 1.
🚩 Not tracking both maxProd AND minProd in the product variant — Negative × negative = large positive; tracking only maxProd loses this when a second negative appears. Swap both on every negative element.
🚩 Circular array: not handling the all-negative edge case — totalSum − minSubarraySum = 0 (empty array) when all elements are negative, which is invalid. If maxNormal < 0, return maxNormal directly.
🚩 Tree path sum: returning both branches to the parent — leftGain + rightGain + node.val is the global candidate, but you can only return max(leftGain, rightGain) + node.val upward. Returning both breaks parent path continuity.
🚩 Deletion variant: wrong initialisation of withDeletion[0] — withDeletion[0] should be 0 (delete first element) or −∞ depending on empty-subarray policy; wrong init cascades through the entire DP.
🚩 Treating "at most one deletion" as a sliding window — The two-state DP (withDelete, withoutDelete) is correct; a sliding window doesn't generalise when the deletion can be anywhere.
🚩 Missing max(0, gain) in the tree variant — Always take max(0, childGain) when propagating from children. Forgetting this includes loss-making subtrees in the path sum.
06
Merge Intervals
Algorithms Greedy · Sweep · DP · Segment Tree
37 problems · merge, insert, sweep, coverage, weighted DP, online CRUD, 2-D, circular
Why It Matters: Merge Intervals models the fundamental computer science primitive of managing contiguous ranges on a linear axis — time slots, memory addresses, genomic base pairs, network port ranges, log timestamps. The core invariant is devastatingly simple: after sorting by start, two closed intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap iff c ≤ b. Every single variant in this topic — from classic merge to sweep-line to segment trees — is a direct consequence of this one inequality. It appears at Google, Meta, Stripe, and Jane Street because it tests greedy reasoning, proof justification, and the ability to escalate a simple idea into an online/streaming/2-D context under pressure.
The Mental Model
Think of intervals as paint strokes on a number line. Merging is asking: after all strokes dry, what are the distinct painted segments? Every sub-variant reduces to this image:
• Classic merge → what are the final painted segments?
• Count overlaps → at which point is the paint thickest?
• Insert interval → add one new stroke, re-report the segments
• Remove overlaps → erase minimum strokes so no two wet strokes touch
• Gap detection → where are the unpainted gaps?
• Weighted scheduling → which strokes to keep to maximise total value?

When stuck on any interval problem, return to this image and ask: "What am I computing about this paint?"
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch
Invariant: After sorting intervals by start time, for any two consecutive intervals Ii = [ai, bi] and Ii+1 = [ai+1, bi+1], they overlap iff ai+1 ≤ bi.

Proof sketch (why sorting by start is sufficient): Suppose intervals are sorted: a1 ≤ a2 ≤ ... ≤ an. If Ii and Ij overlap with j > i+1, then aj ≤ bi. But ai+1 ≤ aj ≤ bi, so Ii and Ii+1 also overlap. Therefore, any non-consecutive overlap is caught by a chain of consecutive overlaps.

Consequence: We never need to look back more than one step — the greedy single-pass is complete.
Corollary: The merged result is unique (order of merging does not matter), which justifies the greedy approach's correctness without needing an exchange argument.
Sub-Variants to Master (18 total with complexity)
1. Classic merge — collapse overlapping into disjoint set · O(n log n) · sorted array + linear scan
2. Insert & merge — insert one into already-merged list · O(n) · three-phase walk
3. Count overlaps / min rooms — max active at any point · O(n log n) · min-heap of end-times OR diff array + prefix
4. Interval removal (Activity Selection) — remove fewest to eliminate overlaps · O(n log n) · sort by end + greedy keep
5. Interval coverage — cover [s,e] with fewest intervals · O(n log n) · sort by start, greedy extend reach
6. Gap / missing-range detection — unpainted segments within boundary · O(n log n) · sorted merge + sentinels
7. Interval intersection — present in BOTH sorted lists · O(m+n) · two-pointer
8. Interval subtraction — subtract one interval from another · O(n) · linear scan + fragment split
9. Weighted non-overlapping selection — maximise total weight · O(n log n) · DP + binary search (Weighted Job Scheduling)
10. Point stabbing — count intervals containing query point · O((n+q) log n) · diff array + coord compression
11. Online interval insert — streaming merged state · O(log n) per insert · TreeMap / balanced BST
12. Online interval CRUD — add/query/remove dynamically · O(log n + k) per op · sorted map of disjoint intervals
13. Sweep-line + active set — skyline, rectangle area · O(n log n) · sorted multiset / segment tree
14. Range update, point query — apply +val to [l,r] · O(n+q) · difference array + prefix sum
15. Range update, range query — simultaneous update/query · O(n log n) per op · segment tree with lazy propagation
16. 2-D interval merge (rectangle union) — union area · O(n log n) · sweep + coord compression + segment tree
17. Circular intervals — wrap around modular domain · O(n log n) · split + linear merge + restitch
18. Multi-list interval merge — K sorted lists simultaneously · O(N log n) · heap or flatten + sort
Problem Set
#LayerLinkProblemDiffPSub-VariantKey Insight
1FoundationLC 56Merge IntervalsMedClassic mergeSort by start; maintain running end = max(end, cur[1]); append when gap appears
2FoundationLC 57Insert IntervalMedInsert & mergeThree-phase walk: copy before, merge overlapping, copy after; no sort needed
3FoundationLC 435Non-overlapping IntervalsMedInterval removal (Activity Selection)Sort by end; greedily keep earliest-ending; answer = n − kept
4FoundationLC 986Interval List IntersectionsMedInterval intersectionTwo pointers; intersection = [max(a,c), min(b,d)] if valid; advance pointer with smaller end
5FoundationLC 252Meeting RoomsMed✅Overlap detection (binary)Sort by start; any intervals[i].start < intervals[i-1].end = conflict
6VariantsLC 253Meeting Rooms IIMed✅Count overlaps / min roomsMin-heap of end-times; heap size at peak = answer; equivalent to sweep +1/−1
7VariantsLC 452Minimum Arrows to Burst BalloonsMedInterval removal (touching = overlap)Same as 435 but cur.start <= prev.end (not strict <); one comparator change, different semantics
8VariantsLC 763Partition LabelsMedDisguised merge on char-range intervalsBuild [first, last] per char; merge; partition boundaries = gaps between merged blocks
9VariantsLC 1272Remove IntervalMed✅Interval subtractionEach existing interval produces ≤ 2 fragments: part before cut-start, part after cut-end
10VariantsLC 1229Meeting SchedulerMed✅Intersection + gap with duration filterIntersect two availability lists; first intersection with length ≥ duration is answer
11VariantsLC 228Summary RangesMedGap / range compressionCompress consecutive integers into [a,b] ranges; foundation for missing-range detection
12VariantsLC 163Find Missing RangesMed✅Gap detection with boundary sentinelsWalk sorted list; emit gap [prev+1, cur−1] whenever it exists; handle lo/hi sentinels
13VariantsLC 2406Divide Intervals Into Minimum GroupsMedCount overlaps via sweep-lineEvents: +1 at start, −1 at end+1; sort events; running sum peak = groups needed
14VariantsLC 1094Car PoolingMedDifference array on sparse axis+passengers at pickup, −passengers at dropoff; check no prefix sum exceeds capacity
15VariantsLC 759Employee Free TimeHard✅Multi-list merge + gap detectionFlatten all employees' schedules, merge globally, return gaps; Hard: input is list-of-lists requiring flatten before merge
16Variants🔧 CustomCircular Interval MergeMedCircular intervalsSplit [L,R] where L>R into [L,M−1] and [0,R]; linear merge; restitch boundary; see custom statement below
17ComboLC 1235Maximum Profit in Job SchedulingHardWeighted non-overlapping (DP + binary search)Sort by end; dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], profit_i + dp[bisect(ends, start_i)]); Hard: fuses DP state design, sort, and binary search into one algorithm
18ComboLC 370Range AdditionMed✅Difference array (range update, point query)d[l]+=val, d[r+1]-=val; prefix sum at end; each interval is one update operation
19ComboLC 729My Calendar IMedOnline insertion (BST sorted set)TreeMap: find predecessor/successor; new interval must not overlap either neighbour
20ComboLC 731My Calendar IIMedOnline insertion + double-booking trackingMaintain second list of overlapping intervals; new interval must not intersect it
21ComboLC 352Data Stream as Disjoint IntervalsHardOnline point insert → merge with neighboursEach addNum merges with at most 2 neighbours in sorted set; Hard: bridging two existing intervals simultaneously has subtle boundary cases
22ComboLC 218The Skyline ProblemHardSweep-line + sorted multiset / lazy-delete heapEvents at starts (add height) and ends (remove height); query current max after each event; Hard: removing stale heights from a max-heap requires lazy deletion or a sorted multiset
23ComboLC 699Falling SquaresHardOnline interval union + range max (segment tree)Each square's height = side + max height in its x-range; range-max query + range-max update; Hard: requires segment tree with lazy propagation for range-max, not a plain merge
24ComboLC 732My Calendar IIIHardOnline intervals + range update, global maxDifference array or segment tree with lazy propagation; must answer max-overlap count after each insert online; Hard: online constraint forbids offline sort; lazy segment tree is required
25ComboLC 715Range ModuleHardOnline interval CRUD (sorted map)Sorted map of disjoint intervals; add, query, remove all in O(log n + k); Hard: three operations must co-exist with correct boundary arithmetic and no off-by-one
26ComboLC 2158Amount of New Area Painted Each DayHard✅Online merge + painted-length delta trackingJump-pointer: after painting a segment, store pointer to next unpainted position; Hard: naïve revisit is O(n²); amortised O(n log n) only with the jump-pointer
27ComboLC 850Rectangle Area IIHard2-D interval merge (sweep + coord compression)Sweep vertical lines; per x-slab compute y-coverage via 1-D merge of active y-intervals; Hard: 2-D extension requires coordinate compression on both axes and active-set management
28HardLC 630Course Schedule IIIHardGreedy scheduling + max-heap evictionSort by deadline; max-heap of durations; if budget exceeded, evict the longest course (not the current one); Hard: eviction-of-largest insight is non-obvious and cannot be derived from standard interval merge intuition
29HardCF 915EPhysical Education LessonsHardOnline interval union on huge axis (set of free intervals)Maintain std::set of free intervals; each "mark used" query shrinks set and tracks total free count; Hard: axis up to 2×10⁹ forbids arrays; must use a balanced BST of live free-interval objects
30HardUSACO SilverHaybale StackingHardDifference array with range-addEach operation adds 1 to [l, r]; final array is prefix sum of difference array; USACO-level because n up to 10⁶ and 10⁵ operations must all be O(n) total
31HardUSACO SilverLifeguardsMedWeighted interval removal (remove exactly 1 to minimise coverage loss)Merge all intervals; identify contribution of each to total coverage; removing a non-overlapping interval loses its full length; removing a partially-overlapping one loses overlap delta
32Hard🔧 CustomPoint-Stabbing Count QueriesHardPoint stabbing offlineCoordinate-compress all endpoints + query points; difference array +1 at L, −1 at R+1; prefix sums; binary search each query; Hard: combining compression + diff array + binary search for per-query O(log n); see statement below
33Hard🔧 CustomMinimum Interval Coverage of Target RangeMedInterval coverage (fewest intervals to cover [s,e])Sort by start; greedy: among intervals with start ≤ current_covered_end+1, pick one maximising end; see statement below
34⚠️TrapLC 312⚠️ Burst BalloonsHard⚠️ Interval DP, not mergeIntervals [i,j] are DP sub-problems, NOT ranges to merge; "last balloon burst" inversion → dp[i][j] = max coins; Hard: backwards DP formulation is counter-intuitive and unrelated to sweep/merge
35⚠️TrapLC 1288⚠️ Remove Covered IntervalsMed⚠️ Containment ≠ overlapSort by start desc then end asc; check b ≤ max_end_seen, NOT standard overlap condition; applying classic merge logic gives wrong count
36⚠️TrapLC 480⚠️ Sliding Window MedianHard⚠️ "Window" is index range, not value intervalTechnique is dual-heap balance (two heaps maintaining lower/upper halves); do not model as interval merge; Hard: independent of interval topic entirely
37⚠️Trap🔧 Concept⚠️ Weighted "merge" intervalsHard⚠️ Greedy merge fails with valuesIf a problem adds "profit per interval, maximise total profit of non-overlapping subset", classic merge is WRONG; use Weighted Job Scheduling DP (see #17); Hard: misclassification is the most common interview mistake at Hard level
Custom Problem Statements
🔧 #16 — Circular Interval Merge: Given a circle with M units (0 to M−1) and N intervals [L_i, R_i]; an interval is circular if L_i > R_i (wraps around). Return the merged disjoint intervals covering the union. If the full circle is covered, return ["FULL"]. Constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 10⁵, 2 ≤ M ≤ 10⁹, 0 ≤ L_i, R_i < M. Example: M=24, [[22,3],[1,5],[4,10],[15,20]] → [[1,10],[15,20],[22,3]]. Key Insight: Split every circular [L,R] with L>R into [L, M−1] and [0, R]. Run standard linear merge over [0, M−1]. Then re-stitch: if first merged interval starts at 0 and last ends at M−1, merge them into one circular interval. O(n log n).
🔧 #32 — Point-Stabbing Count Queries: Given N intervals [L_i, R_i] and Q query points p_j, return for each query the count of intervals containing p_j. Constraints: 1 ≤ N, Q ≤ 10⁵, 1 ≤ L_i ≤ R_i ≤ 10⁹. Example: intervals=[[1,5],[2,8],[4,10],[7,12]], queries=[3,6,11] → [2, 3, 2]. Key Insight: Coordinate-compress all endpoints + query points. Create difference array: +1 at L_i, −1 at R_i+1. Prefix-sum gives count at each compressed coordinate. For each query, binary search its compressed position. O((N+Q) log N).
🔧 #33 — Minimum Interval Coverage of Target Range: Given a target range [S, E] and N intervals [L_i, R_i], find the minimum number of intervals needed to completely cover [S, E]. Each interval may be used at most once. If impossible, return -1. Constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 10⁵, −10⁹ ≤ S ≤ E ≤ 10⁹. Example: S=1, E=10, [[1,3],[2,6],[5,10],[8,12]] → 2. Key Insight: Sort intervals by start. Greedy: at each step, among all intervals with start ≤ current_covered_end + 1, pick the one maximising end. Advance current_covered_end. O(n log n).
Complexity Cheat Sheet
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceKey Bottleneck
Classic mergeO(n log n)O(n)Sort dominates
Insert & mergeO(n)O(n)Linear scan of existing list
Count overlaps (heap)O(n log n)O(n)Heap operations
Count overlaps (sweep)O(n log n)O(n)Sort events
Interval removal (greedy)O(n log n)O(1) extraSort by end
Interval coverage (greedy)O(n log n)O(1) extraSort by start
Gap detectionO(n log n)O(n)Sort + linear scan
Interval intersectionO(m + n)O(m + n)Two-pointer
Interval subtractionO(n)O(n)Linear scan
Weighted job schedulingO(n log n)O(n)DP + binary search
Point stabbing (offline)O((n+q) log n)O(n+q)Coordinate compression
Online insert (TreeMap)O(log n) per opO(n)BST operations
Online CRUD (Range Module)O(log n + k) per opO(n)Boundary merging
Sweep + multiset (Skyline)O(n log n)O(n)Multiset insert/delete
Segment tree lazy (Calendar III)O(log n) per opO(n)Lazy propagation
2-D sweep (Rectangle Area)O(n log n)O(n)Coord compression + seg tree
Circular intervalsO(n log n)O(n)Split + merge + restitch
Difference array (Range Add)O(n + q)O(n)Prefix sum pass
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers (keywords in problem statement):
• "merge all overlapping intervals" → Classic merge; sort by start, greedy extend
• "minimum number of rooms / machines / conference halls" → Count max simultaneous active intervals; sweep-line or min-heap
• "non-overlapping" or "no two intervals overlap" → Either removal (greedy) or weighted selection (DP)
• "free time / available slots / gaps" → Merge first, enumerate gaps between merged blocks
• "insert … into sorted list of intervals" → Three-phase walk (before, overlap, after)
Structural Triggers (shape of input/output):
• Input is int[][] intervals or List<List<Integer>> of [start, end] pairs → Almost always an interval problem
• Output is a list of intervals → Almost certainly merge or insert variant
• Output is a single integer count where input is intervals → Sweep-line / difference array
Constraint Triggers (N and value ranges):
• N ≤ 10⁵ and values up to 10⁹ → Coordinate compression required; cannot use plain array for difference array
• N ≤ 10⁵ and online queries → TreeMap / sorted set, not re-sort every time
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem involve ranges/intervals [start, end] on a linear axis? → NO: Not an interval problem. Stop. → YES: Continue. Q2: Are intervals given all at once (offline) or arrive one-by-one (online)? → OFFLINE: Continue to Q3. → ONLINE: Go to Q8. Q3: Do you need to COMBINE/COLLAPSE intervals? → YES: Q4. → NO: Q5. Q4: Do the intervals carry values/profits? → NO: Classic Merge (#1) or Insert (#2). → YES: Weighted Job Scheduling DP (#17). Q5: Do you need to COUNT something (rooms, overlaps, groups)? → YES: Sweep-line with +1/−1 events OR min-heap of end-times (#6, #13). → NO: Q6. Q6: Do you need to REMOVE intervals (minimise overlaps)? → YES: Greedy Activity Selection — sort by end (#3, #7). → NO: Q7. Q7: Do you need COVERAGE (cover a target range with fewest intervals)? → YES: Greedy interval covering — sort by start, extend reach (#33). → NO: GAPS? → Gap detection (#11, #12). INTERSECTION of two lists? → Two-pointer (#4). SUBTRACTION? → Fragment scan (#9). Q8 (Online): Do you need only INSERT and QUERY overlap? → YES: TreeMap / SortedList BST (#19, #20). → NO: Q9. Q9 (Online): Do you need INSERT + REMOVE + QUERY? → YES: Range Module / sorted map of disjoint intervals (#25). → NO: Q10. Q10 (Online): Do you need RANGE UPDATE + RANGE MAX QUERY? → YES: Segment Tree with lazy propagation (#24, #23). → NO: SKYLINE / HEIGHT tracking? → Sorted multiset (#22).
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 Intervals have weights and you want maximum total value — Greedy merge selects wrong intervals. Use Weighted Job Scheduling DP (binary search + DP sorted by end-time).
🚩 Intervals arrive online with O(log n) per-op requirement — Static sort-and-merge is O(n) rebuild per insert. Use TreeMap / SortedList for O(log n) predecessor/successor lookup.
🚩 Axis is huge (up to 10⁹) and you need a difference array — Plain array of size 10⁹ causes MLE. Use coordinate compression or a Fenwick tree on compressed coordinates.
🚩 Circular/modular intervals — Sort-by-start fails at wrap-around. Split circular intervals at 0/M boundary, run linear merge, then restitch.
🚩 Intervals are 2-D (rectangles) — 1-D merge gives wrong area. Use sweep-line on x-axis + 1-D interval merge/segment tree on y-axis.
🚩 Containment detection (is A fully inside B?) — Standard overlap check c ≤ b catches containment AND partial overlap — you need the stricter check a ≤ c AND d ≤ b. Classic merge logic silently gives wrong answer here.
🚩 "Burst Balloons" style problems — The intervals represent DP sub-problems, not ranges to merge. Use Interval DP (dp[i][j]), not sweep-line.
🚩 Streaming with hard memory limits — Storing all intervals for offline sort is impossible. Use reservoir-style online merge or accept approximate answers with a bounded-error sketch.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
1. Classic Greedy Merge (#1)
Claim: The sort + single-pass algorithm produces the unique minimum set of disjoint intervals covering the same union.
Invariant: After processing the first k intervals (sorted by start), result contains the correct merged intervals for those k intervals.
Inductive step: At interval k+1 with start s and end e: if s > result.last.end, no previously processed interval reaches s (since all starts ≤ s by sort), so k+1 is disjoint → append. If s ≤ result.last.end, they overlap; max(result.last.end, e) is the correct merged end because no future interval (start ≥ s) can "fill" a gap inside [result.last.start, s−1].
Uniqueness: The merged output is unique because the union set is fixed and the greedy produces the coarsest partition.
2. Activity Selection / Non-overlapping Removal (#3)
Claim: Greedy "keep the interval with earliest end-time" maximises the number of kept non-overlapping intervals.
Exchange argument: Let G = greedy solution, O = any optimal, both sorted by end-time. If they agree on the first k intervals but differ at k+1 — G picks g, O picks o with end(g) ≤ end(o) (greedy choice). Swap o for g in O: g doesn't overlap with O's (k+2)-th interval (since end(g) ≤ end(o) and O was valid). Count doesn't decrease.
Conclusion: G can be transformed into O without reducing count → G is optimal.
3. Weighted Job Scheduling DP (#17)
Claim: dp[i] = max(dp[i−1], profit[i] + dp[p(i)]) where p(i) = latest job ending before start[i] gives maximum profit.
Observation: For job i, either it is NOT included (best = dp[i−1]) or it IS included (best = profit[i] + best using only jobs ending before start[i] = dp[p(i)]). These two cases are exhaustive and disjoint, so dp[i] is correct.
Complexity: p(i) found via binary search on end-times in O(log n), giving O(n log n) total.
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 — Forgetting to append the final in-progress interval
# BUG (running variables variant)
start, end = intervals[0]
for cur in intervals[1:]:
    if cur[0] <= end:
        end = max(end, cur[1])
    else:
        result.append([start, end])
        start, end = cur
# FIX: append the last interval AFTER the loop
result.append([start, end])
Bug 2 — Wrong overlap condition (strict vs non-strict)
# BUG: misses touching intervals like [1,3],[3,5]
if cur[0] < end:   # should be <=
# FIX:
if cur[0] <= end:
Bug 3 — Sorting by wrong key (start vs end)
# BUG for Activity Selection (Non-overlapping Intervals)
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])  # sort by START — wrong for greedy removal
# FIX: sort by END
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
Bug 4 — Mutating input array in-place (breaks caller)
# BUG
intervals.sort()  # mutates original
# FIX
intervals = sorted(intervals, key=lambda x: x[0])
Bug 5 — Meeting Rooms II: push without popping (room not reused)
# BUG: allocates new room even when one is free
for start, end in intervals:
    heapq.heappush(heap, end)
# FIX: pop if earliest-ending room is free
for start, end in intervals:
    if heap and heap[0] <= start:
        heapq.heapreplace(heap, end)
    else:
        heapq.heappush(heap, end)
Bug 6 — Insert Interval: off-by-one in overlap check
# CORRECT non-overlap:
if new[0] > interval[1] or new[1] < interval[0]:
# BUG variant (excludes touching):
if new[0] >= interval[1] or new[1] <= interval[0]:
Bug 7 — Difference array: applying −1 at r instead of r+1
# BUG
diff[l] += val
diff[r] -= val    # should be r+1
# FIX
diff[l] += val
diff[r + 1] -= val
Bug 8 — Weighted Job Scheduling: binary searching wrong array
# BUG: binary searching on start times
idx = bisect_right(starts, jobs[i][0]) - 1
# FIX: binary search on END times to find last job ending before current start
idx = bisect_right(ends, jobs[i][0]) - 1
Bug 9 — Integer overflow in rectangle area
// BUG: int * int for large coordinates
int area = width * height;          // overflows if width, height > 46340
// FIX
long area = (long) width * height;
Bug 10 — My Calendar I: checking only one neighbour
# BUG: only checks predecessor
prev = tree.floor(start)
if prev and prev.end > start: return False
# FIX: must also check successor
nxt = tree.ceiling(start)
if nxt and nxt.start < end: return False
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
🚩 Applying greedy merge to weighted intervals — Classic merge has no notion of value — it merges all overlapping intervals regardless. If any interval carries a weight and you need to SELECT a subset, you must use Weighted Job Scheduling DP. This is the single most common wrong approach at the Hard level.
🚩 Confusing "overlap" with "containment" — [1,10] and [3,5] overlap AND contain each other. Problems asking to remove covered intervals (LC 1288) require checking a ≤ c AND d ≤ b, not just c ≤ b. Using the overlap check here silently over-counts removals.
🚩 Forgetting the heap pop in Meeting Rooms II — Not popping before pushing means you never reuse a freed room, inflating the answer. Always check heap[0] ≤ cur.start before deciding to push vs. heapreplace.
🚩 Online vs offline conflation — Collecting all intervals and sorting at the end is wrong for streaming problems (My Calendar, Range Module). It breaks the online contract and will fail any follow-up that asks "what if intervals arrive in real time?"
🚩 Difference array with values up to 10⁹ — Allocating diff = [0] * (10**9 + 1) causes MLE. Coordinate-compress the endpoints first, then build the difference array on compressed indices only.
🚩 Not handling the empty input or single-interval edge cases — Many interval bugs surface only when len(intervals) == 0 or == 1. Always handle these before the loop.
🚩 Circular interval wraparound — Treating [22, 3] on a 24-hour clock as a normal interval with start > end causes incorrect sort ordering and false non-overlap conclusions. Always split first.
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
#Follow-Up QuestionIntended Answer Direction
1"What's the time complexity? Can you do better than O(n log n)?"Sort is the bottleneck; O(n log n) is optimal in comparison-based model (proven by sort lower bound). If input is already sorted, O(n).
2"What if intervals are given as a stream — you can't store all of them?"Use a balanced BST (TreeMap); insert each interval and merge with neighbours in O(log n). Memory = O(k) where k = current distinct merged intervals.
3"What if N = 10⁹ intervals?"Can't store; need a streaming/summarisation approach. If intervals are uniform random, reservoir sampling. If structured (e.g. monotone starts), sliding window. Discuss trade-off between exactness and memory.
4"How would you parallelise this for a distributed system?"Partition intervals by coordinate ranges across machines; each machine merges its partition; a coordinator merges boundary intervals between adjacent partitions. O(n/k) per machine, O(k) merge at coordinator.
5"What if intervals can be added AND removed dynamically?"Range Module (#25): sorted map of disjoint intervals. Each add/remove is O(log n + k) where k = number of intervals affected.
6"For every point x, how many intervals contain x?"Offline: difference array + coordinate compression, O((n+q) log n). Online per-query: Fenwick tree / segment tree on compressed coordinates.
7"What if endpoints are floating point?"Algorithm is identical — float comparison works the same. Beware epsilon issues; use exact rational arithmetic or a problem-specific epsilon if needed.
8"Prove your greedy is correct for Activity Selection."Exchange argument: any optimal solution can have its choices swapped one-by-one with greedy's choices without reducing the count (see Proof section).
9"How would you handle 2-D intervals (rectangles)?"Sweep line on x-axis; maintain active y-intervals in a segment tree; compute y-coverage via interval merge on segment tree after each x-event. O(n log n).
10"What if the problem asks for the MEDIAN overlap count at any point, not the max?"Sweep-line with sorted multiset of depths; at each event record the depth; find median of all recorded depths. Or offline: sort events, track depth array, find median via order statistics.
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (Build Core Intuition):
Order: #1 → #2 → #3 → #7 → #4 → #6 → #13 → #14 → #8 → #12 → #17 → #19 → #20 → #24 → #25 → #22 → #28
#1 internalises the one invariant: c ≤ b = overlap. #2 masters the three-phase walk. #3 teaches exchange-argument proof for greedy. #7 is the one-character difference from #3. #4 is two-pointer on two sorted lists. #6 min-heap pattern (prove heap size = room count). #13 sweep-line variant — solve BOTH ways. #14 difference array; link to intervals. #8 first disguised interval; practise recognition. #12 gap detection with sentinels. #17 full Weighted Job Scheduling; implement DP + bisect. #19, #20, #24 My Calendar I/II/III — online-insertion ramp. #25 Range Module — hardest online CRUD. #22 Skyline — implement with BOTH heap and multiset. #28 Course Schedule III — eviction-of-largest insight.

Phase 2 — Drill (Speed + Pattern Recognition):
Re-solve #1, #3, #6, #17 on a timer: under 12 minutes each from blank editor. For every problem solved, write its one-line invariant AND its decision-tree path (which Q in the Decision Framework does it resolve at?). Do blind classification: take 10 random LeetCode interval problems; before reading constraints carefully, classify them to a sub-variant using only the title and first sentence. Check classification rate. Implement difference array and sweep-line patterns from memory without referencing any code.

Phase 3 — Validate (Unseen Sources):
AtCoder ABC/ARC problems tagged "intervals" — solve any 5 unseen. Codeforces Div 2 D/E with tags sortings + greedy — solve 3 problems where statements don't mention "interval" but solution requires it. USACO Silver/Gold: "Haybale Stacking", "Lifeguards", "Rental Service" without editorial. Google Kickstart / Hash Code archives — search scheduling or resource-allocation rounds.
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 minutes each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LeetCode 1851: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query. Why valid: requires simultaneously sorting intervals by SIZE (not start), sorting queries offline, using a min-heap to track active intervals, and answering each query at the right moment. Tests offline sorting, sweep + heap, and query answering fused into one problem in a non-obvious way. A candidate who has only memorised solutions will not recognise the approach.

Test 2 — Codeforces 1000C: Covered Points Count. Why valid: endpoints up to 10¹⁸ — far beyond any array. Requires coordinate compression, difference array, prefix sum, and careful output of (point, depth) pairs for all points with depth ≥ 2. Pure sweep-line problem disguised as geometry. The 10¹⁸ constraint eliminates any candidate who hasn't internalised coordinate compression as mandatory.

Test 3 — USACO Gold (2019 February): Springboards. Why valid: interval problem fused with DP and 2-D coordinate queries. You must find, for each springboard, the best previous springboard whose interval "dominates" it, combining sweep-line with a Fenwick tree for 2-D range minimum queries. Highest-difficulty fusion of interval thinking with advanced data structures — the exact ceiling of what Google/Meta Hard interviews reach. Solving it cold demonstrates that your mastery is not just interview-deep but competition-deep.
07
Prefix Sums
Algorithms Pre-computation · Hash Map · Difference Array · 2D · BIT
30 problems · 5 stages · range queries, difference arrays, prefix XOR, 2D, dynamic BIT
Why It Matters: Prefix Sums is the foundational pre-computation primitive of competitive programming. The core invariant is: sum(i, j) = prefix[j+1] − prefix[i] — any contiguous subarray sum reduces to a single O(1) subtraction after O(n) preprocessing. This single identity unlocks an enormous class of problems: range sum queries, subarray count problems, 2D rectangle queries, difference arrays for range updates, and hash-map-augmented subarray problems. It appears in virtually every Google/Meta interview round because it tests whether a candidate instinctively pre-computes rather than recomputes — a fundamental signal of algorithmic maturity. At the ICPC/IOI level, prefix sums compose with segment trees, sparse tables, and Fenwick trees to form the backbone of nearly all range query algorithms.
The Mental Model
A prefix sum array is a "running water level" — prefix[i] is the total water accumulated up to position i. The water between two banks i and j is simply the level at j minus the level at i. You measure the river once; you answer every query in one subtraction.

How the mental model scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1: prefix[j+1] − prefix[i] = sum of arr[i..j]. One array, one subtraction.
• Stage 2: The "water" is not integers but modular residues, XORs, or parities. The subtraction becomes a modular operation or XOR — same shape, different algebra.
• Stage 3: The river becomes a 2D lake. prefix[r][c] = total water in the rectangle from (0,0) to (r,c). Inclusion-exclusion replaces the single subtraction.
• Stage 4: The problem never says "subarray sum." You must recognise that a condition like sum(i,j) = k is secretly prefix[j] − prefix[i] = k → prefix[i] = prefix[j] − k. The water level is hidden inside a counting or existence question.
• Stage 5: There are 10⁹ positions but only 10⁵ updates. The river is sparse. A Fenwick tree or segment tree replaces the static array — the identity prefix[j] − prefix[i] still holds, but each level query is now O(log n).
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch
Invariant: For a 1-indexed array a, define prefix[0] = 0 and prefix[i] = a[1] + a[2] + ... + a[i]. Then for any 0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n:
    sum(i+1, j) = prefix[j] − prefix[i]
