Proof sketch (by telescoping): By definition, prefix[j] = a[1] + ... + a[j] and prefix[i] = a[1] + ... + a[i]. Subtracting: prefix[j] − prefix[i] = a[i+1] + ... + a[j] — the shared prefix a[1..i] cancels exactly.

Generalisation: This telescoping works for any associative operation with an inverse: addition (subtraction), XOR (XOR again), multiplication (division if non-zero). It does NOT work for max/min because there is no inverse — this is the fundamental limitation.

2D corollary: rect(r1,c1,r2,c2) = prefix[r2][c2] − prefix[r1−1][c2] − prefix[r2][c1−1] + prefix[r1−1][c1−1] by inclusion-exclusion on the same telescoping identity applied twice.
Sub-Variants to Master (15 total)
1. 1D range sum query — Stage 1 · O(n) build, O(1) query · static array · New idea: prefix array definition; telescoping subtraction
2. 1D range sum with modular/XOR algebra — Stage 2 · O(n) build, O(1) query · static array · New idea: the "subtraction" changes algebra (XOR is its own inverse; mod requires careful handling)
3. Subarray sum equals K (count) — Stage 2 · O(n) · hash map on prefix values · New idea: count pairs (i,j) where prefix[j] − prefix[i] = k → frequency map of prefix values
4. Subarray sum divisible by K (modular) — Stage 2 · O(n) · hash map on prefix mod K · New idea: (prefix[j] − prefix[i]) % K = 0 → same prefix mod → group by remainder
5. 2D rectangle sum query — Stage 3 · O(nm) build, O(1) query · 2D prefix array · New idea: inclusion-exclusion on four corners; prefix is now 2-indexed
6. 2D rectangle sum count / submatrix target — Stage 3 · O(n²m) with hash map · 2D prefix + hash map · New idea: fix two rows, reduce to 1D subarray-sum-equals-K on column sums
7. Difference array (range add, point query) — Stage 3 · O(n+q) · difference array + prefix sum · New idea: prefix sum's INVERSE operation — store differences, recover values via prefix sum
8. Difference array 2D (rectangle add, point query) — Stage 3 · O(nm+q) · 2D difference array · New idea: 2D inclusion-exclusion on the difference array
9. Prefix sum on transformed values (sqrt, log, boolean) — Stage 4 · O(n) · static array · New idea: the array being summed is not the raw input — requires a transformation first
10. Prefix XOR (subarray XOR queries) — Stage 2 · O(n) build, O(1) query · static array · New idea: XOR is its own inverse, so xor(i,j) = prefix_xor[j] ^ prefix_xor[i−1]
11. Prefix XOR + hash map (count subarray XOR = k) — Stage 4 · O(n) · hash map · New idea: same shape as subarray-sum-equals-K but with XOR algebra; requires recognising the reduction
12. Sparse prefix sum (dynamic updates + queries) — Stage 5 · O(log n) per op · Fenwick tree / segment tree · New idea: prefix array is no longer static; each update requires partial reconstruction
13. Prefix sum on a tree (path sum queries) — Stage 4 · O(n) DFS build, O(1) query with LCA · Euler tour + static prefix · New idea: "prefix" runs down a root-to-node path; sum(u,v) = depth[u] + depth[v] − 2·depth[LCA]
14. Sliding window prefix (fixed or variable length) — Stage 4 · O(n) · prefix array + two pointers · New idea: prefix enables O(1) window sum; two-pointer finds the valid window bounds
15. Circular prefix sum — Stage 2 · O(n) · prefix array with modular indexing · New idea: wrap-around subarray splits into two segments; handle via total_sum − complement
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
1D range sum queryO(n) build + O(1)/queryO(n)Baseline
Modular / XOR algebraO(n) + O(1)/queryO(n)Algebra of "subtraction" changes
Subarray sum = K (count)O(n)O(n)Add hash map on prefix values
Subarray sum % K = 0O(n)O(n)Hash map keys are remainders, not values
2D rectangle sumO(nm) + O(1)/queryO(nm)Second index dimension added
2D submatrix sum = targetO(n²m)O(nm)Compress rows, reduce to 1D via hash map
Difference array 1DO(n+q)O(n)Reverse direction: differences → prefix sum
Difference array 2DO(nm+q)O(nm)2D inclusion-exclusion on differences
Prefix XOR + hash mapO(n)O(n)XOR replaces addition; same hash-map pattern
Tree path prefix sumO(n) DFS + O(log n) LCAO(n)Linear → tree topology; needs LCA
Sparse / dynamic updatesO(log n)/opO(n)Static array → Fenwick/segment tree
Mastery Problem Set (30 problems · 5 stages)
#LayerStageSourceProblemDiffPSub-Variant / New IdeaKey Insight
1Foundation1LeetCodeLC 303 Range Sum Query ImmutableMed1D range sum — THE baseline prefix[i] definitionBuild prefix[0..n] with sentinel; query(i,j) = prefix[j+1] − prefix[i]
2Foundation2LeetCodeLC 560 Subarray Sum Equals KMed1D sum + hash map — count pairsprefix[j] − prefix[i] = k → look up prefix[j] − k in map; running frequency map
3Foundation2LeetCodeLC 974 Subarray Sums Divisible by KMedModular prefix sum — group by remainder(prefix[j] − prefix[i]) % K = 0 ↔ same remainder; negative mod pitfall
4Foundation2LeetCodeLC 523 Continuous Subarray SumMedModular prefix, existence + length constraintCheck prefix%K seen before index i−1; store first-occurrence index not count
5Foundation2LeetCodeLC 525 Contiguous ArrayMedBoolean transform + prefix sumTransform 0→−1; longest subarray with sum=0 becomes prefix[i] = prefix[j]
6Variants2LeetCodeLC 1480 ⚠️ Running Sum of 1D ArrayEasy⚠️ TRAP — literally the prefix array itselfIncluded to recognise trivial cases fast; if the ANSWER is the prefix array, no query structure needed
7Variants2LeetCodeLC 1588 Sum of All Odd Length SubarraysMedPrefix sum counting — contribution per indexCount how many odd-length subarrays contain index i; sum by contribution rather than by range
8Variants2LeetCodeLC 1413 Minimum Value to Get Positive Step by Step SumMedPrefix minimum — lowest water levelmin(prefix[0..n]) gives lowest level; offset so it never drops below 1
9Variants3LeetCodeLC 304 Range Sum Query 2D ImmutableMed2D prefix sum — inclusion-exclusion on 4 cornersprefix[r][c] = arr + top + left − top-left; query uses 4-point formula
10Variants3LeetCodeLC 1074 Number of Submatrices That Sum to TargetHard2D prefix + hash map — row-pair compressionFix row pair, apply 1D subarray-sum=K on column sums; Hard: O(n²m) combines #2 + #9
11Variants3LeetCodeLC 370 Range AdditionMed✅Difference array 1D — invert the directiondiff[l] += val, diff[r+1] −= val; final array = prefix sum of diff
12Variants3LeetCodeLC 1094 Car PoolingMedDifference array with capacity feasibilityPrefix sum of diff must never exceed capacity; sparse axis (stops ≤ 1000)
13Variants3🔧 Custom2D Range Add, Point QueryMed2D difference array — inclusion-exclusion on 4 cornersdiff[r1][c1] += v, diff[r1][c2+1] −= v, diff[r2+1][c1] −= v, diff[r2+1][c2+1] += v; see statement below
14Variants3LeetCodeLC 238 Product of Array Except SelfMedPrefix product — no division allowedTwo-pass prefix/suffix product; division explicitly banned → forces the two-array approach
15Combo3LeetCodeLC 1352 Product of the Last K NumbersMedPrefix product with dynamic appends; zero-resetWhen a zero is appended, reset prefix product list; zero invalidates all history before it
16Combo4LeetCodeLC 1590 Make Sum Divisible by PMedModular prefix + minimum window — hidden reductionLooks like sum; is secretly subarray-removal: prefix[j] − prefix[i] ≡ total%P; hash map on residues
17Combo4LeetCodeLC 1124 Longest Well-Performing IntervalMedTransform + prefix + monotone stackTransform hours>8→+1, ≤8→−1; longest prefix[j]>prefix[i] via monotone stack for O(n)
18Combo4LeetCodeLC 2281 Sum of Total Strength of WizardsHardPrefix of prefix (second-order accumulation)Hard: prefix[prefix[]] for sum of all subarray sums with fixed minimum; combined with monotone stack; double-prefix insight is non-obvious
19Combo4LeetCodeLC 1177 Can Make Palindrome from SubstringMed✅Prefix XOR on bitmask — character parityprefix_mask[j] ^ prefix_mask[i] = character parity in [i,j]; bitwise prefix XOR on compact state
20Combo4LeetCodeLC 1915 Number of Wonderful SubstringsMedPrefix XOR bitmask + hash mapCount subarrays with at most one odd-frequency character; hash map on bitmask states + iterate all single-bit-flip variants
21Combo4LeetCodeLC 327 Count of Range SumHardPrefix sum + merge sort / BIT — range conditionHard: equality hash map fails for inequality; need sorted structure (merge sort on prefix or BIT with coord compression)
22Combo4LeetCodeLC 548 Split Array with Equal SumHard✅Prefix sum + set intersection — three-way splitHard: fix middle split point; check valid left & right splits exist via prefix sets; O(n²) with set-intersection insight
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 4 → Stage 5 jump. The next problems require dynamic prefix structures (Fenwick / BIT). Make sure you can implement a BIT from scratch before proceeding.
23Hard5LeetCodeLC 307 Range Sum Query MutableMedDynamic prefix (Fenwick tree)update(i, delta) and query(i) both O(log n); BIT uses lowbit trick i & (−i)
24Hard5LeetCodeLC 308 Range Sum Query 2D MutableHard✅2D dynamic prefix (2D BIT)Hard: 2D BIT with nested lowbit walks; update and query both O(log n · log m)
25Hard5LeetCodeLC 2407 Longest Increasing Subsequence IIHardPrefix max over value range (segment tree)Hard: "prefix" is now over VALUES not INDICES; map value → segment tree position; combines LIS DP with range-max query
26Hard5CodeforcesCF 295A Greg and ArrayMedSecond-order difference array — range-of-rangesApply difference array twice: operations apply to ranges of indices of operations; prefix-sum twice to recover
27Hard5USACO SilverHaybale StackingMedDifference array on large NAxis up to 10⁶; O(n+q) difference array; no coordinate compression needed; validates static vs dynamic choice
28⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 239 ⚠️ Sliding Window MaximumHard⚠️ TRAP — prefix-max has no inverse; use monotone dequemax(i..j) cannot be answered by prefix-max subtraction; use monotone deque for sliding window
29⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 862 ⚠️ Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least KHard⚠️ TRAP — inequality ≥ K; hash map fails; monotone deque on prefix≥ K (not = K) → hash map lookup fails; monotone deque on prefix array for O(n); equality→hash map, inequality→deque
30⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 53 ⚠️ Maximum SubarrayMed⚠️ TRAP — prefix works but Kadane's is canonicalUse Kadane's O(1) space; max(prefix[j] − min(prefix[0..j−1])) works but reaching for prefix here signals over-application
Custom Problem Statements
🔧 #13 — 2D Range Add, Point Query: Given an N×M grid initialised to all zeros, process Q operations. Each operation is (r1, c1, r2, c2, v): add v to every cell (r, c) where r1 ≤ r ≤ r2 and c1 ≤ c ≤ c2. After all operations, answer P point queries: what is the value at cell (r, c)?
Constraints: 1 ≤ N, M ≤ 1000, 1 ≤ Q, P ≤ 10⁵, 1 ≤ v ≤ 100.
Example: N=3, M=3; ops=[(1,1,2,2,5), (2,2,3,3,3)]; queries=[(1,1),(2,2),(3,3)] → [5, 8, 3].
Intended solution: Maintain 2D difference array diff[N+2][M+2]. For each op (r1,c1,r2,c2,v):
  diff[r1][c1] += v; diff[r1][c2+1] −= v; diff[r2+1][c1] −= v; diff[r2+1][c2+1] += v
After all ops, compute 2D prefix sum of diff to recover the grid. O(NM + Q) total.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "sum of elements between index i and j" → Stage 1 — direct range query; build prefix array
• "number of subarrays with sum equal to k" → Stage 2 — prefix[j] − prefix[i] = k; hash map on prefix values
• "add v to all elements in range [l,r], then query point" → Stage 3 — difference array (inverse prefix)
• "submatrix sum" or "rectangle sum" → Stage 3 — 2D prefix with inclusion-exclusion
• "count subarrays where [some property of sum holds]" → Stage 4 — transform property into prefix condition; check what algebra applies
Structural Triggers:
• Input is an array + multiple (l, r) range queries with no updates → Stage 1/2, static prefix array
• Input is an array + multiple (l, r, v) range updates + point queries → Stage 3, difference array
• Input is a 2D grid + rectangle queries or updates → Stage 3, 2D prefix or 2D difference array
Constraint Triggers:
• N ≤ 10⁵ with Q ≤ 10⁵ queries AND updates → Stage 5; static prefix rebuild is O(n) per update → use Fenwick tree
• Values up to 10⁹ and N ≤ 10⁵ with no updates → Stage 1–4; static prefix is fine
Anti-Triggers (when NOT to use prefix sums):
🚩 "maximum subarray" or "maximum of subarray [i..j]" → NOT prefix sum (max has no inverse) → Kadane's algorithm or sparse table (static) or segment tree (dynamic)
🚩 "number of subarrays with sum ≥ k" → NOT hash-map prefix (inequality ≠ equality) → Monotone deque on prefix (LC 862) or binary search + BIT
🚩 "sum of all subsets" → NOT prefix sum; it's a bitmask/combinatorics problem counting each element's contribution across 2ⁿ subsets
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem ask about a contiguous range (subarray, submatrix, path segment) of an array/grid? → NO: Not a prefix sum problem. Stop. → YES: Continue. Q2: Are there UPDATES to array values between queries? → YES: Go to Q8 (dynamic prefix — Stage 5). → NO: Continue. Q3: Is the query asking for RANGE SUM (or equivalent: XOR, product, count of property)? → NO: Go to Q7. → YES: Continue. Q4: Is the answer a single range sum value? → YES: Stage 1 — build prefix array, answer = prefix[j+1] − prefix[i]. Expected complexity: O(n) build + O(1)/query. → NO: Continue (you need to count pairs or find a target). Q5: Is the condition an EQUALITY (sum = k, sum % k = 0, XOR = k)? → YES: Stage 2 — hash map on prefix values. Key: transform "sum(i,j) = k" → "prefix[j] − k exists in map". Expected complexity: O(n). → NO: Go to Q6. Q6: Is the condition an INEQUALITY (sum ≥ k, sum in [lo, hi])? → YES: ⚠️ Hash map BREAKS here. Stage 5 — monotone deque (if sliding window) or merge sort / BIT with coordinate compression (if arbitrary). Expected complexity: O(n log n). → NO: Recheck problem statement. Q7: Are you applying RANGE UPDATES (add v to [l,r]) and then reading point values? → YES: Stage 3 — difference array. diff[l] += v, diff[r+1] −= v; final values = prefix sum of diff. Expected complexity: O(n + q). → NO: Is the input 2D? → YES: Stage 3 — 2D prefix or 2D difference array. → NO: Does the problem have a hidden linear structure? → YES: Stage 4 — identify the transformation first. Q8 (Dynamic): Do you need POINT UPDATES + PREFIX QUERIES? → YES: Stage 5 — Fenwick tree (BIT). O(log n) per op. → NO: Do you need RANGE UPDATES + RANGE QUERIES? → YES: Stage 5 — Segment tree with lazy propagation. Expected complexity: O(log n) per op. Stage identification summary: Q4 YES → Stage 1. O(n) build, O(1) query. Q5 YES → Stage 2. O(n) total. Q7 YES → Stage 3. O(n+q). Hidden transform needed → Stage 4. O(n) after transform. Updates present → Stage 5. O(log n) per op. If your solution is slower than these bounds, look for the Stage 5 data structure optimisation.
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 Range MAX or MIN queries (no inverse exists) — max(i..j) cannot be expressed as f(prefix[j]) − f(prefix[i]) because max has no inverse operation. [Stage 1–2 mistake] → Sparse Table (O(1), static) or Segment Tree (O(log n), dynamic).
🚩 Inequality conditions on subarray sums (≥ k, ≤ k, ∈ [lo,hi]) — Hash map lookup requires exact equality. [Stage 2 mistake] → Monotone Deque for contiguous window variants; Merge Sort on Prefix Array / BIT with coordinate compression for arbitrary ranges.
🚩 Dynamic updates to the array (online, streaming) — Rebuilding the entire prefix array after each update is O(n) per update = O(nq) total. [Stage 5 mistake] → Fenwick Tree (BIT) for point updates + prefix queries; Segment Tree for range updates + range queries.
🚩 Non-associative operations (e.g. GCD, LCM of a range) — GCD has no simple inverse (gcd(a,b) − gcd(a,c) ≠ gcd(b,c)). [Stage 1 mistake] → Sparse Table for static GCD/LCM range queries in O(1).
🚩 2D difference array with overlapping boundaries — A common Stage 3 mistake is applying 1D logic to 2D — forgetting the +v at (r2+1, c2+1) (inclusion-exclusion term). [Stage 3 mistake] → Always derive the 2D formula from first principles: four corners, alternating signs.
🚩 Circular/wrap-around subarrays — prefix[j] − prefix[i] breaks when the subarray wraps around index 0. [Stage 2 mistake] → Handle as total_sum − (non-wrapping complement), or double the array and apply standard prefix on arr + arr.
🚩 Very large axis (up to 10⁹) with sparse updates — A static array of size 10⁹ causes MLE. [Stage 5 mistake] → Coordinate-compress endpoints to O(q) distinct values, then apply difference array or BIT on compressed indices.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — 1D Range Sum (Telescoping Invariant)
Claim: prefix[j] − prefix[i] = a[i+1] + ... + a[j] for all 0 ≤ i ≤ j.
Proof by definition and telescoping: Define prefix[0] = 0; prefix[k] = prefix[k−1] + a[k] for k ≥ 1. Then prefix[j] − prefix[i] = (a[1]+...+a[j]) − (a[1]+...+a[i]) = a[i+1]+...+a[j]. □
Note: Correctness is unconditional — no edge cases beyond index bounds.
Stage 2 — Subarray Sum Equals K (Hash Map Correctness)
Claim: The running hash-map algorithm counts exactly the number of pairs (i,j) with i < j and sum(i+1..j) = k.
Proof by invariant maintenance:
• Invariant: at step j, freq[v] = number of indices i ≤ j with prefix[i] = v.
• At each step, we query freq[prefix[j] − k] = number of i < j with prefix[i] = prefix[j] − k = number of valid left endpoints for subarrays ending at j.
• We initialise freq[0] = 1 (empty prefix at i=0), ensuring subarrays starting from index 1 are counted.
• We update freq[prefix[j]] AFTER querying, preventing i = j from being counted. □
Stage 3 — Difference Array (Inverse Correctness)
Claim: After applying all (l, r, v) operations to diff, computing prefix_sum(diff) recovers the final array value at every index.
Proof by linearity: Each operation contributes v to every index in [l, r]. Encoding as diff[l] += v, diff[r+1] −= v means prefix_sum(diff)[i] = Σ v over all operations where l ≤ i ≤ r. This holds because for operation (l, r, v), prefix sum accumulates v starting at l (where it was added) and stops at r+1 (where it was subtracted). The contribution to index i is v iff l ≤ i ≤ r. □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 (Stage 1) — Off-by-one: 0-indexed vs 1-indexed prefix array 
# BUG: prefix has same length as arr; prefix[0] = arr[0], not 0
prefix = [0] * n
for i in range(n): prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]  # prefix[-1] wraps!
# FIX: use n+1 length with sentinel
prefix = [0] * (n + 1)
for i in range(n): prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + arr[i]
# query sum(i,j): prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]
Bug 2 (Stage 2) — Subarray sum = K: updating map BEFORE querying 
# BUG: counts i==j (empty subarray matching k=0 incorrectly)
for j in range(n):
    freq[prefix[j]] += 1           # update first
    count += freq[prefix[j] - k]   # then query — counts prefix[j] itself
# FIX: query first, then update
for j in range(n):
    count += freq[prefix[j] - k]
    freq[prefix[j]] += 1
Bug 3 (Stage 2) — Modular prefix: negative modulo in Java/C++ 
// BUG in Java: (-1) % 5 = -1, not 4
int rem = prefix % k;
// FIX: normalise remainder
int rem = ((prefix % k) + k) % k;
Bug 4 (Stage 2) — Circular subarray: using prefix directly without complement 
# BUG: prefix[j]-prefix[i] doesn't handle wrap-around
# FIX: max subarray sum in circular array
max_wrap = total - min_subarray_sum(arr)   # complement approach
answer = max(max_linear, max_wrap)
Bug 5 (Stage 3) — 2D prefix: wrong query formula (missing −1 on boundaries) 
# BUG
query = prefix[r2][c2] - prefix[r1][c2] - prefix[r2][c1] + prefix[r1][c1]
# FIX (1-indexed prefix with sentinel row/col of zeros)
query = prefix[r2][c2] - prefix[r1-1][c2] - prefix[r2][c1-1] + prefix[r1-1][c1-1]
Bug 6 (Stage 3) — Difference array: applying −v at r instead of r+1 
# BUG
diff[l] += v
diff[r] -= v      # wrong: subtracts one position too early
# FIX
diff[r + 1] -= v
Bug 7 (Stage 3) — 2D difference array: missing the (+v) at (r2+1, c2+1) 
# BUG: only three of the four corners updated
diff[r1][c1]     += v
diff[r1][c2+1]   -= v
diff[r2+1][c1]   -= v
# MISSING: diff[r2+1][c2+1] += v  ← inclusion-exclusion demands this
# FIX: all four corners
diff[r2+1][c2+1] += v
Bug 8 (Stage 4) — Prefix XOR: using subtraction instead of XOR for range query 
# BUG
xor_range = prefix_xor[j] - prefix_xor[i-1]   # subtraction is wrong for XOR
# FIX: XOR is its own inverse
xor_range = prefix_xor[j] ^ prefix_xor[i-1]
Bug 9 (Stage 5) — Fenwick tree: querying prefix sum instead of range sum 
# BUG: range sum query returns prefix[r] - prefix[l] (should be prefix[l-1])
def range_sum(l, r):
    return query(r) - query(l)       # off by one: excludes a[l]
# FIX
    return query(r) - query(l - 1)
Bug 10 (Stage 5) — Fenwick tree: 0-indexed array with 1-indexed BIT (index shift) 
# BUG: updating BIT at index 0 causes infinite loop in lowbit walk
bit = [0] * (n + 1)
def update(i, delta):
    while i <= n:            # if i starts at 0, loop never executes
        bit[i] += delta
        i += i & (-i)
# FIX: always 1-index the BIT; shift all input indices by +1
update(i + 1, delta)
query(i + 1)
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
🚩 [Stage 1] Initialising prefix array of size n instead of n+1 — Without the sentinel prefix[0] = 0, you cannot represent the subarray starting at index 0. Every range query is off by one. Correct model: "prefix[i] = sum of first i elements; prefix[0] = 0 always."
🚩 [Stage 2] Using hash map for inequality conditions (≥ k, ≤ k) — Hash maps only resolve exact equality. Correct model: "Inequality requires a sorted structure — deque, BIT, or merge sort."
🚩 [Stage 2] Not initialising freq[0] = 1 before the loop — This misses subarrays that start from index 0. A subarray [0..j] has sum = prefix[j] − 0 = prefix[j]. Correct model: "The empty prefix (before index 0) is a valid left boundary."
🚩 [Stage 3] Confusing difference array direction — Applying diff[l]+=v, diff[r]-=v (forgetting the +1 on r) when the interval is inclusive [l,r] subtracts one position too early, corrupting index r. Correct model: "Subtract at the position AFTER the endpoint — prefix sum must carry the value through r."
🚩 [Stage 4] Trying to apply prefix sums to MAX/MIN queries — A candidate drilled on prefix sums will attempt prefix_max[j] − prefix_max[i] for range max. Algebraically unsound. Correct model: "Only operations with an inverse (+, XOR, ×) support the prefix trick. Max/min have no inverse."
🚩 [Stage 5] Rebuilding static prefix after each update (O(n) per update) — For n=10⁵ and q=10⁵ updates, this is 10¹⁰ ops. Correct model: "Any update invalidates all subsequent prefix values — you need a data structure designed for dynamic prefixes."
🚩 [Stage 3] Computing 2D prefix sum in wrong order (column before row) — Building prefix[i][j] requires prefix[i-1][j], prefix[i][j-1], prefix[i-1][j-1] all finalised. Correct model: "Always iterate rows outer, columns inner — or verify all dependencies are filled before use."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
Stage 2 Follow-Ups (Constraint Mutations)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
1"Your solution handles sum = k. What if the condition is sum ≥ k?"Hash map breaks; need monotone deque on prefix array (LC 862 pattern). O(n).
2"What if the array contains negative numbers — does your approach still work?"Yes for sum=k with hash map (Stage 2); No for sliding window approaches that assume positivity. Clarify which variant is being asked.
Stage 3 Follow-Ups (State Expansion)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
3"Extend to 2D: count submatrices with sum = target."Fix two row boundaries (O(n²)), reduce to 1D subarray-sum-equals-K on column prefix sums. O(n²m).
4"Now add range-update operations: add v to all elements in [l,r], then answer sum queries."Difference array for updates (O(1)), prefix sum rebuilt after all updates — or BIT for mixed online update+query.
Stage 4 Follow-Ups (Hidden Reductions)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
5"The array has only 0s and 1s. Count subarrays with equal 0s and 1s."Transform: 0→−1, 1→+1. Count subarrays with sum=0. Now it's a Stage 2 problem.
6"Count subarrays where XOR of all elements equals k."Prefix XOR: xor(i..j) = prefix_xor[j] ^ prefix_xor[i−1]. Hash map on prefix XOR values. Same shape as Stage 2, different algebra.
Stage 5 Follow-Ups (Optimisation Pressure)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
7"Now the array is updated with point changes between queries. What changes?"Static prefix is O(n) rebuild per update. Use Fenwick Tree: O(log n) update + O(log n) prefix query.
8"What if you need both range-update AND range-sum queries dynamically?"Fenwick tree alone is insufficient. Use two BITs (the classic BIT range-update trick) or a segment tree with lazy propagation.
Beyond Stage 5 (Distributed / Adversarial)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
9"The array is distributed across 100 machines. How do you answer range sum queries?"Each machine stores a prefix sum of its local shard. Query the boundary machine and combine. Global prefix = sum of all shard totals up to the relevant machine.
10"An adversary is adding elements in real time; maintain the count of subarrays with sum=k at all times."Each new element a[n] extends the prefix by one value. Count new pairs: freq[prefix[n]−k] where freq is the running hash map. O(1) per new element — the online extension of LC 560.
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):
Stage 1 first: S.No 1 (303), S.No 9 (304), S.No 23 (307) in that order. Build the prefix identity in 1D, then 2D, then dynamic. Do not proceed until you can write all three from memory.
Stage 2 next: S.No 2 (560), S.No 3 (974), S.No 4 (523), S.No 5 (525). Each adds one mutation. Then S.No 19 (1177) for XOR algebra. Feel the hash-map pattern click into a single reusable template.
Stage 3 next: S.No 11 (370), S.No 12 (1094), S.No 13 (🔧 2D diff), S.No 14 (238), S.No 10 (1074). Difference array and 2D prefix in one pass.
Stage 4 next: S.No 16 (1590), S.No 17 (1124), S.No 20 (1915), S.No 21 (327), S.No 18 (2281). Hardest recognition problems — spend the most time here.
Stage 5 last: S.No 24 (308), S.No 25 (2407), S.No 26 (CF 295A).

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and recognition):
Stage 1: target under 8 min from blank editor. Stage 2: under 15 min. Stage 3: under 20 min. Stage 4: under 35 min. Recognition drill — given only the title, name the Stage before reading; aim for 90% accuracy. Template drill — write hash-map prefix template (Stage 2) and Fenwick tree (Stage 5) from memory 3 times each until zero lookup needed.

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources per Stage):
Stage 1: AtCoder ABC tagged "prefix-sums" — any 3 unseen. Stage 2: Codeforces Div 2 C/D tagged hashing + prefix — 3 problems. Stage 3: USACO Silver "Haybale Stacking" variants in recent contests. Stage 4: Google Kickstart archives — search "count subarrays" from 2019–2023. Stage 5: Codeforces Div 1 D tagged data structures + prefix — 2 problems.
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 min each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LC 1508: Range Sum of Sorted Subarray Sums (Stage 3 + 4)
Why valid: The candidate must first compute all subarray sums using prefix arrays, then binary search on a "how many subarray sums ≤ x" predicate using prefix sums again inside the binary search. Prefix sums are used TWICE at different levels of abstraction. A candidate who only knows Stage 1 will produce an O(n² log n) sort-based solution; Stage 4 mastery yields O(n log(sum) · n) with binary search.

Test 2 — Codeforces 1398C: Good Subarrays (Stage 2 + 4)
Why valid: The problem states a condition on subarrays that does not obviously involve sums. The candidate must derive that the condition implies a constraint on prefix values, then apply the counting technique. Solving cold means the candidate recognises prefix-sum applicability when it is not stated.

Test 3 — LC 2281: Sum of Total Strength of Wizards (Stage 4 + 5)
Why valid: Requires prefix sum of prefix sums (second-order accumulation) combined with a monotone stack — two advanced techniques fused without any hint from the problem statement. Passing cold demonstrates that the prefix-sum mental model scales to non-obvious compositions — the highest bar for mastery.
08
Backtracking
Algorithms Recursion · Constraint Search · Pruning · Bitmask · DLX
30 problems · 5 stages · subsets, permutations, grid, CSP, memoised backtracking
Why It Matters: Backtracking is systematic constrained search with undo — the universal algorithm for problems where you must explore a decision tree, commit to choices one at a time, and retract them when they violate constraints or exhaust possibilities. The core invariant is: at every node in the decision tree, the partial solution is valid; when a dead end is reached, exactly one decision is undone and the next alternative is tried. It appears in every top-tier interview because it tests recursive reasoning, state management, pruning intuition, and the ability to convert a combinatorial description into executable code. At ICPC/IOI, backtracking with aggressive pruning (branch-and-bound, constraint propagation) solves NP-complete problems like SAT, graph colouring, and exact cover — appearing in competitions disguised as scheduling, placement, or configuration problems.
The Mental Model
Backtracking is building a sentence word by word. At each position you try every valid word, write it down, move to the next position, and erase it when you get stuck — trying the next word instead. You never erase more than the last word you wrote.

How the mental model scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1: You choose from a fixed set at each position; every choice is independent. The "sentence" is a permutation or combination.
• Stage 2: One constraint flips — elements can repeat, or you can't reuse the same position, or order doesn't matter. The choice set shrinks by one rule.
• Stage 3: The state itself becomes 2-dimensional — you're filling a grid, a board, or making choices across two linked structures simultaneously.
• Stage 4: The problem doesn't say "enumerate" or "find all." It says "is it possible?" or "find minimum cuts." You must recognise that the search space IS a backtracking tree, hidden inside a constraint satisfaction or optimisation problem.
• Stage 5: The naive tree is too wide — exponential branches at every node. Pruning transforms it: constraint propagation eliminates branches before exploring them, memoisation collapses subtrees, or bitmask DP replaces the explicit tree.
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch
Invariant: At every recursive call backtrack(state, choices), the partial state satisfies all constraints imposed so far, and choices is exactly the set of valid extensions of state.

Soundness: Every solution added to the result satisfies all constraints because we only add when the base case is reached AND all constraints have been checked at each decision point. No invalid partial state is ever extended.

Completeness: Every valid solution is found because the algorithm explores ALL branches of the decision tree — it never skips a valid extension. Pruning only eliminates subtrees that can be proven to contain zero valid solutions.

Termination: The recursion depth is bounded by the solution length (at most n), and at each level the choice set strictly decreases (each element is either used or the index advances). The tree is finite.

Undo correctness: The undo step restores the state to exactly what it was before the choice, because backtracking modifies state in exactly one way per call and reverts that exact modification. This is the key implementation invariant — state before = state after for any failed branch.
Sub-Variants to Master (18 total)
1. Subsets / power set enumeration — Stage 1 · O(2ⁿ · n) · recursion with include/exclude · New idea: binary choice tree — include element or skip it
2. Combinations (choose k of n) — Stage 1 · O(C(n,k) · k) · recursion with start-index · New idea: start-index parameter prevents reusing earlier elements
3. Permutations (all orderings) — Stage 1 · O(n! · n) · recursion with visited array · New idea: visited/used array tracks which elements are placed
4. Subsets with duplicates — Stage 2 · O(2ⁿ · n) · sort + skip-duplicate · New idea: sort input first; skip nums[i]==nums[i−1] at same recursion level
5. Combinations with duplicates — Stage 2 · O(2ⁿ · k) · sort + skip-duplicate · New idea: same duplicate-skip logic applied to combinations
6. Combinations with reuse (unbounded) — Stage 2 · O(target/min · n) · same index allowed · New idea: pass i (not i+1) as start index to allow reuse
7. Permutations with duplicates — Stage 2 · O(n!/duplicates!) · sort + used array + dup check · New idea: skip if nums[i]==nums[i−1] AND used[i−1]==False (same-level skip)
8. Palindrome partitioning — Stage 3 · O(n · 2ⁿ) · recursion + palindrome precomputation · New idea: at each position, try all valid prefix lengths; validity = palindrome check
9. Word search on grid — Stage 3 · O(n·m·4^L) · DFS on grid with visited marking · New idea: state is 2D grid position + word index; visited marking replaces used array
10. N-Queens — Stage 3 · O(n!) · row-by-row with column/diagonal sets · New idea: constraint encoded as three sets (col, diag1, diag2); O(1) per placement
11. Sudoku solver — Stage 3 · O(9^m) where m = empty cells · cell-by-cell with row/col/box sets · New idea: constraint propagation (only try digits missing from row+col+box)
12. Graph colouring / m-colouring — Stage 3 · O(mⁿ) · vertex-by-vertex with adjacency check · New idea: constraint is graph-local (neighbours can't share colour)
13. Exact cover / Algorithm X — Stage 4 · O(solution-dependent) · dancing links / recursive cover · New idea: problem reduces to covering all columns of a binary matrix exactly once
14. Expression evaluation / operator insertion — Stage 4 · O(4^(n−1) · n) · recursion with running value · New idea: multiply requires tracking the last-added term to handle operator precedence
15. Constraint satisfaction (CSP) — Stage 4 · varies · backtracking + forward checking + arc consistency · New idea: propagation before each choice reduces future domains
16. Bitmask-pruned backtracking — Stage 5 · O(2ⁿ · n) · bitmask state + memoisation · New idea: state compressed into bitmask; memoisation collapses identical subtrees
17. Branch and bound — Stage 5 · exponential worst-case, fast in practice · upper bound + priority queue · New idea: prune branches where even the best possible completion can't beat current best
18. Dancing Links (DLX) — Stage 5 · O(solution-dependent) · doubly-linked list with O(1) cover/uncover · New idea: undo is O(1) via pointer manipulation instead of array copy
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
SubsetsO(2ⁿ · n)O(n)Baseline binary choice tree
Combinations k-of-nO(C(n,k) · k)O(k)Start-index prunes left branches
PermutationsO(n! · n)O(n)Visited array instead of start-index
With duplicates (any)O(same worst case)O(n)Sort + same-level skip; tree width reduced
With reuseO(target/min · n)O(target/min)Allows revisiting same index
Grid search (word search)O(n·m·4^L)O(L)2D state; 4-directional branching
N-QueensO(n!)O(n)Column/diagonal sets prune to O(n) checks
SudokuO(9^m)O(m)Box constraint; 3 sets per cell
Permutation + memo (bitmask DP)O(2ⁿ · n)O(2ⁿ · n)Memoisation collapses repeated subproblems
Branch and boundExponential worst, fast avgO(n)Upper-bound pruning; best-first traversal
Mastery Problem Set (30 problems · 5 stages)
#LayerStageSourceProblemDiffPSub-Variant / New IdeaKey Insight
1Foundation1LeetCodeLC 78 SubsetsMedPower set — include/exclude binary choicebacktrack(i, path): at each index either include nums[i] or skip; recurse with i+1; result.add(copy) at every node
2Foundation1LeetCodeLC 77 CombinationsMedk-of-n combinations with start-indexStart-index prevents revisiting; range(start, n−k+remaining+1) is the pruning bound
3Foundation1LeetCodeLC 46 PermutationsMedFull permutations — visited array replaces start-indexused[i]=True before recurse; used[i]=False after; every index reachable from every position
4Foundation2LeetCodeLC 90 Subsets IIMedSubsets with duplicates — sort + same-level skipSorting groups duplicates; skip at same level (i > start and nums[i]==nums[i−1]) prevents duplicate subsets without a hash set
5Foundation2LeetCodeLC 40 Combination Sum IIMedCombinations with duplicatesOne-character change from #4: collect only paths summing to target; same skip rule
6Foundation2LeetCodeLC 39 Combination SumMedCombinations with unbounded reuseChange i+1 to i in recursive call — element can be reused; termination guaranteed by remaining < 0 check
7Foundation2LeetCodeLC 47 Permutations IIMedPermutations with duplicatesif nums[i]==nums[i−1] and NOT used[i−1]: skip; the "not used" distinguishes same-level from different-level duplicates
8Variants2LeetCodeLC 17 Letter Combinations of Phone NumberMedMapped-alphabet combinationsReplace numeric choices with character mappings per digit; same backtracking skeleton with different choice sets
9Variants2LeetCodeLC 131 Palindrome PartitioningMedPartition into valid segmentsValidity check before recursing: only recurse if s[start:i+1] is a palindrome; each "choice" is a valid prefix length
10Variants2LeetCodeLC 93 Restore IP AddressesMedConstrained segmentationCompound constraint: segment 0–255 AND no leading zeros AND exactly 4 parts; bounded-depth tree (exactly 4 levels)
11Variants3LeetCodeLC 79 Word SearchMedGrid DFS with visited markingState = 2D grid position + word index; visited[r][c]=True before 4-dir recursion; False after; boundary + visited checks prune
12Variants3LeetCodeLC 980 Unique Paths IIIHardGrid traversal visiting ALL cellsHard: track count of visited cells and verify all cells covered at base case; changes pruning logic
13Variants3LeetCodeLC 51 N-QueensHardRow-by-row with 3 constraint setsHard: diagonal encoding via r−c and r+c is non-obvious; without it, O(n²) conflict checks per row
14Variants3LeetCodeLC 52 N-Queens IIHardN-Queens — count onlyCounter instead of board reconstruction; identical pruning; teaches separation of solution-generation vs counting
15Variants3LeetCodeLC 37 Sudoku SolverHardCell-by-cell with 3 constraint sets (row/col/box)Hard: box_id = (r//3)*3 + (c//3); three overlapping sets must all be checked; box index formula is implementation-critical
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 3 → Stage 4 jump. Next problems don't announce "use backtracking." You must recognise that the problem is a disguised search or partitioning problem. Practise identifying the decision-tree structure BEFORE reading solutions.
16Combo4LeetCodeLC 282 Expression Add OperatorsHardExpression building with operator precedenceHard: multiply requires cur − last + last*digit to handle precedence without re-evaluating; the "last term" tracking is non-obvious
17Combo4LeetCodeLC 140 Word Break IIHardSentence reconstruction from dictionaryHard: naïve O(2ⁿ); memoisation on start index reduces to O(n² · result); first memoisation layer on backtracking
18Combo4LeetCodeLC 294 Flip Game IIMed✅Game theory via backtrackingA position is winning iff any move leads to a losing position for opponent; exhaustive game-tree search
19Combo4LeetCodeLC 291 Word Pattern IIHard✅Pattern matching via bijection backtrackingHard: maintain two maps (pattern→word and word→pattern); invalid bijection must be caught early
20Combo4LeetCodeLC 425 Word SquaresHard✅Constraint-guided word selection with TrieHard: row i and column i share a prefix; Trie lookup of valid continuations is the key optimisation
21Combo4LeetCodeLC 351 Android Unlock PatternsMed✅Graph-constrained path enumerationSkip-point constraint: moving between two non-adjacent numbers requires the middle number to be visited; skip table precomputed
22Combo4CodeforcesCF 1097F Alex and a TV ShowHardExact cover recognitionReduce to "cover all required positions exactly once" — exact cover / Algorithm X structure; DLX not required but shows the structural generalisation
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 4 → Stage 5. Pure backtracking TLEs. You must add bitmask memoisation, branch-and-bound pruning, or Trie-accelerated pruning.
23Hard5LeetCodeLC 996 Number of Squareful ArraysHardBitmask-pruned permutation backtrackingHard: adjacency constraint (pair-sum = perfect square) drastically reduces valid next-choices; bitmask enables memoisation
24Hard5LeetCodeLC 1240 Tiling a Rectangle with the Fewest SquaresHardBranch-and-bound with best-so-far pruningHard: greedy (always largest square) is WRONG for some inputs; branch-and-bound required; non-obvious because greedy looks correct
25Hard5LeetCodeLC 488 Zuma GameHardBFS/backtracking hybrid with run-length pruningHard: state = string × hand; never insert alone — always insert to form a run of ≥ 2; without this, TLE
26Hard5LeetCodeLC 212 Word Search IIHardBacktracking + Trie pruningHard: shared Trie of all words replaces per-word DFS; O(n·m·4^L·W) → O(n·m·4^L); Trie traversed during DFS simultaneously
27Hard5USACO GoldCow PlacementHardBacktracking with forward-checking (geometric)Each placed cow constrains future positions via dominance ordering; forward checking eliminates cells before recursing; constraint propagation distinct from backtracking itself
28⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 22 ⚠️ Generate ParenthesesMed⚠️ TRAP — constrained DFS, no undo neededOpen + close counts are sufficient state; string is built immutably; if state fits in two integers and no undo is needed, it is constrained DFS — not backtracking
29⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 698 ⚠️ Partition to K Equal Sum SubsetsMed⚠️ TRAP — naïve backtracking TLEs; needs bitmask DPNaïve O(k^n); must add sort-descending + equal-bucket dedup OR convert to bitmask DP O(2ⁿ·n); if n ≤ 20 and TLE, reach for bitmask DP
30⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 10 ⚠️ Regular Expression MatchingHard⚠️ TRAP — memoised backtracking IS DP; restate as DPWhen memoised backtracking produces dp[i][j] state, restate as bottom-up DP — it's cleaner and expected
Custom Problem Statement
🔧 Bridge Problem — Backtracking with Constraint Propagation (Stage 4→5 bridge): You are given an N×N grid. Place exactly K non-attacking rooks such that no two rooks share a row or column, AND each rook must be placed in a white cell (cells are 0 = black or 1 = white). Return the count of valid placements.
Constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 8, 1 ≤ K ≤ N.
Example: N=3, K=2, grid=[[1,0,1],[0,1,0],[1,1,1]] → 6.
Intended solution: Backtrack row by row. At row r, try each column c where grid[r][c]==1 and col_used[c]==False. Mark col_used[c]=True, recurse to r+1, unmark. Base case: placed K rooks → count++. Pruning: if remaining_rows < K − placed, return (can't place enough). Bridges Stage 3 (N-Queens structure) into Stage 4 (recognising applicability in a new domain) with Stage 5 entry-level pruning.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "generate all / enumerate all / find all possible" → Stage 1/2 — exhaustive enumeration of a decision tree
• "partition into groups / segments / subsets satisfying [constraint]" → Stage 3/4 — partitioning backtracking; each decision splits the input
• "place N pieces on a board such that no two [conflict]" → Stage 3 — placement backtracking with conflict sets
• "is it possible to [reach / form / achieve] using [limited resources]" → Stage 4 — feasibility search; decision tree over resource allocation
• "restore / reconstruct / decode all valid [strings/sequences]" → Stage 4 — reconstruction backtracking; validity check gates each branch
Structural Triggers:
• Output is List[List[...]] (all combinations, all partitions) → backtracking with path collection at base case
• Output is a single boolean ("is it possible?") with no polynomial algorithm obvious → backtracking with early-exit on first valid solution
• Input is a small N (≤ 20) with exponential search space but no DP state definition → backtracking with pruning
Constraint Triggers:
• N ≤ 20 with output as a count or existence → backtracking (possibly with bitmask DP if overlapping subproblems detected)
• N ≤ 9 for board-filling problems (Sudoku, N-Queens) → backtracking with constraint sets; O(9^N) feasible
Anti-Triggers (when NOT to use backtracking):
🚩 "minimum number of operations to reach target" → NOT backtracking (exponential) → BFS (unweighted) or Dijkstra (weighted)
🚩 "count the number of ways to [fill / partition]" where N > 20 → NOT plain backtracking → DP with memoisation; count is too large to enumerate
🚩 "find the shortest path / minimum cost path" → NOT backtracking → BFS / Dijkstra / Bellman-Ford; backtracking has no shortest-path guarantee
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem ask to ENUMERATE, CONSTRUCT, or CHECK EXISTENCE of solutions in a combinatorial space? → NO: Not a backtracking problem. Stop. → YES: Continue. Q2: Is N ≤ ~20 (making exponential search feasible)? → NO (N > 20): Backtracking will TLE. Go to Q9 (DP/BFS alternative). → YES: Continue. Q3: Does the problem ask for ALL solutions or ONE solution? → ALL: Full enumeration backtracking — collect at base case. → ONE: Backtracking with EARLY EXIT on first valid solution. Q4: Is the choice at each step drawn from a SET (subsets/combinations) or a SEQUENCE (permutations/orderings)? → SET (order doesn't matter): Use START-INDEX to avoid revisiting. → Reusable? YES → pass same i. NO → pass i+1. → Duplicates? YES → sort + same-level skip. → Stage 1/2. O(2ⁿ) to O(n!). → SEQUENCE (order matters): Use VISITED ARRAY. → Duplicates? YES → sort + skip if nums[i]==nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]. → Stage 1/2. O(n!). Q5: Does the state involve a 2D GRID or BOARD? → YES: Grid backtracking. Use 2D visited array or in-place marking. → Fixed depth (N-Queens, Sudoku)? Use constraint SETS for O(1) checks. → Variable path (Word Search)? Use DFS with visited marking. → Stage 3. O(n!) to O(9^m). → NO: Continue. Q6: Is there a CONSTRAINT determining valid choices (not just "not visited")? → YES: Constraint-guided backtracking. → Can you PROPAGATE forward before recursing? YES → Forward checking / constraint propagation (Stage 5). NO → Check at each node (Stage 3/4). → NO: Pure enumeration (Stage 1/2). Q7: Does the problem require tracking RUNNING STATE across decisions (e.g., running sum, current expression value)? → YES: Stage 4 — pass running state as parameter; undo on backtrack. Watch for operator-precedence traps (Expression Add Operators). → NO: Continue. Q8: Are there OVERLAPPING SUBPROBLEMS in the search tree (same partial state reached via different paths)? → YES: Stage 5 — add memoisation on the state (bitmask or tuple). This converts backtracking to bitmask DP. Expected: O(2ⁿ · n). → NO: Pure backtracking without memo. Q9 (N too large): Can the problem be solved with DP, BFS, or greedy? → DP: Overlapping subproblems with optimal substructure. → BFS: Shortest path / minimum steps in state space. → Greedy: Local optimal = global optimal (exchange argument holds). Stage identification summary: Q4 (set/sequence, no grid) → Stage 1/2. O(2ⁿ) to O(n!). Q5 (grid/board) → Stage 3. O(n!) to O(9^m). Q7 (running state, disguised) → Stage 4. Exponential with pruning. Q8 (overlapping subproblems) → Stage 5. O(2ⁿ · n). If your solution is slower than O(2ⁿ · n) for N ≤ 20, look for the Stage 5 memoisation or pruning optimisation.
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 [Stage 1] N > 20 for enumeration problems — O(2²⁰) ≈ 10⁶ is feasible; O(2³⁰) ≈ 10⁹ is not. → Use bitmask DP if overlapping subproblems, or meet-in-the-middle (split into two halves of size n/2, enumerate each, combine).
🚩 [Stage 2] Counting problems with large N — Backtracking counts by enumerating every solution — O(result size), can be astronomical. Use DP where the count is accumulated without enumeration.
🚩 [Stage 3] Shortest-path or minimum-cost problems on a graph — Backtracking finds valid solutions but not minimum-cost ones efficiently. Use BFS (unweighted minimum steps) or Dijkstra (weighted).
🚩 [Stage 4] Polynomial solutions disguised as search — Some "NP-hard-looking" problems have greedy or DP solutions. Always check: optimal substructure (→ DP), exchange argument (→ greedy) before reaching for backtracking.
🚩 [Stage 5] Backtracking without memoisation on repeated states — If same (remaining, constraint-state) is reached via multiple paths, pure backtracking recomputes it. Convert to memoised recursion / bitmask DP. Classic symptom: TLE on N ≈ 15–20.
🚩 [Stage 3] Sudoku-style without constraint propagation — Bare backtracking Sudoku is O(9^81) worst case. Without "only try digits missing from row/col/box," TLE on hard puzzles. Forward checking is mandatory at this stage.
🚩 [Stage 5] Undo bugs making state corrupted — If undo is incomplete (global variable modified in one branch, not restored), subsequent branches see corrupted state. Symptom: wrong answers that are hard to reproduce. Every mutation in the choose step must be explicitly reversed in unchoose.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — Subset Enumeration (Invariant Maintenance)
Claim: backtrack(i, path) adds to result exactly all subsets of nums[i:] that extend path.
Proof by strong induction on n − i:
• Base case (i = n): No elements remain; path itself is the only valid extension → add path to result. ✓
• Inductive step: Assume correct for all calls with i' > i. At index i, we make two recursive calls: include — backtrack(i+1, path+[nums[i]]) correctly enumerates all subsets of nums[i+1:] extended by path+[nums[i]] by induction; exclude — backtrack(i+1, path) correctly enumerates all subsets of nums[i+1:] extended by path. Together they cover all subsets of nums[i:] extended by path. ✓
• Completeness: every include/exclude combination is tried. No duplicates: each path corresponds to a unique include/exclude sequence. □
Stage 3 — N-Queens (Constraint-Set Invariant)
Claim: The algorithm places exactly one queen per row such that no two queens share a column, diagonal, or anti-diagonal.
Proof by invariant maintenance:
• Invariant: Before placing queen in row r, sets cols, diag (r−c), anti_diag (r+c) contain exactly the columns/diagonals attacked by queens in rows 0..r−1.
• Placement: we only place at column c if c ∉ cols, (r−c) ∉ diag, (r+c) ∉ anti_diag — ensuring no conflict.
• Update: add c, r−c, r+c to the three sets, maintaining invariant for row r+1.
• Undo: after recursion, remove c, r−c, r+c, restoring invariant for trying next column in row r. ✓
• Completeness: all columns 0..n−1 are tried for each row. No valid placement is skipped. □
Stage 5 — Memoised Backtracking (Overlapping Subproblem Collapse)
Claim: Adding memoisation on (index, state) to backtracking produces the same result as without memoisation.
Proof by substitution: If backtrack(i, state) is called twice with identical arguments, both operate on identical remaining choices and constraints. By the Stage 1 inductive proof, both produce identical results. Therefore storing and returning the cached result is correct. ✓
Complexity improvement: Without memo, the same (i, state) subtree may be recomputed exponentially many times. With memo, each unique (i, state) is computed once → O(states · branching factor) total.
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 (Stage 1) — Not copying the path when adding to results (reference bug) 
# BUG: all entries in result point to the same list object
result.append(path)     # path is mutated later → all results become identical
# FIX: always append a copy
result.append(path[:])  # or list(path) or path.copy()
Bug 2 (Stage 2) — Duplicate skipping: wrong condition (across-levels instead of same-level) 
# BUG: skips valid elements when previous sibling was used at a DIFFERENT level
if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1]:
    continue
# FIX: skip only at same recursion level
if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i-1]:
    continue
Bug 3 (Stage 2) — Permutations with duplicates: wrong used-array condition 
# BUG: skips too aggressively — misses valid permutations
if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1] and used[i-1]:
    continue
# FIX: skip if previous equal element is NOT used (same-level duplicate)
if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]:
    continue
Bug 4 (Stage 2) — Combination sum: no termination on negative remaining 
# BUG: recurses indefinitely when remaining < 0
def backtrack(start, remaining):
    for i in range(start, len(candidates)):
        path.append(candidates[i])
        backtrack(i, remaining - candidates[i])  # no base case for negative!
# FIX: add early termination
    if remaining < 0: return
    if remaining == 0: result.append(path[:]); return
Bug 5 (Stage 3) — Grid backtracking: forgetting to unmark visited cell 
# BUG: cells remain marked, blocking future valid paths
visited[r][c] = True
for dr, dc in directions:
    backtrack(r+dr, c+dc, idx+1)
# visited[r][c] = False  ← MISSING
# FIX: always restore
visited[r][c] = False
Bug 6 (Stage 3) — N-Queens: wrong diagonal encoding 
# BUG: (row, col) tuple is not the diagonal invariant
diag.add((row, col))
# FIX: use scalar invariants
diag.add(row - col)        # same r-c = same diagonal
anti_diag.add(row + col)   # same r+c = same anti-diagonal
Bug 7 (Stage 3) — Sudoku: box index formula off 
# BUG: wrong box grouping
box_id = row * 3 + col            # mixes rows and columns
# FIX
box_id = (row // 3) * 3 + (col // 3)
Bug 8 (Stage 4) — Expression Add Operators: multiply without adjusting last term 
# BUG: multiply applied to cumulative sum, not just the last term
backtrack(pos+length, cur * num, num)  # cur is wrong base for multiply
# FIX: track `last` (the last operand added) separately
# cur - last + last * num  →  removes last addition, replaces with multiplication
backtrack(pos+length, cur - last + last * num, last * num)
Bug 9 (Stage 5) — Memoisation with mutable path in state key 
# BUG: tuple(path) includes accumulation which varies — wrong key
memo[tuple(path)] = result
# FIX: key should be the REMAINING state, not the accumulated path
memo[(index, frozenset(used))] = result
Bug 10 (Stage 5) — Bitmask: using OR to "undo" instead of XOR or AND-NOT 
# BUG: OR cannot undo a set bit
mask |= (1 << i)  # set bit
# ... recurse ...
mask |= (1 << i)  # BUG: OR again does nothing to undo
# FIX: use XOR (only works if bit was set) or AND-NOT
mask ^= (1 << i)        # toggles bit back off
mask &= ~(1 << i)       # unconditionally clears the bit (safer)
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
🚩 [Stage 1] Appending path reference instead of path copy — result.append(path) stores a reference to a list that will be mutated. All results become identical. Correct model: "I'm storing a snapshot — always path[:]."
🚩 [Stage 2] Skipping duplicates with i > 0 instead of i > start — Using i > 0 skips elements at the beginning of deeper recursion levels, silently dropping valid combinations. Correct model: "Skip a duplicate only if it's at the SAME level — i > start enforces same-level scope."
🚩 [Stage 3] Missing the undo step for grid problems — Marking visited=True but forgetting =False means cells remain blocked for sibling branches. Symptom: fewer results than expected. Correct model: "Every mutation before a recursive call must be reversed after it — no exceptions."
🚩 [Stage 4] Treating a counting problem as enumeration — "How many valid arrangements exist?" can sometimes be solved via DP without enumeration. Backtracking counts by generating every solution — exponential. Correct model: "If N > 15, check for DP before backtracking."
🚩 [Stage 4] Not recognising a game-theory problem as backtracking — "Can player 1 guarantee a win?" is a minimax tree. Candidates try BFS or greedy. Correct model: "Optimal play = exhaustive game-tree search. A position is winning iff any child is losing for the opponent."
🚩 [Stage 5] Running backtracking without pruning on constraint problems — Unconstrained Sudoku is O(9^81) — effectively infinite. Correct model: "Constraint propagation is mandatory, not optional. Always narrow the choice set before recursing."
🚩 [Stage 5] Confusing "backtracking with memo" with DP — When you memoise backtrack(index, state), you have written top-down DP. Not recognising this signals incomplete mastery. Correct model: "Memoised backtracking IS DP. Name it correctly and explain the state definition."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
Stage 2 Follow-Ups (Constraint Mutations)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
1"What if elements can be reused? What changes in your code?"Change backtrack(i+1, ...) to backtrack(i, ...) — one-character change; explain why termination is still guaranteed (remaining decreases monotonically).
2"The input has duplicates. How do you avoid duplicate results without using a set?"Sort the input; at each level skip nums[i] if nums[i]==nums[i−1] and i > start; explain WHY i > start (not i > 0).
Stage 3 Follow-Ups (State Expansion)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
3"Extend Word Search to return ALL paths that spell the word, not just one."Remove early-exit (return True); collect all valid paths; deep-copy path at base case; complexity becomes O(n·m·4^L·results).
4"Solve N-Queens for N=100 — clearly backtracking won't work. What changes?"Use mathematical constructions (explicit queen placement formulas exist for all N ≥ 4); backtracking is infeasible beyond N ≈ 15.
Stage 4 Follow-Ups (Hidden Reductions)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
5"How would you solve Sudoku faster than pure backtracking?"Constraint propagation (AC-3 / arc consistency): after placing a digit, propagate to all related cells; reduces branching factor from 9 to ~2–3 on average.
6"Can you model this as an exact cover problem? What does that give you?"Exact cover reduces to binary matrix; Knuth's Algorithm X (Dancing Links) solves with efficient undo (O(1) cover/uncover); DLX is the fastest known Sudoku solver.
Stage 5 Follow-Ups (Optimisation Pressure)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
7"Your backtracking is O(n!). Can memoisation help here?"Only if overlapping subproblems exist — same remaining set reached via different orderings. If state = bitmask of used elements, memo table has 2ⁿ entries; total O(2ⁿ · n).
8"N = 20 and your bitmask DP is O(2²⁰ · 20) ≈ 2×10⁷. What if N = 40?"Meet in the middle: enumerate subsets of first 20 (2²⁰) and last 20 (2²⁰), then combine. Still exponential but halved exponent: O(2^(n/2)).
Beyond Stage 5 (Adversarial / Distributed)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
9"You're solving Sudoku puzzles submitted by users in real time. How do you scale this?"Each puzzle is independent — embarrassingly parallel. Worker pool; each worker runs constraint propagation + backtracking. Hardest puzzles can be solved in < 1 ms with DLX.
10"An adversary constructs the worst-case input for your N-Queens solver. What does it look like; how do you defend?"Worst case: no pruning fires until the last placement. Defence: forward checking; worst-case N-Queens is still O(n!) but average case drops dramatically; for N > 20, use explicit construction formulas.
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):
Stage 1 first: S.No 1 (78 Subsets) → S.No 2 (77 Combinations) → S.No 3 (46 Permutations). Write all three from the SAME template: backtrack(start_or_index, path). The only difference should be 2–3 lines. If implementations look very different, you haven't internalised the template.
Stage 2 next: S.No 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 (all duplicate/reuse variants). You should derive each from the previous with one change. Then S.No 8 (Phone), 9 (Palindrome), 10 (IP).
Stage 3 next: S.No 11 (Word Search) → 12 (Unique Paths III) → 13 (N-Queens) → 15 (Sudoku). Grid problems introduce 2D state; N-Queens introduces constraint sets.
Stage 4 next: S.No 16 (Expressions) → 17 (Word Break II) → 18 (Flip Game) → 19 (Word Pattern II) → 21 (Android Unlock). Hardest recognition problems. Time yourself; if > 40 min, review the pattern before continuing.
Stage 5 last: S.No 23 (Squareful Arrays) → 24 (Tiling Rectangle) → 26 (Word Search II with Trie). Implement each optimisation explicitly — do not look up the Trie integration.

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and recognition):
Stage 1: under 10 min. Stage 2: under 15 min. Stage 3: under 25 min (N-Queens: under 20). Stage 4: under 35 min. Template drill: write the complete backtracking template (choose/recurse/unchoose) from memory in under 2 minutes, daily for 1 week. Recognition drill: given only the title, identify (a) sub-variant, (b) Stage, (c) key pruning needed. Target 90% before Phase 3. Undo drill: for every problem, explicitly write "What exactly do I undo?" as a comment before coding.

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources per Stage):
Stage 1/2: AtCoder ABC tagged "brute-force + backtracking" — any 3 unseen. Stage 3: USACO Silver grid-placement problems from recent years. Stage 4: Codeforces Div 2 D/E tagged "backtracking + implementation" — 3 problems. Stage 5: Google Kickstart archives — "arrangement" or "placement" problems requiring pruned search.
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 min each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LC 1659: Maximize Grid Happiness (Stage 3 + Stage 5)
Why valid: Naïve backtracking (place each person, check all neighbours) is O(3^(m·n)) — TLE. The correct solution uses bitmask DP on the current row state, treating the previous row as a bitmask. A candidate who reaches Stage 5 naturally arrives at this — solving it cold proves they can identify when backtracking needs to become DP.

Test 2 — CF 1097F: Alex and a TV Show (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: Looks like a set-cover backtracking problem but is solved via Möbius inversion on a bitset. The candidate must first recognise exact-cover structure (Stage 4), then apply the bitset optimisation (Stage 5). Highest-level "backtracking to combinatorics" reduction tested at Codeforces Div 1 D level.

Test 3 — LC 489: Robot Room Cleaner (Stage 3 + Stage 4)
Why valid: A backtracking problem where the state (grid layout) is hidden. The candidate must track relative position manually, simulate the grid in memory, and implement the undo step as a physical robot movement sequence. Tests that the mental model is robust enough when state is not directly accessible — the hardest form of Stage 4 recognition, analogous to implicit-graph problems.
09
Greedy
Algorithms Exchange Argument · Sort Key · Heap · Regret · Matroid
32 problems · 5 stages · activity selection, heap greedy, regret, matroid, greedy + binary search
Why It Matters: Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step — the proof that local optimality cascades into global optimality is an exchange argument: any optimal solution can be transformed into the greedy solution by swapping choices one at a time without worsening the result. Greedy is the most dangerous topic in competitive programming because it is simultaneously the easiest to apply and the easiest to apply wrongly — a greedy that looks correct on examples can fail on a single adversarial case. It appears in every Google interview because it tests mathematical reasoning (can you prove your greedy?), not just implementation. At ICPC/IOI, the hardest greedy problems require recognising that a problem with no obvious greedy structure reduces to a canonical greedy form (scheduling, matroid, exchange-argument) after a non-obvious transformation.
The Mental Model
Greedy is a vending machine that always dispenses the "best available" item. The key question is never "which item is best?" — it's "what does 'best' mean in this problem, and does picking it now guarantee we never regret it later?"

How the mental model scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1: "Best" is obvious — largest value, smallest cost, earliest deadline. Pick greedily in sorted order. No regret possible.
• Stage 2: "Best" changes based on one flipped constraint — earliest end instead of largest value, or smallest ratio instead of smallest absolute value. The sort key changes; the algorithm skeleton is identical.
• Stage 3: Two competing greedy criteria must be balanced simultaneously. The state tracks two quantities; the sort key is a composite function of both.
• Stage 4: The problem doesn't look like a greedy problem. You must first transform the input or reframe the objective before the "best available" principle becomes visible.
• Stage 5: The greedy choice requires knowing the current minimum/maximum of a dynamic set — a data structure (heap, segment tree, sorted set) makes each greedy step O(log n) instead of O(n).
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch — The Exchange Argument
Invariant: Let G = greedy solution, O = any optimal solution. We show G is at least as good as O.

3-step template applicable to ANY greedy problem:
• Step 1 (Align): Order both solutions by the greedy sort key. Find the first position k where G and O differ — G chose element g, O chose element o, with greedy_key(g) ≤ greedy_key(o) (G chose "better" by its criterion).
• Step 2 (Exchange): Swap o for g in O at position k. Show that after the swap, O' is still a valid solution AND cost(O') ≤ cost(O) (or value(O') ≥ value(O)). The entire proof lives here.
• Step 3 (Iterate): Repeat until O has been transformed into G. Since each swap didn't worsen O, cost(G) ≤ cost(O). Since O was optimal, G is also optimal. □

Why this matters: If you cannot execute Step 2, your greedy is wrong. The exchange argument IS the proof. Every greedy problem at Google requires you to sketch this argument when asked "how do you know this is correct?"
Sub-Variants to Master (15 total)
1. Sort + linear scan (activity selection) — Stage 1 · O(n log n) · sort array · New idea: sort by end time; keep if start ≥ last end
2. Sort by ratio / composite key (fractional knapsack) — Stage 1 · O(n log n) · sort by value/weight · New idea: sort key is a ratio, not a raw value
3. Two-pointer greedy (matching min to max) — Stage 2 · O(n log n) · two pointers on sorted array · New idea: optimal pairing uses two sorted ends simultaneously
4. Greedy with constraint flip (earliest deadline first) — Stage 2 · O(n log n) · sort by deadline · New idea: sort key changes from value to deadline; same skeleton, different comparator
5. Prefix-greedy (always extend current reach) — Stage 2 · O(n) · linear scan with reach variable · New idea: maintain max_reach greedily; answer is steps taken when reach is exhausted
6. Stack-based greedy (remove to optimise) — Stage 3 · O(n) · monotone stack · New idea: greedy removal uses a stack to maintain order; each element compared to stack top
7. Heap-based greedy (always process best available) — Stage 3 · O(n log n) · min/max heap · New idea: "best available" is dynamic — heap maintains sorted order across insertions
8. Two-heap greedy (balance two populations) — Stage 3 · O(n log n) · two heaps · New idea: two competing criteria; median or balance point maintained via dual heaps
9. Greedy on sorted events (sweep line) — Stage 3 · O(n log n) · sort events + active set · New idea: process start/end events in order; greedy assigns resources at each event
10. Greedy with exchange argument on strings — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · custom comparator sort · New idea: comparator derived from exchange argument, not natural order (e.g., ab < ba iff a+b < b+a)
11. Regret-based greedy (undo a previous choice) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · heap + virtual undo · New idea: make a greedy choice, then "undo" it by adding a negative-weight virtual item; enables globally optimal correction
12. Matroid greedy (independent set maximisation) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · sort + independence oracle · New idea: greedy on a matroid is always optimal; recognising matroid structure IS the insight
13. Greedy + binary search (optimise the sort-key threshold) — Stage 5 · O(n log² n) · binary search on answer + greedy check · New idea: binary search on a parameter; greedy verifies feasibility at each midpoint
14. Greedy + segment tree (range min/max for greedy choice) — Stage 5 · O(n log n) · segment tree · New idea: greedy choice requires range query on dynamic set; segment tree replaces O(n) scan
15. Online greedy (decisions without future knowledge) — Stage 5 · O(n log n) · sorted set / BIT · New idea: items arrive in stream; greedy must use only past information; sorted set enables O(log n) best-match lookup
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Sort + linear scanO(n log n)O(1)Baseline: sort then greedy scan
Sort by ratioO(n log n)O(1)Sort key is ratio, not scalar
Two-pointer greedyO(n log n)O(1)Two sorted pointers instead of one
Prefix-reach greedyO(n)O(1)No sort needed; single-pass with reach variable
Stack-based greedyO(n)O(n)Stack replaces comparison with top
Heap-based greedyO(n log n)O(n)Dynamic "best available" via heap
Two-heap greedyO(n log n)O(n)Two heaps for dual-criterion balance
Sweep line greedyO(n log n)O(n)Events sorted; active set managed
String comparator greedyO(n log n)O(n)Comparator derived from exchange argument
Regret-based greedyO(n log n)O(n)Virtual undo item added to heap
Greedy + binary searchO(n log² n)O(1)Binary search wraps greedy check
Greedy + segment treeO(n log n)O(n)Range query replaces O(n) scan
Mastery Problem Set (32 problems · 5 stages)
#LayerStageSourceProblemDiffPSub-Variant / New IdeaKey Insight
1Foundation1LeetCodeLC 1029 Two City SchedulingMedSort by cost-difference (composite key)THE baseline — sort key is (cost_A − cost_B); cheapest half to A, rest to B. Exchange argument proves optimality; greedy on a composite key
2Foundation1LeetCodeLC 435 Non-overlapping IntervalsMedActivity selection (sort by end)Sort by END time (not start or value). Keep interval if start ≥ last_end; exchange argument: earliest-ending is always safe
3Foundation1LeetCodeLC 452 Minimum Arrows to Burst BalloonsMedActivity selection (touching = overlap)One comparator flip from #2: start <= last_end (not <) changes semantics; same skeleton, different boundary condition
4Foundation2LeetCodeLC 55 Jump GameMedPrefix-reach greedyReplace interval tracking with max_reach variable; no sort needed. Single pass: reach = max(reach, i + nums[i]); return reach ≥ n−1; O(n)
5Foundation2LeetCodeLC 45 Jump Game IIMedPrefix-reach greedy (count steps)Extends #4: track cur_end and next_end; steps++ when i == cur_end; O(n)
6Foundation2LeetCodeLC 763 Partition LabelsMedPrefix-reach on last-occurrence mapBuild last-occurrence map; same reach-extension greedy with last[s[i]] instead of nums[i]; partition when i == reach
7Foundation2LeetCodeLC 406 Queue Reconstruction by HeightMedTwo-attribute sort with insertionSort by height desc, k asc; insert each person at index k. Taller people are unaffected by shorter insertions — the enabling invariant
8Variants2LeetCodeLC 134 Gas StationMedCircular scan with running totalTwo observations fused: if total gas ≥ total cost, solution exists; start from first index after every negative-tank reset
9Variants2LeetCodeLC 860 Lemonade ChangeMedGreedy with state trackingTrack counts of $5, $10; greedy uses LARGEST denomination first when making change. State across greedy decisions
10Variants2LeetCodeLC 881 Boats to Save PeopleMedTwo-pointer greedy on sorted arraySort; pair heaviest + lightest if they fit; else heaviest alone. Exchange argument: pairing the extremes never worsens the count
11Variants3LeetCodeLC 253 Meeting Rooms IIMed✅Heap-based greedy (room assignment)Min-heap of end-times; heap size = rooms used. Heap enables O(log n) "find earliest-ending room"; greedy assigns to that room or opens new one
12Variants3LeetCodeLC 621 Task SchedulerMedFrequency-driven greedy with idle slotsMost-frequent task constrains minimum length; formula (max_freq−1) * (n+1) + count_of_max_freq, take max with len(tasks)
13Variants3LeetCodeLC 767 Reorganize StringMedInterleaving greedy via max-heapAlways pick two most-frequent characters alternately using max-heap; no fixed "idle" — just interleave top-2 frequencies
14Variants3LeetCodeLC 1353 Max Events That Can Be AttendedMedSweep + min-heap greedyEvents arrive by start day; on each day, add available events to heap; attend min-end event to maximise future options
15Variants3LeetCodeLC 630 Course Schedule IIIHardHeap-based regret greedyHard: "evict largest" insight requires tracking past choices via max-heap; if new course doesn't fit, swap out the longest-taken course. Pure forward greedy fails
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 3 → Stage 4 jump. The sort key is NOT given. You must derive it from the exchange argument BEFORE coding. Practise writing the 3-step exchange argument for each problem first.
16Combo4LeetCodeLC 179 Largest NumberMedString comparator from exchange argumentCustom comparator a+b vs b+a as strings; exchange argument proves this is the ONLY valid comparator. No natural ordering applies
17Combo4LeetCodeLC 135 CandyHardTwo-pass greedy with conflicting constraintsHard: neither pass alone is sufficient. Left-to-right handles left-neighbour; right-to-left handles right-neighbour; combine with max(). Directional-constraint insight
18Combo4LeetCodeLC 316 Remove Duplicate LettersMedMonotone stack greedy with frequency trackingStack maintains lexicographic order; only pop if character appears later (last-occurrence constraint)
19Combo4LeetCodeLC 402 Remove K DigitsMedMonotone stack greedy with budgetSame stack skeleton as #18 but constraint is exactly k removals (budget, not last-occurrence); pop while stack[-1] > cur AND k > 0; trim tail for remaining k
20Combo4LeetCodeLC 1642 Furthest Building You Can ReachMedRegret greedy: ladders as "undo tokens"Treat each ladder as a retrospective upgrade of the worst-used jump; min-heap of past jumps; swap heap-top with current if beneficial
21Combo4LeetCodeLC 871 Minimum Number of Refueling StopsHardGreedy with delayed decision (max-heap of passed stations)Hard: decision is deferred — you don't refuel when you pass a station; you refuel retrospectively from best available. The "lazy refuel" insight is non-obvious
22Combo4LeetCodeLC 1326 Minimum Number of TapsHardInterval coverage greedy (Jump Game in disguise)Hard: each tap [i−ranges[i], i+ranges[i]] becomes an interval; problem reduces to Jump Game II after transformation. Recognising the reduction IS the insight
23Combo4CodeforcesCF 1041D GliderHardGreedy + sliding window on sorted eventsHard: sort thermal columns; sliding window of width H; greedily count columns in window. Brute force O(n²) → O(n log n) via the sliding-window insight
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 4 → Stage 5. The greedy check must now live INSIDE binary search or use a segment tree / sorted set for O(log n) queries. Pure O(n) per-step scans TLE.
24Hard5LeetCodeLC 1834 Single-Threaded CPUMedGreedy + heap on sorted task arrivalsSimulation with two sorted structures: arrival-sorted array + min-heap of (duration, index). At each CPU-free moment, pick shortest available task
25Hard5LeetCodeLC 2402 Meeting Rooms IIIHardGreedy with two heaps (free + busy rooms)Hard: both earliest-ending AND lowest-numbered room must be tracked simultaneously via two heaps — free room numbers + (end_time, room) for busy
26Hard5LeetCodeLC 1675 Minimize Deviation in ArrayHardGreedy on sorted set with bounded operationsHard: the normalisation insight (all odds → multiplied by 2 once) is non-obvious; sorted set enables O(log n) per greedy step of halving max
27Hard5LeetCodeLC 2071 Maximum Number of Tasks You Can AssignHardGreedy + binary search on task count + dequeHard: binary search on answer k wraps a non-trivial greedy check using sorted workers + pills via deque. Greedy-within-binary-search fusion is a Stage 5 signature pattern
28Hard5USACO GoldTalent ShowHardFractional knapsack + binary search on ratioHard: binary search on a CONTINUOUS parameter (ratio threshold); greedy feasibility check is the inner loop. Classic binary-search-on-answer template
29Hard4Custom🔧 Weighted Matroid Greedy (Max Spanning Forest)HardMatroid greedy (graphic matroid)Hard: recognising edge-acyclicity as a graphic matroid unlocks Kruskal — greedy on a matroid is provably optimal. See full statement below
⚠️ TRAP ZONE — Problems that LOOK greedy but either aren't, or require a specific non-greedy algorithm. Recognise them in < 30 seconds.
30⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 122 ⚠️ Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IIMed⚠️ TRAP — "sum all upswings" works ONLY with unlimited free transactionsAny constraint (k transactions, cooldown, fee) immediately breaks the greedy → use DP. Clean greedy is a special-case coincidence, not the general pattern
31⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 1053 ⚠️ Previous Permutation With One SwapMed⚠️ TRAP — careful case analysis, NOT sort / exchange argumentRight-to-left scan for the drop point; specific algorithm with exact rules — no exchange argument applies
32⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 738 ⚠️ Monotone Increasing DigitsMed⚠️ TRAP — right-to-left scan with cascade, NOT left-to-right digit greedySetting digits greedily LEFT-to-RIGHT cascades violations. Correct direction is RIGHT-to-LEFT: find drop point, set trailing 9s
Custom Problem Statement (#29)
🔧 Weighted Matroid Greedy — Maximum Weight Spanning Forest (Stage 4): Given an undirected graph with N vertices and M weighted edges, find the maximum-weight spanning forest (a set of edges with no cycle, not necessarily connected, with maximum total weight).
Constraints: 2 ≤ N ≤ 10⁵, 1 ≤ M ≤ 3×10⁵, 1 ≤ weight ≤ 10⁹.
Example: N=4, M=5, edges=[(1,2,10), (2,3,6), (1,3,5), (3,4,4), (1,4,3)] → 20 (edges: (1,2,10) + (2,3,6) + (3,4,4)).
Intended solution: Sort edges by weight descending. Greedily add each edge if it doesn't form a cycle (Union-Find check). This is Kruskal's algorithm for maximum spanning forest. Why greedy works: the set of acyclic subgraphs of a graph forms a graphic matroid — and greedy always finds the maximum-weight independent set in a matroid. Exchange argument: any two spanning forests of the same size can exchange edges one at a time without losing the forest property.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "minimum number of [operations / jumps / arrows] to [achieve]" → Stage 1/2 greedy — activity selection or reach-extension; local minimum choice is never regretted
• "maximum number of [events / tasks / intervals] you can [attend / complete]" → Stage 2/3 — activity selection sorted by end time; heap for dynamic scheduling
• "arrange / order / sequence to [maximise / minimise] total" → Stage 4 — sort key derived from exchange argument; comparator a·B vs b·A or a+b vs b+a
• "at each step, you can [action]; find minimum steps" → Stage 2 — prefix-reach greedy; reach = max(reach, f(i))
• "assign [resources] to [tasks] optimally" → Stage 3/5 — heap-based greedy; assign best-available resource at each step
Structural Triggers:
• Input is a list of items each with two attributes (value + weight, start + end, cost_A + cost_B) → sort by a function of both attributes; exchange argument determines the function
• Output is a single number (min count, max count) NOT a specific arrangement → likely greedy with proof, not backtracking
• Problem has a "never regret" property visible in examples (choosing X now never prevents a better choice later) → greedy applicable
Constraint Triggers:
• N ≤ 10⁵ with no obvious DP state → greedy is the intended solution; DP states would require O(n²) or more
• Items have two attributes and you must select ALL items (not a subset) in an optimal order → custom comparator sort; exchange argument for sort key
Anti-Triggers (when NOT to use greedy):
🚩 "maximum value subset of weight ≤ W" → NOT greedy (fractional knapsack is greedy, 0/1 knapsack is NOT) → use 0/1 Knapsack DP
🚩 "minimum cost path from source to destination" → NOT greedy scan → Dijkstra (non-negative) / Bellman-Ford (negative)
🚩 "number of ways to [achieve target]" → NOT greedy (greedy gives one way, not a count) → DP with counting recurrence
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem ask for an OPTIMAL (min/max) outcome achievable by making sequential choices? → NO: Not a greedy problem. Stop. → YES: Continue. Q2: Does the problem have OPTIMAL SUBSTRUCTURE without OVERLAPPING SUBPROBLEMS (i.e., choices don't interact with each other)? → NO (choices interact): → Use DP. → YES (choices are independent once sorted): Continue.
