Q3: Can you identify a SORT KEY such that processing in that order never creates regret (a later element is NEVER hurt by an earlier choice)? → YES (obvious: end time, ratio, etc.): → Stage 1/2. Sort, then linear scan. Expected: O(n log n). → MAYBE (sort key requires exchange argument): → Stage 4. Derive comparator: "does choosing a before b improve result?" Q4: Does the greedy choice depend on the CURRENT BEST AVAILABLE item in a DYNAMIC collection (items arrive over time or are unlocked)? → YES: → Stage 3/5 — use a HEAP. Min-heap if smallest-cost; max-heap if largest-value. Expected: O(n log n). → NO: Continue. Q5: Does making a greedy choice sometimes turn out to be WRONG and need to be UNDONE based on future information? → YES: → Stage 4 — REGRET GREEDY. Make the greedy choice; push an "undo token" to a heap; if a better option arrives, swap via the heap. Examples: Course Schedule III, Furthest Building, Min Refueling Stops. → NO: Continue. Q6: Is the problem a STRING ORDERING or ARRANGEMENT problem? → YES: → Stage 4 — EXCHANGE ARGUMENT COMPARATOR. For two items a, b, ask: "Is a+b better than b+a?" Derive the comparator from this comparison. Sort by custom comparator. Expected: O(n log n). → NO: Continue. Q7: Is the greedy check INSIDE a binary search on the answer? → YES: → Stage 5 — GREEDY + BINARY SEARCH. Binary search on threshold/count; greedy verifies feasibility. Expected: O(n log² n) or O(n log n · log(value_range)). → NO: → Stage 5 — GREEDY + SEGMENT TREE / SORTED SET. Greedy choice requires range min/max on dynamic collection. Stage identification summary: Q3 YES (obvious sort key) → Stage 1/2. O(n log n). Q4 YES (dynamic heap) → Stage 3. O(n log n). Q5 YES (regret/undo) → Stage 4. O(n log n). Q6 YES (string comparator) → Stage 4. O(n log n). Q7 YES (binary search wrapper) → Stage 5. O(n log² n). If your solution is O(n²), look for the heap or sorted-set optimisation that makes each greedy step O(log n).
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 [Stage 1] 0/1 Knapsack disguised as fractional knapsack — Greedy by value/weight ratio is optimal for fractional but provably WRONG for 0/1. Always check: are items divisible? If not, use 0/1 Knapsack DP.
🚩 [Stage 1] Activity selection with weighted intervals — Greedy (sort by end, take non-overlapping) maximises COUNT but not total WEIGHT. For weighted intervals, use Weighted Job Scheduling DP (sort by end + binary search + DP).
🚩 [Stage 2] Greedy on circular arrays without handling wrap-around — Circular problems (Gas Station, circular interval coverage) silently fail without wrap-around handling. Strategy: run the greedy twice on the doubled array, or use the "total feasibility + start-point" two-observation approach.
🚩 [Stage 3] Greedy on strings without the exchange-argument comparator — Alphabetical or length-based order for "largest number" or "minimum cost arrangement" gives wrong answers. Always derive the comparator from the exchange argument: a+b vs b+a.
🚩 [Stage 4] Regret greedy without the "virtual undo" mechanism — Problems like Min Refueling Stops require decisions that look non-greedy. Without the deferred-decision heap mechanism, the solution seems to require DP. Recognise: "collect all options passed; pick best when forced to decide."
🚩 [Stage 4] Exchange argument comparator that is not a total order — If a < b is not transitive or antisymmetric, std::sort / Python's sorted gives undefined behaviour. Always verify: key(a) < key(b) and key(b) < key(c) implies key(a) < key(c).
🚩 [Stage 5] Greedy + binary search: checking feasibility incorrectly — A bug in the inner greedy check produces a wrong binary-search boundary. Test the greedy check independently before wrapping in binary search.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — Activity Selection (Exchange Argument)
Claim: Sorting by end time and greedily keeping non-overlapping intervals maximises the count.
• Let G = greedy solution (sorted by end), O = optimal (same sort). Suppose they first differ at position k: G chose interval g, O chose o, with end(g) ≤ end(o).
• Swap o for g in O → O'. Since end(g) ≤ end(o), interval g ends no later than o. Therefore any interval compatible with o after k is also compatible with g. O' is valid and |O'| = |O|.
• Repeat for all differing positions. G transforms to O without reducing count. ∴ G is optimal. □
Stage 4 — Largest Number (String Comparator)
Claim: Sorting strings by the comparator a+b > b+a produces the largest concatenation.
• Consider any two adjacent strings a, b in the sorted order. If a+b ≥ b+a, then a comes before b.
• Swapping them produces ...b+a... whose concatenation is ≤ ...a+b.... So no swap improves the result — sorted order is globally optimal by the bubble-sort argument.
• Transitivity: must be verified; string concatenation comparison over the same alphabet IS transitive. □
Stage 4 — Regret Greedy: Minimum Refueling Stops
Claim: The "collect all passed stations in max-heap, refuel from best when empty" strategy minimises refueling stops.
• Invariant: At every moment, the heap holds exactly the stations we could have refueled at and are still available for retrospective use.
• Retrospectively choosing the highest-capacity passed station is equivalent to having made the optimal real-time choice at that station.
• Proof: Consider any optimal O that refuels at station i before our greedy does. Our greedy has the same tank level when reaching i (same route, same prior choices). If greedy later picks a higher-capacity station j > i, it has at least as much fuel at all subsequent points. □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 (Stage 1) — Activity selection: sorting by START instead of END 
# BUG: sorts by start — classic wrong answer
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
# FIX: sort by END time
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
Bug 2 (Stage 2) — Jump Game II: incrementing steps at wrong position 
# BUG: steps for every index, not every jump boundary
for i in range(n):
    next_end = max(next_end, i + nums[i])
    steps += 1
# FIX: step only when crossing current boundary
    if i == cur_end and i < n - 1:
        steps += 1
        cur_end = next_end
Bug 3 (Stage 2) — Gas Station: not resetting tank when finding new start 
# BUG: tank carries over across candidate start resets
for i in range(n):
    tank += gas[i] - cost[i]
    if tank < 0:
        start = i + 1
        # tank = 0  ← MISSING
# FIX: reset tank to 0 when choosing new start
        tank = 0
Bug 4 (Stage 1) — Two City Scheduling: sorting with wrong sign 
# Correct: ascending on (cost_A - cost_B) → small diff = cheaper for A, goes to A
people.sort(key=lambda x: x[0] - x[1])
# BUG: flipped sign sends the wrong half to A
people.sort(key=lambda x: x[1] - x[0])
Bug 5 (Stage 4) — Largest Number: using integer comparison instead of string concat 
# BUG: plain numeric/lexicographic sort
nums.sort(reverse=True)
# FIX: compare as concatenated strings
from functools import cmp_to_key
nums.sort(key=cmp_to_key(
    lambda a, b: 1 if str(a)+str(b) > str(b)+str(a) else -1),
    reverse=True)
Bug 6 (Stage 3) — Task Scheduler: formula misses when all frequencies equal 
# BUG: formula can be less than len(tasks) when idle slots = 0
result = (max_freq - 1) * (n + 1) + count_max_freq
return result
# FIX: take max with actual task count
return max(result, len(tasks))
Bug 7 (Stage 3) — Meeting Rooms II: not reusing freed rooms 
# BUG: always pushes, never reuses
for start, end in intervals:
    heapq.heappush(heap, end)
# FIX: reuse freed room if available
for start, end in intervals:
    if heap and heap[0] <= start:
        heapq.heapreplace(heap, end)
    else:
        heapq.heappush(heap, end)
Bug 8 (Stage 4) — Remove K Digits: not stripping leading zeros 
# BUG: "0200" returned as-is
result = ''.join(stack)
return result
# FIX:
return result.lstrip('0') or '0'
Bug 9 (Stage 4) — Course Schedule III: not tracking cumulative time 
# BUG: only checks single course fit; ignores cumulative
for duration, end in courses:
    if duration <= end:
        taken.append(duration)
# FIX: track running total; regret-swap if exceeded
    total_time += duration
    heapq.heappush(max_heap, -duration)
    if total_time > end:
        total_time += heapq.heappop(max_heap)  # pops largest
Bug 10 (Stage 5) — Minimize Deviation: forgetting to re-add halved max 
# BUG: pops max, halves it, but doesn't add back
while max_val % 2 == 0:
    sorted_set.remove(max_val)
    max_val //= 2
    # sorted_set.add(max_val)  ← MISSING
# FIX: always re-add the halved value
    sorted_set.add(max_val)
    deviation = sorted_set[-1] - sorted_set[0]
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
🚩 [Stage 1] Applying greedy to 0/1 knapsack — Ratio-based greedy is a common instinct. It is ONLY correct when items are divisible or structure is a matroid. For 0/1 knapsack, two medium items beat one large. Correct model: "Greedy on ratios requires provable independence — verify via exchange argument or matroid."
🚩 [Stage 1] Forgetting that exchange argument requires TOTALITY — If the comparator is not transitive, sort() produces implementation-defined results. Correct model: "If a < b and b < c then a < c. Test with three examples before trusting your comparator."
🚩 [Stage 3] Not considering the regret mechanism — Min Refueling Stops and Course Schedule III LOOK like DP because "future info seems necessary." Regret greedy gives the optimal answer because the "best retrospective choice" equals the "best real-time choice." Correct model: "If the best retrospective and best real-time choice are the same element, regret greedy is correct."
🚩 [Stage 4] Left-to-right greedy on digit problems — Remove K Digits, Monotone Increasing Digits require right-to-left or a monotone stack because cascading effects propagate rightward. Correct model: "Digit problems with cascading effects need a stack (to undo recent choices) or right-to-left processing."
🚩 [Stage 4] Missing the input transformation — Jump Game variants, Min Taps, Talent Show require a non-trivial pre-processing step before greedy. Correct model: "First ask: what is the canonical form? Transform input, THEN apply greedy."
🚩 [Stage 2] Using < instead of <= for interval overlap — Balloon problems use <= (touching counts); meeting-room problems use <. One character determines correctness. Correct model: "Always check: does the problem treat touching endpoints as overlapping? Match the comparator precisely."
🚩 [Stage 3] Two-pass greedy: both passes in the same direction — For Candy, running both passes left-to-right misses the right-to-left constraint. Correct model: "Directional constraints require one pass in each direction; merge with max() to satisfy both simultaneously."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
Stage 2 Follow-Ups (Constraint Mutations)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
1"What if each interval has a value — maximise total value of non-overlapping intervals?"Greedy by end-time now fails (maximises COUNT not VALUE). Use Weighted Job Scheduling DP: sort by end, dp[i] = max(dp[i−1], val[i] + dp[last_non_overlap(i)]) with binary search.
2"What if the gas station problem is on a LINE, not a circle?"No wrap-around; find leftmost start where running sum never goes negative. Single pass, O(n).
Stage 3 Follow-Ups (State Expansion)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
3"Extend Meeting Rooms II: each room has a capacity; tasks have worker-counts."Heap stores (end_time, capacity); assign to the room with earliest end that has sufficient capacity. Sorted set or multi-key heap.
4"Task Scheduler but tasks have dependencies (must complete A before B)."Frequency approach breaks — dependencies create a DAG. Use topological sort + greedy (always pick highest-frequency available task).
Stage 4 Follow-Ups (Hidden Reductions)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
5"Prove your Largest Number comparator is correct."Exchange argument on any adjacent pair a, b: swapping produces b+a ≤ a+b by comparator. Since no swap improves, sorted order is globally optimal.
6"What if Candy requires each child gets at most 3 candies?"Adds upper bound to directional greedy. Two-pass still works but each pass also enforces ≤ 3: min(max(left_pass, right_pass), 3) at each position.
Stage 5 Follow-Ups (Optimisation Pressure)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
7"Minimize Deviation: what if numbers can also be doubled (not just halved)?"All odd numbers can be multiplied by 2 once (doubling more is wasteful). Normalise all odds first; then only halving is needed. Same algorithm.
8"What if tasks in the scheduler arrive online and you must schedule in real time?"Same min-heap; tasks added as they arrive. This is Shortest Remaining Processing Time (SRPT) — optimal for minimising average completion time.
Beyond Stage 5 (Adversarial / Distributed)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
9"You're scheduling tasks across 1000 machines in a distributed system. How does greedy scale?"Each machine runs a local greedy scheduler; a global coordinator uses a heap of (next_available_time, machine_id). MapReduce-style partitioning: O(n log k) for k machines.
10"An adversary designs task sequences to maximise idle time in Task Scheduler. What's the worst case?"All tasks frequency 1 and n large → max idle. Adversary sets max_freq ≈ ceil(total / (n+1)). Defence: no algorithm beats max(len(tasks), (max_freq−1)*(n+1)+count_max) — this bound is tight.
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):
Stage 1 first: S.No 2 (435) → S.No 3 (452) → S.No 4 (55) → S.No 5 (45) → S.No 1 (1029). After each, write the one-line exchange argument. If you cannot write it, re-solve.
Stage 2 next: S.No 6 (763) → S.No 7 (406) → S.No 8 (134) → S.No 9 (860) → S.No 10 (881). Focus on identifying what ONE thing changed from the previous problem.
Stage 3 next: S.No 11 (253) → S.No 12 (621) → S.No 13 (767) → S.No 14 (1353) → S.No 15 (630). Every problem here introduces a heap. Implement the heap from scratch for the first two.
Stage 4 next: S.No 16 (179) → S.No 17 (135) → S.No 18 (316) → S.No 19 (402) → S.No 20 (1642) → S.No 21 (871) → S.No 22 (1326). For each, write the exchange argument BEFORE looking at the solution.
Stage 5 last: S.No 24 (1834) → S.No 25 (2402) → S.No 26 (1675) → S.No 27 (2071) → S.No 28 (Talent Show) → S.No 29 (Matroid).

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and recognition):
Stage 1/2: under 12 min. Stage 3: under 20 min. Stage 4: under 35 min. Exchange argument drill: for every problem solved, write the 3-step argument in under 3 minutes — your interview proof. Sort-key drill: given a problem, identify the sort key in under 60 seconds before writing code — the sort key IS the solution. Anti-greedy recognition: for trap problems (S.No 30–32), identify in under 30 seconds WHY greedy fails and what replaces it.

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources per Stage):
Stage 1/2: AtCoder ABC D/E tagged "greedy" — any 3 unseen. Stage 3: Codeforces Div 2 D tagged "greedy + data structures" — 3 problems. Stage 4: USACO Gold greedy problems from recent years. Stage 5: Codeforces Div 1 C/D tagged "greedy + binary search" — 2 problems.
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 min each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LC 1686: Stone Game VI (Stage 3 + Stage 4)
Why valid: The greedy is to sort stones by alice_value + bob_value descending and alternate picks. This is NOT obvious — sorting by either player's value alone is wrong. The sort key comes purely from the exchange argument: "does Alice gain more by taking stone i before j?" Deriving this cold proves Stage 4 mastery. Implementing the alternating simulation proves Stage 3.

Test 2 — CF 1257E: The Contest (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: Requires binary searching on a threshold combined with a greedy feasibility check that itself needs a data structure. A candidate who has not internalised the "greedy inside binary search" pattern (Stage 5) will not recognise the approach. Solving cold under 45 minutes certifies Stage 4+5 fusion.

Test 3 — USACO Gold: Rental Service (Stage 3 + Stage 4)
Why valid: Disguises a multi-criteria greedy as a farm optimisation problem. Candidate must (1) recognise the greedy structure, (2) derive the correct ordering (sort cows by milk, high-milk cows rented first), and (3) use prefix sums to optimise matching. All three stages firing simultaneously on an unseen USACO problem is the highest bar for greedy mastery.
Content for Sieve of Eratosthenes is coming soon.
Content for Fenwick Tree / BIT is coming soon.
Content for Segment Tree is coming soon.
Content for KMP Pattern Matching is coming soon.
Content for LIS O(N log N) is coming soon.
10
Top K Elements — Heap / Priority Queue
Data Structures Heap Gate · Scheduler · Multi-Source Merge · Two-Heap Split · Retroactive Greedy · QuickSelect
42 problems · 5 stages · size-K gate, scheduler, multi-source merge, two-heap split, retroactive greedy, QuickSelect, bucket sort, binary search on value
Why This Topic Matters: The Heap / Priority Queue / Top K Elements cluster solves one precisely-defined class of problems: given n items and a criterion, efficiently identify, maintain, or repeatedly extract the best K items without fully sorting all n. The core invariant is: you never need to know the exact rank of all n elements — you only need to maintain a boundary element (the K-th best so far) and a structure that efficiently evicts anything worse than that boundary. This pattern dominates interviews because it sits at the intersection of three fundamental techniques: heap-based online maintenance O(n log K), QuickSelect-based offline selection O(n) expected, and bucket/counting-based linear-time selection O(n) when value range is bounded. It escalates into multi-source merging, sliding-window maintenance, retroactive greedy, two-heap boundary splitting, interval scheduling, and segment-tree rank queries — making it one of the richest single patterns in the entire DSA curriculum and a direct prerequisite for Dijkstra, Prim, Huffman coding, and external merge sort.
The Mental Model
A heap is a gatekeeping structure. You are a nightclub bouncer keeping exactly K VIPs inside at all times. The gate rule: maintain a min-heap of size K. The root is always the WEAKEST VIP currently inside (the K-th best). Every new arrival is compared only to the weakest VIP. Better → weakest evicted, new arrival enters. Worse → turned away. After all n candidates, the K people inside are exactly the K best.

The universal heap invariant: A size-K min-heap contains exactly the K largest elements seen so far, and heap[0] = the K-th largest element seen so far.

How this scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1 — Simplest form: Key = element value. Min-heap of size K. Push every element; pop when size > K. O(n log K). heap[0] = answer.
• Stage 2 — What changes: The comparison key changes — frequency, distance to target, custom tiebreak, or the heap TYPE flips (min → max) depending on which end you want to evict. The heap structure is identical; only the comparator mutates.
• Stage 3 — What expands: Input is not a flat array. It is a matrix, K sorted lists, or a stream with expiry. The heap must track a SOURCE (list index, row/col) to generate the next candidate. Two heaps work together as a split boundary.
• Stage 4 — What is hidden: The problem does not say "find top K." The heap is a TOOL inside a larger algorithm — retroactive greedy, interval scheduler, graph traversal frontier, or running order statistic. Identifying WHAT to store in the heap is the hard part.
• Stage 5 — What is optimised: O(n log K) is too slow or space is insufficient. Replace with QuickSelect O(n), bucket sort O(n) when values are bounded, or binary search on answer value + count function O(n log V). For dynamic rank queries with deletions, use an order-statistics tree O(log n) per operation.
Core Invariants & Proof Sketches
Invariant 1 — Size-K Min-Heap Gate: After processing i elements, the heap contains exactly min(i, K) elements, and every heap element ≥ every non-heap element (among the first i elements).
Proof sketch (loop invariant): Base (i=0): heap empty, vacuously true. Step i < K: push unconditionally, all elements in heap, trivially holds. Step i ≥ K, new element x: if x ≤ heap[0] → discard, heap unchanged ✓; if x > heap[0] → evict root (now (K+1)-th best), push x, new root = new K-th best ✓. Termination: K heap elements ≥ all n−K discarded elements. □

Invariant 2 — Two-Heap Median Split: lower = max-heap of smaller half; upper = min-heap of larger half. Invariants: |lower| − |upper| ∈ 1 AND lower.top() ≤ upper.top().
Proof sketch: Every insert routes to the correct half (order invariant), then rebalancing restores the size invariant. After both steps: lower.top() = largest of smaller half; upper.top() = smallest of larger half. Median = lower.top() (odd) or (lower.top() + upper.top()) / 2 (even). □

Invariant 3 — QuickSelect Correctness: QuickSelect returns the K-th largest in O(n) expected.
Proof sketch: Each partition places the pivot at its FINAL sorted index. If pivot lands at K−1: return it. Otherwise recurse into only one side. With random pivot, expected search space halves each step → T(n) = T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n) by geometric series. Median-of-medians: T(n) ≤ T(7n/10) + O(n) → O(n) worst-case. □

Invariant 4 — Retroactive Greedy Optimality: Always using the best available skipped option when stuck gives the globally optimal solution.
Proof sketch (exchange argument): Suppose optimal uses option s instead of s* (s* > s). Swap: use s* instead of s → at least as good a result with same number of decisions. No other decision's feasibility changes. By induction, always popping the max from the heap of skipped options is never suboptimal. □
Sub-Variants to Master
Group 1 — Static Size-K Gate (Single Source):
1. K-th largest / Top-K from flat array — Stage 1 · O(n log K) · min-heap size K · THE baseline; root = K-th largest always
2. K-th smallest — Stage 1 · O(n log K) · max-heap size K (negate) · ONE flip: evict largest; root = K-th smallest
3. Top-K by frequency — Stage 2 · O(n log K) · HashMap + min-heap (freq, elem) · derived key from preprocessing map
4. Top-K by distance to target — Stage 2 · O(n log K) · max-heap; key = −|x−target| · keep K smallest distances → max-heap evicts farthest
5. K closest points to origin (2D) — Stage 2 · O(n log K) · max-heap; key = −(x²+y²) · 2D computed key; no sqrt needed
6. Top-K frequent words with tiebreak — Stage 2 · O(n log K) · min-heap; key = (−freq, word) · compound sort key; string comparison in heap tuple

Group 2 — Online / Streaming:
7. K-th largest in a stream — Stage 2 · O(log K) per insert · size-K min-heap persistent across calls · heap lives between queries
8. Running K-th order statistic with deletions — Stage 5 · O(log n) per op · SortedList / order-statistics tree · heap cannot delete

Group 3 — Max-Heap Iterative Merge / Scheduler:
9. Min-heap iterative merge (Huffman) — Stage 2 · O(n log n) · min-heap as source AND sink · heap both consumes and produces values
10. Max-heap iterative extraction (Last Stone) — Stage 2 · O(n log n) · max-heap; extract two, reinsert |a−b|
11. Max-heap greedy transform (halving) — Stage 2 · O(n log n) · extract max, transform, reinsert; reinserted = f(popped)
12. Task scheduler (max-heap + cooldown queue) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · heap stores frequencies + deque tracks cooldown
13. Reorganise string — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · max-heap of char frequencies; output char by char
14. Longest happy string (≤2 consecutive) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · skip top if last 2 chars match

Group 4 — Multi-Source Merge:
15. K-th smallest in sorted matrix — Stage 3 · O(K log K) · min-heap of (val, row, col) · SOURCE TRACKING in heap entries
16. Merge K sorted lists — Stage 3 · O(N log K) · min-heap of (val, list_idx, node)
17. K pairs with smallest sums — Stage 3 · O(K log K) · Cartesian product lazy expansion
18. K-th smallest prime fraction — Stage 3 · O(K log n) · implicit sorted structure; same seeding as K pairs
19. Smallest range covering K lists — Stage 4 · O(N log K) · min-heap + running max variable

Group 5 — Two-Heap Boundary Split:
20. Find median from data stream — Stage 4 · O(log n) insert / O(1) query · max-heap (lower) + min-heap (upper)
21. Sliding window median (with expiry) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · two-heap + lazy deletion dict
22. Dynamic K-th order statistic — Stage 5 · O(log n) per op · split point is K not n/2
23. Dual order-book — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · max-heap (bids) + min-heap (asks); match when buy ≥ sell

Group 6 — Interval / Scheduling Heap:
24. Meeting rooms II (min rooms) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · sort by start + min-heap of end times
25. Meeting rooms III (assign by room ID) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · dual min-heaps with DIFFERENT keys
26. Employee free time — Stage 4 · O(N log K) · multi-source interval merge + gap detection

Group 7 — Retroactive Greedy:
27. Minimum refuelling stops — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · max-heap of skipped fuel stations
28. Furthest building (ladder→bricks swap) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · min-heap of ladder assignments
29. Maximum subsequence score — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · sort by second array desc + min-heap size K
30. Potions greedy (CF 1526C) — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · max-heap of accepted; evict worst when negative

Group 8 — Stage 5 Optimisations (Heap Replacement):
31. K-th largest via QuickSelect — Stage 5 · O(n) expected · in-place randomised partition
32. Top-K frequent via bucket sort — Stage 5 · O(n) · frequency buckets [1..n]
33. K-th smallest pair distance (BS + two-pointer) — Stage 5 · O(n log(max−min)) · binary search on answer + O(n) count
34. K-th in multiplication table (BS + row count) — Stage 5 · O(m log(mn)) · BS + Σ floor(v/i) per row
35. Segment tree rank queries — Stage 5 · O(log V) per query · value-domain tree; handles updates + arbitrary rank
36. Order-statistics tree (insert + delete + rank) — Stage 5 · O(log n) per op · SortedList; only structure supporting insert + delete + K-th rank simultaneously
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Sort everything, take KO(n log n)O(n)Naive baseline
Size-K min-heap gateO(n log K)O(K)Heap gate: only K elements in memory
K-th largest in streamO(log K) per addO(K)Each new element = one heap op
Top-K by frequencyO(n log K)O(n)HashMap preprocessing + derived key
Multi-source merge (K lists)O(N log K)O(K)Source tracking (val, src, idx) in heap
Two-heap median streamO(log n) per addO(n)Two heaps as split boundary; O(1) median
Sliding window medianO(n log n)O(n)Expiry: lazy deletion on both heaps
Retroactive greedy heapO(n log n)O(n)Heap stores past decisions for revision
QuickSelect (offline K-th)O(n) expectedO(1)Partition replaces heap; in-place
Bucket sort Top-K frequentO(n)O(n)Bounded frequency → counting replaces heap
Binary search on value + countO(n log V)O(1)Search value space; count function O(n)
Order-statistics treeO(log n) per opO(n)Delete + arbitrary rank; heap cannot do this
Max vs. Min Heap — Master Decision Table
GoalHeap TypePythonEviction RuleRoot Equals
Keep K largestMin-heap size Kheapq defaultPop when new > heap[0]; push newK-th largest
Keep K smallestMax-heap size KNegate all valuesPop when new < −heap[0]; push newK-th smallest
Repeatedly get maximumMax-heap unboundedNegate all valuesAlways pop rootGlobal max
Repeatedly get minimumMin-heap unboundedheapq defaultAlways pop rootGlobal min
Median — lower halfMax-heapNegateRoot = largest of lower halfLower median
Median — upper halfMin-heapheapq defaultRoot = smallest of upper halfUpper median
Retroactive keep-best decisionsMax-heap of costsNegate if neededPop worst when over-budgetWorst kept so far
Interval: free earliest roomMin-heap of end timesheapq defaultPop when new_start ≥ heap[0]Earliest ending interval
Order-book buy sideMax-heap of bid pricesNegateMatch when buy ≥ sell topHighest buy
Order-book sell sideMin-heap of ask pricesheapq defaultMatch when sell ≤ buy topLowest sell
Mastery Problem Set (42 problems · 5 stages)
#LayerStageSourceProblemDiffPSub-Variant / New IdeaKey Insight
1Foundation1LeetCodeLC 215 Kth Largest Element in an ArrayMedStatic top-K; min-heap size KTHE baseline: min-heap size K; push every element; pop when size > K; root = answer. heap[0] = K-th largest always; O(n log K); establishes the size-K gate invariant everything else builds on
2Foundation1LeetCodeLC 1046 Last Stone WeightMedMax-heap iterative extraction; conditional reinsertionMax-heap baseline: extract two largest, push |a−b| if non-zero; loop until ≤ 1. Establishes MAX-HEAP pattern; heap is BOTH source and sink; contrast with min-heap gate in #1
3Foundation1LeetCodeLC 703 Kth Largest in a StreamMedOnline top-K; persistent min-heap across queriesHeap LIVES between queries; add(val) = push + conditional pop + return root; O(log K) per call. ONE change from #1: data arrives online; heap is a persistent object not a one-shot computation
4Foundation2LeetCodeLC 347 Top K Frequent ElementsMedTop-K by derived frequency keyKey = frequency from HashMap not element value; min-heap of (freq, elem). Adds HashMap preprocessing; comparator changes from value to frequency; same size-K gate
5Foundation2LeetCodeLC 973 K Closest Points to OriginMedTop-K by 2D computed distanceKey = x²+y²; max-heap size K; evict farthest; no sqrt needed (monotone). Direction flip: K SMALLEST distances → max-heap (evict largest); key computed from two coordinates
6Foundation2LeetCodeLC 692 Top K Frequent WordsMedTop-K with lexicographic tiebreakTuple key (−freq, word); string comparison is lexicographic; min-heap evicts correctly under ties. Adds COMPOUND SORT KEY; equal-freq → larger word evicted first
7Foundation2LeetCodeLC 2208 Min Ops to Halve Array SumMedMax-heap; extracted value transformed then reinsertedExtract max; push val/2; stop when sum ≤ original/2. ONE change from #2: reinserted value = transformation of popped value (halved), not a difference
8Variants2LeetCodeLC 451 Sort Characters By FrequencyMedK = n; full frequency sort; bucket sort is O(n)K = all elements; bucket array indexed by freq; scan high to low; O(n). When K = n AND freq bounded, bucket array O(n) beats heap O(n log n)
9Variants2LeetCodeLC 1167 Min Cost to Connect SticksMed✅Min-heap iterative merge (Huffman)Always merge TWO SMALLEST; cost += sum; push sum back. Greedy: merge cheapest pair first minimises total (exchange argument). Min-heap counterpart to #2
10Variants2LeetCodeLC 1801 Number of Orders in BacklogMedDual opposing heap: buy max-heap + sell min-heapBuy backlog = max-heap; sell backlog = min-heap; match when buy ≥ sell. Introduces OPPOSING DUAL HEAP: two heaps face each other; match on boundary condition; order-book simulation
11Variants2LeetCodeLC 253 Meeting Rooms IIMed✅Min-heap of end times; interval schedulingSort by start time; min-heap of end times; if new_start ≥ root → heapreplace; else push. Heap tracks ACTIVE INTERVALS not values; heap size = minimum rooms needed
12Variants3LeetCodeLC 767 Reorganize StringMedMax-heap greedy string builderMax-heap of (freq, char); always pick most-frequent char ≠ previous; decrement; reinsert if > 0. Scheduler without cooldown window; impossible if max_freq > (n+1)//2
13Variants3LeetCodeLC 621 Task SchedulerMedMax-heap of frequencies + cooldown queueMax-heap of task counts; cooldown deque = (remaining_count, available_at); advance time when heap empty. Adds COOLDOWN QUEUE alongside max-heap; release after n cycles
14Variants3LeetCodeLC 1405 Longest Happy StringMedMax-heap scheduler with per-char consecutive limit ≤ 2If top char = last 2 chars, pop second-top instead; reinsert skipped top after. ONE change from #12: consecutive limit = 2; must temporarily skip top when limit hit
15Combo3LeetCodeLC 378 Kth Smallest in Sorted MatrixMedMulti-source min-heap; track (val, row, col)Heap entry = (val, r, c); seed first column; advance: push (matrix[r+1][c], r+1, c). SOURCE TRACKING in heap entries; bridges single-array heap to multi-source pattern
16Combo3LeetCodeLC 373 Find K Pairs with Smallest SumsMedCartesian product lazy expansionSeed (a[i]+b[0], i, 0) for i in 0..min(K, len(a)); advance j: push (a[i]+b[j+1], i, j+1). CARTESIAN PRODUCT generated on demand; seeding only first column avoids O(n²) enumeration
17Combo3LeetCodeLC 786 K-th Smallest Prime FractionMedImplicit sorted structure; heap or binary searchHeap of (arr[i]/arr[j], i, j); OR binary search on fraction value with count. Implicit (unstored) sorted structure; same seeding as #16; introduces BS-on-answer as alternative
18Combo3LeetCodeLC 23 Merge K Sorted ListsHardK-source merge with linked list pointer advancementHard: heap entry = (node.val, list_idx, node); push node.next after extraction. Pointer advancement replaces index arithmetic; None-checking; K up to 10⁴; N up to 5×10⁴
19Combo3LeetCodeLC 632 Smallest Range Covering K ListsHardMulti-source heap + running max; dual-boundary rangeHard: heap minimises left boundary (min of current reps); cur_max variable tracks right boundary. Heap controls ONLY one side of the range; right boundary tracked separately; two boundaries move asymmetrically
20Combo4LeetCodeLC 295 Find Median from Data StreamHardTwo-heap boundary split: max-heap (lower) + min-heap (upper)Hard: lower = max-heap of smaller half; upper = min-heap of larger half; invariants: size and order. Two heaps as SPLIT BOUNDARY; median from tops in O(1); rebalancing on every insert
21Combo4LeetCodeLC 759 Employee Free TimeHard✅Multi-source interval merge; find GAPSHard: K employee schedules; min-heap of (start, emp, idx); track max_end; gap when next_start > max_end. Multi-source INTERVAL merge not value merge; gap detection requires tracking max_end outside the heap
22Combo4LeetCodeLC 2402 Meeting Rooms IIIHardDual min-heaps with DIFFERENT comparison keysHard: free = min-heap of room IDs; used = min-heap of (end_time, room_id); interact on every meeting. Two min-heaps with different keys interact on every event; tie-breaking by room ID
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Layer 3 → Layer 4. The next problems require using the heap as a RETROACTIVE GREEDY instrument. The heap stores past decisions that can be revised. The insight shifts from "what is the current best?" to "which past decision should I undo to improve the outcome?"
23Hard4LeetCodeLC 871 Minimum Number of Refueling StopsHardMax-heap retroactive greedy; defer refuel until stuckHard: push all SKIPPABLE stations to max-heap while driving; when tank empty, retroactively use best skipped station. Max-heap enables RETROACTIVE GREEDY — store options as you pass them; exercise only when forced
24Hard4LeetCodeLC 1642 Furthest Building You Can ReachMedMin-heap retroactive swap; ladder→bricks conversionPush every positive climb to min-heap as "ladder use"; when size > k, pop smallest (convert to bricks). Same retroactive pattern as #23 but direction reversed: assign ladders to BIGGEST climbs by evicting smallest
25Hard4LeetCodeLC 2542 Maximum Subsequence ScoreMedSort by second array desc; min-heap size K on first arraySort pairs by nums2[i] desc; for each i maintain min-heap of K largest nums1 values; score = sum × nums2[i]. Stage 4 disguise: no "top-K" stated; must recognise fixing minimum multiplier
26Hard4LeetCodeLC 2163 Min Difference in Sums After RemovalHardTwo independent heap scans: left max-heap + right min-heapHard: left scan: max-heap size n → tracks sum of n SMALLEST; right scan: min-heap size n → sum of n LARGEST; outputs combined at each split point. WHY hard: correct heap TYPE per direction
27Hard4LeetCodeLC 480 Sliding Window MedianHardTwo-heap split with ELEMENT EXPIRY; lazy deletionHard: two heaps + lazy-deletion dict; prune stale tops of BOTH heaps before reading median; effective size counters. WHY hard: stale pruning must happen on both heaps before every median read
28Hard4LeetCodeLC 407 Trapping Rain Water IIHardMin-heap as BFS frontier on 2D gridHard: init heap with all boundary cells; pop min-height cell; water at neighbour = max(popped, neighbour_height) − neighbour_height. Heap as PRIORITY BFS FRONTIER not a top-K extractor
29Hard5CodeforcesCF 1526C Potions (Hard)HardMax-heap retroactive DP greedyHard: accept each potion greedily; if health < 0, evict worst accepted (max-heap of negative values); count survivors. Retroactive greedy on DP-structured problem; evicting worst past decision = not taking it
30Hard5LeetCodeLC 1792 Maximum Average Pass RatioMedMax-heap of MARGINAL GAIN; key changes after each popHeap key = gain from adding one student: (p+1)/(t+1) − p/t; always improve class with max gain; recompute after pop. Max-heap of DELTA VALUES; key changes after every extraction; pure greedy via marginal improvement
31Hard5LeetCodeLC 2462 Total Cost to Hire K WorkersMedDual min-heaps from both ends; sliding frontierLeft min-heap: first candidates workers; right min-heap: last candidates; each hire = pop global min; advance that heap's frontier inward. Stage 4 disguise: "hire K workers" hides dual sliding-window + min-heaps
32Hard5LeetCodeLC 778 Swim in Rising WaterMedMin-heap Dijkstra on grid; minimise path BOTTLENECKMin-heap of (max_height_on_path, r, c); pop minimum; expand; answer = heap root when destination reached. Looks like BFS but needs heap (minimise maximum height = minimax path); Dijkstra pattern on grid
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Layer 4 → Layer 5. The next problems require recognising that a heap is SUBOPTIMAL and must be replaced entirely. The Stage 5 instinct: "Can I search the VALUE SPACE instead of the element space? Can I count elements ≤ x faster than sorting them?"
33Hard5LeetCodeLC 215 Kth Largest (QuickSelect variant)MedO(n) expected QuickSelect; randomised pivotIn-place Lomuto partition; recurse into only one side; O(n) expected with random pivot. Stage 5 upgrade of #1: O(n) replaces O(n log K); no extra space; random pivot prevents adversarial O(n²)
34Hard5LeetCodeLC 347 Top K Frequent (bucket sort variant)MedO(n) bucket sort; frequency bounded by nbucket[freq] = list of elements; scan from bucket[n] downward; stop at K collected. Stage 5 upgrade of #4: frequency ∈ [1,n] → bucket array replaces heap; O(n) time and space
35Hard5LeetCodeLC 719 Find K-th Smallest Pair DistanceHardBinary search on distance value + two-pointer countHard: sort; binary search on answer d; count(d) = pairs with distance ≤ d via sliding two-pointer O(n); total O(n log(max−min)). Stage 4 recognition (K-th problem but NOT heap) + Stage 5 BS-on-answer
36Hard5LeetCodeLC 668 Kth Smallest in Multiplication TableHardBinary search on value; count = Σ min(n, floor(v/i)) per rowHard: binary search on v; count(v) = Σᵢ₌₁ᵐ min(n, floor(v/i)); O(m) per BS step. Implicit matrix; heap infeasible for large m,n; deriving the O(m) count function is the key
37Hard5CodeforcesCF 1354C Not SittingHardSort seat distances; binary search on rankHard: precompute all seat distances; binary search on rank; count seats with dist ≤ mid. No "top-K" stated; reduce "K-th best seat" to sorted rank problem; Stage 4 reduction + Stage 5 binary search
⚠️ TRAP ZONE — Problems that LOOK like Heap / Top-K but require a completely different technique. Recognise them in < 30 seconds.
38⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 658 ⚠️ Find K Closest ElementsMed⚠️ TRAP — sorted input; binary search + two-pointer beats heapArray SORTED; binary search for insertion point; two-pointer expansion O(K); total O(log n + K). NOT a heap problem: sorted input eliminates heap maintenance; always check if input is sorted before defaulting to heap
39⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 4 ⚠️ Median of Two Sorted ArraysHard⚠️ TRAP — two sorted arrays; binary partition O(log min(m,n))Binary partition both arrays; left_half = (m+n)/2 with left_max ≤ right_min; O(log min(m,n)). NOT a heap problem despite being K-th across two sorted sequences; heap merge = correct but 100× slower
40⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 239 ⚠️ Sliding Window MaximumHard⚠️ TRAP — K=1 sliding window; monotonic deque beats heapMonotonic deque maintains decreasing order; front = window max; O(n) total. NOT optimal as heap: K=1 sliding window → deque O(n) vs lazy-deletion heap O(n log n)
41⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 1686 ⚠️ Stone Game VIMed⚠️ TRAP — two players; sort by combined value a[i]+b[i]Sort by a[i]+b[i] desc; alternate picks; O(n log n) greedy. NOT a heap problem: combined-value sort makes globally optimal greedy; heap = 10× more code for same result
42⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 2140 ⚠️ Solving Questions with BrainpowerMed⚠️ TRAP — looks like scheduler but is single-player DPSingle decision-maker; standard 1D DP dp[i] = max(pts[i]+dp[i+bp[i]+1], dp[i+1]). NOT a heap/scheduler problem: no opponent, no cooldown priority; applying task-scheduler logic gives wrong answer
Two-Heap Complete Implementation Reference
Static two-heap (Find Median from Data Stream): 
import heapq
lower = []   # max-heap (negate): stores the SMALLER half, size = ceil(n/2)
upper = []   # min-heap (default): stores the LARGER half, size = floor(n/2)

def add(val):
    if not lower or val <= -lower[0]:
        heapq.heappush(lower, -val)
    else:
        heapq.heappush(upper, val)
    # rebalance: |lower| - |upper| must be 0 or 1
    if len(lower) > len(upper) + 1:
        heapq.heappush(upper, -heapq.heappop(lower))
    elif len(upper) > len(lower):
        heapq.heappush(lower, -heapq.heappop(upper))

def get_median():
    if len(lower) > len(upper): return float(-lower[0])
    return (-lower[0] + upper[0]) / 2.0
Sliding-window two-heap (Sliding Window Median) with lazy deletion: 
from collections import defaultdict
lazy = defaultdict(int)   # lazy[v] = times v is marked deleted but not pruned
lower_sz = upper_sz = 0

def _prune(heap, is_lower):
    while heap:
        top = -heap[0] if is_lower else heap[0]
        if lazy[top] > 0:
            lazy[top] -= 1; heapq.heappop(heap)
        else: break

def add_w(val):
    global lower_sz, upper_sz
    if not lower or val <= -lower[0]: heapq.heappush(lower, -val); lower_sz += 1
    else: heapq.heappush(upper, val); upper_sz += 1
    _rebalance_w()

def remove_w(val):
    global lower_sz, upper_sz
    lazy[val] += 1
    if val <= -lower[0]: lower_sz -= 1
    else: upper_sz -= 1
    _rebalance_w()

def _rebalance_w():
    global lower_sz, upper_sz
    while lower_sz > upper_sz + 1:
        _prune(lower, True)
        heapq.heappush(upper, -heapq.heappop(lower))
        lower_sz -= 1; upper_sz += 1
    while upper_sz > lower_sz:
        _prune(upper, False)
        heapq.heappush(lower, -heapq.heappop(upper))
        upper_sz -= 1; lower_sz += 1

def get_median_w():
    _prune(lower, True); _prune(upper, False)
    if lower_sz > upper_sz: return float(-lower[0])
    return (-lower[0] + upper[0]) / 2.0
Retroactive Greedy Universal Template
import heapq
heap   = []   # stores past decisions as (cost_key, metadata)
result = 0    # current state (fuel, health, total cost, etc.)

for item in items:
    heapq.heappush(heap, cost_key(item))   # greedily accept current item
    result = accept(result, item)

    while constraint_violated(result):     # if constraint broken...
        worst = heapq.heappop(heap)        # undo worst past decision
        result = undo(result, worst)

# result = optimal value; len(heap) = optimal count of decisions
Retroactive problems and their heap type:
• LC 871 Refueling Stops — max-heap of fuel at skipped stations; pop when tank empty = "I retroactively stopped there"
• LC 1642 Furthest Building — min-heap of climb sizes; pop smallest when size > k = convert that climb to bricks
• CF 1526C Potions — max-heap of negative potion values; pop worst when health < 0 = never took that potion
• LC 2542 Max Subsequence Score — min-heap of nums1 values in window; pop when size > k = exclude that element from sum
State Evolution Across Stages
Stage 1 — Flat array, simple value key: 
heap = []
for x in arr:
    heapq.heappush(heap, x)        # min-heap; push raw value
    if len(heap) > k:
        heapq.heappop(heap)        # evict the SMALLEST (weakest VIP)
# heap[0] = K-th largest; list(heap) = the K largest (unsorted)
Stage 2 — Derived key (frequency / distance / tuple): 
from collections import Counter
freq = Counter(arr)
heap = []
for elem, cnt in freq.items():
    heapq.heappush(heap, (cnt, elem))   # tuple: (primary_key, secondary_key)
    if len(heap) > k:
        heapq.heappop(heap)             # evicts the tuple with smallest (cnt, elem)
# Tiebreak: for lexicographic-ascending result, use (-cnt, word)
Stage 3 — Multi-source with source pointer: 
heap = [(matrix[0][c], 0, c) for c in range(min(k, cols))]
heapq.heapify(heap)
for _ in range(k - 1):
    val, r, c = heapq.heappop(heap)
    if r + 1 < rows:
        heapq.heappush(heap, (matrix[r+1][c], r+1, c))  # advance source
return heapq.heappop(heap)[0]
Stage 5 — QuickSelect O(n) replaces O(n log K): 
import random
def quickselect(nums, lo, hi, k_idx):
    if lo >= hi: return nums[lo]
    pivot_pos = random.randint(lo, hi)
    nums[pivot_pos], nums[hi] = nums[hi], nums[pivot_pos]
    i = lo
    for j in range(lo, hi):
        if nums[j] >= nums[hi]:
            nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
            i += 1
    nums[i], nums[hi] = nums[hi], nums[i]
    if i == k_idx:  return nums[i]
    elif i > k_idx: return quickselect(nums, lo, i - 1, k_idx)
    else:            return quickselect(nums, i + 1, hi, k_idx)
# K-th largest = quickselect(nums, 0, n-1, k-1)
Stage 5 — Bucket sort O(n) for bounded frequency: 
from collections import Counter
freq   = Counter(nums)
bucket = [[] for _ in range(len(nums) + 1)]
for elem, cnt in freq.items():
    bucket[cnt].append(elem)
result = []
for f in range(len(bucket) - 1, 0, -1):
    result.extend(bucket[f])
    if len(result) >= k:
        return result[:k]
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "find the K largest / K smallest / K most frequent / K closest" → Stage 1/2; establish which END to evict; key may be derived; default to size-K heap
• "return the K-th element" → Static → QuickSelect O(n); online/stream → persistent size-K heap O(log K) per add; check if sorted first (→ binary search)
• "merge K sorted [lists / arrays / streams]" → Stage 3 multi-source heap; (value, source_id, position) in heap; advance pointer on extraction
• "find the median of a stream / running median" → Stage 4 two-heap split; max-heap lower half + min-heap upper half; O(1) median, O(log n) insert
• "schedule tasks with cooldown / no two adjacent same" → Stage 4 max-heap scheduler; heap stores frequencies; greedy = most-frequent valid task each round
• "minimum number of [stops / ladders / operations]" → Stage 4 retroactive greedy; max-heap of past decisions; pop worst when constraint violated

Structural Triggers:
• Unsorted array/stream AND K ≪ n → size-K gate; O(n log K) time, O(K) space; do NOT sort
• K sorted sequences and you want merged top-M elements → multi-source heap; (value, src, pos); O(M log K)
• Ongoing rank maintenance with both insertions AND deletions → SortedList or order-statistics tree; heap cannot arbitrarily delete

Anti-Triggers (Do NOT use heap):
• "find K closest in a SORTED array" → NOT a heap → two-pointer binary search O(log n + K)
• "find median of TWO sorted arrays" → NOT a heap → binary search on partition O(log min(m,n))
• "sliding window MAXIMUM" → NOT optimal as heap → monotonic deque O(n) total
When This Technique Breaks
Bug 1 — Max-heap of all n elements instead of size-K min-heap (wrong space complexity): 
# BUG: stores all n elements; O(n) space; crashes for n=10^8, K=10
heap = []
for num in nums: heapq.heappush(heap, -num)   # unbounded max-heap
# FIX: size-K min-heap; O(K) space
heap = []
for num in nums:
    heapq.heappush(heap, num)
    if len(heap) > k: heapq.heappop(heap)
return heap[0]
Bug 2 — Stream heap: returning root before evicting oversized element: 
# BUG: heap may have k+1 elements when root is returned
def add(self, val):
    heapq.heappush(self.heap, val)
    return self.heap[0]   # BUG: k+1 elements possible
# FIX: evict first, then return
def add(self, val):
    heapq.heappush(self.heap, val)
    if len(self.heap) > self.k: heapq.heappop(self.heap)
    return self.heap[0]
Bug 3 — Top-K frequent words: wrong tiebreak direction in tuple: 
# BUG: (freq, word) evicts lex-smaller word among equal freq; problem wants to KEEP lex-smaller
heapq.heappush(heap, (freq, word))   # BUG
# FIX: (-freq, word); among same -freq, min-heap evicts lex-larger → keeps lex-smaller
heapq.heappush(heap, (-freq, word))
Bug 4 — K Closest Points: using sqrt introduces float errors: 
# BUG: sqrt is monotone; introduces unnecessary float errors
dist = math.sqrt(x**2 + y**2)
heapq.heappush(heap, (-dist, x, y))   # BUG
# FIX: compare squared distance directly (integer arithmetic)
dist_sq = x**2 + y**2
heapq.heappush(heap, (-dist_sq, x, y))
Bug 5 — QuickSelect: deterministic pivot causes O(n²) on sorted input: 
# BUG: always last element as pivot; O(n^2) on sorted input (most common interview input)
pivot = nums[hi]
# FIX: always randomise
pivot_pos = random.randint(lo, hi)
nums[pivot_pos], nums[hi] = nums[hi], nums[pivot_pos]
Bug 6 — Multi-source merge: forgetting to advance the source pointer:
Every extraction from source s MUST trigger a push from source s (if next element exists). Missing node.next or (a[i], b[j+1]) produces incomplete results. Invariant: extraction from source s = push next element from source s.
Bug 7 — Not recognising retroactive greedy pattern:
If greedy gives wrong answers, ask "can I undo my worst past decision?" If yes: store past decisions in a heap; when constraint is violated, pop the worst one. Max-heap evicts most costly; min-heap evicts least gainful.
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem ask for a SUBSET of size K ranked by some criterion?
  → NO:  Not Top-K. Check if it is sorting, DP, or binary search.
  → YES: Continue.

Q2: Is the input ALREADY SORTED?
  → YES: Do NOT use a heap.
         K closest in sorted array     → binary search + two-pointer  O(log n + K)
         K-th across two sorted arrays → binary search on partition   O(log min(m,n))
         K-th in sorted matrix         → binary search on value+count O(n log V)
  → NO:  Continue.

Q3: Is data ONLINE (stream) or does it require INSERT + DELETE?
  → Online stream, no deletions → persistent size-K heap  O(log K) per element
  → With deletions / arbitrary rank → SortedList / order-statistics tree  O(log n) per op
  → Static offline batch → Continue.

Q4: Do you need ALL K elements, or only THE K-TH element?
  → Only K-th element → QuickSelect  O(n) expected
  → All K elements → size-K heap  O(n log K)  Continue.

Q5: Is the comparison key SIMPLE VALUE or DERIVED?
  → Simple value → standard min/max heap; push raw value
  → Derived key → precompute (HashMap) or compute inline; heap stores tuple
  → Compound key with tiebreak → heap stores (primary, secondary, element) tuple

Q6: Does the problem involve K SORTED SOURCES (merge pattern)?
  → YES → multi-source min-heap. Store (value, source_id, pos).
           Advance pointer in source_id on every extraction.  O(output_size × log K)

Q7: Does the problem need a RUNNING MEDIAN or DYNAMIC K-TH ORDER STAT?
  → YES (median only) → two-heap split  O(log n) insert, O(1) median
  → YES (with sliding window) → two-heap + lazy deletion dict

Q8: Is there a RETROACTIVE GREEDY structure?
  → YES → heap of past decisions; pop worst when constraint violated
           Max-heap if evicting most costly; min-heap if evicting least gainful

Q9: Is the VALUE RANGE BOUNDED (values in [0, V], V ≤ 10^6)?
  → YES (Top-K frequent) → bucket sort  O(n)
  → YES (K-th rank with updates) → segment tree on value domain  O(log V)

Q10: Is O(n log K) too slow?
  → QuickSelect → O(n) expected
  → Bounded key → bucket sort O(n)
  → "K-th over implicit structure" → binary search on answer value + count function

Advanced Graph Algorithms
Advanced Overview
Network flows, SCC, bridges, articulation points, MST variations
Advanced Topics: Max Flow (Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp, Dinic's), Strongly Connected Components (Kosaraju's, Tarjan's), Bridges & Articulation Points (Tarjan's), Minimum Spanning Tree variations (Kruskal, Prim), and other network flow applications.
30
1D Linear DP
Dynamic Programming Fixed-Window · Kadane · Knapsack · LIS · State Machine · CHT
45 problems · 5 stages · fixed-window, knapsack, LIS family, state machines, CHT/deque/D&C optimisations
Why It Matters: 1D Linear DP is the foundational engine of all dynamic programming — it solves problems where the answer at position i depends only on answers at earlier positions in a single sequence. The core invariant is: dp[i] represents the optimal answer for the subproblem ending at or considering only the first i elements, and this value is computed exactly once from previously computed values. It appears in virtually every interview and competition because it is both the gateway to all harder DP (2D, tree, bitmask) and a source of non-trivial hard problems in its own right. The critical skill tested is not implementation — the recurrence is often one line — but state definition: choosing what dp[i] means precisely enough that the recurrence is correct and the transitions are O(1) or O(log n). At the ICPC/IOI level, the gap between a naïve O(n²) 1D DP and its O(n log n) optimised form separates correct solutions from AC solutions.
The Mental Model
1D Linear DP is a conveyor belt. Each item i arrives and you ask one question: "What is the best outcome I can achieve that ends with or includes item i?" You answer using only items already processed. You stamp the answer on item i and it joins the belt, available for all future items.

How the mental model scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1: The stamp (dp[i]) depends on a fixed, small number of previous stamps — dp[i-1], dp[i-2]. Constant-time recurrence.
• Stage 2: One constraint changes what "best" means — no two adjacent, at most k uses, circular boundary, count instead of optimise. The formula shifts by one condition.
• Stage 3: The stamp now has two dimensions — you track TWO aspects of the state simultaneously. dp[i][state] where state encodes an additional binary, small-integer, or hash-map-keyed condition.
• Stage 4: The conveyor belt is not the raw input. You must transform the input (sort, re-index, convert problem) before the belt runs. The stamping logic is identical to a known pattern — recognition of the transformation IS the hard part.
• Stage 5: Computing the stamp requires the best of all previous stamps satisfying some range condition — naïvely O(n) per step. A data structure (binary search, deque, segment tree, CHT) collapses this to O(log n) per step.
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch
Invariant: dp[i] holds the optimal value of the subproblem defined on the prefix arr[0..i], with arr[i] as the "anchor" (last/included element).

Proof sketch (by strong induction):
• Base case: dp[0] is correct by direct computation — no previous elements.
• Inductive hypothesis: Assume dp[j] correct for all j < i.
• Inductive step: dp[i] = f(dp[j₁], dp[j₂], ..., arr[i]) where all jₖ < i. Each dp[jₖ] is correct by hypothesis → dp[i] is correct.
• Optimal substructure check: The recurrence is valid ONLY if "the optimal solution for arr[0..i] that uses arr[i] as its last element consists of arr[i] itself, plus the optimal solution for some arr[0..j]." Verify this explicitly for every new problem.
• Answer extraction: Either dp[n-1], max(dp[0..n-1]), or some aggregation — depends on problem definition.
Sub-Variants to Master (29 total)
1. Fixed-window recurrence (Fibonacci / Climbing Stairs) — Stage 1 · O(n) · two variables
2. Linear scan with reset (Kadane / max subarray) — Stage 1 · O(n) · single variable with reset
3. Count of valid subarrays ending at i (Arithmetic Slices) — Stage 2 · O(n) · additive reset recurrence
4. Skip-one constraint (House Robber) — Stage 2 · O(n) · two variables
5. Circular skip-one constraint — Stage 2 · O(n) · run linear DP twice
6. Counting variant (number of ways) — Stage 2 · O(n) · additive recurrence
7. Catalan number DP (product of two sub-DPs) — Stage 3 · O(n²) · product recurrence
8. Boolean partition DP (Word Break) — Stage 2 · O(n²) · boolean DP with inner loop
9. Unbounded knapsack (Coin Change min/count) — Stage 2 · O(n·target) · forward iteration
10. 0/1 Knapsack (boolean/value) — Stage 2 · O(n·target) · reverse iteration
11. Tiling DP with partial boundary state (Domino/Tromino) — Stage 3 · O(n) · profile state
12. Two-state Kadane (one deletion allowed) — Stage 3 · O(n) · two parallel Kadane states
13. 3-state machine DP (Paint House) — Stage 3 · O(n) · 3 variables per row
14. k-state machine, optimised (Paint House II) — Stage 5 · O(nk) · min1/min2 trick
15. Multi-constraint state machine (Student Attendance II) — Stage 3 · O(n) · 6 states
16. Two-state machine DP (Stock buy/sell) — Stage 3 · O(n) · hold/not-hold states
17. Multi-state machine DP (Stock cooldown/fee) — Stage 3 · O(n) · 3+ states
18. Dual-tracking DP (max AND min product) — Stage 3 · O(n) · track both extremes for sign flip
19. LIS O(n²) — Stage 3 · O(n²) · range max over valid predecessors
20. LIS on pairs (Fibonacci Subsequence) — Stage 3 · O(n²) · hash map for predecessor lookup
21. LIS with difference as dimension (Arithmetic Subsequence) — Stage 3 · O(n²) · hash map per index
22. LCS space-optimised to 1D — Stage 3 · O(nm) · rolling array
23. Palindrome DP (count / longest) — Stage 3 · O(n²) · expand or precompute table
24. Weighted LIS / LIS with value — Stage 4 · O(n log n) · BIT/segment tree on value domain
25. String segmentation / palindrome min-cut — Stage 4 · O(n²) · precompute palindrome table
26. LIS O(n log n) — Stage 5 · O(n log n) · patience sorting / binary search on tails
27. Sliding window max on DP array — Stage 5 · O(n) · monotone deque
28. Divide and Conquer DP — Stage 5 · O(n log n) · monotone opt point
29. Convex Hull Trick (CHT) — Stage 5 · O(n) amortised · convex hull of lines
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Fixed-window (Fibonacci)O(n)O(1)Baseline
Kadane (max subarray)O(n)O(1)Reset condition in recurrence
Arithmetic Slices (count ending at i)O(n)O(1)Aggregation changes from max to sum
Skip-one (House Robber)O(n)O(1)Adjacency constraint; uses dp[i-2]
Circular skip-oneO(n)O(1)Run twice; no new structure
Boolean partition (Word Break)O(n²)O(n)Inner loop over all split points
Unbounded / 0-1 KnapsackO(n·T)O(T)Second dimension T = target
Catalan (Unique BST)O(n²)O(n)Product of two sub-DPs
Two-state Kadane (one deletion)O(n)O(1)Second parallel state added
3-state machine (Paint House)O(n)O(1)3 states, exclude-self transitions
Multi-constraint state machineO(n·S)O(S)S = Cartesian product of constraints
Dual min/max tracking (product)O(n)O(1)Track both extremes for sign flip
LIS O(n²)O(n²)O(n)Range max over valid predecessors
LIS on pairs (Fibonacci Subseq)O(n²)O(n²)2D state; hash map predecessor
LIS with difference (Arith Subseq)O(n²)O(n²)Difference d as explicit dimension
LIS O(n log n)O(n log n)O(n)Binary search on tails array
Palindrome min-cutO(n²)O(n)Palindrome table precomputed
Sliding window max on DPO(n)O(n)Monotone deque on DP array
CHT optimised DPO(n)O(n)Convex hull of lines
D&C DPO(n log n)O(n)Monotone opt enables D&C
Mastery Problem Set (45 problems · 5 stages)
#LayerStageSourceProblemDiffPSub-Variant / New IdeaKey Insight
1Foundation1LeetCodeLC 746 Min Cost Climbing StairsMedFixed-window recurrenceTHE baseline — dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]); two base cases; answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]); teaches sentinel-free two-variable rolling
2Foundation1LeetCodeLC 53 Maximum SubarrayMedKadane (reset recurrence)dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1] + nums[i]); reset when extending previous makes it worse; answer = max(dp)
3Foundation2LeetCodeLC 413 Arithmetic SlicesMedCount of valid subarrays ending at idp[i] = dp[i-1]+1 if arithmetic; else 0; sum(dp) = total count; teaches count-via-ending-at-i
4Foundation2LeetCodeLC 198 House RobberMedSkip-one constraintdp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]); same two-variable structure as #1 with value accumulation; skip-one is the one new constraint
5Foundation2LeetCodeLC 213 House Robber IIMedCircular skip-oneCircular → run on [0..n-2] AND [1..n-1]; take max. Two linear subproblems; identical recurrence, different input bounds
6Foundation2LeetCodeLC 740 Delete and EarnMedSkip-one on transformed inputTransform: bucket[v] = v × count(v); then House Robber. Input transformation precedes identical recurrence; teaches pre-processing before DP
7Foundation2LeetCodeLC 322 Coin ChangeMedUnbounded knapsack (min)dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j-coin]+1); second dimension is target sum j; forward iteration enables unlimited reuse
8Variants2LeetCodeLC 518 Coin Change IIMedUnbounded knapsack (count)dp[j] += dp[j-coin]; loop order: coins outer, target inner — critical for deduplication; changes min to +=
9Variants2LeetCodeLC 416 Partition Equal Subset SumMed0/1 knapsack (boolean)dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j-nums[i]]; reverse iteration is the SINGLE new idea separating 0/1 from unbounded
10Variants2LeetCodeLC 139 Word BreakMedBoolean partition DPdp[i] = any(dp[j] and s[j:i] in wordSet for j<i); dp[0]=True; inner loop over all split points; string validity check gates transition
11Variants2LeetCodeLC 279 Perfect SquaresMedUnbounded knapsack on implicit coin setGenerate coin set first (all squares ≤ n); then identical to #7. Implicit coin generation before DP
12Variants2LeetCodeLC 91 Decode WaysMedConditional partition DP1-digit and 2-digit transitions only if substring is valid (1–26, no leading zero); dp[0]=1; teaches encoding validity as transition guards
13Variants2LeetCodeLC 639 Decode Ways IIHardCounting DP with wildcard multiplierHard: exhaustive case analysis of * × *, * × digit, digit × *; multiplier up to 9 (1-digit) / 15 (2-digit); modular arithmetic. Draw 3×3 case grid to avoid missing branches
14Variants3LeetCodeLC 96 Unique Binary Search TreesMedCatalan number DP (product recurrence)dp[i] = sum(dp[j-1] * dp[i-j]) — FIRST product of two sub-DPs; left and right subtrees independent → multiply counts; differs from all previous additive recurrences
15Variants3LeetCodeLC 152 Maximum Product SubarrayMedDual-tracking DP (max AND min)Track both max_dp[i] and min_dp[i]; two parallel states per position; sign flip makes minimum the future maximum
16Variants3LeetCodeLC 2708 Maximum Strength of a GroupMedDual-tracking on subsequences (not subarrays)Three candidates: start fresh, extend max, extend min; "start fresh" is NEW vs. #15; non-contiguous subsequence generalisation
17Variants3LeetCodeLC 1186 Maximum Subarray Sum with One DeletionMedTwo-state Kadane (deletion budget)dp_one_del[i] = max(dp_no_del[i-1], dp_one_del[i-1]+arr[i]); one-way valve: no_del → one_del by "deleting" current element; transition between states is new
18Variants3LeetCodeLC 256 Paint HouseMed✅3-state machine DPdp[i][c] = costs[i][c] + min(dp[i-1][c'] for c'≠c) — exclude-self transitions; first 3-state machine; generalises to k colors
19Combo3LeetCodeLC 790 Domino and Tromino TilingMedTiling DP with partial boundary (profile DP)full[i] = full[i-1] + full[i-2] + 2*partial[i-1]; partial profile state for tromino column-straddling shape; introduces profile tracking
20Combo3LeetCodeLC 309 Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with CooldownMed3-state machine with mandatory gapThree states: held, sold, rest; mandatory skip day after sell. Three-variable rolling; cooldown forces rest before held can be re-entered
21Combo3LeetCodeLC 552 Student Attendance Record IIHardMulti-constraint state machine (6 states)Hard: (a ∈ 1) × (l ∈ 2) = 6-state Cartesian product; transitions must enforce BOTH constraints simultaneously; state-count explosion is non-obvious
22Combo3LeetCodeLC 300 Longest Increasing SubsequenceMedLIS O(n²)dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for j<i if nums[j]<nums[i]) — first range over all predecessors; teaches the "ending-at-i" LIS invariant; answer = max(dp) NOT dp[n-1]
23Combo3LeetCodeLC 873 Length of Longest Fibonacci SubsequenceMedLIS on pairs (2D state with hash map)State is pair of indices dp[i][j]; predecessor lookup via hash map in O(1); state explosion to O(n²) is unavoidable — two elements needed to define the "difference" constraint
24Combo3LeetCodeLC 1027 Longest Arithmetic SubsequenceMedLIS with difference as hash-map dimensiondp[i][d] — difference d is EXPLICIT state dimension stored per index as hash map; unlike Fibonacci (implicit diff), arithmetic diff must be explicit because same index ends many APs
25Combo3LeetCodeLC 1143 Longest Common SubsequenceMedLCS (2D DP → 1D rolling array)dp[j] = LCS of text1[0..i] and text2[0..j]; teaches space optimisation of 2D recurrence via rolling array
26Combo3LeetCodeLC 647 Palindromic SubstringsMedPalindrome counting DPdp[i][j] = is s[i..j] a palindrome; base cases single chars + adjacent pairs; expand outward; answer = sum of True
27Combo3LeetCodeLC 5 Longest Palindromic SubstringMedPalindrome span DPReuses palindrome table from #26; changes aggregation from count to argmax of length
28Combo3LeetCodeLC 123 Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IIIHardMulti-state machine (k=2 transactions)Hard: dp[i][t][0/1] — adds transaction counter as third state dimension; three-dimensional state (day × transactions × holding); transaction increments on SELL only
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 3 → Stage 4. Next problems don't announce "use DP." The input must be TRANSFORMED and the state definition is non-obvious. For every Stage 4 problem: (1) name the transformation, (2) name which known DP pattern it reduces to, BEFORE writing any code.
29Combo4LeetCodeLC 376 Wiggle SubsequenceMedTwo-state alternating DPup[i] / down[i]; input looks greedy but the two-state DP formulation is the hidden structure; alternating condition = two states referencing each other in transitions
30Combo4LeetCodeLC 1048 Longest String ChainMedLIS on transformed input (word predecessors)Sort by length; dp[word] = longest chain ending at word; predecessor = remove one letter; hash map replaces array; predecessor generation (all length-1 deletions) IS the transformation
31Combo4LeetCodeLC 368 Largest Divisible SubsetMedLIS with divisibility as order relationSort first; replace < with b % a == 0; parent tracking for reconstruction; LIS recurrence with custom order; sort is MANDATORY because divisibility ≠ array order
32Hard4LeetCodeLC 1235 Maximum Profit in Job SchedulingHardWeighted job scheduling (LIS + binary search)Hard: sort by end time; dp[i] = max profit; binary search finds last non-overlapping job. Reduction to weighted LIS is non-obvious; binary search for predecessor is the Stage 5 entry point
33Hard4LeetCodeLC 132 Palindrome Partitioning IIHardMin-cut DP with palindrome precomputationHard: two-phase — precompute palindrome table then run min-cut DP. Naïve O(n³); O(n²) requires SEPARATING palindrome check from DP loop entirely
34Hard4LeetCodeLC 472 Concatenated WordsHardWord Break applied to each word as targetHard: each word IS the segmentation target; run Word Break with remaining words as dictionary. The reduction (each word = target string for #10) is non-obvious; outer loop over words
35Hard4LeetCodeLC 1326 Minimum Number of Taps to OpenHardInterval coverage DP (Jump Game in disguise)Hard: transform tap i → interval [i-r, i+r]; then identical to Jump Game II. "Taps" disguise hides interval-coverage structure; recognising the reduction IS the problem
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 4 → Stage 5. Next problems have correct O(n²) solutions that TLE. Identify WHICH inner loop is the bottleneck and match it to a data structure: binary search (LIS), monotone deque (window max), CHT (linear recurrence), D&C (monotone opt).
36Hard5LeetCodeLC 300 LIS O(n log n)MedLIS with binary search on tailsReplace O(n) inner scan with bisect_left on tails array; tails[k] = smallest tail of IS of length k+1; binary search finds position; len(tails) = LIS length
37Hard5LeetCodeLC 265 Paint House IIHard✅k-color DP with min1/min2 optimisationHard: track only two smallest values of previous row; O(nk²) → O(nk). Proving only top-2 values suffice regardless of k requires careful case analysis
38Hard5LeetCodeLC 1425 Constrained Subsequence SumHardSliding window max on DP array (monotone deque)Hard: dp[i] = nums[i] + max(0, max(dp[i-k..i-1])); the inner O(k) max is the bottleneck; monotone deque maintains decreasing dp values in window → O(1) amortised
39Hard5LeetCodeLC 1691 Maximum Height by Stacking CuboidsHard3D LIS with multi-dimensional sortHard: sort dimensions within each box AND sort boxes; apply 3D-dominance LIS. Sorting both within and across cuboids before LIS is non-obvious; 3-attribute dominance
40Hard5LeetCodeLC 2407 Longest Increasing Subsequence IIHardLIS with range max on value domain (segment tree)Hard: constraint on value DIFFERENCE (not index); segment tree indexed by value, not position. Mapping value → index is the implementation challenge
41Hard5CodeforcesCF 311E Strictly Increasing SequenceHardD&C DP optimisation (monotone opt)Hard: optimal split point opt(i) is monotone → D&C reduces O(n²) to O(n log n). Recognising the Knuth-Yao monotone-opt condition; D&C structure over the recurrence
42Hard5Custom🔧 Minimum Cost Segment Cover (CHT)HardConvex Hull Trick (CHT)Hard: dp[i] = min(dp[j] + (i-j)*a[i]) → expand to min(dp[j] − j*a[i]) + i*a[i]; CHT collapses inner loop. Lines y = −j·x + dp[j]; query at x = a[i]; O(1) amortised with monotone queries. See full statement below
⚠️ TRAP ZONE — Problems that LOOK like DP but either aren't DP at all, or where DP is the wrong canonical solution. Recognise them in < 30 seconds.
43⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 70 ⚠️ Climbing StairsMed⚠️ TRAP — Trivial Fibonacci; solve in 2 minutesIf the entire problem is dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] with no constraint, solve in 2 min; no optimisation needed — recognise trivial cases instantly
44⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 121 ⚠️ Best Time to Buy and Sell StockMed⚠️ TRAP — looks like DP, is a running min scanmin_price = min(min_price, p); profit = max(profit, p − min_price); O(1) space, no DP array. If "DP" reduces to a running min/max with O(1) state, it is a GREEDY scan — not DP
45⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 32 ⚠️ Longest Valid ParenthesesHard⚠️ TRAP — DP works but stack is canonicalDP: dp[i] = longest valid ending at i; correct but harder to derive than stack solution. When stack-based solution is equally fast and cleaner, prefer it; don't force DP
Custom Problem Statement (#42)
🔧 Minimum Cost Segment Cover — Convex Hull Trick (Stage 5): You are given an array a[1..n] of positive integers. You must partition the array into contiguous segments; the cost of a segment ending at index i (with the previous segment ending at j, so the current segment is (j, i]) is (i − j) · a[i]. Find the minimum total cost to cover the entire array (segment ending at i = n).
Constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10⁶, 1 ≤ a[i] ≤ 10⁹.
Example: a = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9], n = 6 → answer = 15 (optimal partition [1][1,4][1][5,9]: costs 3 + 2·4 + 1 + 2·9 = 3 + 8 + 1 + 18 = 30 would be a naïve partition; the minimum via CHT is 15 by a better split).
Intended solution: dp[i] = min over j < i of (dp[j] + (i − j) · a[i]). Expand: dp[i] = i · a[i] + min over j of (dp[j] − j · a[i]). Treat each j as line y = −j · x + dp[j]; query at x = a[i]. Maintain the lower convex hull of lines — O(1) amortised per query when a[i] is monotone, else O(log n) via Li Chao tree. Total: O(n) or O(n log n).
State Evolution Across Stages
Group 1: Fixed-Window / Skip-Constraint
• Stage 1 (Min Cost Climbing): dp[i] = min cost reaching step i. Recurrence: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Base: dp[0]=cost[0], dp[1]=cost[1]. Bottom-up, O(1) space with two variables.
• Stage 2 (House Robber): dp[i] = max loot robbing from houses 0..i with house i robbed. Recurrence: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Base: dp[0]=nums[0], dp[1]=max(nums[0],nums[1]). Circular → run twice; transform → bucket first.
Group 2: Knapsack
• Stage 2 (Unbounded): dp[j] = min coins / ways to reach sum j; forward iteration. dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j-c]+1) or dp[j] += dp[j-c]. Base: dp[0]=0 (min-coins) / dp[0]=1 (count). Forward iteration → reuse allowed.
• Stage 2 (0/1): Reverse iteration → each item used once. dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j-nums[i]] (iterate j from target down).
• Stage 3 (Full 2D): dp[i][j] = optimal using first i items with weight j. Needed only when reconstructing which items were chosen.
Group 3: LIS Family
• Stage 3 (LIS O(n²)): dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for j<i if arr[j]<arr[i]). Base: dp[i]=1. Answer: max(dp) — NOT dp[n-1].
• Stage 3 (Fibonacci Subsequence): dp[i][j] = length of Fib-like subseq ending at indices i, j. Look up arr[j]−arr[i] in hash map for predecessor. O(n²) states, O(1) per transition with hash map.
• Stage 3 (Arithmetic Subsequence): dp[i][d] = length of AP ending at index i with difference d. Hash map per index; same O(n²) structure.
• Stage 5 (LIS O(n log n)): tails[k] = smallest tail of IS of length k+1. Update: pos = bisect_left(tails, arr[i]); tails[pos] = arr[i]. Answer: len(tails). bisect_left for strict; bisect_right for non-strict.
Group 4: State Machine (Stock / Paint / Attendance)
• Stage 3 (2-state, unlimited transactions): held = max(held, rest − price); not_held = max(not_held, held + price). Two rolling variables.
• Stage 3 (3-state, cooldown): held = max(held, rest − price); sold = prev_held + price; rest = max(rest, prev_sold). Three rolling variables; save previous before updating.
• Stage 3 (Paint House, 3 colors): dp[i][c] = costs[i][c] + min(dp[i-1][c'] for c'≠c). Three variables per row; exclude-self transitions.
• Stage 3 (6-state, Student Attendance): States (a ∈ 1) × (l ∈ 2) = 6 variables. 'P' resets l; 'A' increments a if a<1; 'L' increments l if l<2. Write all 6 transitions explicitly — avoid index arithmetic.
• Stage 3 (k transactions, Stock III/IV): dp[i][t][0/1] = max profit, day i, t transactions, holding/not. Counter increments on SELL, not buy.
Group 5: Counting DP
• Stage 2 (Additive): dp[i] = ways to reach state i. dp[i] = sum(dp[j] for valid j). Base: dp[0]=1 (one way to reach empty/start state).
• Stage 3 (Catalan — Product): dp[i] = sum(dp[j-1] * dp[i-j] for j in 1..i). Product because two sub-problems are independent. Base: dp[0]=1.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "maximum sum / minimum cost of a contiguous subarray" → Stage 1 Kadane — reset when extending is worse
• "minimum number of coins / steps to reach target" → Stage 2 unbounded knapsack — dp[j] = min(dp[j-c]+1)
• "number of ways to [decode / tile / reach / arrange]" → Stage 2/3 counting DP — additive or product recurrence
• "longest subsequence where each element satisfies [condition vs. previous]" → Stage 3 LIS-style — sort if needed; custom order relation
• "partition / segment string or array into [valid pieces]; minimise / count" → Stage 3/4 — partition DP with inner loop over valid split points
Structural Triggers:
• Input is a 1D array and answer is a single scalar (max, min, count) → 1D DP where dp[i] anchors at index i
• Output is a subsequence (not subarray) satisfying an ordering condition → LIS-style; sort may be needed
• Problem has discrete states (holding/not, color, absence count) that transition at each step → state machine DP
Constraint Triggers:
• N ≤ 10⁵ with no obvious O(n log n) algorithm → check for 1D DP with O(n) or O(n log n) optimisation
• target ≤ 10⁴ and items up to 10⁴ → knapsack DP; target is the second dimension
Anti-Triggers (when NOT to use 1D DP):
🚩 "minimum number of swaps to sort" → NOT 1D DP → Cycle decomposition on permutation; O(n)
🚩 "find ALL subsequences satisfying [condition]" → NOT optimisation DP → Backtracking (enumeration)
🚩 "shortest path from A to B in a graph" → NOT 1D DP → BFS / Dijkstra; graph ≠ linear ordering
Decision Framework
Q1: Is the input a 1D sequence (array, string) and the answer a scalar (max, min, count, boolean)? → NO: Consider 2D DP, graph, or greedy. → YES: Continue. Q2: Does dp[i] depend on a FIXED number of previous positions only? → YES: Stage 1/2 — fixed-window. Adjacency constraint? → House Robber pattern. Circular? → Run twice. Count instead of optimise? → Sum dp[i], same recurrence. Expected: O(n) time, O(1) space. → NO: Continue. Q3: Is there a TARGET SUM / CAPACITY as a second dimension? → YES: Knapsack DP. Reuse allowed? → Forward iteration (unbounded). No reuse? → Reverse iteration (0/1). Count ways? → dp[j] += dp[j-c]; base dp[0]=1. Expected: O(n·target). → NO: Continue. Q4: Does dp[i] depend on ALL previous positions satisfying a COMPARISON or RELATION (arr[j] < arr[i], divides, etc.)? → YES: LIS-style DP. Simple comparison? → O(n²) inner scan; Stage 3. N ≤ 10⁵? → Stage 5: O(n log n) via tails + binary search. Value-range condition? → Segment tree on value domain. Two previous elements needed? → dp[i][j] pair state. Difference is explicit? → dp[i][d] hash map state. → NO: Continue. Q5: Are there DISCRETE STATES (holding, color, absent, cooldown) that transition at each step? → YES: State machine DP. How many constraints? → States = product of constraint ranges. Expected: O(n · S) time, O(S) space. → NO: Continue. Q6: Is the recurrence a PARTITION (split s[0..i] at some j)? → YES: Partition DP. Validity of segment = palindrome? → Precompute palindrome table. Validity = dictionary lookup? → Word Break / hash set. Expected: O(n²). → NO: Continue. Q7: Is dp[i] of the form min/max over j of (f(j) + g(j)*h(i))? → YES: Stage 5 — Convex Hull Trick. Queries monotone? → Deque, O(n) amortised. Queries non-monotone? → Li Chao tree, O(n log n). → NO: Is the optimal split point opt(i) monotone in i? → YES: Stage 5 — D&C DP, O(n log n). → NO: Is there a k-window max in the recurrence? → YES: Stage 5 — Monotone deque, O(n). Stage identification summary: Q2 YES → Stage 1/2. O(n). Q3 YES → Stage 2. O(n·target). Q4 YES (simple) → Stage 3. O(n²); Stage 5 → O(n log n). Q5 YES → Stage 3. O(n·S). Q6 YES → Stage 3/4. O(n²). Q7 YES → Stage 5. O(n) to O(n log n). If O(n²) TLEs: binary search (LIS), deque (window max), CHT (linear recurrence), D&C (monotone opt).
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 [Stage 1] Circular array without two-run split — Applying linear DP directly to a circular problem corrupts boundary results. Always split into two linear subproblems.
🚩 [Stage 2] 0/1 Knapsack with forward iteration — Forward iteration silently converts 0/1 to unbounded — items get reused. Reverse iteration for 0/1. No exceptions.
🚩 [Stage 3] LIS state defined as "LIS of first i elements" instead of "LIS ending at i" — The former has no clean recurrence for the strictly-increasing constraint. The anchor-at-i definition is mandatory.
🚩 [Stage 3] State machine with wrong base case for holding state — dp[0][1] = −prices[0], not 0. Holding on day 0 means you paid for the stock.
🚩 [Stage 3] Returning dp[n-1] instead of max(dp) for LIS — The LIS might end anywhere, not necessarily at the last index.
🚩 [Stage 4] Applying LIS to unsorted input for divisibility / chain problems — Divisibility is a partial order — valid predecessors may appear anywhere. Sort first so predecessors always appear before the current element.
🚩 [Stage 5] Tails array used to reconstruct the actual LIS — tails gives correct LENGTH but is NOT a valid LIS (elements may come from different subsequences). Reconstruct with a separate parent array during binary search updates.
🚩 [Stage 5] CHT deque with non-monotone queries — Deque CHT requires monotone query order. Non-monotone queries → Li Chao tree or sort queries offline.
🚩 [Stage 5] Palindrome min-cut without precomputing palindrome table — Computing isPalin(j,i) inside DP loop is O(n) per check → O(n³) total. Precompute the full O(n²) table first.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — House Robber (Strong Induction)
Claim: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]) = max loot from houses 0..i.
• Base: dp[0]=nums[0], dp[1]=max(nums[0],nums[1]). Both correct by inspection.
