• Step: Any plan for 0..i either includes house i (→ skip i-1, best is dp[i-2]+nums[i]) or excludes it (→ best is dp[i-1]). The recurrence takes the max. No other cases exist. □
Stage 3 — LIS O(n²) (Invariant Maintenance)
Claim: dp[i] = length of longest strictly increasing subsequence of arr[0..i] ending at arr[i].
• Base: dp[0]=1. Only [arr[0]] is valid. ✓
• Step: Longest IS ending at arr[i] either starts fresh (length 1) or extends some IS ending at arr[j]<arr[i], j<i. Take max over all valid j. By induction all dp[j] are correct. No valid IS ending at arr[i] is missed. □
Stage 5 — LIS O(n log n) Tails Invariant
Claim: After processing arr[0..i], tails[k] = smallest tail of all IS of length k+1. len(tails) = LIS length.
• Update: pos = bisect_left(tails, arr[i]).
• pos == len(tails): extends longest IS → append. Length increases. ✓
• Otherwise: replaces tails[pos] with smaller tail → strictly better for future extensions. ✓
• Sorted invariant: tails is always sorted (replacement preserves order by binary search). ✓
• Answer: len(tails) = longest IS length. □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 (Stage 1-2) — Off-by-one: dp array size n instead of n+1 
# BUG: dp[n] out of bounds for partition DP where answer is dp[n]
dp = [False] * n
# FIX:
dp = [False] * (n + 1)
dp[0] = True
Bug 2 (Stage 2) — House Robber: dp initialised to nums only 
# BUG: ignores that house 0 alone might be better
dp[1] = nums[1]
# FIX:
dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1])
Bug 3 (Stage 2) — 0/1 Knapsack: forward iteration causes item reuse 
# BUG: forward loop → same item used multiple times
for num in nums:
    for j in range(num, target + 1):   # forward
        dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - num]
# FIX: reverse for 0/1
    for j in range(target, num - 1, -1):  # reverse
        dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - num]
Bug 4 (Stage 2) — Circular House Robber: single DP on full array 
# BUG: first and last house can both be robbed
return rob(nums)
# FIX:
return max(rob(nums[:-1]), rob(nums[1:]))
Bug 5 (Stage 2) — Coin Change: dp initialised to infinity for dp[0] 
# BUG: dp[0]=inf → dp[coin]=inf+1 → wrong
dp = [float('inf')] * (amount + 1)
# FIX:
dp[0] = 0  # zero coins needed to make amount 0
Bug 6 (Stage 3) — LIS: <= instead of < for strict increase 
# BUG: equal elements allowed in "strictly increasing" LIS
if nums[j] <= nums[i]:
# FIX:
if nums[j] < nums[i]:
Bug 7 (Stage 3) — Stock with cooldown: using updated held to compute sold 
# BUG: sold uses new held value, not previous day's held
held = max(held, rest - price)
sold = held + price   # BUG: held already updated
# FIX: save previous held first
prev_held = held
held = max(held, rest - price)
sold = prev_held + price
Bug 8 (Stage 3) — Student Attendance: updating states in wrong order 
# BUG: later state reads already-updated earlier state from same day
p00 = p00 + p01 + p02  # p01, p02 about to be overwritten
p01 = p00              # BUG: p00 is already the NEW value
# FIX: compute ALL new values first, then assign
new_p00 = p00 + p01 + p02
new_p01 = p00          # use OLD p00
# ... assign all new_ values at end
Bug 9 (Stage 4) — Word Break: inner loop starting at j=1 misses j=0 
# BUG: misses full-prefix matches starting at index 0
for i in range(1, n + 1):
    for j in range(1, i):   # BUG: j=0 needed
# FIX:
    for j in range(0, i):
        if dp[j] and s[j:i] in word_set:
Bug 10 (Stage 5) — LIS O(n log n): bisect_right instead of bisect_left for strict LIS 
# BUG: allows equal elements → non-strict subsequence
pos = bisect.bisect_right(tails, num)
# FIX: bisect_left for STRICT; bisect_right for non-strict
pos = bisect.bisect_left(tails, num)
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
🚩 [Stage 1] Defining dp[i] as "optimal for first i elements" instead of "optimal ending at element i" — The "ending at i" definition anchors the state, making the recurrence tractable. The "first i elements" definition silently adds a hidden dimension (what was the last chosen element?). Correct model: "dp[i] = best answer for the prefix WITH arr[i] as the last chosen element; final answer = max/min over all i."
🚩 [Stage 2] Forgetting that the knapsack base case identity depends on the objective — For min-coins: dp[0]=0, dp[j]=∞. For count-ways: dp[0]=1, dp[j]=0. For boolean: dp[0]=True. Swapping these produces subtly wrong answers that pass small tests. Correct model: "dp is the identity element of the recurrence operation: 0 for min, 1 for product/count, True for OR."
🚩 [Stage 3] LIS: returning dp[n-1] instead of max(dp) — The LIS might end at any index, not the last. House Robber's answer is dp[n-1] because you must consider all houses. LIS terminates the subsequence at the best possible position. Correct model: "For 'ending-at-i' formulations, aggregate over ALL positions."
🚩 [Stage 3] State machine DP: transaction counter incremented on both buy and sell — One buy + one sell = one transaction. Increment counter exactly once per complete cycle. Correct model: "Pick one side (buy OR sell) to increment; be consistent."
🚩 [Stage 4] Applying LIS to unsorted input for divisibility/chain problems — Valid predecessors may appear anywhere in an unsorted array. Sort first so that valid predecessors always precede the current element in array order. Correct model: "LIS-style DP requires valid predecessors to always appear earlier in the array. If the problem's order relation doesn't match array order, sort first."
🚩 [Stage 5] Tails array mistaken for the actual LIS — tails gives the correct length, but the array itself is not a valid subsequence. To recover the actual sequence, maintain a separate parent/predecessor array indexed by the position where each element was placed in tails. Correct model: "Tails is a bookkeeping structure for length computation, not a valid output."
🚩 [Stage 5] CHT deque with non-monotone queries producing silent wrong answers — Deque CHT gives O(1) amortised only when queries are monotone. Non-monotone queries cause the pointer to move backward, violating the deque invariant and reading wrong hull segments. Correct model: "Verify query monotonicity before using deque CHT. If in doubt, use Li Chao tree."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
Stage 2 Follow-Ups (Constraint Mutations)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
1"What if House Robber allows skipping at most 2 consecutive houses?"dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]+nums[i], dp[i-3]+nums[i]); one extra term; O(n) time, O(1) space.
2"What if each coin denomination can be used at most twice?"Bounded knapsack: add each coin twice as separate items; run 0/1 knapsack with reverse iteration. Or binary splitting for larger bounds.
Stage 3 Follow-Ups (State Expansion)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
3"Extend LIS to also count the NUMBER of LIS, not just the length."Parallel array cnt[i]: cnt[i] = sum(cnt[j] for j where dp[j]=dp[i]-1 and arr[j]<arr[i]); O(n²).
4"Extend stock trading to unlimited transactions with a transaction fee."Add fee to sell transition: not_held = max(not_held, held + price − fee); one-line change; O(n).
Stage 4 Follow-Ups (Hidden Reductions)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
5"How would you solve palindrome min-cut if string length is 10⁵?"O(n²) DP with Manacher's O(n) palindrome precomputation; still O(n²) overall — no known sub-quadratic algorithm.
6"Word Break II: return ALL valid sentences, not just whether possible."Backtrack on the boolean DP table: for each i where dp[i]=True, recurse from all valid j; memoisation on start index.
Stage 5 Follow-Ups (Optimisation Pressure)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
7"Your O(n²) LIS TLEs for N=10⁵. How do you optimise?"Tails array + bisect_left; explain the tails invariant and why it correctly tracks LIS length; reconstruct with parent array.
8"Constrained subsequence sum with gap ≤ k is O(nk). Can you do O(n)?"Monotone deque on dp array: maintain decreasing deque of dp values in window [i-k, i-1]; O(1) amortised max.
Beyond Stage 5 (Online / Distributed)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
9"Array elements arrive in a stream. Maintain LIS length at all times."Tails array supports online updates naturally: each new element → bisect_left + replace or append → O(log n) per element.
10"Array is distributed across 100 machines. Compute LIS in parallel."Each machine computes partial tails; merge tails arrays via tournament merge preserving sorted-tails invariant; O(n/p · log n) per machine + O(p log n) merge.
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):
Stage 1 first: S.No 1 (746) → S.No 2 (53). After each: write the recurrence in one line, write the base cases, state what dp[i] represents in one sentence. Do not proceed until all three are crisp.
Stage 2 next: S.No 3 (413) → S.No 4 (198) → S.No 5 (213) → S.No 6 (740) → S.No 7 (322) → S.No 8 (518) → S.No 9 (416) → S.No 10 (139) → S.No 11 (279) → S.No 12 (91) → S.No 13 (639).
Stage 3 next: S.No 14 (96) → S.No 15 (152) → S.No 16 (2708) → S.No 17 (1186) → S.No 18 (256) → S.No 19 (790) → S.No 20 (309) → S.No 21 (552) → S.No 22 (300 O(n²)) → S.No 23 (873) → S.No 24 (1027) → S.No 25 (1143) → S.No 26 (647) → S.No 27 (5) → S.No 28 (123). For every state machine problem: draw the state diagram before coding.
Stage 4 next: S.No 29–35. For each: (a) name the disguise, (b) name the transformation, (c) name which known DP pattern it reduces to — before writing any code.
Stage 5 last: S.No 36–42. Solve the O(n²) version first, confirm correctness, then optimise. Implement tails array, monotone deque, and CHT from scratch at least once each.

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and recognition):
Stage 1: under 8 min. Stage 2: under 12 min. Stage 3: under 25 min. Stage 4: under 35 min. Recurrence drill: given problem title + difficulty, write the recurrence and base cases before reading the problem. State definition drill: verbalise dp[i] in one sentence before writing any code. If you can't, stop and define it. Space optimisation drill: after every problem, reduce to O(1) or O(n) space. If you can't, explain why. Anti-pattern drill: for S.No 43–45, identify in under 30 seconds why DP is not the right tool and what replaces it.

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources per Stage):
Stage 1/2: AtCoder ABC D/E DP problems — 3 problems from recent contests. Stage 3: Codeforces Div 2 C/D DP problems — 3 unseen problems. Stage 4: USACO Silver/Gold DP problems from 2022–2025. Stage 5: Codeforces Div 1 C/D tagged "dp + binary search" or "CHT" — 2 problems.
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 min each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LC 1187: Make Array Strictly Increasing (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: The LIS structure is disguised as a "make strictly increasing" problem. Recognising the reduction (Stage 4 — LIS-style DP where elements can be replaced from a second sorted array) AND optimising the replacement search (Stage 5 — binary search on sorted arr2) must fire simultaneously. Solving cold proves both layers are internalised.

Test 2 — CF 1603C: Extreme Extension (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: The direction reversal is a non-obvious Stage 4 transformation — each element's optimal split depends on the NEXT element, forcing right-to-left processing. The divisor enumeration is a Stage 5 optimisation — inner loop over divisors bounded by O(√a[i]). Both must be recognised cold on a Codeforces Div 2 E problem under time pressure.

Test 3 — LC 2263: Make Array Non-decreasing / Non-increasing (Stage 3 + Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: All three layers fire simultaneously: state definition on value domain (Stage 3 — weighted LIS), problem reduction (Stage 4 — reduce "minimum deletions to make non-decreasing" to weighted LIS on value-frequency pairs), and data structure optimisation (Stage 5 — BIT/segment tree for range-max on value domain → O(n log n)). Passing this cold on an unseen problem is the highest bar for 1D DP mastery.
31
2D Grid DP
Dynamic Programming Path · Reverse · Multi-Agent · DFS-Memo · Histogram · Step-Reduction
30 problems · 5 stages · path DP, geometric DP, multi-agent, 3D state expansion, DFS+memo, histogram reduction
Why It Matters: 2D Grid DP solves problems where the state is a position in a 2D structure (grid, matrix, triangle, or multi-agent path through a grid) and the optimal value at each cell depends on values at adjacent previously-computed cells. The core invariant is: dp[i][j] represents the optimal answer for the subproblem rooted at cell (i,j), reachable from a defined starting boundary, and this value is computed exactly once in a topological order consistent with the movement rules. It is one of the most tested DP categories in Google-level interviews because: (1) the state definition is immediately visual, making it approachable; (2) the hard variants (multi-agent, boundary paths, constrained movement) require precise state expansion that separates good from great candidates; and (3) the transition direction (top-down vs bottom-up, which direction is "earlier") must be chosen deliberately — a common failure point under pressure.
The Mental Model
2D Grid DP is a flooding process. You fill cells with optimal values, flowing in from the boundary. Every cell asks: "What is the best I can achieve given where I am?" It answers using only cells that have already been filled. The shape of the flood (where it starts, which directions it flows) defines the entire algorithm.

How the mental model scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1: Flood starts at one corner, flows right and down. dp[i][j] depends on dp[i-1][j] and dp[i][j-1]. One source, one destination, no obstacles.
• Stage 2: The flood hits an obstacle or a boundary constraint — some cells are blocked, some paths are invalid. Same direction, same recurrence — just add a validity check.
• Stage 3: The state expands — track something beyond position: remaining moves, number of cuts, a second agent moving simultaneously. The recurrence grows but the flooding direction is unchanged.
• Stage 4: The grid is implicit or disguised — the problem doesn't hand you a grid. You must model the state space as a grid, then flood it.
• Stage 5: The naïve flood is O(n²) per cell or has an inner scan. Replace with a data structure (sparse table, segment tree, deque) to reduce the per-cell cost.
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch
Invariant: dp[i][j] holds the optimal value of the subproblem for cell (i,j), computed after all cells that can transition INTO (i,j) have already been computed.

Proof sketch (by induction on topological order):
• DAG structure: The movement rules (e.g., only right/down) define a DAG on the grid cells. Topological order = row-major scan.
• Base case: Boundary cells (row 0 and column 0, or the starting cell) are computed directly from the problem definition.
• Inductive step: When computing dp[i][j], all cells (i-1,j) and (i,j-1) (and others depending on movement) have already been computed by the row-major scan. The recurrence dp[i][j] = f(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], grid[i][j]) is therefore valid.
• Correctness of recurrence: The recurrence is valid IFF the problem has optimal substructure: "the optimal path to (i,j) consists of the optimal path to one of (i,j)'s predecessors, plus the cost/value of (i,j) itself." Verify this once for each new problem.
• Answer extraction: The answer is at dp[n-1][m-1], or along a boundary row/column, or the max/min over all cells depending on the problem.
Sub-Variants to Master (20 total)
1. Unique path counting (no obstacles) — Stage 1 · O(nm) · combinatorics or DP table
2. Path counting with obstacles — Stage 2 · O(nm) · same recurrence with cell validity check
3. Minimum/maximum path sum (corner to corner) — Stage 1 · O(nm) · bottom-up row-major
4. Triangle path sum (top-to-bottom) — Stage 2 · O(n²) · in-place bottom-up; jagged grid
5. Minimum falling path sum (any column start) — Stage 2 · O(n²) · free boundary; scan all start columns
6. Minimum falling path sum II (non-same-column) — Stage 3/5 · O(n²) · min1/min2 trick
7. Dungeon game (minimum HP, reverse DP) — Stage 2 · O(nm) · bottom-up from destination; lower-bound state
8. Out of boundary paths (count paths leaving grid) — Stage 3 · O(nm·steps) · 3D DP with step counter
9. Maximal square of 1s — Stage 2 · O(nm) · dp[i][j] = side of largest square; min of three neighbours
10. Maximal rectangle of 1s — Stage 4 · O(nm) · row → histogram reduction + stack
11. Number of Ways of Cutting a Pizza — Stage 3 · O(n²m²·k) · 3D DP + 2D prefix sums
12. Cherry Pickup (two-agent single grid, 4D → 3D) — Stage 4 · O(n³) · step-reduction trick
13. Cherry Pickup II (two agents from top, different starts) — Stage 3 · O(n·m²) · explicit 3D state
14. Minimum path cost with transition cost matrix — Stage 3 · O(nm²) · inner scan over columns
15. Maximum amount of money robot collects (boustrophedon) — Stage 3 · O(nm) · direction flips per row
16. Longest increasing path in matrix (DFS + memo) — Stage 4 · O(nm) · data-dependent DAG
17. Number of paths with max score — Stage 3 · O(nm) · (max_score, count) pair per cell
18. Out of boundary paths with exact steps — Stage 3 · O(nm·steps) · step dimension
19. Gold Mine / paths with revisit ban — Stage 4 · O(nm·2^nm) · bitmask DP
20. DP on grid with adversarial obstacles — Stage 5 · O(nm log(nm)) · Dijkstra-style relaxation
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Unique paths (no obstacles)O(nm)O(nm) → O(m)Baseline
Minimum path sumO(nm)O(nm) → O(m)Value accumulation instead of counting
Path with obstaclesO(nm)O(m)Cell validity check; blocked = 0
Triangle pathO(n²)O(n) in-placeJagged grid; free left/right boundary
Min falling path (free start)O(n²)O(n)Free start column; scan last row for answer
Min falling path II (no same col)O(n²)O(n)min1/min2 trick; O(n) inner → O(1) per cell
Dungeon game (reverse)O(nm)O(nm)Reverse traversal from destination
Maximal squareO(nm)O(m)Geometric constraint; min of three neighbours
Out of boundary pathsO(nm·k)O(nm·k)Step counter k added as third dimension
Number of ways of cutting pizzaO(n²m²k)O(nmk)k cuts + 2D prefix sum for O(1) cut validation
Cherry Pickup IIO(n·m²)O(m²)Two-agent 3D state (row, c1, c2)
Cherry Pickup IO(n³)O(n²)Synchronised step; reduce 4D → 3D
Min path cost (transition matrix)O(nm²)O(nm)Column-transition cost; inner scan over columns
Longest increasing pathO(nm)O(nm)4-directional; DFS + memo
Maximal rectangleO(nm)O(m)Row-to-histogram reduction + stack
Gold mine (revisit ban)O(nm·2^nm)O(nm·2^nm)Bitmask for visited state; exponential
Mastery Problem Set (30 problems · 5 stages)
#LayerStageSourceProblemDiffPSub-Variant / New IdeaKey Insight
1Foundation1LeetCodeLC 62 Unique PathsMedPath counting, no obstaclesTHE baseline — dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]; entire first row and first column = 1; answer = dp[m-1][n-1]; establishes the 2D grid flood mental model
2Foundation1LeetCodeLC 64 Minimum Path SumMedMin-cost path (corner to corner)dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]); same structure as #1, only the operation changes (min+accumulate vs. count); operation-swap pattern
3Foundation2LeetCodeLC 63 Unique Paths IIMedPath counting with obstaclesIf obstacleGrid[i][j]==1 then dp[i][j]=0; first row/col must STOP propagating after first obstacle — base case changes
4Foundation2LeetCodeLC 120 TriangleMedMin path on jagged gridBottom-up in-place: triangle[i][j] += min(triangle[i+1][j], triangle[i+1][j+1]); answer = triangle[0][0]; jagged shape requires j ∈ [0, i]
5Foundation2LeetCodeLC 931 Minimum Falling Path SumMedMin path, free start columnEntire first row = grid values (all valid starts); answer = min(dp[n-1]) over all columns; answer aggregation changes from single cell to row minimum
6Foundation2LeetCodeLC 174 Dungeon GameHardMin HP path, reverse DPHard: forward DP fails because min-cost path ≠ max-survival path; state = min HP needed to survive → forces reverse traversal; max(1, ...) enforces HP ≥ 1
7Foundation2LeetCodeLC 221 Maximal SquareMedGeometric constraint (square side length)dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1 — encodes square shape; three-way minimum invariant; answer = max(dp)²
8Variants2LeetCodeLC 1289 Minimum Falling Path Sum IIHardNon-same-column constraintHard: naïve O(n³); O(n²) requires min1/min2 trick — if prev min was at same column, use min2; otherwise use min1; track min1_col
9Variants2LeetCodeLC 1594 Maximum Non-Negative Product in a MatrixMedDual min/max tracking on grid pathTrack both max_dp[i][j] and min_dp[i][j]; negative × negative = positive; same right/down movement but need both extremes per cell
10Variants2LeetCodeLC 576 Out of Boundary PathsMed3D DP (position + step counter)dp[i][j][k] = ways to leave grid from (i,j) in k steps; all 4 directions valid; out-of-bounds step contributes 1; modular arithmetic throughout
11Variants3LeetCodeLC 1444 Number of Ways of Cutting a PizzaHardPartition DP + 2D prefix sumsHard: naïve O(n²m²k) with O(n²) per cut; 2D prefix sum of apple counts reduces cut validation to O(1); the 3D state + prefix sum interaction is the non-obvious combination
12Variants3LeetCodeLC 2435 Paths in Matrix Whose Sum is Divisible by KHard3D DP (position + running sum mod k)dp[i][j][r] = paths to (i,j) with sum ≡ r (mod k); modular constraint added as DP dimension of size k; "constraint tracking as DP dimension"
13Variants3LeetCodeLC 1301 Number of Paths with Max ScoreHardTrack (max_score, path_count) pair per cellHard: dual-value state; tied updates must ADD counts, not overwrite; on new_score > old: replace; on equal: add counts; on less: ignore
14Variants3LeetCodeLC 2304 Minimum Path Cost in a GridMedPath DP with column-transition cost matrixMoving from (i,j) to (i+1,j') costs moveCost[grid[i][j]][j']; O(nm²) inner scan over all j'; transition cost indexed by cell VALUE, not position
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 2 → Stage 3 (multi-agent). Two-agent problems require thinking about JOINT state (c1, c2) simultaneously, not sequentially. Ask: "if two robots run simultaneously, what is the minimal state that captures both positions without redundancy?"
15Combo3LeetCodeLC 1463 Cherry Pickup IIHardTwo-agent DP (explicit 3D state)Hard: dp[row][c1][c2] — two agents, same row, different columns; 9 transitions per step (3×3 column moves); cherry collected once if c1==c2 needs deduplication
16Combo4LeetCodeLC 741 Cherry PickupHardTwo-agent synchronised (4D → 3D reduction)Hard: go + return reduced to two forward agents via c2 = step − c1; step = i+j is fixed for any synchronised pair → 4D to 3D; without this reduction intractable at interview size
17Combo3LeetCodeLC 2088 Count Fertile Pyramids in a LandHardPyramid-shape DP (geometric variant)Hard: dp[i][j] = height of tallest pyramid with apex at (i,j); min(dp[i+1][j-1], dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]) + 1; two passes (upward + downward pyramids)
18Combo3LeetCodeLC 2120 ⚠️ Execution of All Suffix InstructionsMed⚠️ Partial trap — boundary recognitionGrid movement exists but optimal is O(n²) per-suffix simulation, NOT grid DP; teaches boundary between simulation and DP — when state space doesn't compose, don't force DP
19Combo4LeetCodeLC 329 Longest Increasing Path in a MatrixHardDFS + memo (data-dependent DAG)Hard: row-major scan fails — DAG direction is data-dependent (strictly increasing); DFS discovers topological order implicitly; memo converts exponential DFS to O(nm)
20Combo4LeetCodeLC 363 Max Sum of Rectangle No Larger Than KHard2D DP + 1D prefix sum + sorted setHard: fix top/bottom rows → reduce to 1D "max subarray sum ≤ k"; sorted set (TreeSet/SortedList) for O(log n) query; 2D → 1D reduction is non-obvious
21Combo4LeetCodeLC 85 Maximal RectangleHardGrid → histogram → stack DPHard: each row → height histogram; largest rectangle in histogram (monotone stack) per row; two-level reduction (grid → histogram → stack DP); stack algo itself non-trivial
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 4 → Stage 5. Next problems require data structures embedded inside the DP transition. Identify "this transition is a range max/min/sum over a dimension" before implementing; then choose the right structure.
22Hard5LeetCodeLC 1289 Min Falling Path II (Stage 5 revisit)Hardmin1/min2 optimisationThe min1/min2 trick collapses O(n) per row scan → O(1); maintain two smallest values + their column indices; canonical Stage 5 example for 2D grid DP
23Hard4LeetCodeLC 956 Tallest BillboardHardGrid DP on difference state (disguised)Hard: state = dp[diff] = max common height with rod difference = diff; grid of differences, not a literal grid; recognising difference as the 2D axis IS the reduction
24Hard5CodeforcesCF 559C Gerald's HexagonHardGrid DP on irregular polygon areaHard: decompose polygon into grid-aligned strips; area DP with boundary transitions requiring coordinate compression; polygon is implicit — compression creates the effective grid
25Hard5USACOUSACO Gold — Painting the BarnHard2D difference array + path DPHard: 2D difference array accumulates rectangle updates in O(1); then run path DP on the resulting grid; rectangle-update input requires diff array preprocessing
26Hard5LeetCodeLC 2977 Minimum Cost to Convert String IHardGrid DP + all-pairs shortest pathHard: build cost matrix via Floyd-Warshall on transformation graph; then 1D DP over string positions; grid DP is trivial once APSP preprocessing is done — recognising the graph IS the step
27Hard4Custom🔧 Maximum Cherries with Exactly K RobotsHardMulti-agent generalised to k agentsHard: generalise Cherry Pickup II to k agents; state = (row, c₁..cₖ); O(n·mᴷ) naïve; D&C on agent axis + sorted column tuple cuts by K! — see full statement below
⚠️ TRAP ZONE — Problems that LOOK like 2D Grid DP but require different techniques. Recognise them in < 30 seconds.
28⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 542 ⚠️ 01 MatrixMed⚠️ TRAP — looks like grid DP, is multi-source BFSDistance to nearest 0 for each cell; DP works (two-pass) but multi-source BFS is O(nm) with simpler correctness argument. "Distance to nearest feature" → prefer multi-source BFS
29⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 695 ⚠️ Max Area of IslandMed⚠️ TRAP — irregular region, not DPIslands are irregular connected regions; maximal square only works for rectangles. "Connected region" shape → DFS/BFS flood fill, not grid DP
30⚠️Trap—LeetCodeLC 1091 ⚠️ Shortest Path in Binary MatrixMed⚠️ TRAP — 8-directional, no topological order8-directional movement means no topological order exists; DP would require iterating to convergence = BFS. Movement allowing UP/LEFT → BFS (unweighted) or Dijkstra (weighted)
Custom Problem Statement (#27)
🔧 Maximum Cherries with Exactly K Robots — Multi-Agent Generalisation (Stage 4): You are given an n×m grid where each cell contains a non-negative integer of cherries. K robots start at different columns of row 0 (you choose which columns). All robots move simultaneously, one row per step, and each robot moves to an adjacent column (left, same, or right) at each step. A cherry at cell (i,j) is collected only ONCE regardless of how many robots visit it. Find the maximum cherries collectible when all K robots reach the last row.
Constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 50, 1 ≤ m ≤ 50, 1 ≤ K ≤ min(m, 5), 0 ≤ grid[i][j] ≤ 100.
Example: n=3, m=3, K=2, grid=[[3,1,1],[2,5,1],[1,5,5]] → Answer: 24.
Intended solution: Bottom-up DP with state dp[row][c₁][c₂]...[cₖ]; cherry deduplication when any two columns are equal. For K=2 this is Cherry Pickup II. For K>2, state space is O(n·mᴷ). Maintain c₁ ≤ c₂ ≤ ... ≤ cₖ (column order doesn't matter) to cut state space by K! Divide-and-conquer on the agent axis (process pairs of agents, merge their optimal column assignments) reduces to O(n·m²·K).
State Evolution Across Stages
Group 1: Single-Agent Path DP
• Stage 1 — Min Path Sum: dp[i][j] = minimum cost to reach (i,j) from (0,0). dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Base: dp[0][0]=grid[0][0]; first row/col are prefix sums. Bottom-up row-major; O(m) space with rolling row.
• Stage 2 — Dungeon Game (Reverse): dp[i][j] = minimum HP needed AT cell (i,j) to reach exit alive. dp[i][j] = max(1, min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j+1]) − dungeon[i][j]). Base: dp[n-1][m-1] = max(1, 1 − dungeon[n-1][m-1]). Bottom-up from bottom-right — forward DP cannot express lower-bound constraint.
• Stage 3 — Out of Boundary Paths (3D): dp[i][j][k] = number of ways to leave grid from (i,j) in exactly k steps. dp[i][j][k] = sum over 4 directions of dp[ni][nj][k-1] (out-of-bounds counts as 1). Fill layer by layer; O(m) space per step.
Group 2: Geometric Constraint DP
• Stage 1 — Unique Paths: dp[i][j] = number of distinct paths from (0,0) to (i,j) moving right/down. dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Base: dp[0][j]=1, dp[i][0]=1. Equivalent to Pascal's triangle.
• Stage 2 — Maximal Square: dp[i][j] = side length of largest square of 1s with bottom-right at (i,j). dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1 if grid[i][j]=='1' else 0. Answer = max(dp)².
• Stage 4 — Maximal Rectangle: For each row i, compute heights[j] = number of consecutive 1s in column j ending at row i. Then largest rectangle in histogram (monotone stack) → O(m) per row. Total: O(nm), O(m) space. The 2D → 1D reduction IS the Stage 4 transformation.
Group 3: Multi-Agent DP
• Stage 3 — Cherry Pickup II: dp[row][c1][c2] = max cherries with two robots ending at columns c1, c2 in first row+1 rows. Transitions over (dc1, dc2) ∈ 1²; cherries(row, c1, c2) = grid[row][c1] + (grid[row][c2] if c2≠c1 else 0). By symmetry c1 ≤ c2 cuts state space in half.
• Stage 4 — Cherry Pickup I (step reduction): Both agents travel (0,0)→(n-1,n-1). On step t, r1+c1 = r2+c2 = t → r = t − c. State reduces from dp[r1][c1][r2][c2] to dp[t][c1][c2]. Transitions over (dc1, dc2) ∈ 1². Base: dp[0][0][0] = grid[0][0]. Answer: max(dp[2(n-1)][n-1][n-1], 0).
Group 4: Falling Path DP (Optimised)
• Stage 2 — Minimum Falling Path Sum II: dp[i][j] = min falling path sum ending at (i,j) with no consecutive same-column. Naïve: dp[i][j] = matrix[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j'] for j'≠j) — O(n³).
• Stage 5 optimisation: Track min1 (global row min), min2 (second row min), min1_col: dp[i][j] = matrix[i][j] + (min1 if j≠min1_col else min2). O(n²) time, O(n) space.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "minimum/maximum cost path from top-left to bottom-right" → Stage 1/2 grid DP — dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min/max(up, left)
• "number of distinct paths / ways to reach" → Stage 1 counting DP — dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]
• "maximum square / rectangle of 1s" → Stage 2 geometric DP — dp[i][j] = side length or histogram height
• "two robots / players move simultaneously through the same grid" → Stage 3/4 multi-agent — joint state (c1, c2) per row; deduplication when positions coincide
• "minimum health / resource required to survive the path" → Stage 2 reverse DP — traverse from exit to start; state = lower bound to survive
Structural Triggers:
• Input is n×m grid, movement only right/down → row-major bottom-up; O(m) rolling space
• n×m grid + 4-directional movement + monotone condition (strictly inc/dec) → DFS + memoisation (data-dependent topological order)
• Two (r1,c1) AND (r2,c2) both move through the same grid → check if step = r+c is conserved; if so, reduce 4D to 3D
Constraint Triggers:
• 1 ≤ n, m ≤ 200 with O(nm) or O(nm²) budget → standard grid DP, optional rolling space
• 1 ≤ n, m ≤ 50 with third dimension k ≤ 50 → 3D DP acceptable; O(nm·k) ≤ 125,000
Anti-Triggers (when NOT to use 2D Grid DP):
🚩 "distance to nearest 0/obstacle for each cell" → NOT grid DP → multi-source BFS; DP two-pass works but BFS is simpler and more general
🚩 "minimum path with 8-directional movement or can revisit" → NOT standard grid DP → BFS (unweighted) or Dijkstra (weighted); DP breaks when topological order doesn't exist
🚩 "largest connected region of 1s" → NOT geometric grid DP → DFS/BFS flood fill; irregular shape can't be expressed as square/rectangle invariant
Decision Framework
Q1: Is the input a 2D grid (matrix) or triangle/jagged array, and is the answer a scalar (count, min, max)? → NO: Not 2D grid DP. Consider 1D DP, graph, or greedy. → YES: Continue. Q2: What is the movement rule? → RIGHT/DOWN only (or DOWN only, fixed direction): Row-major bottom-up DP. O(nm) time, O(m) space. Go to Q3. → 4-directional with a MONOTONE condition (strict inc/dec): DFS + memoisation (topological order data-dependent). Stage 4. O(nm). → 4-directional with NO monotone condition (arbitrary): → Unweighted: BFS. → Weighted: Dijkstra. NOT grid DP. Q3: Is the answer a path VALUE (sum, product, min, max along path)? → YES: Value-accumulation DP. Negative values? → Track both max_dp and min_dp (dual-tracking). Lower-bound constraint (survive)? → Reverse DP from exit. Go to Q4. → NO (counting paths): Counting DP. dp[i][j] = sum of valid predecessors. Go to Q4. Q4: Are there additional constraints beyond position? → Step budget / remaining moves: Step counter as third dim. O(nm·steps). → Running sum mod k: Sum mod k as third dim. O(nm·k). → Cut count / partition: Cut counter as third dim + 2D prefix sum. O(nm·k) to O(n²m²k). → NO: Stage 1/2. Expected O(nm). Q5: Are there TWO or MORE AGENTS moving simultaneously? → YES: Multi-agent DP. Same grid, both go corner-to-corner? → Check if step = i+j is conserved → reduce 4D to 3D. Stage 4. Different starts/ends? → Explicit 3D state (row, c1, c2). Stage 3. O(n·m²). → NO: Continue. Q6: Is the per-cell transition an inner SCAN over a row/column? → YES: Stage 5 — look for optimisation. Non-same-column exclusion? → min1/min2 trick. O(1) per cell. Range max/min query? → Sparse table or segment tree. Max subarray sum ≤ K? → Sorted set + binary search. → NO: Solution is complete at current stage. Stage identification summary: Q3 YES, Q4 NO → Stage 1/2. O(nm). Q4 YES (extra dimension) → Stage 3. O(nm·k). Q5 YES (multi-agent) → Stage 3 (explicit) or Stage 4 (reduction). Q6 YES (inner scan) → Stage 5. O(nm) to O(nm log n). If O(nm²) or worse and n,m > 200: look for Stage 5 optimisation.
When This Technique BREAKS
🚩 [Stage 1] Movement allows going UP or LEFT — If movement is not strictly in the direction of increasing (i, j), there is no topological order for a simple row-major scan. The recurrence would form cycles. Use BFS (unweighted) or Dijkstra (weighted) instead.
🚩 [Stage 2] Reverse DP forgotten for lower-bound problems (Dungeon Game) — Forward DP accumulates the path value achieved — it cannot express "minimum resource needed to survive." Problems asking for a lower bound on a resource (HP, fuel) always require reverse traversal from the destination.
🚩 [Stage 2] Blocked cells in first row/column not stopping propagation — For Unique Paths II, once a blocked cell appears in row 0 or column 0, all cells AFTER it must also be 0. Forgetting to stop propagation gives wrong answers for the first row/column.
🚩 [Stage 3] Two-agent DP: double-counting cherries when c1==c2 — When both agents are at the same column, counting grid[row][c1] + grid[row][c2] gives twice the cherries. Always check: cherries = grid[row][c1] + (grid[row][c2] if c2 != c1 else 0).
🚩 [Stage 4] Cherry Pickup I: not enforcing valid cell bounds in 3D state — Since r = step − c, when step − c < 0 or step − c ≥ n, the cell is out of bounds. Failing to check 0 ≤ r < n for both agents gives wrong transitions.
🚩 [Stage 4] Applying row-major DP to Longest Increasing Path — The DAG direction is data-dependent (increasing values). Row-major scan processes cells in the wrong order — a cell might be computed before all its predecessors. Use DFS + memoisation which discovers the correct topological order implicitly.
🚩 [Stage 5] min1/min2 trick: forgetting to track the COLUMN of min1 — When the current column equals min1_col, you must use min2. If you only track the VALUE of min1 (not its column), you can't determine when to fall back to min2.
🚩 [Stage 5] Maximal Rectangle: applying histogram stack to the wrong axis — The histogram DP must run on columns (heights of consecutive 1s in each column per row). Running it on rows gives wrong heights. Recompute heights row by row.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — Minimum Path Sum (Induction on i+j)
Claim: dp[i][j] = minimum sum path from (0,0) to (i,j).
• Base: dp[0][0] = grid[0][0]. Trivially the only path to itself. ✓
• Inductive step: Assume dp[i'][j'] correct for all i'+j' < i+j. Any path to (i,j) passes through exactly one of (i-1,j) or (i,j-1) as its last step. Minimum-sum path to (i,j) = grid[i][j] + minimum of the two optimal predecessors. Both predecessors have i'+j' < i+j, so they are correct by hypothesis.
• No other cases: Movement is strictly right/down; no other predecessors exist. □
Stage 2 — Maximal Square (Invariant on dp[i][j])
Claim: dp[i][j] = side length of largest all-1s square with bottom-right at (i,j).
• Invariant: A square of side s with bottom-right at (i,j) requires all three sub-squares of side s-1 ending at (i-1,j), (i,j-1), (i-1,j-1). The side is limited by the minimum of all three.
• Forward: If dp[i][j] = k, a k×k all-1s square exists (constructible from the three sub-squares). ✓
• Reverse: If a k×k all-1s square ends at (i,j), all three neighbours have dp ≥ k-1. ✓
• Combined: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1 when grid[i][j]='1'. □
Stage 4 — Cherry Pickup I (Step-Reduction Correctness)
Claim: The 4D state dp[r1][c1][r2][c2] (where r1+c1 = r2+c2 = t) reduces correctly to 3D dp[t][c1][c2].
• Reduction validity: On step t, any valid forward path has r+c=t for each agent. Given c1 and c2, r1 and r2 are uniquely determined as r1 = t-c1, r2 = t-c2. No information lost.
• Transition validity: Moving right increments c; moving down keeps r+c=t+1. Transitions (dc1, dc2) ∈ 1² correctly enumerate all valid joint moves.
• Deduplication: Cherry at (r,c) is collected by both agents only if r1=r2 AND c1=c2, i.e., c1=c2. Correct: add grid[r1][c1] + (grid[r2][c2] if c1≠c2 else 0). □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 (Stage 2) — First row/column base case: not stopping after obstacle (Unique Paths II) 
# BUG: sets all first-row cells to 1, ignoring obstacle
for j in range(m):
    dp[0][j] = 1   # wrong if obstacle exists in first row
# FIX: stop propagating after first obstacle
for j in range(m):
    if obstacleGrid[0][j] == 1:
        break
    dp[0][j] = 1
Bug 2 (Stage 2) — Dungeon game: forgetting max(1, ...) at destination 
# BUG: knight can have 0 or negative HP at exit
dp[n-1][m-1] = 1 - dungeon[n-1][m-1]   # can be ≤ 0
# FIX:
dp[n-1][m-1] = max(1, 1 - dungeon[n-1][m-1])
Bug 3 (Stage 2) — Triangle bottom-up: using wrong column indices 
# BUG: iterates j to full row length, accessing out of bounds
for i in range(n-2, -1, -1):
    for j in range(n):   # BUG: should be range(i+1)
        triangle[i][j] += min(triangle[i+1][j], triangle[i+1][j+1])
# FIX:
    for j in range(i+1):   # row i has i+1 elements
Bug 4 (Stage 2) — Maximal square: not checking grid[i][j]=='1' before applying recurrence 
# BUG: applies recurrence to 0-cells, inflating dp
dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1
# FIX:
if grid[i][j] == '1':
    dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1
else:
    dp[i][j] = 0
Bug 5 (Stage 5) — Min falling path II: not saving min1_col 
# BUG: always uses min1, even when j equals min1_col
dp[i][j] = matrix[i][j] + min1_prev   # wrong when j == min1_col
# FIX:
dp[i][j] = matrix[i][j] + (min1_prev if j != min1_col else min2_prev)
Bug 6 (Stage 3) — Cherry Pickup II: double-counting cherries when c1==c2 
# BUG: counts grid[row][c1] + grid[row][c2] even when c1==c2
val = grid[row][c1] + grid[row][c2]
# FIX:
val = grid[row][c1] + (grid[row][c2] if c1 != c2 else 0)
Bug 7 (Stage 3) — Out of Boundary Paths: forgetting modulo on intermediate values 
# BUG: dp values overflow without modulo
dp[i][j][k] += dp[ni][nj][k-1]
# FIX:
dp[i][j][k] = (dp[i][j][k] + dp[ni][nj][k-1]) % MOD
Bug 8 (Stage 3) — 3D DP rolling array: overwriting current layer 
# BUG: in-place update of dp[k] overwrites values needed for same layer
for i in range(n):
    for j in range(m):
        dp[i][j][k] = sum(dp[ni][nj][k-1] for ...)   # using dp[k] before done
# FIX: allocate a new 2D layer, fill from dp[k-1] only
new_layer = [[0]*m for _ in range(n)]
# populate new_layer from dp_prev[...]
dp_prev = new_layer
Bug 9 (Stage 4) — Cherry Pickup I: not bounding r1, r2 in [0, n-1] 
# BUG: accesses grid[-1][c] when step < c (r = step - c < 0)
val = grid[step-c1][c1]   # step-c1 can be negative
# FIX:
r1, r2 = step - c1, step - c2
if not (0 <= r1 < n and 0 <= r2 < n):
    continue   # skip invalid states
Bug 10 (Stage 4) — Longest Increasing Path: only checking right and down 
# BUG: only 2 directions, misses left and up
dirs = [(0,1),(1,0)]   # misses left and up
# FIX: all 4 directions + strictly increasing condition
dirs = [(0,1),(1,0),(0,-1),(-1,0)]
if 0 <= ni < n and 0 <= nj < m and matrix[ni][nj] > matrix[i][j]:
    dfs(ni, nj)
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
🚩 [Stage 1] Using the same base case formula for all grid DPs — The base case is NOT always "first row and first column = 1." For min-path-sum it's the prefix sum of costs. For Dungeon Game it's a reverse computation from the exit. For triangle it's the bottom row values. Correct model: "The base case encodes the problem's boundary condition: what is the answer when there are no choices left?"
🚩 [Stage 2] Applying forward DP to problems with a survival/resource lower bound — Forward DP accumulates "how much I've collected" — it cannot express "minimum I need to start with." Dungeon Game requires the state to be a lower bound, which only makes sense computed backwards. Wrong model: "dp[i][j] = max HP arriving at (i,j)." Correct model: "dp[i][j] = min HP needed at (i,j) to survive to exit."
🚩 [Stage 3] Multi-agent DP: processing agents sequentially instead of simultaneously — Computing the first agent's optimal path, then the second agent's "greedy best given what's left" is provably suboptimal. Agents must be modelled with a JOINT state because their collections interact. Wrong model: "Run two separate DPs." Correct model: "State = (row, c1, c2); both agents advance one row per step; deduplication at c1==c2."
🚩 [Stage 4] Applying row-major DP to Longest Increasing Path — Row-major DP processes cells in row 0, then row 1. For LIP, cell (0,3) might depend on (1,3) if matrix[1][3] < matrix[0][3]. The DAG direction is data-dependent. Wrong model: "Use bottom-up DP like other grid problems." Correct model: "When topological order is data-dependent, use DFS + memoisation; DFS naturally follows edges in the correct order."
🚩 [Stage 3] Number of Paths with Max Score: overwriting count on score tie — When dp[i][j].score == candidate_score, the correct action is to ADD the incoming count to the existing count, not overwrite it. Correct model: "When updating a (score, count) pair: new_score > old → replace; equal → add counts; less → ignore."
🚩 [Stage 5] Maximal Rectangle: not resetting heights on 0-cell — If grid[i][j]='0', the height must reset to 0 — the consecutive-1s streak is broken. Correct model: heights[j] = heights[j] + 1 if grid[i][j]=='1' else 0.
🚩 [Stage 2/5] Space optimisation breaking the recurrence (rolling row overwriting needed values) — Rolling a 2D DP into O(m) space requires that dp[i][j] only needs the PREVIOUS row and already-computed current row values. Correct model: "Before optimising space, list which previous cells the recurrence reads; ensure none are overwritten before they are read."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
Stage 2 Follow-Ups (Constraint Mutations)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
1"In Minimum Path Sum, what if you can also move UP in addition to right/down?"Topological order breaks; use BFS or Dijkstra on the grid as a weighted graph; DP no longer directly applicable
2"What if the grid has negative weights in Minimum Path Sum? Does your solution still work?"Yes — the recurrence handles negative values correctly; key is that movement is still strictly right/down, so topological order is preserved
Stage 3 Follow-Ups (State Expansion)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
3"Extend Unique Paths to count paths where the total sum of cells visited equals exactly S."Add sum S as third dimension: dp[i][j][s] = number of paths to (i,j) with cell-sum exactly s; O(nm·S); feasible only for small S
4"In Cherry Pickup II, what if three robots start from row 0 instead of two?"State = dp[row][c1][c2][c3]; O(n·m³) — feasible for m≤20; cherry deduplication: add grid value once for each unique column among {c1,c2,c3}
Stage 4 Follow-Ups (Hidden Reductions)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
5"How would you solve Maximal Square if the constraint is rectangles instead of squares?"Reduce to Maximal Rectangle (LC 85): for each row, compute column heights; apply largest rectangle in histogram (monotone stack); O(nm)
6"In Longest Increasing Path, what if you need the actual path, not just the length?"During DFS+memo, also store the next cell in the optimal direction; during answer extraction, follow parent pointers; O(nm) space for parent array
Stage 5 Follow-Ups (Optimisation Pressure)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
7"Minimum Falling Path Sum II TLEs for n=1000. How do you speed it up from O(n³) to O(n²)?"Track min1, min2, min1_col across the previous row; each cell in the new row uses min1 unless column equals min1_col, then uses min2; O(1) per cell
8"What if in Minimum Path Sum you must avoid all cells with value > K?"Validity check: if grid[i][j] > K, set dp[i][j] = ∞; propagation avoids infeasible cells; if dp[n-1][m-1] = ∞, no valid path exists
Beyond Stage 5 (Online / Distributed)
#QuestionIntended Answer Direction
9"The grid is too large to store in memory (n=10⁶). How do you compute min path sum row-by-row?"Only the previous row's dp values are needed; stream the grid row by row, keeping a single O(m) array; update in-place left to right; O(m) memory
10"Grid values change dynamically (online updates). How do you maintain min path sum efficiently?"2D segment tree or 2D BIT for point updates + range queries; a single cell update affects O(nm) downstream cells in the worst case — motivates caching the DP and only recomputing the affected subgraph
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):
Stage 1 first: S.No 1 (62) → S.No 2 (64). After each: write dp[i][j] in one sentence, write the recurrence, write the base cases, identify the traversal direction. Do not proceed until all four are crisp.
Stage 2 next: S.No 3 (63) → S.No 4 (120) → S.No 5 (931) → S.No 6 (174) → S.No 7 (221) → S.No 8 (1289) → S.No 9 (1594). For each: draw the state transition as arrows on a 3×3 grid before coding.
Stage 3 next: S.No 10 (576) → S.No 11 (1444) → S.No 12 (2435) → S.No 13 (1301) → S.No 14 (2304) → S.No 15 (1463) → S.No 17 (2088). For each: identify the new dimension added and justify why it's necessary. For every multi-agent problem: draw the state diagram with arrows before coding.
Stage 4 next: S.No 16 (741) → S.No 19 (329) → S.No 20 (363) → S.No 21 (85). For each: (a) name the disguise, (b) write the transformation, (c) name which known DP pattern it becomes.
Stage 5 last: S.No 22 (1289 revisited) → S.No 23 (956) → S.No 24–26. Implement min1/min2 optimisation and monotone stack from scratch.

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and recognition):
Stage 1: under 8 min. Stage 2: under 15 min. Stage 3: under 25 min. Stage 4: under 35 min.
State definition drill: For every problem, name dp[i][j][...] in one sentence before writing code. If you can't, stop.
Direction drill: For every problem, identify traversal direction (top-down, bottom-up, reverse, DFS) and justify it before coding.
Space optimisation drill: After every problem, reduce to minimum space — know whether you need O(1), O(m), O(m²), or O(nm).

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources per Stage):
Stage 1/2: AtCoder ABC/ARC Grid DP problems — 3 unseen from recent contests. Stage 3: Codeforces Div 2 D/E grid DP — 2 unseen problems. Stage 4: USACO Silver/Gold grid DP from 2022–2025. Stage 5: Codeforces Div 1 C/D tagged "dp + data structures" on grid problems.
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 min each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LC 2267: Check if There Is a Valid Parentheses String Path (Stage 3 + Stage 4)
Why valid: The "valid parentheses" constraint is disguised as a grid path problem. Recognising that running balance is the right third dimension (Stage 4 reduction — balance is a state) and implementing the 3D DP correctly (dp[i][j][bal] = reachable) must both fire. Solving cold proves the candidate can expand grid DP state to encode string constraints.

Test 2 — LC 741: Cherry Pickup (if not already solved) OR CF 319C – Kalila and Dimna in the Logging Industry (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: LC 741 — the 4D→3D step-invariant reduction (Stage 4, non-obvious) must be found cold. CF 319C — adds a CHT-optimisable transition over agent columns to the step-reduced state (Stage 5). Either problem requires both layers activating simultaneously.

Test 3 — USACO Gold 2020 February – Sprinklers II (Stage 3 + Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Why valid: All three upper layers must fire: model the non-obvious multi-constraint state (Stage 3), recognise the implicit grid structure from watering constraints (Stage 4), and apply the monotone deque to the predecessor range (Stage 5). A cold solve within 45 minutes certifies complete 2D Grid DP mastery.
32
The Knapsack Family
Dynamic Programming 0/1 · Unbounded · Bounded · 2D · Grouped · Tree · Bitset · Meet-in-Middle
31 problems · 5 stages · subset sum, count ways, multi-dimensional capacity, disguised reductions, bitset optimisation
Why It Matters: The Knapsack Family solves problems where you must select a subset of items to optimise a value subject to one or more capacity constraints, where each item can be taken 0 times (0/1), unlimited times (unbounded), or a bounded number of times. The core invariant is: dp[j] = the optimal value achievable using exactly (or at most) capacity j, computed by iterating items one by one and deciding whether to include each. It appears in nearly every top-tier interview because the surface variants (boolean, count, min, max) look different but share identical recurrence structure — the only thing that changes is the operation (OR, +, min, max) and the iteration direction (forward for unbounded reuse, reverse for 0/1). At ICPC level, the hard variants involve multi-dimensional capacity, grouped items, and disguised reductions where the "items" and "capacity" are not obvious from the problem statement.
The Mental Model
The Knapsack is a budget problem. You have a budget (capacity). Each item has a price (weight) and a benefit (value). You fill the budget optimally, one item at a time, by asking: "For every budget level j, does including this item improve the answer?" The answer at each budget level is computed from previously computed budget levels.

How this scales through the 5 stages:
• Stage 1: One item type, one capacity. dp[j] = can we hit exactly sum j? Reverse iterate to prevent reuse (0/1).
• Stage 2: Change the operation — count ways instead of boolean. Or change the item limit — unbounded (forward iterate). Same 1D array, one rule flips.
• Stage 3: Add a second capacity dimension. dp[j][k] — two independent budgets must both be satisfied simultaneously. State grows but invariant is identical.
• Stage 4: The problem doesn't hand you items and a capacity. You must derive what the items are, what the weights are, and what the capacity is. Recognition IS the hard part.
• Stage 5: The capacity is too large for a table. Replace with a sparse representation (bitset, hash map) or reduce via mathematical insight (meet-in-the-middle, binary grouping of bounded items).
Core Invariant & Proof Sketch
Invariant: After processing item i, dp[j] = optimal value achievable using items from {1..i} with capacity exactly (or at most) j.

Proof sketch (by induction on items processed):
• Base case: Before any item is processed, dp[0] = 0 (or True/1 for counting) and dp[j] = ∞/False/0 for j>0 (infeasible). Correct vacuously.
• Inductive hypothesis: After processing items {1..i-1}, dp[j] is correct for all j.
• Inductive step for 0/1: For item i with weight w and value v, iterating j from W down to w: dp[j] = op(dp[j], dp[j-w] + v). Since we iterate high-to-low, dp[j-w] still reflects items {1..i-1} (not yet updated for item i) — so item i is counted at most once. ✓
• Inductive step for unbounded: Forward iteration: dp[j-w] may already include item i, allowing unlimited reuse. ✓
• Correctness of operation: The recurrence is valid IFF "the optimal solution for budget j either includes item i (using dp[j-w] from a sub-budget) or doesn't (using the old dp[j])." This is optimal substructure — verify once per problem.
Sub-Variants to Master
VariantStageTimeSpaceNew Idea
0/1 Knapsack — boolean (subset sum)1O(nW)O(W)dp[j] |= dp[j-w[i]]; reverse iteration is the ONLY mechanism preventing reuse
0/1 Knapsack — count ways2O(nW)O(W)Additive recurrence replaces boolean OR; dp[0]=1
0/1 Knapsack — max value2O(nW)O(W)Value array added; recurrence maximises instead of counting
Unbounded Knapsack — min cost2O(nW)O(W)Forward iteration enables unlimited item reuse; base case dp[0]=0, rest =∞
Unbounded Knapsack — count ways2O(nW)O(W)Same forward iterate but additive; counts ordered vs unordered depends on loop nesting
Ordered vs unordered count2O(nW)O(W)Putting capacity loop OUTER, items INNER counts ordered compositions (permutations); items OUTER counts unordered combinations
Bounded Knapsack (each item i usable at most k[i] times)3O(nWK) naïve → O(nW log K) binary grouping → O(nW) dequeO(W)Per-item count limit; binary grouping converts to 0/1 knapsack in O(log K) items per type
2D Knapsack (two capacity dimensions)3O(n·W₁·W₂)O(W₁·W₂)Second independent capacity constraint; state is dp[j][k]
Grouped Knapsack (exactly one item from each group)3O(n·W·G)O(W)Items are partitioned into groups; exactly one from each group must be chosen
Multiple-choice Knapsack3O(n·W)O(W)From each group, choose at most one item (not exactly one)
Knapsack with item dependency (tree knapsack)4O(n²W)O(nW)Item j can only be selected if parent item is also selected; tree structure on items
Subset-sum with balanced partition4O(nW)O(W)"Partition into two equal halves" → find subset summing to total/2; reduction IS the insight
Bitset-optimised knapsack5O(nW/64)O(W/64)Boolean knapsack as bitset; each bit shift = adding one item weight; 64× speedup
Meet-in-the-middle knapsack5O(2^(n/2) log n)O(2^(n/2))When n is small (≤40) but W is huge, enumerate all subset sums of half the items; binary search for complement
Knapsack on difference / "closest to target"4O(nW)O(W)Instead of exact target, find the achievable sum closest to W; scan dp array after filling
Knapsack with profit threshold (Profitable Schemes)3O(n·members·profit)O(members·profit)Second value dimension (profit) capped/bucketed at threshold; overflow treated as exact target
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Subset sum (0/1 boolean)O(nW)O(W)Baseline: reverse iterate, OR
Count ways (0/1)O(nW)O(W)OR → add; dp[0]=1
Max value (0/1)O(nW)O(W)Add value array; max instead of add
Unbounded (min cost)O(nW)O(W)Forward iteration; unlimited reuse
Unbounded count (ordered)O(nW)O(W)Loop nesting swap → counts permutations
2D KnapsackO(n·W₁·W₂)O(W₁·W₂)Second capacity dimension
Grouped KnapsackO(n·W·G)O(W)Group constraint; inner loop over group
Bounded Knapsack (naïve)O(nWK)O(W)Per-item count limit; K copies
Bounded Knapsack (binary split)O(nW log K)O(W)Binary grouping converts to 0/1
Bounded Knapsack (deque)O(nW)O(W)Monotone deque; optimal
Tree KnapsackO(n²W)O(nW)Tree dependency; DFS + merge
Bitset-optimisedO(nW/64)O(W/64)64× via bitset shift+OR
Meet-in-the-middleO(2^(n/2) log n)O(2^(n/2))Exponential n, huge W
Mastery Problem Set
S.NoLayerStageSource# / NameDifficultyPremium?Sub-VariantNew Idea AddedKey Insight
111LeetCode416 – Partition Equal Subset SumMedium0/1 Subset sum (boolean)THE baseline — dp[j] |= dp[j-num]; reverse iterate; target = total/2Base: dp[0]=True; target = sum/2; if sum is odd → impossible immediately; answer = dp[target]
211LeetCode322 – Coin ChangeMediumUnbounded knapsack (min coins)Change to forward iterate + min operation: dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j-c]+1)Forward iteration allows unlimited coin reuse; base: dp[0]=0, rest =∞; establishes unbounded pattern
312LeetCode518 – Coin Change IIMediumUnbounded count (unordered combinations)Change min to +=; items outer, capacity inner counts unordered combinationsdp[j] += dp[j-c]; loop order is critical: coins outer → each coin used in sorted order → no permutation duplicates
412LeetCode279 – Perfect SquaresMediumUnbounded min on implicit coin setGenerate coin set first (all squares ≤ n); then identical to S.No 2Coins are not given — compute {1,4,9,...,k²} before DP; remainder of algorithm unchanged
512LeetCode377 – Combination Sum IVMediumUnbounded count (ordered permutations)Swap loop order: capacity outer, items inner → counts ordered sequences (permutations)dp[j] += dp[j-num] with capacity outer; one loop-order flip converts combinations to permutations
612LeetCode2266 – Count Number of TextsMediumUnbounded knapsack with bounded-window recurrencePhone keypad: digit d maps to 3 or 4 letters; dp[i] += dp[i-k] for k ∈ {1,2,3} or {1,2,3,4}Unbounded but with a FIXED small window; recurrence sums over last 3 or 4 positions; teaches window-bounded unbounded DP
722LeetCode494 – Target SumMedium0/1 count ways with sign assignmentTransform: assign +/- to each num; reduce to subset sum count where S⁺ - S⁻ = target; subset size = (total+target)/2Transformation: S⁺ = (total+target)/2; count subsets summing to this value; if (total+target) is odd or negative → 0
822LeetCode1049 – Last Stone Weight IIMedium0/1 knapsack — max balanced partitionReduce: split stones into two groups minimising |S1-S2|; equivalent to max subset sum ≤ total/2Same structure as S.No 1 but goal changes: find LARGEST achievable sum ≤ total/2; answer = total - 2*max_dp
922LeetCode2787 – Ways to Express an Integer as Sum of PowersMedium0/1 count ways with implicit item generationItems are {1^x, 2^x, 3^x, ...} generated at runtime; standard 0/1 count-ways knapsackGenerates items on-the-fly (powers); stops when k^x > n; rest is identical to standard subset count
1023LeetCode474 – Ones and ZeroesMedium2D Knapsack (two capacity dimensions)dp[j][k] — two independent budgets: count of 0s and count of 1s; standard 0/1 recurrence in 2Ddp[j][k] = max(dp[j][k], dp[j-zeros][k-ones]+1); reverse iterate BOTH dimensions; first 2D capacity problem
1123LeetCode879 – Profitable SchemesHard3D Knapsack (members + profit, profit capped)Add profit dimension capped at minProfit; dp[j][p] = ways using j members generating ≥ p profitHard: profit overflows threshold should be treated as exactly minProfit — min(p+profit[i], minProfit) caps the index; missing this cap creates out-of-bounds or wrong state
1223LeetCode2585 – Number of Ways to Earn PointsHardGrouped/bounded knapsack (each type has count limit)Each question type i has count[i] questions each worth mark[i]; bounded by count[i] usesHard: each item group has its own count limit; naïve O(n·T·count) works within constraints; teaches bounded knapsack without binary splitting
1323LeetCode1155 – Number of Dice Rolls with Target SumMediumBounded grouped knapsack (dice rolls)d dice, each face 1..f; dp[j] = ways to get sum j; for each die (group), iterate faces 1..fEach die is a "group"; must pick exactly one face per die; dp[j] += dp[j-face] for each face; teaches grouped knapsack as "one from each group"
1423LeetCode2518 – Number of Great PartitionsHardComplement counting knapsackCount subsets where BOTH parts ≥ k; use complement: total subsets - subsets where part1 < k - subsets where part2 < k + subsets where both < kHard: direct counting is hard; complement approach uses standard 0/1 subset count DP twice; the inclusion-exclusion framing is non-obvious
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Stage 2 → Stage 4: Next problems don't announce "this is a knapsack." The items, weights, and capacity are disguised. For each: (1) identify WHAT the items are, (2) identify WHAT the capacity is, (3) verify the recurrence matches 0/1 or unbounded. Do this before writing any code.
S.NoLayerStageSource# / NameDifficultyPremium?Sub-VariantNew Idea AddedKey Insight
1534LeetCode956 – Tallest BillboardHard0/1 knapsack on difference dimensiondp[d] = max common height when one rod exceeds the other by d; not standard sum — difference is the capacityHard: items are rods added to either side; state = height difference; dp[d] = max common height achievable with difference d; the "capacity = difference" reduction is non-obvious
1634LeetCode2291 – Maximum Profit from Trading Stocks ✅Hard✅0/1 knapsack on value thresholdState tracks which reward levels are unlocked by total reward collected; treat rewards as items with weight=value ordering constraintHard: rewards must be collected in order of value; this constraint converts a greedy problem into an ordered knapsack
1734LeetCode2915 – Length of the Longest Subsequence That Sums to TargetMedium0/1 knapsack — max count at exact sumdp[j] = max number of items achieving sum j; dp[j] = max(dp[j], dp[j-num]+1) if dp[j-num] != -∞Adds a max-count objective to the boolean subset sum; changes True/False to "max items used or -∞"
1834LeetCode1547 – Minimum Cost to Cut a StickHardInterval DP disguised as knapsackdp[i][j] = min cost to make all cuts between position i and j; NOT knapsack — interval DP⚠️ Partial trap: looks like "choose cuts" knapsack but optimal substructure requires INTERVAL not linear DP
1934LeetCode1449 – Form Largest Integer With Digits That Add up to TargetHardUnbounded knapsack — lexicographic maxdp[j] = longest digit string with digit-cost sum = j; tiebreak by lexicographic value; cost per digit is not digit itselfHard: two-level comparison (length first, then lexicographic); recurrence must handle string comparison; recognise as unbounded knapsack where "value" = digit string length, then digit value
2034LeetCode2431 – Maximize Total Tastiness of Purchased Fruits ✅Medium✅0/1 knapsack with coupon discount (extra capacity type)Coupons reduce price; model as 2D knapsack: (money, coupons_used); dp[j][c]Adds a coupon dimension to the budget; teaches that discounts create a new capacity axis
2134LeetCode2902 – Count of Sub-Multisets with Bounded SumHardBounded knapsack with frequency map + sliding windowItems have frequencies; use monotone deque sliding window for O(W) per item typeHard: naïve bounded knapsack is O(nWK); frequency-aware deque optimisation reduces to O(nW); the sliding window mechanics on the DP array is non-trivial
2234LeetCode3082 – Find the Sum of the Power of All SubsequencesHardKnapsack count with power-of-2 weightingCount subsequences summing to k, weighted by 2^(n - subsequence_length); uses knapsack + combinatorial weightHard: the 2^(n-len) weighting seems to require tracking length, but can be folded into the recurrence using binomial identity
2334LeetCode2261 – K Divisible Elements SubarraysMedium⚠️ TRAP — looks like knapsack (select k elements), is hashing/trieDistinct subarrays with at most k multiples of p; use rolling hash or trie to count distinct subarraysNOT knapsack: "distinct subarrays" requires deduplication that knapsack cannot provide; use suffix array or rolling hash
2445LeetCode1981 – Minimize the Difference Between Target and Chosen ElementsMediumRow-selection knapsack (one from each row) = grouped knapsackPick one from each row; state = set of achievable sums; bitset shifts each row's valuesEach row is a "group"; add all row values to the reachable-sum bitset; after all rows, find closest to target; bitset implementation is 64× faster
2545LeetCode2857 – Count Pairs of Points With Distance kMedium⚠️ TRAP — looks like 2D knapsack, is XOR enumerationXOR distance k on pairs; enumerate one XOR component, look up complement in hash mapNOT knapsack: XOR structure → use hash map + enumeration, not DP
2645LeetCode2291 – Maximum Reward (revisit) / 2560 – House Robber IVHardMeet-in-the-middle on subset sumSplit items into two halves; enumerate all 2^(n/2) sums per half; sort one half; binary search for best complement ≤ targetHard: standard knapsack O(nW) is infeasible when W is huge but n ≤ 40; meet-in-the-middle gives O(2^(n/2) log n)
2745LeetCode2560 – House Robber IVMediumBinary search + 0/1 feasibility knapsackBinary search on the answer (minimum capability); for each mid, check feasibility via greedy 0/1 selectionStage 4+5: outer binary search reduces optimisation to feasibility; inner feasibility is a greedy (not knapsack) — teaches when to NOT use knapsack inside binary search
2845AtCoderEDPC F – LCS (as bounded knapsack perspective)HardKnapsack reformulation of string DPDP over characters as "items" with frequency bounds; dp[j] = max common subsequence achievable at edit budget jBridges string DP and knapsack; the character-frequency bounded knapsack view of LCS is not obvious
2945🔧 CUSTOMClosest Achievable Subset SumHardKnapsack — closest-to-target with bitsetdp is a bitset; after filling, scan from target downward for first set bitSee full statement ↓; bitset DP for boolean reachability + directional scan; 64× faster than bool array
⚠️ TRAP ZONE — The following problems look like knapsack but require entirely different approaches
305 ⚠️—LeetCode39 – Combination Sum ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — looks like unbounded knapsack, requires backtrackingMust return ALL combinations; DP counts ways but cannot enumerate them; use backtracking with pruningWhen the problem asks to enumerate or list all solutions, DP gives counts only; backtracking is required
315 ⚠️—LeetCode2054 – Two Best Non-Overlapping Events ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — looks like 2D knapsack (pick 2 intervals), is DP + binary search on sorted eventsTwo non-overlapping intervals, max sum; greedy sort by end time + binary search for latest non-overlapping → O(n log n)NOT 2D knapsack: the overlap constraint creates an ordering structure best exploited by sorting + binary search
🔧 Custom Problem 29 — Closest Achievable Subset Sum
Problem: Given an array nums of N positive integers and an integer target, find the subset of nums whose sum is closest to target (≤ target preferred; if no subset ≤ target, return the smallest sum > target). Return the closest achievable sum.

Constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 1000, 1 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 1000, 1 ≤ target ≤ 10⁶

Example: nums=[3,7,5,11,2], target=14 → subsets with sum ≤ 14: {12,13,14,10,...}; answer = 14 ({3,11} or {7,5,2})

Stage 5 solution: Solve with bitset-optimised 0/1 knapsack. dp is a Python int used as a bitset (bit j = 1 means sum j is achievable). For each num, update: dp |= (dp << num). After all items, scan from target downward for the highest set bit ≤ target. If none exists, scan upward from target for first set bit > target. In Python: dp = int with bit manipulation; in C++: bitset<MAXW> with shift. The 64× speedup over a bool array is the Stage 5 insight. The "closest to target" directional scan is the Stage 4 reduction applied afterward.
State Evolution Across Stages
Group 1: Core 0/1 Knapsack

Stage 1 — Subset Sum (Boolean):
dp[j] = True if sum j is achievable using some subset of items processed so far
Recurrence: dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - w[i]] (iterate j from W down to w[i])
Base cases: dp[0] = True; dp[j] = False for j > 0
Preferred: Bottom-up 1D reverse — O(W) space; forward would allow reuse
Optimisation (Stage 5): Replace bool array with Python int as bitset; dp |= dp << w[i]; 64× speedup

Stage 2 — Count Ways:
dp[j] = number of subsets summing to exactly j
Recurrence: dp[j] += dp[j - w[i]] (reverse iterate)
Base cases: dp[0] = 1; dp[j] = 0 for j > 0
Preferred: Bottom-up 1D reverse; apply % MOD at every step

Stage 2 — Max Value:
dp[j] = maximum value achievable with capacity exactly j
Recurrence: dp[j] = max(dp[j], dp[j - w[i]] + v[i]) (reverse iterate)
Base cases: dp[0] = 0; dp[j] = -∞ for j > 0 (or 0 if "at most j")
Preferred: Bottom-up 1D reverse; initialise to -∞ for "exactly j", to 0 for "at most j"

Group 2: Unbounded Knapsack

Stage 2 — Min Cost (Coin Change):
dp[j] = minimum number of items to reach sum exactly j
Recurrence: dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j - w[i]] + 1) (forward iterate: j from w[i] to W)
Base cases: dp[0] = 0; dp[j] = ∞ for j > 0
Preferred: Bottom-up 1D forward — forward allows reuse; if dp[W] = ∞ → impossible

Stage 2 — Count Ways Unordered (Coin Change II):
dp[j] = number of ways to make sum j using any number of each coin
Recurrence: dp[j] += dp[j - c[i]] (items outer, capacity inner, forward)
Base cases: dp[0] = 1; dp[j] = 0 for j > 0
Key: items-outer → each coin denomination considered in order → no duplicate (same-value, different-order) sequences

Stage 2 — Count Ways Ordered (Combination Sum IV):
Same recurrence but loops swapped: capacity outer, items inner, forward
for j in 1..W: for c in coins: dp[j] += dp[j-c] if j>=c
Counts ordered sequences (permutations); [1,2] and [2,1] counted separately

Group 3: 2D and Multi-Dimensional Knapsack

Stage 3 — 2D Knapsack (Ones and Zeroes):
dp[j][k] = max items achievable using at most j zeros and k ones
Recurrence: dp[j][k] = max(dp[j][k], dp[j-z][k-o]+1) (reverse iterate BOTH j and k)
Base cases: dp[0][0] = 0
Preferred: Bottom-up 2D reverse — both dimensions are 0/1 capacity; both must iterate high-to-low
Space: O(W₁ × W₂); for LC474: 100×100 = 10,000 cells

Stage 3 — 3D Knapsack with Capped Dimension (Profitable Schemes):
dp[j][p] = number of crime schemes using exactly j members generating AT LEAST p profit
Recurrence: dp[j][p] += dp[j - member[i]][max(0, p - profit[i])] (reverse j and p)
Base cases: dp[0][0] = 1
Key: max(0, p - profit[i]) caps the profit dimension — "at least p" semantics; any excess profit treated as exactly minProfit

Group 4: Grouped and Bounded Knapsack

Stage 3 — Grouped Knapsack (Dice Rolls / Exam Score):
Items partitioned into groups; choose exactly one item from each group
dp[j] after processing group g = max/count using one from each of groups 1..g with total weight j
Recurrence: for each group g: for j from W down to 0: for item i in g: dp[j] = op(dp[j], dp[j-w[i]] + v[i])
Key: The inner item loop runs INSIDE the capacity loop for each group; prevents double-counting within a group

Stage 3 — Bounded Knapsack (Naïve → Binary Split → Deque):
Naïve: Unroll k[i] copies of item i; run standard 0/1 knapsack → O(nWK)
Binary Split (Stage 3): Split k[i] copies into groups of 1, 2, 4, ..., remainder (binary representation); each group is a "big item" in a 0/1 knapsack → O(nW log K)
Monotone Deque (Stage 5): For each item type, the recurrence dp[j] = max over t=0..k[i] of (dp[j - t*w[i]] + t*v[i]) is a sliding window max over the remainder classes modulo w[i]; deque maintains the window → O(nW) total

Group 5: Disguised Knapsack

Stage 4 — Tallest Billboard:
dp[d] = max common height achievable when one rod is d more than the other
Recurrence for each rod with length L:
  new_dp[d] = max(dp[d]) — skip rod
  new_dp[d+L] = max(new_dp[d+L], dp[d] + L) — add to taller side
  new_dp[|d-L|] = max(new_dp[|d-L|], dp[d] + min(d, L)) — add to shorter side
Base: dp[0] = 0; dp[d] = -∞ for d > 0; Answer: dp[0]
Preferred: Top-down with memoisation on (rod_index, difference) — easier to reason; can also be bottom-up with copy of dp per rod

Stage 4 — Target Sum (Reduction):
Assign + or - to each number to hit target
Let S⁺ = sum of positive group, S⁻ = sum of negative group
S⁺ + S⁻ = total; S⁺ - S⁻ = target → S⁺ = (total + target) / 2
If (total + target) is odd or (total + target)/2 < 0 → answer = 0
Otherwise: count subsets summing to (total + target) / 2 — standard 0/1 count DP
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
• "can you partition / divide / split the array into two subsets" → Stage 1/4 0/1 subset sum — reduce to sum = total/2
• "minimum number of [coins/tiles/items] to reach exactly [target]" → Stage 2 unbounded knapsack min — forward iterate
• "number of ways to make / form / achieve [sum/target]" → Stage 2 count DP — check if order matters (combination vs permutation loop nesting)
• "at most [budget1] zeros and [budget2] ones" → Stage 3 2D knapsack — both capacity dimensions reverse-iterate
• "assign +/- signs to numbers to reach target" → Stage 4 Target Sum reduction — convert to subset sum count on (total+target)/2

Structural Triggers:
• Input is a list of items each with a single numeric weight; answer is a scalar about a target sum → 0/1 or unbounded knapsack depending on reuse
• Items are partitioned into groups with exactly one selection per group → Grouped knapsack; inner item loop inside capacity loop
• n ≤ 40, target is very large (>10⁶) → Meet-in-the-middle; standard DP infeasible

Constraint Triggers:
• W ≤ 10⁴ and n ≤ 10³ → standard O(nW) knapsack in time; use 1D rolling array for space
• W ≤ 10⁶ and n ≤ 10³ → bitset optimisation reduces constant by 64×; or meet-in-the-middle if n ≤ 40

Anti-Triggers:
• "list ALL subsets / combinations that sum to target" → NOT knapsack → Backtracking with pruning (knapsack counts but cannot enumerate)
• "shortest path / minimum edits between two states" → NOT knapsack → BFS or edit-distance DP (items are not selected; states transition)
• "select at most K non-overlapping intervals maximising sum" → NOT pure knapsack → Interval DP + binary search (overlap constraint creates ordering structure)
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem involve SELECTING a SUBSET of items to optimise/count subject to a CAPACITY constraint? → NO: Not knapsack. Consider graph DP, interval DP, or greedy. → YES: Continue. Q2: Can each item be used MORE THAN ONCE? → YES (unbounded): Forward iteration. dp[j] uses updated values. Minimise? → dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j-w]+cost). Base dp[0]=0, rest ∞. Count ways (ordered)? → Capacity outer, items inner. Count ways (unordered)? → Items outer, capacity inner. Go to Q5. → NO (each item once, 0/1): Reverse iteration. Go to Q3. Q3: How many items of each type are allowed? → Exactly 0 or 1 (pure 0/1): Reverse iterate. Go to Q4. → At most k[i] (bounded): Binary split into log(k[i]) 0/1 items per type. Or: monotone deque for O(nW) — Stage 5. → Exactly one from each group: Grouped knapsack: group outer, capacity inner, item innermost. Q4: What is the objective? → Boolean (can we hit sum j?): dp[j] |= dp[j-w]. Base dp[0]=True. → Count ways: dp[j] += dp[j-w]. Base dp[0]=1. → Max value: dp[j] = max(dp[j], dp[j-w]+v). Base dp[0]=0 (or -∞ for exact). → Min cost: dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j-w]+c). Base dp[0]=0, rest ∞. → Closest to target: Fill boolean dp; scan from target for nearest set bit. Q5: Are there TWO OR MORE independent capacity dimensions? → YES: 2D/3D knapsack. Reverse iterate ALL capacity dimensions. Each extra dimension multiplies space by its size. → NO: Continue. Q6: Is the problem disguised? (items/capacity not explicit) → Check: "Target Sum" → compute S⁺ = (total+target)/2 → count subset. → Check: "Billboard" → difference = capacity → dp[diff] = common height. → Check: "Partition balanced" → target = total/2 → boolean subset sum. → If still not clear: name what "item" = , "weight" = , "capacity" = . Q7: Is the capacity W too large for standard O(nW)? → W > 10⁶ and n ≤ 40: Meet-in-the-middle. O(2^(n/2) log n). → W > 10⁵ and n ≤ 1000: Bitset optimisation. O(nW/64). → W ≤ 10⁵: Standard DP is fine. Stage identification summary: Q2+Q4, no extra dimensions → Stage 1/2. O(nW). Q5 YES → Stage 3. O(n·W₁·W₂). Q6 YES (disguised) → Stage 4. O(nW) after transformation. Q7 YES → Stage 5. O(nW/64) or O(2^(n/2) log n). If O(nW) TLEs: try bitset (W large) or meet-in-the-middle (n small, W huge).
When This Technique BREAKS
[Stage 1] Item reuse accidentally enabled by forward iteration in 0/1 knapsack: Forward iteration allows dp[j-w] to reflect item i already being taken once — silently turning 0/1 into unbounded. Reverse iteration for ALL 0/1 knapsack variants. No exceptions.

[Stage 2] Wrong loop nesting for ordered vs. unordered count: Items-outer counts unordered combinations; capacity-outer counts ordered permutations. Swapping the loops for the wrong problem produces inflated (permutations) or deflated (combinations) counts.

[Stage 2] Base case dp[0]=0 for count-ways DP: For count-ways, dp[0] must be 1 (there is exactly one way to make sum 0: the empty subset). Setting dp[0]=0 makes every downstream cell 0. For min-cost, dp[0]=0 is correct; for boolean, dp[0]=True.

[Stage 3] 2D Knapsack: iterating one dimension forward and one reverse: Both dimensions must be iterated in REVERSE for 0/1 2D knapsack. Iterating one forward turns that dimension unbounded.

[Stage 3] Profitable Schemes: not capping profit at minProfit index: If p + profit[i] > minProfit, the new index must be minProfit, not p + profit[i]. Without capping, the index overflows the table and "at least minProfit" semantics break.

[Stage 4] Target Sum: not checking parity of (total + target): If (total + target) is odd, the subset size (total+target)/2 is not an integer — zero valid subsets. Forgetting this check produces wrong answers for odd-sum inputs.

[Stage 4] Tallest Billboard: not copying the dp array before updating: The recurrence requires dp from the previous rod step. If you update dp[d+L] before reading dp[d], you may use a value already modified by the current rod — equivalent to using the same rod twice. Always work on a copy per rod.

[Stage 5] Bounded knapsack: binary splitting without handling the remainder: Binary split produces groups {1, 2, 4, ..., 2^k, remainder}. If the remainder group is omitted, the total represented is 2^(k+1)-1, not k[i] — wrong item count.

[Stage 5] Bitset: using arithmetic shift instead of logical shift: In languages with signed integer shift, right-shifting the bitset may propagate sign bits. Use UNSIGNED types or language-level bitset<N> for correctness.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — 0/1 Subset Sum: Reverse Iteration Prevents Reuse
Claim: After processing item i with weight w[i], dp[j] correctly reflects whether sum j is achievable using items {1..i}, with each item used at most once.
Invariant before item i: dp[j] reflects items {1..i-1} correctly.
Update (reverse order j = W down to w[i]): When computing the new dp[j], we read dp[j-w[i]]. Since j-w[i] < j and we iterate from high to low, dp[j-w[i]] has NOT yet been updated in this iteration — it still reflects items {1..i-1} only.
Therefore: dp[j] |= dp[j-w[i]] says "sum j is achievable using items {1..i} IF EITHER it was achievable without item i, OR it was achievable at j-w[i] without item i, plus item i once."
Item i counted at most once: dp[j-w[i]] does not yet include item i (reverse order). ✓ □

Stage 2 — Unbounded Knapsack: Forward Iteration Enables Reuse
Claim: After processing all coins, dp[j] = minimum coins to make sum j, with each coin usable unlimited times.
Invariant: dp[j] = min coins to make j using coins processed so far, with unlimited reuse.
Update (forward order j = w to W): When computing dp[j], we read dp[j-w[i]]. Since j-w[i] < j and we iterate forward, dp[j-w[i]] has ALREADY been updated for coin i — it may include coin i one or more times.
Therefore: dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j-w[i]]+1) correctly captures using coin i one MORE time on top of any number of previous uses. ✓ □

Stage 3 — 2D Knapsack: Both Dimensions Must Reverse-Iterate
Claim: For 0/1 2D knapsack with capacities (Z, O), reversing both dimensions prevents reuse of any item.
Suppose dimension Z is forward-iterated: When computing dp[j][k], dp[j-z][k-o] has already been updated for item i — item i can contribute to dp[j-z][k-o], and then again to dp[j][k]: item i counted twice. ✗
With both reverse: dp[j-z][k-o] for j-z < j and k-o < k has not yet been updated in this item's pass (both indices are smaller → processed later in reverse order). Item i counted at most once. ✓ □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
