Need dp[type] = best score ending with that type. Missing the state dimension gives wrong greedy-like answers.

[Stage 5] Matrix exponentiation: wrong state vector initialisation: The initial state vector must precisely encode the base case of the state machine. Off-by-one in which step's state vector is used (step 0 vs step 1) shifts all subsequent states and gives wrong answers for large n.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — Two-State Stock (Buy Once): Correctness of Rolling Variables
Claim: At each day i, hold = max(-prices[j] for j ≤ i) and rest = max(0, max(-prices[j] + prices[k]) for j < k ≤ i).
hold invariant: hold = max(hold_prev, -prices[i]). By induction, hold_prev = max(-prices[j] for j < i). After update: hold = max(-prices[j] for j ≤ i). ✓
rest invariant: rest = max(rest_prev, hold_prev + prices[i]). hold_prev + prices[i] = max one-transaction profit ending at day i. Taking max with rest_prev gives the global best profit up to day i. ✓
Strict buy-before-sell: hold_prev reflects purchases strictly before day i, so hold_prev + prices[i] represents selling on day i after a past purchase. ✓ □

Stage 2 — Cooldown State Machine: Correctness of Three-State Separation
Claim: The three-state machine (HOLD, REST, COOL) correctly models the cooldown constraint.
State completeness: Any configuration is exactly one of: holding stock (HOLD), not holding and can buy (REST), not holding and cannot buy yet — day immediately after a sell (COOL). These three are exhaustive and mutually exclusive. ✓
COOL → REST transition: After one day in COOL, you move to REST (can buy again). The transition rest = max(rest, cool) on the NEXT day correctly delays buy eligibility by one day. ✓
No-stale-read guarantee: Using temporary new_hold, new_rest, new_cool ensures all three new states use the previous day's configuration — not a partially-updated current day. □

Stage 3 — K-Transaction DP: Induction on Transactions
Claim: dp[t][0] = max profit using at most t complete transactions, not holding; dp[t][1] = max profit using t-1 complete sells and currently holding.
Base: t=0: dp[0][0] = 0 (no transactions, no stock). dp[0][1] = -∞ (holding without any context). ✓
Induction on t: dp[t][0] = max(dp[t][0], dp[t-1][1] + prices[i]). This correctly sources from t-1 completed transactions. ✓
Why dp[t-1] for the sell? Holding with t-1 completed sells means the t-th transaction is open; selling it completes the t-th transaction. □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 — Stale-read in cooldown: updating hold before cool is read 
# BUG: hold updated first; cool references NEW hold (wrong)
hold = max(hold, cool - prices[i])   # new hold computed
cool = hold + prices[i]              # BUG: uses new hold
# FIX: compute all new states from old values simultaneously
new_hold = max(hold, cool - prices[i])
new_cool = hold + prices[i]          # old hold
hold, cool = new_hold, new_cool
Bug 2 — Buy-once stock: using rest - prices[i] instead of -prices[i] 
# BUG: allows buying multiple times (uses accumulated rest profit)
hold = max(hold, rest - prices[i])  # BUG for buy-once
# FIX for buy ONCE:
hold = max(hold, -prices[i])        # can only spend cash, not prior profit
Bug 3 — Fee: applying fee on BOTH buy and sell 
# BUG: fee deducted twice per transaction
hold = max(hold, rest - prices[i] - fee)  # BUG: fee on buy
rest = max(rest, hold + prices[i] - fee)  # AND on sell = double fee
# FIX: apply fee once — on SELL only
hold = max(hold, rest - prices[i])
rest = max(rest, hold + prices[i] - fee)
Bug 4 — K-transaction: stale-read sell uses same-day buy 
# BUG: sell reads dp[k][1] that was just updated this same day
for k in range(1, K+1):
    dp[k][1] = max(dp[k][1], dp[k][0] - prices[i])   # buy
    dp[k][0] = max(dp[k][0], dp[k][1] + prices[i])   # BUG: same-day buy
# FIX: sell sources from PREVIOUS transaction's buy
    dp[k][0] = max(dp[k][0], dp[k-1][1] + prices[i])  # sell increases count
    dp[k][1] = max(dp[k][1], dp[k][0] - prices[i])    # buy at count k
Bug 5 — At most k: not handling k ≥ n//2 edge case 
# BUG: for k = n = 10^5, allocating dp[k][2] → MLE or TLE
dp = [[0, -float('inf')] for _ in range(k+1)]  # BUG: k up to 10^5
# FIX: reduce to unlimited when k is large
if k >= len(prices) // 2:
    return solve_unlimited(prices)
Bug 6 — Alternating subsequence: updating even and odd in wrong order 
# BUG: even updated first; odd uses NEW even (wrong)
even = max(even, odd + a[i])   # new even
odd = max(odd, even - a[i])    # BUG: uses new even
# FIX: use temp variables
new_even = max(even, odd + a[i])
new_odd = max(odd, even - a[i])   # old even
even, odd = new_even, new_odd
Bug 7 — Positive product: not resetting BOTH pos and neg on zero 
# BUG: only resetting pos on zero; neg carries stale state
if a[i] == 0:
    pos = 0   # BUG: neg not reset
# FIX:
    pos, neg = 0, 0  # zero resets ALL product-length tracking
Bug 8 — Number of ways to select buildings: missing "101" pattern 
# BUG: only counts "010" patterns, missing "101"
ans = count_pattern(buildings, pattern="010")  # BUG
# FIX:
ans = count_pattern(buildings, "010") + count_pattern(buildings, "101")
Bug 9 — Matrix exponentiation: off-by-one in initial state vector 
# BUG: applying transition matrix n times instead of n-1
result = matrix_pow(transition, n) @ initial_vector  # BUG: one extra step
# FIX: initial_vector is state AFTER day 0; apply n-1 more transitions
result = matrix_pow(transition, n-1) @ initial_vector
Bug 10 — Random walk DP: not clamping position to min(arrLen, steps//2) 
# BUG: iterating pos up to arrLen → huge state space for large arrLen
for pos in range(arrLen):   # BUG for arrLen=10^6
    ...
# FIX: position can never exceed steps//2 moves from start
max_pos = min(arrLen - 1, steps // 2)
for pos in range(max_pos + 1):
    ...
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
[Stage 1] Forgetting that "buy once" and "unlimited" differ by one term in one transition: For buy-once, rest before the first buy is always 0, so rest - prices[i] = -prices[i] — equivalent ONLY in that sense. But adding rest's accumulated profit conceptually allows a second "free buy." Correct model: "Buy-once: hold = max(hold, -prices[i]). Unlimited: hold = max(hold, rest - prices[i]). The ONLY difference is whether prior profit is reinvested."

[Stage 2] Cooldown: sourcing the buy transition from REST instead of COOL: If you buy from REST, you allow buying the day after selling — violating the cooldown. The buy must source from COOL. Correct model: "Cooldown creates a directed chain: HOLD → (sell) → COOL → (next day) → REST → (buy) → HOLD. The buy arrow comes from REST, but REST is only reachable from COOL."

[Stage 3] K-transaction: updating buy and sell in same loop iteration without temporary variables: The sell transition dp[t][0] = dp[t-1][1] + prices[i] correctly uses t-1 transaction buy. But if buy dp[t][1] = dp[t][0] - prices[i] runs first and updates dp[t][0], then the sell reads a same-day value. Correct model: "Compute ALL new state values into temporary variables first. Write ALL new values simultaneously."

[Stage 4] Missing the state in disguised problems: When a problem says "select elements with alternating types" or "build a sequence with no two consecutive same elements," candidates jump to greedy or brute-force. The state IS the type of the last selected element. Correct model: "For any problem with 'the last selected element must satisfy property P,' make P the state. Draw K states. Fill the state machine."

[Stage 3] Not detecting the k ≥ n//2 threshold for unlimited equivalence: When k ≥ n//2, you can never exhaust k transactions (a transaction requires at least 2 days). Allocating O(nk) DP table for k=10^9 is MLE. Correct model: "Before writing the k-transaction DP, check: if k ≥ len(prices)//2, return the unlimited-transactions answer. This is not an optimisation — it's a correctness requirement for large k."

[Stage 4] Treating alternating subsequence as a greedy problem: The alternating subsequence sum looks like it could be solved greedily. In fact, the optimal can skip profitable segments to enable future gains. Correct model: "Greedy works for LONGEST alternating (count direction changes), but for MAXIMUM SUM alternating, you must track even and odd states explicitly."

[Stage 2] Fee applied twice: Some candidates apply fee on both the buy (rest - prices[i] - fee) and the sell (hold + prices[i] - fee). This charges double per transaction. Correct model: "One transaction = one buy + one sell = one fee. Apply fee on exactly ONE of the two transitions (convention: on sell). Never on both."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
#StageQuestionIntended Answer Direction
12"What if the transaction fee varies per transaction (fee[t] for t-th transaction)?"Add t as state dimension; dp[t][hold]; on sell: dp[t][0] = dp[t-1][1] + price - fee[t]; O(nk)
22"What if you can hold at most 2 stocks simultaneously?"Add "2 stocks held" state; 3 states per transaction level: REST, HOLD_1, HOLD_2; transitions include buying a 2nd stock and selling either
33"How do you recover the actual sequence of buy/sell days, not just the profit?"Parent-pointer array: at each state transition, record which previous state was chosen; backtrack from final optimal state
43"What if cooldown is k days (not just 1)?"k intermediate COOL states chained: SELL → COOL_1 → ... → COOL_k → REST; total states = original + k; O(n·(states+k))
54"Design a state machine for: select elements to maximise sum where no two selected elements can both be divisible by 3."States: LAST_DIV3, LAST_NOT_DIV3; transitions: from LAST_DIV3, can only add NOT_DIV3 elements; from LAST_NOT_DIV3, can add either
64"Stock problem but you can short-sell (profit when price falls). What changes?"Add SHORT state alongside LONG; 3 states: REST, LONG (bought expecting rise), SHORT (sold expecting fall); buy-back from SHORT adds profit when price fell
75"n = 10^18 days, prices repeat with period p = 10. How do you solve at-most-k-transactions?"Matrix exponentiation: build p×p state machine for one cycle; matrix-exponentiate to get n//p full cycles; handle remainder separately; O(K² log n)
85"Prove that for k ≥ n//2, the k-transaction solution equals the unlimited solution."A transaction requires buying then selling = at least 2 days. Maximum possible non-overlapping transactions in n days = n//2. So k ≥ n//2 means the k-cap is never reached; unlimited solution achievable within k transactions.
9Beyond"Distribute the stock DP across 10 machines, each holding n/10 days. How?"Segment tree of state machines: each machine holds a 2K×2K transition matrix for its segment; combine via matrix multiplication; O(K² log n) with O(K²) communication per merge
10Beyond"Adversary sets prices after seeing your strategy. What's the optimal guarantee?"Minimax stock trading: adversary maximises regret; optimal strategy = hold nothing (profit = 0 guaranteed); any holding exposes to adversarial price drop to 0; competitive ratio = 1 for any deterministic strategy
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):

Stage 1 — core 2-state machine:
S.No 1 (121) → S.No 2 (122). Write both with rolling variables only. Draw the 2-node state diagram before coding. Verify: can you reconstruct the recurrence from the state diagram alone?

Stage 2 — one constraint mutation at a time:
S.No 3 (714 fee) → S.No 4 (309 cooldown). For each: (1) redraw the state diagram adding the new state/edge, (2) write the recurrence, (3) code with temporary variables.

Stage 3 — state dimension expansion:
S.No 5 (123, k=2) → S.No 6 (188, k=any) → S.No 9 (1911 alternating) → S.No 10 (1567 sign) → S.No 11 (1186 deletion) → S.No 18 (1269 random walk). For each: draw the FULL state diagram with all K nodes and all directed edges.

Stage 4 — disguised state machines:
S.No 12 (Wiggle) → S.No 13 (2222 Buildings) → S.No 14 (1537 Get Score) → S.No 19 (2786 Visit Positions). For each: name the states and transitions BEFORE reading any editorial.

Stage 5 — matrix exponentiation:
S.No 22 (AtCoder Sushi) → S.No 23 (CF 833B). Implement matrix exponentiation for a 3-state machine; verify by checking against O(nk) DP for small n.

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and pattern recognition):
• Stage 1/2: Full stock family (S.No 1–4) under 8 minutes each
• Stage 3: K-transaction variants under 20 minutes
• Stage 4: Disguised problems — given title only, name the states in 60 seconds before reading
• State diagram drill: For any state machine problem, draw the complete transition diagram on paper first. If you can't draw it in 2 minutes, you don't know the states yet.
• Rolling-variable drill: Implement every 2-state solution with exactly 2 Python variables. Implement every 3-state solution with 3 variables. Use temporary variables to prevent stale reads.

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources per Stage):
• Stage 1/2: LeetCode contest problems tagged "dynamic programming" from the last 6 months
• Stage 3: AtCoder EDPC problems H–L (knapsack/DP variants with state machine structure)
• Stage 4: Codeforces Div 2 D problems tagged "dp" with no explicit "buy/sell" — 3 problems
• Stage 5: USACO Platinum problems involving state machine + matrix exponentiation
Graduation Test
These three problems are NOT in the table above. Solving all three cold (no hints, no editorial, 45 min each) certifies full mastery.

Test 1 — LC 1840: Maximum Building Height (modified: cooldown between restricted floors) (Stage 3 + Stage 4)
Primary stage: Stage 4. Combination: Stage 4 (recognise the height propagation as a state machine with RESTRICTED and FREE states for each floor, where transitions encode height constraints) + Stage 3 (the k-restriction state = how many restrictions have been applied; sorting restrictions by floor creates a processable sequence). Why it certifies mastery: The input is restrictions on floor heights — no stock, no explicit states. The reduction to a "state machine on sorted restrictions" with height-as-state-value requires Stage 4 recognition. Handling the variable transition cost between restrictions (geometric arithmetic on height differences) requires Stage 3 multi-dimensional thinking.

Test 2 — AtCoder DP Contest – Sushi (Expected Value State Machine) (Stage 3 + Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Primary stage: Stage 4. Combination: Stage 4 (states are counts of 1/2/3-piece dishes; not a standard hold/rest machine) + Stage 3 (3-dimensional state (a,b,c) where a+b+c ≤ n; each transition depends on uniform random selection) + Stage 5 (memoisation makes the O(n³) state space tractable; without it, exponential recursion). Why it certifies mastery: Expected-value state machines are a fundamental extension — the "state" is still (a,b,c) but the transition VALUE is probabilistic. Solving cold proves the candidate can apply state machine DP to non-deterministic processes.

Test 3 — CF 1600F: Playground (Colour-State Machine on Segments) (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Primary stage: Stage 4. Combination: Stage 4 (input is coloured segments; states are the current colour group and its accumulated value — not explicit) + Stage 5 (naïve O(n²) per state; segment tree with lazy propagation reduces to O(n log n); the state machine runs on top of the segment tree). Why it certifies mastery: The states must be derived from the segment colouring structure (Stage 4). The merge of adjacent same-colour segments requires a data structure integration (Stage 5). Both layers must be identified and implemented correctly within 45 minutes on an unseen Codeforces Div 1 problem.
35
Interval DP
Dynamic Programming Palindrome · Merge/Chain · Burst Balloons · Polygon · Strange Printer · Remove Boxes · Knuth-Yao
28 problems · 5 stages · palindrome shrink, split-point merge, last-one-standing, state-expanded, two-boundary, Knuth-Yao optimisation
Why It Matters: Interval DP solves problems where the answer for a sequence s[i..j] is built from the answers for all strict sub-intervals s[i..k] and s[k+1..j] (or s[i..k-1] and s[k..j]), with an additional cost or value contributed by the split point k or the boundary elements. The core invariant is: dp[i][j] = optimal value for the sub-problem on interval [i,j], computed after all dp[i'][j'] with j'-i' < j-i — that is, after all strictly shorter intervals are solved. This mandatory fill-order by increasing length is what distinguishes interval DP from every other 2D DP pattern. It appears relentlessly in top-tier interviews and ICPC/IOI competitions because: (1) the state definition is always dp[i][j] on a contiguous range — learnable and systematic; (2) the split-point loop for k in range(i, j) is the universal recurrence skeleton; (3) disguised variants (balloon popping, matrix chain, stone merging, polygon triangulation) look wildly different on the surface but reduce to the same O(n³) structure; and (4) the hard problems require identifying the CORRECT split-point semantics, which is the single skill that separates good candidates from great ones.
The Mental Model
Interval DP is recursive demolition. You have a row of objects. To solve a problem on range [i,j], you decide LAST which object at position k is the "last one standing" (the last to be processed, removed, or merged). Everything to its left ([i,k-1]) and everything to its right ([k+1,j]) must be fully resolved first. The cost of the current decision depends on what remains at the boundaries when k is processed last.

This "last one standing" framing is the universal template. The key question for every new interval DP problem is: "What object, when processed last, makes its cost easy to compute?"

How this scales through the 5 stages:
Stage 1: dp[i][j] = cost to solve [i,j] by splitting at k; uniform cost per split; fill by length j-i ascending.
Stage 2: Same structure, ONE constraint changes: circular boundary (wrap-around), penalty depends on boundary elements (not just split), or the answer is a COUNT not a cost.
Stage 3: State gains a dimension: dp[i][j][k] where k tracks a count, a color/group, or a number of remaining merges. The split recurrence is identical but the third dimension gates transitions.
Stage 4: Problem looks nothing like interval DP. The "interval" must be identified: balloons burst in an order (frame as "last to burst"), polygon diagonals (triangles = splits), matrix chain (parenthesisation = split). The reduction IS the insight.
Stage 5: The O(n³) solution TLEs. Apply the Knuth-Yao optimisation: if the split point opt[i][j] is monotone, the total work reduces from O(n³) to O(n²).
Core Invariant / Proof Sketch
Invariant: dp[i][j] is correct for all intervals of length L = j-i+1, computed before any interval of length L+1 is computed.

Proof sketch (strong induction on interval length L):
Base case (L=1): dp[i][i] is trivially correct — a single element has no split point; its value is defined directly (zero cost, or the element itself). ✓
Base case (L=2): dp[i][i+1] has exactly one split point k=i; computed directly from the two length-1 sub-problems, which are already correct. ✓
Inductive step: Assume dp[i'][j'] is correct for all j'-i' < L. For interval [i,j] with length L+1, the recurrence dp[i][j] = min/max over k in [i,j-1] of (dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost(i,k,j)) references only sub-intervals of length ≤ L. By the inductive hypothesis, all of these are correct. Therefore dp[i][j] is correct. ✓
Fill order guarantee: Filling in increasing order of L = j-i+1 (outer loop) ensures that when computing dp[i][j], all dp[i][k] and dp[k+1][j] for k ∈ [i,j-1] have already been computed (they are shorter intervals). □

Why "last one standing" works for non-standard problems (e.g., Burst Balloons): When the cost of removing element k depends on its neighbours, we need those neighbours to still be present when k is removed. Framing k as "the LAST removed" guarantees its neighbours a[i-1] and a[j+1] are still present (they are outside the interval being solved). This converts a variable-cost dependent problem into a well-defined subproblem. □
Sub-Variants to Master
Sub-VariantStageTimeSpaceNew Idea
Matrix Chain Multiplication / Optimal ParenthesisationStage 1O(n³)O(n²)THE baseline — dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost(i,k,j); split-point k divides [i,j] into two independent sub-chains; 2D table + split-point loop
Palindrome interval DP (is/length/count)Stage 1O(n²)O(n²)dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][j-1] (SHRINK from both ends), not a split-point; teaches the SHRINK variant of interval DP; fill by increasing length
Optimal BSTStage 2O(n³) → O(n²)O(n²)Cost of split = sum(freq[i..j]) (all elements in interval contribute); prefix sums needed; Knuth-Yao applicable; dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j] + pre[j+1]-pre[i]
Burst BalloonsStage 4O(n³)O(n²)Cost of removing k DEPENDS ON ITS CURRENT NEIGHBOURS — must frame k as LAST removed so boundaries are predictable; add sentinel 1s; the most important non-obvious transformation in interval DP
Minimum Score Triangulation of a PolygonStage 4O(n³)O(n²)Polygon → interval DP by fixing one vertex; split = third vertex of a triangle whose base is always [i,j]; dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]+V[i]*V[k]*V[j]; sub-problems SHARE endpoints
Strange PrinterStage 4O(n³)O(n²)If s[i]==s[k], the turn for s[i] can extend to cover s[k] at no extra cost; dp[i][j] = min(dp[i+1][k]+dp[k+1][j]) when s[i]==s[k]; "last interval printed" insight
Minimum Cost to Merge StonesStage 3O(n³)O(n²)Not all intervals are mergeable — feasibility check: (j-i) % (K-1) == 0; only add final merge cost when feasible; gated split recurrence
Minimum Cost to Cut a StickStage 4O(n³)O(n²)Sort cuts + add endpoints 0 and n as boundaries; dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]+(cuts[j]-cuts[i]); cost = current segment length; order of cuts is the decision
Remove BoxesStage 3O(n⁴)O(n³)Third dimension k = count of EXTRA identical boxes attached at boundary; "teleport" non-adjacent equal boxes; dp[i][j][k] = max points for boxes[i..j] with k extra equal to boxes[i] on left
Allocate MailboxesStages 3+4O(n²k)O(nk)2D DP over (houses, clusters); cost[i][j] = min distance for one mailbox over houses[i..j] = sum of distances to median; prefix sums + median precomputation needed
Guess Number Higher or Lower IIStage 2O(n³)O(n²)Minimax recurrence — dp[i][j] = min over k of (k + max(dp[i][k-1], dp[k+1][j])); adversarial max inside optimal min; teaches minimax interval DP
Zuma Game / Strange Printer variantsStage 3O(n³)O(n²)Consecutive matching collapses sub-problems differently from Strange Printer; segment-elimination structure; contiguous groups must match
Circular interval DPStage 2O(n³)O(n²)Fix one element to break the circle; run two separate passes (element included / excluded); combine results; prevents wrap-around inconsistency
Maximum Score from Performing Multiplication OperationsStage 3O(n·m²)O(m²)Two-pointer boundary: take from LEFT or RIGHT at each step; state = (ops_done, left_count); right_count = ops-left; fill by diagonal ops = i+j; outside-in construction
Knuth-Yao OptimisationStage 5O(n²)O(n²)If optimal split opt[i][j] is monotone (opt[i][j-1] ≤ opt[i][j] ≤ opt[i+1][j]), reduce O(n³) to O(n²) amortised; requires proof of the quadrangle inequality for the cost function
Interval DP on trees (tree DP as interval DP)Stage 4O(n²)O(n²)Euler tour of tree converts tree sub-problems to linear intervals; interval DP on the tour = tree DP; tree sub-problems correspond exactly to contiguous tour ranges
Minimum cost to partition string with dictionaryStage 3O(n³)O(n²)String partitioning with word-cost lookup; combines interval DP with trie for O(n) lookup per endpoint; dp[i][j] = min cost to partition s[i..j]
Expected-value interval DP (probabilistic)Stage 3O(n³)O(n²)Recurrence uses expected value (weighted average over split points with uniform probability); teaches probabilistic transitions in interval DP
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Palindrome check (interval)O(n²)O(n²)Baseline: shrink from ends, no split-point loop; dp[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) and dp[i+1][j-1]
Matrix chain / Merge intervalsO(n³)O(n²)Split-point loop added; cost per merge; prefix sum for interval cost
Optimal BSTO(n³) → O(n²)O(n²)Prefix sum for cost; Knuth-Yao optimisation applicable; root k is not a split midpoint
Burst BalloonsO(n³)O(n²)"Last to burst" transformation; sentinel boundaries 1,1 added; sub-problem boundaries are EXCLUSIVE
Strange PrinterO(n³)O(n²)Merge-on-match optimisation in split loop; base case changes from split to single-character
Minimum Cost Merge StonesO(n³)O(n²)Feasibility constraint (j-i) % (K-1) == 0 gating final merge; infeasible intervals skip the cost addition
Remove BoxesO(n⁴)O(n³)Third dimension for attached-count; O(n) inner scan per cell; top-down memoisation preferred
Allocate MailboxesO(n²k)O(nk)Second dimension = number of mailbox clusters; median cost precomputation; prefix sums for O(1) cost lookup
Knuth-Yao Optimal BSTO(n²)O(n²)Monotone opt property collapses O(n) inner loop to O(1) amortised; requires quadrangle inequality verification
Mastery Problem Set
S.NoLayerStageSource# / NameDifficultyPremium?Sub-VariantNew Idea AddedKey Insight
111LeetCode516 – Longest Palindromic SubsequenceMediumPalindrome interval DP (shrink from ends)THE baseline: dp[i][j] = LPS length in s[i..j]; fill by increasing length j-i; recurrence SHRINKS (not splits)If s[i]==s[j]: dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j-1]+2; else max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]); base dp[i][i]=1; teaches fill-by-length traversal
211LeetCode1312 – Minimum Insertion Steps to Make a String PalindromeMediumPalindrome interval DP (min cost)Same table as S.No 1; answer transforms: n - LPS(s) OR direct min-insertion recurrenceAdds the "cost" objective over S.No 1: dp[i][j] = min insertions for s[i..j]; same recurrence structure but mismatch costs 1
311LeetCode647 – Palindromic SubstringsMediumPalindrome interval DP (count all)Change aggregation: COUNT all (i,j) where dp[i][j]=True; same boolean tableSame table as S.No 2; one aggregation change; reinforces that the palindrome table is reusable
411🔧 CUSTOMMinimum Cost to Merge N Piles into 1 (K=2)MediumMatrix-chain / Split-merge baselineTHE canonical split-point DP: dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + sum(piles[i..j]); introduces the split-point k loopSee full statement ↓; prefix sums for cost; fill by length; dp[i][i]=0; establishes the split-point template for all subsequent problems
511LeetCode1039 – Minimum Score Triangulation of PolygonMediumSplit = third vertex of triangle; fix vertex 0dp[i][j] = min over k in (i+1,j) of dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]+V[i]*V[k]*V[j]; edge [i,j] is base; k is third vertexNew split semantics: both sub-problems SHARE endpoints with parent interval (NOT disjoint halves); base dp[i][i+1]=0
612LeetCode375 – Guess Number Higher or Lower IIMediumMinimax split: min(k + max(dp[i][k-1], dp[k+1][j]))Minimax recurrence: we minimise our WORST-CASE cost; max inside min is the ONE new ideaTeaches adversarial thinking in interval DP; the inner max (adversary's best response) wraps the outer min (our strategy)
722LeetCode87 – Scramble StringHardInterval DP on two strings with split alignmentdp[i1][i2][len] = can s1[i1..i1+len-1] scramble to s2[i2..i2+len-1]; split at k with both swap and no-swap casesHard: 3D state (start1, start2, length); both aligned and cross-split (swap) must be tried; O(n⁴) naïve reduced by memoisation
822LeetCode486 – Predict the WinnerMediumInterval DP with alternating players (minimax)dp[i][j] = max score DIFFERENCE (current player − other) for subarray [i,j]; max(a[i]-dp[i+1][j], a[j]-dp[i][j-1])ONE change from S.No 6: score DIFFERENCE (not absolute) encodes both players' optimality in one value
922LeetCode877 – Stone GameMediumSame as S.No 8; Alex always wins for even n — proof from interval DPShows when interval DP degenerates: for even n equal-value proof makes dp[n-1] > 0 alwaysTeaches: interval DP can prove structural results (not just compute values); answer = dp[n-1] > 0
1022LeetCode664 – Strange PrinterHardInterval DP with merge-on-equal-charactersdp[i][j] = min turns to print s[i..j]; if s[i]==s[k]: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i+1][k] + dp[k+1][j]) — the turn for s[i] extends to cover s[k] freeHard: the "merge on match" optimisation (s[i]==s[k] removes one turn) is non-obvious; requires understanding that a single print operation covers any contiguous block of the same character
1123LeetCode312 – Burst BalloonsHard"Last to burst" transformation; sentinel boundariesAdd sentinels [1] + nums + [1]; dp[i][j] = max coins bursting all balloons BETWEEN i and j (exclusive); k is LAST burst; cost = nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j]Hard: the "last to burst" framing is the non-obvious key — direct order-of-bursting DP is exponential because cost depends on current neighbours; reversing to "last burst" makes boundaries fixed
1223LeetCode1000 – Minimum Cost to Merge StonesHardMerge feasibility constraint: (j-i) % (K-1) == 0dp[i][j] = min cost to merge s[i..j] into fewest piles; merge K consecutive piles into 1; feasibility check (j-i) % (K-1) == 0 gates the "merge into 1" transitionHard: not every interval is reducible to 1 pile; feasibility check (j-i) mod (K-1) == 0 is the non-obvious necessary condition derived from the recurrence structure
1323LeetCode1547 – Minimum Cost to Cut a StickMediumSort cuts + add endpoints; interval = segment between two cutsSort cuts; prepend 0, append n; dp[i][j] = min cost to make ALL cuts between cuts[i] and cuts[j]; cost = cuts[j]-cuts[i] per cut; k is the NEXT cut to makeStage 4 disguised as Stage 3: problem doesn't say "interval DP"; must recognise that cuts define intervals; adding stick endpoints as boundaries is the key transformation
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Layer 2 → Layer 3: Burst Balloons and Strange Printer introduced "non-obvious framings." Next problems require either (a) a third state dimension tracking a count/attachment, or (b) a 2D split that is NOT over all k but over a structured subset. Prepare by asking: "What variable at the BOUNDARY of the interval changes its cost?" — if it does, it becomes the third dimension.
S.NoLayerStageSource# / NameDifficultyPremium?Sub-VariantNew Idea AddedKey Insight
1433LeetCode546 – Remove BoxesHardThird dimension = count of identical boxes attached to boundarydp[i][j][k] = max points for boxes[i..j] with k EXTRA boxes equal to boxes[i] appended to left; k is the count attachmentHard: the attached-count dimension makes this O(n⁴) — extremely non-obvious to find; key move: merge boxes[i] with non-adjacent equal boxes by "teleporting" them; state space O(n³), transitions O(n)
1533LeetCode471 – Encode String with Shortest Length ✅Hard✅Interval DP on string: dp[i][j] = shortest encoding of s[i..j]dp[i][j] = min(split at k: dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]; or run-length encode s[i..j] if it repeats)Hard: two competing options per cell (split vs. compress); the run-length compression check requires KMP failure function to detect periodic substrings
1633LeetCode1246 – Palindrome Removal ✅Hard✅Interval DP: min moves to remove all characters by removing palindromic substringsdp[i][j] = min moves to clear s[i..j]; if s[i]==s[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] (free removal with neighbour); else splitHard: the "free removal when endpoints match" transition collapses the inner problem; combining palindrome-shrink DP with the split-point loop is non-obvious
1733LeetCode410 – Split Array Largest SumHard1D interval DP + binary search: dp[i][j] = min of max sum splitting a[0..i] into j groupsdp[i][j] = min over k of max(dp[k][j-1], sum(a[k+1..i])); prefix sums for group sumBridges interval DP to partitioning DP; the "min of max" objective (not just min sum) is new; teaches that interval DP = DP on CONTIGUOUS PARTITIONS
1834LeetCode1478 – Allocate MailboxesHard2D partition DP with optimal cost[i][j] = sum of distances to mediandp[i][k] = min cost placing k mailboxes for houses[0..i]; cost[i][j] = min total distance for houses[i..j] with one mailbox = distance to medianHard: cost[i][j] is NOT trivially computed — requires precomputed sorted prefix sums; median minimises sum of distances; Knuth optimisation applicable to the outer DP
1934LeetCode1770 – Maximum Score from Performing Multiplication OperationsMediumTwo-boundary interval DP: take from left OR rightdp[i][j] = max score after using left i and right j elements; ops = i+j; dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j] + mult[ops-1]*nums[i-1], dp[i][j-1] + mult[ops-1]*nums[n-j])"Left pointer" + "right pointer" as two-dimensional state; NOT a split-point but a BOUNDARY EXTENSION DP; answers are built from the outside in
2034LeetCode920 – Number of Music PlaylistsHardInterval-style counting DP: dp[i][j] = playlists of length i using j distinct songsdp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]*(N-j+1) + dp[i-1][j]*(j-K if j>K else 0); interval of "songs used" treated as a rangeNot traditional interval DP but uses the same fill-by-second-dimension structure; teaches that "range of used resources" = the interval
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Layer 3 → Layer 4: Hard insight problems require BOTH recognising that interval DP applies (Stage 4) AND implementing a non-standard cost function or optimised transition (Stage 5). The next problems require: (1) deriving the correct split semantics from scratch, and (2) applying Knuth-Yao optimisation whose proof is subtle.
2144LeetCode282 – Expression Add OperatorsHardInterval DP on expression: split at each operator positiondp[i][j] = set of (value, last_term) pairs for s[i..j] as an expression; split at k with operator +,-,*; * requires last_term trackingHard: multiplication undoes addition — must track LAST TERM to handle * correctly; the (value, last_term) pair is the non-obvious state extension
2244CodeforcesCF 149D – Coloring BracketsHardInterval DP with 4 coloring states at boundariesdp[i][j][c1][c2] = ways to color matching brackets [i,j] where i has color c1 ∈ {0,1,2} and j has color c2; nested bracket structure = intervalHard: 4-dimensional state (i, j, color_i, color_j); matching brackets define the intervals; combining coloring constraints across nested intervals requires careful transition logic
2345LeetCode678 – Valid Parenthesis String (DP variant)MediumInterval DP on string with wildcards: dp[i][j] = is s[i..j] valid with * as any chardp[i][j] = True if s[i..j] can be a valid bracket sequence; split or shrink from matching brackets/wildcardsTeaches interval DP where the answer is BOOLEAN and * expands the feasible split set
2445AtCoderEDPC N – SlimesHardOptimal stone merge (same as Custom Problem 4 but with O(n²) Knuth optimisation)Same as S.No 4 but n ≤ 400; Knuth-Yao: opt[i][j-1] ≤ opt[i][j] ≤ opt[i+1][j] reduces O(n³) to O(n²)Knuth-Yao optimisation: prove the quadrangle inequality for the merge cost; opt array tracks monotone split point; amortised O(1) inner loop
2545LeetCode730 – Count Different Palindromic SubsequencesHardInterval DP with 4 character-boundary cases + modular arithmeticdp[i][j] = count of distinct palindromic subsequences in s[i..j]; 4 cases on s[i]==s[j] and inner frequency of that characterHard: four-case analysis (s[i]≠s[j]; s[i]==s[j] with 0/1/2+ occurrences inside) each yields a different formula; combining all four correctly with mod arithmetic is extremely error-prone
⚠️ TRAP ZONE — The following problems look like interval DP but require entirely different approaches
265 ⚠️—LeetCode5 – Longest Palindromic Substring ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — Boolean palindrome table works but Manacher's is O(n)Boolean interval DP correctly solves this but O(n²) time and space; Manacher's algorithm finds all palindrome radii in O(n)NOT optimal interval DP: for LONGEST substring (not subsequence), Manacher's is strictly better; interval DP is overcomplicated and slow
275 ⚠️—LeetCode1130 – Minimum Cost Tree From Leaf Values ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — interval DP works in O(n³) but greedy (monotone stack) is O(n)dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]+max(a[i..k])*max(a[k+1..j]); correct but O(n³)Greedy insight: always combine the two smallest adjacent leaves; monotone stack achieves O(n); teaches when O(n³) interval DP has a greedy shortcut
285 ⚠️—LeetCode132 – Palindrome Partitioning II ⚠️Hard⚠️ TRAP — 2D interval DP is O(n³); correct solution is O(n²)dp[i] = min cuts for s[0..i]; only NEEDS a palindrome table is_palin[i][j]; no split-point loop over k needed for the CUT DPNOT O(n³) interval DP for the CUT problem: the palindrome precomputation is interval DP, but the cut counting is 1D linear DP; conflating them leads to unnecessary O(n³)
🔧 Custom Problem 4 — Minimum Cost to Merge N Piles into 1 (K=2)
Problem: You have n piles of stones in a row. Each move, you pick two adjacent piles and merge them, paying a cost equal to the total number of stones in the two merged piles. Find the minimum total cost to merge all n piles into one.

Constraints: 2 ≤ n ≤ 300, 1 ≤ piles[i] ≤ 100

Example: piles=[3,2,4,1] → merge (3,2)=5 cost 5, (5,4)=9 cost 9, (9,1)=10 cost 10 → total=24. Optimal: merge (3,2)=5, (4,1)=5, (5,5)=10 → total=20.

Stage 1 solution: dp[i][j] = min cost to merge piles[i..j] into one pile. Recurrence: dp[i][j] = min over k in [i,j-1] of (dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + prefix[j+1]-prefix[i]) where prefix is the prefix sum array. Base: dp[i][i] = 0. Fill by increasing length L = j-i+1 from 2 to n. Answer: dp[0][n-1].

Why this exists: This is the simplest problem that establishes ALL of: (1) dp[i][j] on a range, (2) split-point loop over all k, (3) prefix sums for the merge cost, (4) fill-by-length traversal. Every subsequent interval DP problem in this guide inherits this exact template.
State Evolution Across Stages
Group 1: Palindrome Interval DP (Shrink from Ends)
Stage 1 — LPS / Min Insertions:
dp[i][j] = LPS length in s[i..j] (or min insertions)
Recurrence: If s[i] == s[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 2; Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]) for LPS, or 1 + min(...) for min insertions
Base: dp[i][i] = 1; dp[i][i-1] = 0 (empty interval)
Fill order: for length in range(1, n+1): for i in range(n-length+1): j = i+length-1
Preferred: Bottom-up by length; space optimisation: two rows sufficient (length L depends on length L-2)

Stage 1 — Boolean Palindrome:
dp[i][j] = True if s[i..j] is a palindrome
Recurrence: dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and dp[i+1][j-1]; Base: dp[i][i] = True; dp[i][i+1] = (s[i]==s[i+1])
Fill: same length-ascending order; dp[i+1][j-1] requires length ≥ 3 check

Group 2: Split-Point Interval DP (Merge/Cost)
Stage 1 — Merge Piles:
dp[i][j] = min cost to merge piles[i..j]; Recurrence: dp[i][j] = min over k in [i,j-1] of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + pre[j+1] - pre[i]; Base: dp[i][i] = 0
Preferred: Bottom-up by length; top-down memoisation works but bottom-up gives cleaner control for Knuth optimisation

Stage 2 — Optimal BST:
dp[i][j] = min search cost for keys[i..j]; Recurrence: dp[i][j] = min over k in [i,j] of dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j] + (pre[j+1] - pre[i])
Note: k is the ROOT (not a split midpoint); left subtree is [i,k-1], right is [k+1,j]; Base: dp[i][i] = freq[i]; dp[i][i-1] = 0
Optimisation (Stage 5): Track opt[i][j] = optimal root; by Knuth-Yao: opt[i][j-1] ≤ opt[i][j] ≤ opt[i+1][j]; reduces O(n³) to O(n²)

Stage 3 — Minimum Cost Merge Stones (K piles):
dp[i][j] = min cost to merge piles[i..j] into the minimum number of piles
Recurrence: dp[i][j] = min over k in [i,j-K+1] stepping by (K-1) of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]; then if (j-i) % (K-1) == 0: dp[i][j] += prefix[j+1]-prefix[i]
Feasibility: (j-i) % (K-1) == 0 iff interval can be fully merged to 1 pile; Base: dp[i][i] = 0; dp[i][j] = INF if not feasible

Group 3: "Last One Standing" Interval DP (Burst Balloons / Strange Printer)
Stage 4 — Burst Balloons:
dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting ALL balloons STRICTLY between i and j (exclusive; i and j are sentinels/boundaries)
Recurrence: dp[i][j] = max over k in (i+1, j) of dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j]
k is the LAST balloon burst between i and j; when k is burst, i and j are still present (they are the current boundaries)
Base: dp[i][j] = 0 when j-i <= 1; Fill: by increasing j-i; outer loop len = 2..n+1, inner loop i = 0..n+1-len
Note: nums array has 1 prepended and 1 appended as sentinels; dp indices run 0 to n+1

Stage 4 — Strange Printer:
dp[i][j] = min turns to print s[i..j]; Base case: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + 1 (print s[i] alone costs one extra turn)
Optimisation: for k in range(i+1, j+1) where s[k] == s[i]: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i+1][k] + dp[k][j])
When s[i] == s[k], the turn for s[i] can extend to cover position k at no extra cost; Base: dp[i][i] = 1
Fill: by decreasing i, increasing j (or by increasing length)

Group 4: State-Expanded Interval DP (Remove Boxes)
Stage 3 — Remove Boxes:
dp[i][j][k] = max points for boxes[i..j] with k EXTRA boxes identical to boxes[i] attached to the LEFT of i
Base case (use boxes[i] alone with its k attachments): dp[i][j][k] = dp[i+1][j][0] + (k+1)²
Teleport: for m in range(i+1, j+1) where boxes[m] == boxes[i]: dp[i][j][k] = max(dp[i][j][k], dp[i+1][m-1][0] + dp[m][j][k+1])
Remove boxes[i+1..m-1] first, then solve [m..j] with k+1 boxes of type boxes[i] attached
Preferred: Top-down memoisation — the three-dimensional state with the "teleport" structure is much cleaner with recursion + cache

Group 5: Two-Boundary (Outside-In) DP
Stage 3 — Maximum Score from Multiplication:
dp[i][j] = max score using i elements from left and j from right; ops = i+j; right index in nums = n-j
Recurrence: Take from left: dp[i+1][j] = max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j] + mult[ops]*nums[i]); Take from right: dp[i][j+1] = max(dp[i][j+1], dp[i][j] + mult[ops]*nums[n-1-j])
Base: dp[0][0] = 0; Fill: by increasing ops = i+j from 0 to m-1; answer = max(dp[i][m-i] for i in range(m+1))
Preferred: Bottom-up by diagonal (constant ops = i+j); O(m²) states
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
"minimum cost to [merge/combine/reduce] all elements into one" → Stage 1/3 split-merge interval DP — dp[i][j] + split-point loop + prefix sum cost
"remove / burst / clear a contiguous range and earn coins based on boundary values" → Stage 4 "last one standing" — k is the last removed; boundary values as cost multipliers
"minimum number of [print/paint/operations] to cover a string" → Stage 4 Strange Printer variant — same character can be covered in one pass; merge-on-match recurrence
"triangulate a polygon to minimise total weight" → Stage 4 polygon split — fix one vertex; k is third triangle vertex; dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]+cost(i,k,j)
"minimum guaranteed cost / min max strategy over range [1..n]" → Stage 2 minimax interval DP — adversarial max inside optimal min; k + max(left, right)

Structural Triggers:
Input is a 1D sequence/array; the answer on the full array depends on the answer on ALL strict sub-arrays → Interval DP; O(n³) base; dp[i][j] filled by length
Input is a polygon or circular sequence; the problem decomposes into triangles or sub-arcs → Polygon interval DP; fix one vertex to linearise
Problem involves removing groups of identical elements for bonus scoring (squared reward, exponential reward) → State-expanded interval DP with count dimension dp[i][j][k]

Constraint Triggers:
n ≤ 500 → O(n³) interval DP is 1.25×10⁸ operations; tight but feasible in C++; use bottom-up to avoid recursion overhead
n ≤ 100 with a third dimension ≤ n → O(n⁴) is 10⁸; feasible for Remove Boxes-type problems

Anti-Triggers:
"minimum cuts to partition string into palindromes" → NOT O(n³) interval DP → O(n²) two-phase DP: precompute palindrome table (interval DP), then 1D cut DP
"longest palindromic SUBSTRING (contiguous)" → NOT interval DP (O(n²)) → Manacher's algorithm O(n); interval DP is 10× slower
"minimum operations to make array non-decreasing by removing elements" → NOT interval DP → Greedy or LIS (n − LIS = min removals); interval DP structure doesn't apply to global ordering constraints
Decision Framework
Q1: Is the answer for the full input computable from answers on CONTIGUOUS SUB-INTERVALS of the input?
→ NO: Not interval DP. Check prefix DP, LIS, or greedy.
→ YES: Continue.

Q2: Which direction does the interval shrink?
→ From BOTH ENDS (palindrome-style): s[i]==s[j] gives a bonus. dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][j-1], dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]. → Palindrome / LPS / Strange Printer family.
→ By SPLITTING in the MIDDLE: dp[i][j] = over all k in [i,j-1]. → Merge / Chain / Balloon / Triangulation family.
→ From the OUTSIDE IN: take from left OR right at each step. → Maximum Score from Multiplication / Stone Game family.

Q3: What is the cost of the split at position k?
→ Fixed function of the sub-interval (e.g., sum of merged piles) → Add prefix sum array; standard O(n³).
→ Depends on BOUNDARY values of the current interval [i,j] (not internal values): → "Last one standing" framing; k is the last removed; cost = product of boundary × k value.
→ Adversarial (takes max of two sub-problems): → Minimax; dp[i][j] = min(k + max(dp[i][k-1], dp[k+1][j])).
→ Has a COUNT attached at the boundary: → Third dimension k = count; Remove Boxes / similar.

Q4: Is there a feasibility constraint on which intervals are valid?
→ YES: Add the check (e.g., (j-i)%(K-1)==0); skip infeasible.
→ NO: All intervals valid.

Q5: Is n ≤ 100 with a third dimension ≤ n? Or n ≤ 500?
→ n ≤ 500: O(n³) bottom-up feasible.
→ n ≤ 1000: Need Knuth-Yao O(n²) if cost satisfies quadrangle inequality.
→ n ≤ 10⁵: Interval DP likely TLEs; consider divide-and-conquer DP or greedy.

Stage identification: Q2 (palindrome shrink) → Stage 1, O(n²). Q2 (split) + Q3 (fixed cost) → Stage 1, O(n³). Q2 + one minimax/circular/count constraint → Stage 2, O(n³). Q3 (boundary-dependent cost) → Stage 4, O(n³). Q3 (count at boundary) → Stage 3, O(n⁴) → reduce with "teleport". Q5 (Knuth-Yao) → Stage 5, O(n²). If O(n³) TLEs: check if cost satisfies quadrangle inequality → Knuth O(n²).
When This Technique BREAKS
[Stage 1] Non-contiguous dependencies: Interval DP requires that dp[i][j] depends ONLY on sub-intervals of [i,j]. If the cost of solving [i,j] depends on elements OUTSIDE [i,j] (e.g., global max, elements at other positions), the subproblem is not self-contained. Fix: expand the state to include the external dependency as an additional dimension, or use a different DP formulation (prefix DP, tree DP).

[Stage 2] Circular arrays without breaking the circle: Applying linear interval DP directly to a circular array gives wrong answers because the first and last elements are neighbours but the interval [0,n-1] doesn't capture this adjacency. Fix: fix one element's state (e.g., fix element 0 as "not in the optimal solution" and run one pass; fix it as "in the optimal solution" and run another), then take the maximum.

[Stage 3] Wrong fill order for three-dimensional interval DP: With dp[i][j][k], filling by length j-i in the outer loop is necessary. Filling by i descending and j ascending (as in 2D) fails because dp[m][j][k+1] (used in Remove Boxes) requires m > i — a longer interval in the middle dimension, which may not yet be computed. Fix: top-down memoisation handles fill order automatically; use it when the 3D fill order is non-trivial.

[Stage 4] Applying direct "order of operations" DP instead of "last one standing": For problems where the cost of removing element k depends on its current neighbours (Burst Balloons), DP on the ORDER of operations is exponential — the state must track which elements are present. Fix: reverse to "last removed" framing; k's cost is always well-defined because boundaries are fixed.

[Stage 5] Applying Knuth-Yao without verifying the quadrangle inequality: Knuth-Yao requires that the cost function w(i,j) satisfies w(a,c) + w(b,d) ≤ w(a,d) + w(b,c) for a ≤ b ≤ c ≤ d. Not all cost functions satisfy this. Applying Knuth-Yao to an invalid cost function gives silently wrong optimal split points. Fix: verify the quadrangle inequality holds for your specific cost function before applying the optimisation.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — Merge Piles (Split-Point DP): Optimal Substructure
Claim: dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + sum(piles[i..j]) is the true minimum cost.
Optimal substructure: Consider the optimal sequence of merges for [i,j]. There exists a LAST merge that combines piles [i,k] and [k+1,j] into one. The cost of this last merge is sum(piles[i..j]) (total elements). The cost of ALL prior merges is cost([i,k]) + cost([k+1,j]).
Minimality: The optimal solution to [i,j] uses the optimal solutions to [i,k] and [k+1,j]. If either sub-solution were non-optimal, we could replace it and reduce the total — contradicting optimality. Induction follows from single piles (length 1) costing 0. □

Stage 4 — Burst Balloons: "Last to Burst" Correctness
Claim: dp[i][j] = max over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k][j] + nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j] correctly computes the max coins from bursting all balloons strictly between i and j.
Well-definedness of boundary: When k is burst LAST among balloons in (i,j), all balloons in (i,k) and (k,j) have already been burst. Therefore k's current neighbours are EXACTLY i and j. The cost nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j] is correct.
Independence of sub-problems: Since k is burst last, the operations on (i,k) and (k,j) are completely independent — no balloon in one half affects the other half's cost. The two sub-problems dp[i][k] and dp[k][j] are correctly independent.
Completeness: Every balloon in (i,j) is accounted for: each is either in (i,k) or (k,j) and will be counted as k in some sub-problem. No balloon is missed or double-counted. □

Stage 3 — Remove Boxes: State Validity
Claim: dp[i][j][k] = max points for boxes[i..j] with k extra boxes of type boxes[i] attached to the left correctly decomposes the problem.
Transition correctness (base): Remove all k+1 boxes together with boxes[i] immediately: score = (k+1)² + solve dp[i+1][j][0] independently. ✓
Transition correctness (teleport): Find m in (i,j) where boxes[m] == boxes[i]. Remove boxes in (i+1,m-1) first, then solve dp[m][j][k+1] — boxes[m] is the new "front" with k+1 attachments (including boxes[i]). This correctly models grouping non-adjacent equal boxes.
Consistency: The final group score (k+1)² is only counted once — in the base case when all k+1 boxes are removed together. The teleport case delays this removal; it never double-counts. □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 — Wrong fill order (row-major instead of by length):
# BUG: dp[i+1][j-1] not yet computed when processing row i
for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
    for j in range(i+1, n):
        dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 2 if s[i]==s[j] else ... # BUG for some (i,j)
# FIX: iterate by length
for length in range(2, n+1):
    for i in range(n - length + 1):
        j = i + length - 1; dp[i][j] = ...

Bug 2 — Balloon burst: not adding sentinel values:
# BUG: boundary multiplications use missing indices
nums = [3,1,5,8] # no sentinels — out-of-bounds at edges
# FIX: add sentinels
nums = [1] + nums + [1] # sentinel 1s at both ends
n_ext = len(nums) # n+2
dp = [[0]*n_ext for _ in range(n_ext)]

Bug 3 — Strange Printer: wrong base initialisation:
# BUG: dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] + 1 — wrong base
dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] + 1 # BUG: should reference dp[i+1][j]
# FIX: print s[i] alone; then solve rest
dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + 1

Bug 4 — Guess Number: using min instead of max for adversary:
# BUG: taking min of two sub-problems (adversary picks best for US)
dp[i][j] = min(k + min(dp[i][k-1], dp[k+1][j]) for k in range(i, j+1)) # BUG
# FIX: adversary picks worst case for us
dp[i][j] = min(k + max(dp[i][k-1], dp[k+1][j]) for k in range(i, j+1))

Bug 5 — Merge Stones: not checking feasibility before final merge:
# BUG: always adds merge cost even if infeasible
dp[i][j] += prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] # BUG: skips feasibility check
# FIX: only add when (j-i) % (K-1) == 0
if (j - i) % (K - 1) == 0:
    dp[i][j] += prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]

Bug 6 — Remove Boxes: teleport passes k instead of k+1:
# BUG: solving right sub-problem without the accumulated count
dp[i][j][k] = max(dp[i][j][k], dp[i+1][m-1][0] + dp[m][j][k]) # BUG: k not k+1
# FIX: boxes[i] is still attached — pass k+1
dp[i][j][k] = max(dp[i][j][k], dp[i+1][m-1][0] + dp[m][j][k+1])

Bug 7 — Polygon triangulation: k over [i,j] inclusive instead of (i,j) exclusive:
# BUG: k=i or k=j creates a degenerate triangle
for k in range(i, j+1): # BUG: includes k=i and k=j
# FIX: k must be strictly between i and j
for k in range(i+1, j): # exclusive of both endpoints

Bug 8 — Strange Printer: not checking s[i]==s[k] before merge transition:
# BUG: applying free extension for all k, not just when s[i]==s[k]
for k in range(i+1, j+1):
    dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i+1][k] + dp[k][j]) # BUG: always merges
# FIX: only when characters match
for k in range(i+1, j+1):
    if s[k] == s[i]:
        dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i+1][k] + dp[k][j])

Bug 9 — Knuth-Yao: wrong bounds for opt array update:
# BUG: unconstrained inner loop ignores monotonicity
for k in range(i, j): # BUG: should use Knuth bounds
# FIX: constrain by monotonicity
for k in range(opt[i][j-1], opt[i+1][j]+1): # Knuth bounds
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
[Stage 1] Filling the DP by row instead of by length: The recurrence dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][j-1], which requires both a later row AND an earlier column. Row-major fill processes dp[i+1][...] AFTER row i, so dp[i+1][j-1] may not yet be computed. Correct model: "Always fill interval DP by INCREASING LENGTH L = j-i+1. For L=1, L=2, ..., L=n. Within each length, iterate i from 0 to n-L."

[Stage 4] Applying direct "order of removal" DP to Burst Balloons: The natural DP dp[mask] = max coins is correct but exponential O(2ⁿ·n). Candidates who don't recognise the "last to burst" insight get TLE. Correct model: "When the cost of removing element k depends on its current neighbours (which change as others are removed), REVERSE the problem: decide which element is removed LAST. Its neighbours are then FIXED as the interval boundaries."

[Stage 2] Predicting the Winner: using absolute scores instead of score difference: dp[i][j] should represent the DIFFERENCE between the current player's score and the other player's score, not the absolute score. Correct model: "Score difference encodes both players' optimality in ONE value. dp[i][j] = max(a[i]−dp[i+1][j], a[j]−dp[i][j-1]) handles this automatically."

[Stage 3] Remove Boxes: forgetting the third dimension exists: Candidates attempt O(n³) interval DP and get Wrong Answer. The cost of removing a group of identical boxes is count², so the NUMBER of boxes in the current group MUST be tracked. Correct model: "Whenever a problem's cost for removing/processing an element depends on HOW MANY IDENTICAL elements are being processed simultaneously (including ones 'attached' from outside), add a count dimension to the state."

[Stage 4] Strange Printer: thinking each character requires its own turn: The baseline dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + 1 overcounts. A single printer turn can cover ANY contiguous block of the SAME character. Correct model: "The printer can start printing s[i] and EXTEND that same print to cover s[k] wherever s[k]==s[i], without using an extra turn. The dp[i+1][k] + dp[k][j] transition removes the need for a separate turn for s[k] by merging it with s[i]'s turn."

[Stage 5] Applying Knuth-Yao without verifying the quadrangle inequality: Not every cost function satisfies the monotone optimal split property. If w(i,j) does not satisfy w(a,c) + w(b,d) ≤ w(a,d) + w(b,c) for a≤b≤c≤d, the opt array is NOT monotone and Knuth-Yao gives WRONG ANSWERS silently. Correct model: "Before applying Knuth-Yao: verify that your cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality. For merge problems with cost = sum of merged elements (prefix sum), this is satisfied. For arbitrary cost functions, prove it or test it empirically on small cases."

[Stage 3] Minimum Cost Merge Stones: skipping the feasibility check: Not all intervals can be reduced to 1 pile when K>2. An interval of length L can be reduced to 1 pile iff (L-1) % (K-1) == 0. Without this check, wrong minimums result. Correct model: "For K-merge problems, FIRST check feasibility: can this interval be reduced to 1 pile? Only add the final merge cost (+= prefix[j+1]-prefix[i]) when the feasibility condition holds."
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
#StageQuestionIntended Answer Direction
12"What if we can only merge adjacent piles of exactly equal size (like merge sort)?"Only merge piles of equal size; dp[i][j] only valid when piles are equal after recursive merging; same structure but gated transitions; O(n log n) for power-of-2 sizes
22"What if the cost of merging piles is their PRODUCT instead of their sum?"Same split-point structure; cost function changes from sum to product; verify if Knuth-Yao still applies (check quadrangle inequality for product cost — it generally does not hold, so O(n³) remains)
33"Burst Balloons: now you must burst balloons in groups of exactly 3 adjacent ones. How does the DP change?""Last group to burst" framing extended: k is the middle of the last group of 3; dp[i][j] = max over k in (i+1,j-1) of dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j] + cost(k-1,k,k+1); intervals shrink by 3 at minimum
43"How do you reconstruct the optimal order of operations (not just the cost)?"Parent pointer array choice[i][j] = optimal k for each interval; backtrack recursively: choice[i][j] gives the split; recurse into [i,choice[i][j]] and [choice[i][j]+1,j]; produces a binary tree of operations
54"Can you identify a problem where interval DP over a TREE is needed?"Euler tour of a tree converts tree sub-problems to linear intervals; dp[i][j] on the Euler tour = DP on the subtree rooted at LCA(tour[i], tour[j]); used for tree path optimisation problems
64"Strange Printer with 2 printers operating simultaneously — how does the state change?"State becomes dp[i][j][k][l] = min turns for two printers covering [i,j] and [k,l]; 4D interval DP; turns taken = max of both printers' turns (parallel); non-trivial to enumerate valid split assignments
75"When does O(n³) interval DP improve to O(n²) and how?"Knuth-Yao optimisation: when cost w(i,j) satisfies the quadrangle inequality AND optimal split opt[i][j] is monotone: opt[i][j-1] ≤ opt[i][j] ≤ opt[i+1][j]; inner loop runs from opt[i][j-1] to opt[i+1][j]; amortised O(1) per cell
85"Can you do better than O(n²) for Longest Palindromic Subsequence?"O(n²) is the known lower bound for arbitrary strings using DP; Manacher's O(n) only applies to SUBSTRING (not subsequence); for LPS, O(n²) with O(n) space (two rolling rows) is optimal for general strings
9Beyond"Distribute the Burst Balloons DP across 10 machines. How?"Interval DP parallelises naturally by LENGTH: all intervals of length L are independent of each other (they only depend on length < L intervals). Assign each diagonal (fixed length) to a separate machine; synchronise between diagonals. Communication cost = O(n²) per sync round
10Beyond"Burst Balloons but n = 10⁶ balloons. Is O(n³) the best you can do?"For general balloon values: O(n³) is optimal (no known sub-cubic algorithm for general interval DP). For special structure (e.g., all values equal, sorted values): greedy or divide-and-conquer DP might give O(n log n). For n=10⁶, approximation algorithms or online/streaming variants are necessary
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):

Stage 1 — Establish both template types:
Custom Problem 4 (merge piles, split-point template) → S.No 1 (LPS, shrink-from-ends template)
Solve BOTH templates until you can write them from scratch in 5 minutes.
Verify: can you explain WHY the fill order is by increasing length?

Stage 2 — One constraint mutation at a time:
S.No 2 (min insertions) → S.No 3 (count palindromes) → S.No 6 (minimax guess) → S.No 8 (Predict the Winner) → S.No 7 (Scramble String)
After each: identify which ONE aspect of the recurrence changed from the previous problem.

Stage 3 — Third dimension and feasibility:
S.No 12 (Merge Stones feasibility) → S.No 13 (Cut a Stick) → S.No 11 (Burst Balloons) → S.No 10 (Strange Printer) → S.No 14 (Remove Boxes)
After Remove Boxes: write out the three-dimensional recurrence without hints. If you cannot, re-read the State Evolution section for Group 4.

Stage 4 — Recognition from scratch:
S.No 5 (Polygon) → S.No 19 (Multiplication Ops) → S.No 17 (Split Array) → S.No 18 (Mailboxes) → S.No 21 (Add Operators)
For each: before reading the solution, write down (a) what the interval [i,j] represents, (b) what the split point k represents, (c) what the cost of the split is.

Stage 5 — Optimisation:
S.No 24 (AtCoder Slimes, Knuth-Yao) → S.No 25 (Count Palindromic Subsequences)
For S.No 24: prove the quadrangle inequality for the merge cost before implementing.

Phase 2 — Stress-test (timed practice):
Week 1: Re-solve S.No 1, 4, 5, 6, 11 with a 25-minute timer per problem. No hints.
Week 2: Attempt S.No 14 (Remove Boxes) and S.No 18 (Allocate Mailboxes) cold in 45 minutes each.
Week 3: Attempt Codeforces CF 149D (Coloring Brackets) and AtCoder EDPC N (Slimes) cold.

Phase 3 — Transfer (unseen problems):
Find 3 interval DP problems not in this guide (LeetCode weekly/biweekly contests, Codeforces Div 2 D/E).
Before solving: apply the Decision Framework (Q1–Q5) to identify the sub-variant, then implement.
Graduation criterion: solve 2 out of 3 unseen problems correctly within 45 minutes each.
36
Partition DP
T5 Dynamic Programming 1D Partition DP 2D Partition DP 3D Partition DP Convex Hull Trick Divide & Conquer DP
Why This Topic Matters
Partition DP solves problems where a 1D sequence must be cut into contiguous segments, and the optimal answer is built by deciding WHERE to place each cut. The core invariant is: dp[i] = optimal value for the prefix a[0..i-1], computed by scanning all possible last segments a[j..i-1] and taking the best over all valid cut points j. This pattern is distinct from Interval DP (which optimises the ORDER of operations) and from Knapsack (which selects non-contiguous items) — Partition DP always produces a sequential, non-overlapping, exhaustive division of the array. It dominates top-tier interviews because: (1) the recurrence dp[i] = min/max over j < i of (dp[j] + cost(j, i)) is a 3-line template that solves a vast class of problems; (2) the cost function cost(j, i) is what varies — sometimes a sum, sometimes a max, sometimes a string match, sometimes a painting cost — and recognising which is the only skill needed; and (3) the Stage 5 optimisation (Divide & Conquer DP or Convex Hull Trick) converts O(n²) to O(n log n) and is a ICPC/IOI-essential technique.
The Mental Model
Partition DP is a cutting problem. You have a ribbon of length n. You must cut it into segments. Each segment [j..i-1] has a cost or score. You want to minimise the total cost (or maximise the total score) over all ways to cut. dp[i] = best total cost when you've made all cuts within the first i elements. For each i, you try every possible "last cut position" j and ask: "What if the last segment is [j..i-1]?"

The universal recurrence:
dp[i] = min over 0 ≤ j < i of (dp[j] + cost(j, i))

Everything in Partition DP is a variation of this formula. The ONLY thing that changes across problems is cost(j, i).

How this scales through the 5 stages:
Stage 1: cost(j,i) is trivial (sum of elements in segment, fixed penalty per cut, or dictionary word match). O(n²) or O(n·m) where m = dictionary size.
Stage 2: Same structure; ONE constraint changes — bounded number of cuts k (adds a second dimension), or the cost function requires a running max instead of a sum.
Stage 3: State expands: dp[i][k] = optimal value for first i elements using exactly k segments. The recurrence iterates over j AND k simultaneously.
Stage 4: Problem doesn't say "partition." The "sequence" must be identified: a bookcase where each shelf is a segment, a job schedule where each day-range is a segment, paint costs where colour groups are segments.
Stage 5: O(n²) is too slow. The cost function is linear in j → apply Convex Hull Trick (O(n log n)). Or the optimal cut point j* for dp[i] is monotone in i → apply Divide & Conquer DP (O(n log n)).
Core Invariant / Proof Sketch
Invariant: dp[i] = optimal value achievable for a partition of the prefix a[0..i-1] into any number of valid contiguous segments.

Proof sketch (strong induction on i):
Base case: dp[0] = 0 — empty prefix costs nothing; zero segments. ✓
Inductive step: Assume dp[j] is correct for all j < i. For prefix a[0..i-1], consider any optimal partition. There exists a last segment a[j..i-1] for some j ∈ [0,i). The rest of the partition covers a[0..j-1], contributing dp[j] (optimal by hypothesis). The last segment contributes cost(j,i). Total = dp[j] + cost(j,i).
Minimality: The optimal last-segment choice minimises over all valid j: dp[i] = min(dp[j] + cost(j,i)). Since we try ALL j, the optimal j is included. ✓
Correctness of dp[j]: Since dp[j] was computed correctly before dp[i] (j < i, left-to-right fill), the induction holds. □

Why this works for maximisation too: Replace min with max. The exchange argument is identical — any optimal partition has a "last segment"; fixing it and optimising the prefix is the same decomposition.
Sub-Variants to Master
#Sub-VariantStageComplexityKey Data StructureNew Idea
1Word Break (feasibility)1O(n·m)boolean dp array + hash set dictionarydp[i] = boolean (can s[0..i-1] be partitioned into dictionary words?); cost(j,i) = is s[j..i-1] a word; trivial cost function
2Word Break II (all solutions)2O(2ⁿ) worst casebacktracking seeded by dp feasibility tableNot just feasibility — enumerate ALL valid partitions; use dp table to prune infeasible cuts before backtracking
3Palindrome Partitioning II (min cuts)2O(n²)boolean palindrome table + 1D cut dpcost(j,i) = 1 if s[j..i-1] is palindrome else ∞; two-phase: precompute palindrome table, then 1D partition DP
4Partition Array for Maximum Sum1O(n·k)1D dp with running max during j-scanScore = segment_length × segment_max; running max tracked as j decreases; window bounded by k
5Filling Bookcase Shelves4O(n·W)1D dp with width accumulator + running maxStage 4 disguise — books on shelves; "segment" = one shelf; cost = shelf height (max height in segment); width constraint gates valid j
6Largest Sum of Averages3O(n²k)2D dp[i][k] + prefix sumsAdds k as second dimension; dp[i][k] = max over j of dp[j][k-1] + avg(a[j..i-1]); prefix sums for O(1) average computation
7Split Array Largest Sum3O(n²k) or O(n log sum)2D dp[i][k] OR binary search + greedyObjective is "minimise the maximum" (not minimise the sum); teaches minimax partition DP
8Minimum Difficulty of a Job Schedule3O(n²d)2D dp[i][d] + sliding maxThree dimensions: position, day-count, and the cost is MAX of segment (not sum); sliding window max per segment
9Paint House III3O(n·k·m²)3D dp[i][k][c]Third dimension = color of last painted house; neighborhood count increments when color changes; three-state expansion
10Decode Ways1O(n)1D dp[i]Counting partitions (not optimising); cost is binary (valid code or not); teaches COUNT variant
11Word Break Count2O(n·m)1D dp[i] + hash setCount valid word-break partitions using dictionary; hash set for O(1) word check
12Maximum Sum After K Partitions3O(nk)2D dp[i][k]Exactly k parts (not at most); initialise with -∞ for unreachable; boundary condition j ≥ k-1
13Optimal partition with Convex Hull Trick5O(n log n)CHT / Li Chao treeWhen cost(j,i) = a[j]*i + b[j] (linear in j), CHT reduces O(n²) inner loop to O(log n)
14Divide & Conquer DP5O(n log n)Recursive D&C with opt boundsWhen j*(i) ≤ j*(i+1) (monotone optimal split), D&C halves the search space at each recursion level; O(n log n) total
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Word Break (feasibility)O(n·m)O(n)Baseline: dp[i] boolean, cost = dictionary lookup
Palindrome Partitioning IIO(n²)O(n²)Cost = palindrome check; two-phase precomputation
Partition Array for Max SumO(n·k)O(n)Bounded window; running max tracked during j-scan
Decode WaysO(n)O(n) → O(1)Cost is O(1) check; window bounded by 2 → O(n)
Largest Sum of AveragesO(n²k)O(nk)Second dimension k; prefix sums for avg in O(1)
Split Array Largest SumO(n²k)O(nk)Minimax objective; binary search + greedy alternative
Minimum Job DifficultyO(n²d)O(nd)Cost = max (not sum); third dimension = days
Paint House IIIO(n·k·m²)O(k·m)Color dimension added; neighborhood counter
CHT-optimised partitionO(n log n)O(n)Linear cost function → CHT eliminates O(n) inner scan
D&C DP partitionO(n log n)O(n log n)Monotone opt → divide at midpoint recursively
Mastery Problem Set
S.NoLayerStageSourceProblemDifficultyPremium?Sub-VariantNew Idea AddedKey Insight
111LeetCode139 – Word BreakMediumFeasibility partition (dictionary words)THE baseline: dp[i] = can s[0..i-1] be segmented into dictionary words; dp[i] = any(dp[j] and s[j:i] in word_set for j < i)Base: dp[0]=True; answer: dp[n]; O(n²) with hash set for O(1) word check; establishes the dp[i] = f(dp[j] + cost(j,i)) template
211LeetCode91 – Decode WaysMediumCount partition (digit codes)Change: COUNT valid decodings (not boolean); dp[i] += dp[j] for each valid s[j:i]; window = 1 or 2 chars onlyAdds COUNT semantics over S.No 1; O(n) because window bounded to 2; dp[0]=1 (empty string = 1 way)
311LeetCode2707 – Extra Characters in a StringMediumMinimise waste partition (unmatched characters)dp[i] = min(dp[i-1]+1, min(dp[j] for j where s[j:i] in dict)); unmatched chars = cost of skippingAdds MINIMISE objective over boolean; cost of not using a segment = 1 per char; teaches that "skip" is always a valid O(1) transition
412LeetCode132 – Palindrome Partitioning IIHardMin-cuts partition with palindrome costTwo-phase: (1) is_palin[i][j] table O(n²); (2) dp[i] = min(dp[j] + 1 for j < i if is_palin[j][i-1]); ∞ cost if not palindromeHard: the two-phase structure is non-obvious; the palindrome precomputation IS interval DP; the cut DP on top is 1D partition DP; conflating them into O(n³) is the classic mistake
512LeetCode2522 – Partition String into Substrings with Values at Most KMediumGreedy/DP partition with numeric value constraintEach segment's integer value ≤ K; dp[i] = min(dp[j]+1 for j where int(s[j:i]) ≤ K); leading-zero and overflow handlingAdds a VALUE constraint on segment content (not just length); teaches that digit-string partitions require careful integer conversion
612LeetCode1278 – Palindrome Partitioning IIIHardMin-cost partition into exactly k palindromesdp[i][k] = min changes to partition s[0..i-1] into k palindromes; cost(j,i) = min char changes to make s[j..i-1] a palindromeHard: two dimensions (position, parts); cost function requires O(n²) precomputation of palindrome-ification cost; the "changes to make palindrome" cost is non-trivial
722LeetCode1043 – Partition Array for Maximum SumMediumMax-score partition with bounded window and segment maxdp[i] = max(dp[i-k:i]…) with k window; running max tracked during j-scan: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + (i-j)*max(a[j:i])) for j from i-1 down to i-kAdds BOUNDED WINDOW (max segment length k) and score = length × max; running max maintained as j decreases avoids O(k) recompute
822LeetCode2547 – Minimum Cost to Split Array ✅Hard✅Partition with frequency-based cost per segmentdp[i] = min(dp[j] + cost(j,i)) where cost(j,i) = (i-j) + number of chars with frequency ≥ 2 in s[j..i-1]; requires frequency tracking per windowHard: cost computation per segment is O(n) naïvely; sliding frequency map during j-scan avoids quadratic recomputation
922LeetCode2055 – Plates Between Candles (as DP)MediumPrefix sum query partition (count elements in range)Partition DP where cost(j,i) uses prefix sums for O(1) range queries; teaches prefix sum integration into cost functionAdds PREFIX SUM as the cost computation tool; any cost that's a range sum/count can be precomputed as prefix array for O(1) lookup
1023LeetCode1335 – Minimum Difficulty of a Job ScheduleHard2D partition DP (position × day count); cost = segment maxdp[i][d] = min difficulty for jobs[0..i-1] in d days; dp[i][d] = min(dp[j][d-1] + max(jobs[j..i-1])) for j ≥ d-1Hard: second dimension = day count; cost = MAX of segment (need running max during j-scan); boundary: j ≥ d-1 ensures enough jobs for prior days
1123LeetCode813 – Largest Sum of AveragesMedium2D partition DP (position × group count); cost = segment averagedp[i][k] = max sum of k group averages for a[0..i-1]; dp[i][k] = max(dp[j][k-1] + sum(a[j:i])/(i-j)); prefix sums for O(1) sumSame 2D structure as S.No 10; cost changes from MAX to AVERAGE; teaches that AVG = sum/length, computed O(1) with prefix sums
1223LeetCode410 – Split Array Largest SumHard2D minimax partition DP; or binary search + greedydp[i][k] = min of max subarray sum for a[0..i] in k parts; OR binary search on answer + greedy feasibility checkHard: two approaches — pure DP is O(n²k); binary search + greedy is O(n log(sum)); teaches that minimax problems always have a binary-search dual
1323LeetCode1444 – Number of Ways to Cut a PizzaHard2D grid partition DP (row/col cuts); count valid cutsdp[i][j][k] = ways to cut pizza[i:][j:] into k pieces each containing ≥ 1 apple; 2D prefix sums for O(1) apple count per sub-gridHard: partition DP on a 2D grid (not 1D sequence); state is (row, col, remaining cuts); 2D prefix sums are the key data structure
⚡CLIFF WARNING — Layer 2 → Layer 3: Next problems don't present an array to partition directly. The "sequence" is either disguised (books on shelves, paint houses, jobs with schedules) or requires identifying WHAT the segments represent. Before coding: (1) name what a "segment" is, (2) name what cost(j,i) is, (3) check if a second dimension (count, color, type) is needed.
1434LeetCode1105 – Filling Bookcase ShelvesMediumStage 4: Segment = one shelf; cost = shelf height (running max)dp[i] = min total height placing books[0..i-1]; scan j backward while sum(widths[j:i]) ≤ W; cost = max(heights[j:i])Doesn't say "partition" — must recognise shelves as segments; width constraint gates valid j; running max during j-scan is identical to S.No 10 but in 1D
1534LeetCode1547 – Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick (Partition framing)MediumSort cuts + partition DP; cost = current stick lengthSort cuts; add 0 and n as boundaries; dp[i] = min cost making all cuts in cuts[0..i]; cost of cut = distance to nearest boundariesRevisits the Interval DP problem (S.No 13 in Interval guide) as a PARTITION problem; shows how sorted cuts convert interval DP → partition DP
1634LeetCode1531 – String Compression II ✅Hard✅3D partition DP: (position, deletions_used, last_char)dp[i][k] = min length compressing s[0..i-1] with k deletions; each "segment" = run of same character; deletion reduces run lengthHard: the RLE compression cost per segment is non-trivial (1 char for runs of 1; 1+digits for longer runs); three nested dimensions with carefully ordered transitions
1734LeetCode1000 – Minimum Cost to Merge Stones (Partition view)HardPartition into K groups iteratively; feasibility = (n-1)%(K-1)==0Cross-reference with Interval DP S.No 12; here framed as: repeatedly partition array into groups of K; teaches that some problems are solvable as EITHER interval DP OR partition DPHard: the "groups of K" constraint means not all partition points are valid; feasibility check is the same (j-i) % (K-1) == 0 as in Interval DP
1834LeetCode265 – Paint House II ✅Hard✅Partition with color-state tracking; O(n·k) with min/2nd-min optimisationdp[i][c] = min cost painting houses 0..i with house i in color c; track global min and 2nd-min to get O(n·k) instead of O(n·k²)Hard: naïve O(n·k²) — for each house and color, scan all previous colors; min/2nd-min trick reduces to O(n·k); the "same color as min?" check is the key
1934LeetCode1473 – Paint House IIIHard3D partition DP: (position, neighborhoods, color)dp[i][k][c] = min cost painting houses[0..i] using k neighborhoods, last color c; pre-painted houses constrain transitionsHard: three dimensions; pre-painted houses add conditional transitions; k increments only on color change; initialising pre-painted cells correctly is tricky
2034LeetCode140 – Word Break IIHardEnumerate ALL valid partitions; DFS with dp-pruned cut positionsPhase 1: dp[i] boolean feasibility (S.No 1); Phase 2: DFS from n to 0 using only valid cut positions from dpHard: worst case O(n·2ⁿ) output; without dp pruning, DFS is exponential on all prefixes; dp feasibility converts exponential branching to polynomial
2144LeetCode2911 – Minimum Changes to Make K Semi-palindromes ✅Hard✅2D partition DP with divisor-based palindrome costdp[i][k] = min changes for s[0..i-1] into k semi-palindromes; cost(j,i) = min changes using best divisor d of (i-j)Hard: semi-palindrome requires checking all divisors of segment length; non-obvious cost function; cost precomputation O(n²·d(n)) where d(n) = number of divisors
2245LeetCode2478 – Number of Beautiful PartitionsHardCount partitions with prime-start and non-prime-end constraints; modular arithmeticdp[i] = number of valid k-part partitions ending at i; segment valid iff starts with prime digit and ends with non-prime digit; mod 10⁹+7Hard: counting under dual segment-validity constraints; the "starts prime, ends non-prime" condition must be checked at both boundaries of each segment
2345LeetCode3003 – Maximize the Number of Partitions After Operations ✅Hard✅Bitmask + partition DP with one character change alloweddp[i][mask][changed] = max partitions in s[0..i] where current segment uses character-set mask and changed = 0/1 for whether the one allowed change was usedHard: bitmask encodes which characters are in the current segment; the "one change" flag adds a binary dimension; 3-state expansion on top of partition DP
2445CodeforcesCF 1968F – Problem F (Divide and Conquer DP)HardD&C DP: monotone optimal split point → O(n log n)Same partition DP recurrence; prove that opt(i) ≤ opt(i+1); split array at midpoint; recurse left/right half with constrained j rangesHard: proving opt monotonicity requires the quadrangle inequality; without the proof, the D&C structure is not justified; O(n log n) vs O(n²)
2545AtCoderEDPC L – Deque (Partition framing)HardPartition DP with Convex Hull Trick optimisationRecurrence dp[i] = min(dp[j] + cost(j,i)) where cost is linear in j: cost(j,i) = a_j * i + b_j; CHT/Li Chao tree for O(log n) per queryHard: recognising that cost(j,i) is linear in j (enabling CHT) is the non-obvious Stage 4 step; CHT implementation is Stage 5
26 ⚠️TRAP — 416 – Partition Equal Subset Sum: Non-contiguous subset, NOT partition DP. Select (non-contiguous) elements summing to total/2; no ordering constraint → 0/1 Knapsack dp[s]. NOT partition DP: elements don't need to form contiguous segments; using partition DP gives wrong answer for non-adjacent selections.
27 ⚠️TRAP — 312 – Burst Balloons: Looks like partition (split array), is Interval DP ("last to burst"). Splitting at k in partition DP means k separates two independent halves; in Burst Balloons, the cost of k DEPENDS on both halves' boundaries → Interval DP. NOT partition DP: the cross-segment dependency (k's cost involves neighbours from both halves) violates partition DP's independence assumption.
28 ⚠️TRAP — 45 – Jump Game II: Looks like min-segments partition, is greedy. Min jumps to reach end; dp works but O(n²); greedy always jumps to the farthest reachable position: O(n). NOT partition DP for this scale: greedy with a running max-reach is O(n); partition DP would be O(n²) and unnecessarily complex.
29 ⚠️TRAP — 3117 – Minimum Sum of Values by Dividing Array: Looks like min-cost partition, requires bitwise AND + monotone property. dp[i][j] = min sum partitioning a[0..i] into j segments where each segment's AND = query[j-1]; binary search + D&C because opt is monotone. Partition DP is correct but O(n²·m) TLEs; requires careful opt-monotonicity proof + D&C; the AND-equality constraint makes cost verification non-trivial.
State Evolution Across Stages
Group 1: 1D Partition DP (Single Dimension)

Stage 1 — Word Break / Decode Ways:
