dp[i] = boolean (can prefix of length i be partitioned?) or integer (count/cost for prefix of length i)
Recurrence: dp[i] = OR(dp[j] AND valid(j,i)) for boolean; dp[i] = SUM(dp[j] for valid j) for count; dp[i] = MIN(dp[j] + cost(j,i)) for optimisation
Base: dp[0] = True (or 1 for count, 0 for min-cost)
Fill: left to right; dp[i] uses all dp[j] for j < i
Preferred: Bottom-up left-to-right — memoised top-down also works; bottom-up avoids recursion overhead for n ≤ 10⁵
Space: O(n) — only the dp array needed

Stage 1 — Partition Array for Maximum Sum:
dp[i] = max sum partitioning a[0..i-1] with each segment length ≤ k
Recurrence: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + (i-j) * max(a[j..i-1]) for j in range(max(0,i-k), i))
Running max: as j decreases from i-1 to i-k, maintain cur_max = max(cur_max, a[j]); avoids O(k) recompute per j
Base: dp[0] = 0
Fill: left to right; inner loop runs at most k iterations → O(n·k)
Group 2: 2D Partition DP (Position × Count)

Stage 3 — Largest Sum of Averages / Min Job Difficulty / Split Array:
dp[i][k] = optimal value for first i elements partitioned into exactly k segments
Recurrence: dp[i][k] = opt(dp[j][k-1] + cost(j, i) for j in range(k-1, i))
j ≥ k-1 ensures prior k-1 segments had at least one element each
Base: dp[0][0] = 0; dp[i][k] = +∞ (or -∞) for k > i (not enough elements)
Cost functions:
  Sum of averages: cost(j,i) = (prefix[i] - prefix[j]) / (i-j) — O(1) with prefix sums
  Job difficulty: cost(j,i) = max(jobs[j..i-1]) — O(1) with sliding max during j-scan
  Split array: cost(j,i) = max(a[j..i-1]) — same as job difficulty
Preferred: Bottom-up, outer loop over k (1 to K), inner loop over i (k to n), innermost over j — fill order guarantees dp[j][k-1] is available when computing dp[i][k]
Optimisation (Stage 5): If cost(j,i) satisfies the concave SMAWK condition, D&C DP applies → O(n log n)
Group 3: 3D Partition DP (Position × Count × Mode)

Stage 3/4 — Paint House III / String Compression / Word Break with Budget:
dp[i][k][c] = optimal value for first i elements, k "groups/segments used", last-segment type/color c
Recurrence: dp[i][k][c] = opt(dp[j][k-1][c'] + cost(j,i,c) for all j < i and c' compatible with c)
Base: depends on problem; typically dp[0][0][*] = 0; all others +∞
Key: the third dimension c GATES transitions — same color c as previous → don't increment k; different → increment k
Preferred: Top-down memoisation — three-dimensional state with conditional third-dimension transitions is error-prone in bottom-up; memoisation handles order automatically
Group 4: Optimised Partition DP (Stage 5)

Stage 5a — Convex Hull Trick:
Applicable when: dp[i] = min over j of (dp[j] + a[j]*i + b[j]) — cost is a linear function of the query variable i
View each j as a line y = a[j]*x + b[j] where y = dp[j] + cost; query at x=i
Maintain lower convex hull of lines; binary search for minimum → O(log n) per query
Total: O(n log n); or O(n) if queries are monotone (deque-based CHT)
Li Chao tree: alternative for non-monotone j or i; O(n log V) where V = value range

Stage 5b — Divide & Conquer DP:
Applicable when: opt[i] (optimal j for dp[i]) is monotone: opt[i] ≤ opt[i+1]
Condition: cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality: cost(a,c) + cost(b,d) ≤ cost(a,d) + cost(b,c) for a ≤ b ≤ c ≤ d
Algorithm:
solve(lo, hi, opt_lo, opt_hi):
  mid = (lo + hi) // 2
  scan j from opt_lo to min(opt_hi, mid-1); find opt_mid
  dp[mid] = best value found
  solve(lo, mid-1, opt_lo, opt_mid)
  solve(mid+1, hi, opt_mid, opt_hi)
Total: O(n log n) — each level of recursion scans O(n) j values total; O(log n) levels
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
"partition / split / divide array into [k] contiguous parts" → Stage 3 2D partition DP — dp[i][k]; cost = function of last segment
"minimum number of [cuts / splits] to make each segment satisfy property P" → Stage 1/2 1D partition DP — dp[i] = min cuts; cost(j,i) = ∞ if P not satisfied
"fill shelves / schedule jobs / assign tasks to days" → Stage 4 disguised partition DP — identify what a "segment" is; cost = max or sum per segment
"word break / can string be decoded as [words / codes]" → Stage 1 feasibility partition DP — dp[i] boolean; hash set for O(1) segment validity
"maximum score from partitioning where score = f(segment)" → Stage 1/2 optimisation partition DP — dp[i] = max(dp[j] + f(s[j:i]))

Structural Triggers:
Input is a 1D sequence; output is a scalar (min cost, max score, count); answer requires dividing the sequence into non-overlapping contiguous parts → Partition DP; O(n²) base; dp[i] with inner j-loop
Problem has an explicit "k parts" or "d days" constraint — answer depends on BOTH position AND count → 2D partition DP dp[i][k]; O(n²k)
Third dimension appears when the "type" or "color" of the last segment affects future segment costs → 3D dp[i][k][c]

Constraint Triggers:
n ≤ 1000 and k ≤ 100 → O(n²k) feasible; standard 2D partition DP
n ≤ 10⁵ with O(n²) TLE → check if cost function is linear in j (CHT) or if opt is monotone (D&C)

Anti-Triggers:
"partition array into two subsets with minimum difference" → NOT contiguous partition DP → 0/1 Knapsack dp[s]; elements need not be contiguous
"burst balloons / remove elements with cost depending on neighbours" → NOT partition DP → Interval DP ("last one standing"); cost has cross-segment dependencies
"minimum cost to merge non-adjacent segments" → NOT 1D partition DP → Interval DP (arbitrary split order); the "non-adjacent" or "arbitrary order" property breaks partition DP's left-to-right structure
Decision Framework
Q1: Does the problem ask for optimal value (min/max/count) of a partition of a 1D sequence into CONTIGUOUS, NON-OVERLAPPING segments covering all elements?
  → NO: Not partition DP. Check knapsack (non-contiguous), interval DP (arbitrary order), or greedy.
  → YES: Continue.

Q2: What does cost(j, i) depend on?
  → Segment content (sum, max, avg, dict lookup, palindrome check): → Standard partition DP. O(n²). Go to Q3.
  → Cross-segment interaction (cost of segment [j,i] depends on what's in [0,j-1] or [i,n-1]): → NOT partition DP. Use interval DP or state machine DP.

Q3: Is there a BOUND on the number of segments (exactly k or at most k)?
  → YES: Add k as second dimension. dp[i][k]. O(n²k). Go to Q4.
  → NO: 1D dp[i]. O(n²). Go to Q5.

Q4: Does the cost or transition also depend on the TYPE/COLOR/MODE of the last segment?
  → YES: Third dimension. dp[i][k][c]. O(n²·k·C). Top-down recommended.
  → NO: Standard 2D. dp[i][k].

Q5: Is n > 5000 and O(n²) TLEs?
  → Is cost(j,i) LINEAR in j? → YES: Convex Hull Trick. O(n log n).
  → Is the optimal j* monotone (opt[i] ≤ opt[i+1])? → YES: Divide & Conquer DP. O(n log n).
  → Is the window of valid j fixed (e.g., |j-i| ≤ k)? → YES: Sliding window max/deque DP. O(n·k) → O(n).
  → None apply: O(n²) may be unavoidable. Check if n ≤ 5000.

Stage identification:
Q2 (simple cost) + Q3 (no k) → Stage 1. O(n²) or better.
Q2 + Q3 (k bound) → Stage 3. O(n²k).
Q4 (color/type) → Stage 3. O(n²·k·C). Use top-down.
Problem disguised (shelves/jobs/paint) → Stage 4. Same complexity.
Q5 (CHT or D&C) → Stage 5. O(n log n).
If O(n²) TLEs: check linearity of cost (CHT) or opt monotonicity (D&C).
When This Technique BREAKS
[Stage 1] Cost function has cross-segment dependencies: Partition DP requires that cost(j,i) depends ONLY on the segment a[j..i-1], never on segments outside it. If the cost of a segment changes based on what's already been computed, the subproblem is not self-contained. Fix: expand the state to include the relevant property of the adjacent segment as a dimension (making it a state machine DP or 3D partition DP).

[Stage 2] Counting ALL partitions without dp pruning (Word Break II): Enumerating all valid partitions by backtracking without first building the feasibility dp table leads to exponential time on strings where many prefixes are reachable but the full string is not. Fix: always run the O(n²) boolean dp pass first; only DFS from positions where dp[pos] = True.

[Stage 3] Wrong boundary for the j-loop in k-segment DP: In dp[i][k], the inner j-loop must start at j = k-1 (not j = 0). Starting at j = 0 allows segments where previous k-1 groups don't have enough elements — dp[j][k-1] would be -∞ or invalid. Additionally, dp[i][k] is only valid for i ≥ k (need at least k elements for k segments). Fix: always enforce j ≥ k-1 in the inner loop and initialise dp[i][k] = +∞ for i < k.

[Stage 5] Applying CHT when cost is not linear in j: CHT requires cost(j,i) = a(j)*i + b(j) — strictly linear in the query variable i. If the cost involves max/min of segment (not sum), it is NOT linear → CHT doesn't apply. Use D&C DP if opt is monotone, or accept O(n²). Silently applying CHT to a non-linear cost gives wrong answers.

[Stage 5] D&C DP when opt is NOT monotone: If the cost function does not satisfy the quadrangle inequality, opt[i] is not monotone and D&C DP gives wrong answers. Fix: always verify opt monotonicity on small cases before applying D&C DP. If in doubt, verify the quadrangle inequality algebraically.

[Stage 4] Treating "Burst Balloons" / "Remove Boxes" as partition DP: These problems have costs that depend on what's adjacent to the current segment — which changes as elements are removed. Partition DP assumes each segment's cost is fixed once the endpoints are known. Fix: use Interval DP with the "last one standing" framing.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — Word Break: Optimal Substructure
Claim: dp[i] = OR(dp[j] AND s[j:i] in dictionary for j in 0..i-1) correctly determines if s[0..i-1] can be word-broken.
Optimal substructure: If s[0..i-1] can be word-broken, there exists a LAST word s[j..i-1] in the dictionary, and s[0..j-1] can also be word-broken (dp[j] = True).
Completeness: We try ALL j ∈ [0,i); therefore the correct last-word split point is always considered.
Base case: dp[0] = True — empty string is trivially word-breakable (zero words).
Induction: dp[i] uses only dp[j] for j < i (computed before i in left-to-right order). ✓ □

Stage 3 — Largest Sum of Averages: Correctness of 2D DP
Claim: dp[i][k] = max(dp[j][k-1] + sum(a[j..i-1])/(i-j) for j in k-1..i-1) = max sum of k group averages for a[0..i-1].
Invariant: dp[i][k] = max sum of k group averages for ANY k-partition of a[0..i-1].
Base: dp[i][1] = avg(a[0..i-1]) = average of the single group. ✓
Induction on k: For k groups, the LAST group is a[j..i-1] for some j. Its average is sum(a[j..i-1])/(i-j). The first k-1 groups cover a[0..j-1], contributing dp[j][k-1] (optimal by induction). Since we maximise over all valid j (j ≥ k-1 ensures k-1 groups have ≥ 1 element each), we find the true optimum. ✓
Prefix sums: sum(a[j..i-1]) = prefix[i] - prefix[j] in O(1); does not affect correctness, only efficiency. □

Stage 5 — Divide & Conquer DP: Correctness of Pruned Search
Claim: If opt[lo] ≤ opt[mid] ≤ opt[hi] (monotone optimal split), the D&C search finds the true optimal j for every i.
Monotonicity assumption: The cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality, ensuring opt[i] is non-decreasing.
D&C invariant: When solve(lo, hi, opt_lo, opt_hi) is called, the true opt[i] for any i ∈ [lo,hi] lies in [opt_lo, opt_hi]. We scan only this range for i = mid.
Left recursion: For i ∈ [lo, mid-1], opt[i] ≤ opt[mid], so the true opt lies in [opt_lo, opt_mid]. ✓
Right recursion: For i ∈ [mid+1, hi], opt[i] ≥ opt[mid], so the true opt lies in [opt_mid, opt_hi]. ✓
Work per level: At each recursion level, the sum of opt_hi - opt_lo across all calls = O(n). With O(log n) levels, total work = O(n log n). □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Bug 1 — Word Break: using s[i:j] instead of s[j:i]
# BUG: slice indices reversed
if dp[j] and s[i:j] in word_set: # BUG: i > j always here
# FIX:
if dp[j] and s[j:i] in word_set: # segment from j to i (exclusive)

Bug 2 — Decode Ways: not handling leading zero / single-digit zero
# BUG omission: "0" should NOT be decoded as a single digit
if s[i-1] != '0': # missing this guard
dp[i] += dp[i-1]
# FIX: always add this zero-guard for single-digit check

Bug 3 — Palindrome Partitioning II: initialising dp[i] = i instead of i-1
# BUG: worst case is i-1 cuts (each char separate), not i cuts
dp = list(range(n+1)) # dp[i] = i: BUG, off by 1
# FIX:
dp[0] = -1 # sentinel so dp[0]+1 = 0 cuts for valid palindrome at 0
dp = [i-1 for i in range(n+1)]

Bug 4 — Partition Array for Max Sum: not resetting running max per i
# BUG: cur_max carries over from previous i
for i in range(1, n+1):
for j in range(max(0, i-k), i):
cur_max = max(cur_max, a[j]) # BUG: not reset
# FIX: reset cur_max inside outer loop
for i in range(1, n+1):
cur_max = 0
for j in range(i-1, max(-1, i-k-1), -1):
cur_max = max(cur_max, a[j])
dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + (i-j) * cur_max)

Bug 5 — 2D DP: j-loop starting at 0 instead of k-1
# BUG: allows k-1 prior groups over 0 elements (infeasible)
for j in range(0, i): # BUG
# FIX:
for j in range(k-1, i): # j must be at least k-1

Bug 6 — Min Job Difficulty: not checking i ≥ d
# BUG: dp[i][d] computed even when i < d
for d in range(1, D+1):
for i in range(1, n+1): ... # BUG: no check for i >= d
# FIX:
for d in range(1, D+1):
for i in range(d, n+1):
for j in range(d-1, i): ...

Bug 7 — Paint House III: overwriting pre-painted houses
# BUG: ignores houses[i-1] != 0 constraint
for c in range(1, m+1):
dp[i][k][c] = min(...) # BUG
# FIX: if house is pre-painted, only update its actual color
if houses[i-1] != 0:
c = houses[i-1]
dp[i][...][c] = ...

Bug 8 — Filling Bookcase Shelves: missing width constraint check
# BUG: no width constraint
for j in range(i-1, -1, -1):
cur_max = max(cur_max, height[j])
dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[j] + cur_max) # BUG
# FIX: break when width exceeded
for j in range(i-1, -1, -1):
total_width += width[j]
if total_width > shelf_width: break
cur_max = max(cur_max, height[j])
dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[j] + cur_max)

Bug 9 — D&C DP: processing hi half before computing opt_mid
# BUG: left half uses stale opt before opt_mid is found
def solve(lo, hi, opt_lo, opt_hi):
mid = (lo + hi) // 2
solve(lo, mid-1, opt_lo, mid) # BUG: opt_mid not computed yet
# FIX: compute opt_mid FIRST
def solve(lo, hi, opt_lo, opt_hi):
mid = (lo + hi) // 2
opt_mid = find_opt(mid, opt_lo, min(opt_hi, mid))
solve(lo, mid-1, opt_lo, opt_mid)
solve(mid+1, hi, opt_mid, opt_hi)

Bug 10 — CHT: inserting lines non-monotonically into deque-CHT
# BUG: deque-CHT requires slopes to be monotone; arbitrary order breaks hull
for j in range(n):
hull.add_line(slope=a[j], intercept=dp[j]) # BUG if a[j] non-monotone
# FIX: use Li Chao tree for arbitrary insertion order
tree = LiChaoTree(x_range)
for j in range(n):
tree.add_line(a[j], dp[j])
dp[i] = tree.query(i)
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
[Stage 1] Confusing partition DP with knapsack DP: "Partition array into two equal-sum subsets" (LC 416) looks like a partition problem but is a 0/1 Knapsack because the subsets need not be contiguous. Partition DP ONLY applies when every element must belong to exactly one contiguous segment. The word "partition" in a problem title does NOT guarantee partition DP — check for contiguity.

[Stage 2] Palindrome Partitioning II: building O(n³) DP instead of O(n²) two-phase: Candidates attempt dp[i][j][k] (start, end, cuts) which is O(n³) in space and O(n⁴) in time. The correct model separates the palindrome table (O(n²) interval DP) from the cut DP (O(n) partition DP). The cut DP is 1D: dp[i] = min cuts for prefix of length i. NEVER combine these into a single 2D or 3D table.

[Stage 3] 2D partition DP: wrong initialisation of base cases: dp[0][0] = 0 and all other dp[0][k] = +∞ (or dp[i][0] = +∞ for i > 0). A common mistake is initialising dp[i][1] = cost(0,i) manually instead of letting the recurrence handle it — this creates inconsistencies when k=1 is computed both manually and through the recurrence, sometimes double-counting or missing valid partitions.

[Stage 5] Applying D&C DP without verifying opt monotonicity: The D&C DP prunes the j-search range for each i. If opt[i] is NOT monotone, this pruning is INCORRECT — it will skip the true optimal j for some i and return wrong answers. Fix: verify the quadrangle inequality on paper (or empirically compute opt[] for small inputs and check monotonicity) before applying D&C DP.

[Stage 3] Paint House III: incrementing neighborhood count incorrectly: The neighborhood count k increments when the current house's color DIFFERS from the previous house's color. A common mistake is incrementing k when the same color is used again (off-by-one in the "same color" check). The invariant is: k = number of maximal monochromatic runs so far; it increments exactly when a color TRANSITION happens.

[Stage 1] Word Break: O(n³) from naïve string hashing: s[j:i] in word_set is O(i-j) (string hashing). The total cost is O(n² · avg_word_length). For n=10⁴ and avg_word_length=10, this is 10⁹ operations. Fix: use a Trie to match words starting from each position in one O(n·max_word_length) pass.

[Stage 5] CHT: using it when the cost function has MAX/MIN (not SUM/PRODUCT): CHT requires cost(j,i) = m(j)*i + b(j) — a linear function of i. Costs like max(a[j..i-1]) are NOT linear in i (they depend on all elements between j and i). Applying CHT to max-based costs gives wrong answers. Fix: for max-based costs, check if D&C DP applies (via sliding window for monotone opt) or accept O(n²).
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
#StageQuestionIntended Answer Direction
12"Word Break: what if a word can be used at most c times?"Add count[word] tracking; during the j-scan, check usage[s[j:i]] < c; this adds a usage dictionary alongside dp; O(n·m·c)
22"Split Array Largest Sum: now minimise the SUM of squares of subarray sums (not max)."Same 2D DP structure dp[i][k]; cost = sum(a[j..i-1])²; cost is a quadratic function of j — CHT applies since (Σa)² expands to 2*prefix[j]*prefix[i] - prefix[j]² which is linear in i; O(nk log n)
33"Minimum Job Difficulty: what if you must complete the jobs in ANY order (not fixed order)?"Assign jobs optimally to days by sorting; on each day, assign the hardest remaining jobs; greedy: sort jobs DESC; each day takes the top ⌈n/d⌉ or ⌊n/d⌋ jobs; this changes the problem from partition DP to greedy scheduling
43"Recover the actual partition (not just the optimal value) from the dp table."Parent pointer array par[i] = j at which dp[i] was achieved; backtrack from n: cut at par[n], then par[par[n]], etc.; for 2D dp[i][k], par[i][k] = optimal j; O(n) reconstruction
54"Filling Bookcase Shelves: books can be placed in any order (not fixed)."Sort books by height descending; greedy fills each shelf with the tallest remaining books; or DP with sorted input — but sorting changes the problem from "contiguous partition" to "assignment"; may require a different DP formulation
64"Palindrome Partitioning II: palindromes must have odd length only."Only change the palindrome precomputation: is_palin[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) and is_palin[i+1][j-1] and (j-i+1) is odd; the 1D cut DP is unchanged; teaches that cost function modification is isolated from the DP structure
75"Split Array into k parts minimise max sum, n = 10⁶. How?"Binary search on answer + greedy feasibility check: O(n log(sum)); the partition DP is O(n²k) which TLEs; binary search: "can we partition into k parts with max sum ≤ mid?" answered greedily in O(n); total O(n log(sum))
85"Prove when the Divide & Conquer DP applies to Largest Sum of Averages."Show that cost(j,i) = avg(a[j..i-1]) satisfies the concave version of the quadrangle inequality (opt is monotone for maximisation variant); increasing j* with increasing i means that adding more elements always prefers a later start for the last group
9Beyond"Partition DP across a distributed system — array is sharded across 10 machines."Compute prefix sums on each shard locally; communicate only prefix sums to coordinator; coordinator runs partition DP with O(1) cost queries; for D&C DP: each recursion level's work is O(n) — parallelise each level; O(n log n / machines) with synchronisation
10Beyond"Streaming partition DP: elements arrive online; answer must be updated after each element."For unbounded k (no segment-count limit): Convex Hull Trick supports online line insertion (Li Chao tree); dp[i] updated in O(log n) per new element; for bounded k: requires a functional DP approach — maintain k layers of Li Chao trees
3-Phase Mastery Plan
Phase 1 — Solve (strictly by Stage across all sub-variants):

Stage 1 — Establish the template in all three flavours:
S.No 1 (Word Break — boolean) → S.No 2 (Decode Ways — count) → S.No 3 (Extra Characters — minimise)
After these three: write the universal recurrence dp[i] = f(dp[j] + cost(j,i)) from memory in all three modes (bool/count/optimise)
Verify: can you identify cost(j,i) for any new problem in 30 seconds?

Stage 2 — One constraint mutation:
S.No 4 (Palindrome Partitioning II — palindrome cost) → S.No 7 (Partition Array Max Sum — bounded window + running max) → S.No 5 (String Compression II)
For each: identify the ONE change from the previous problem

Stage 3 — Add the k dimension:
S.No 10 (Min Job Difficulty — cost=max) → S.No 11 (Largest Sum of Averages — cost=avg) → S.No 12 (Split Array — minimax)
Write dp[i][k] for all three. The ONLY difference is the cost function.

Stage 4 — Disguised problems:
S.No 14 (Bookcase Shelves) → S.No 17 (Paint House II) → S.No 19 (Paint House III)
For each: state "sequence = ?, segment = ?, cost(j,i) = ?" before coding

Stage 5 — Optimisations:
Binary search + greedy (Split Array LC 410) → Implement D&C DP on Largest Sum of Averages → Implement CHT on a linear-cost partition problem

Phase 2 — Drill (speed and pattern recognition):
Stage 1: Word Break, Decode Ways, Extra Characters → under 8 minutes each
Stage 3: Given a new "partition into k groups" problem — state dp[i][k] definition and recurrence verbally in under 60 seconds before coding

Cost function flash drill — identify cost(j,i) in 10 seconds:
Bookcase Shelves → max(height[j..i-1]) constrained by sum(width[j..i-1]) ≤ W
Largest Sum of Averages → sum(a[j..i-1]) / (i-j)
Paint House III → cost[i-1][c] + neighborhood increment if color changed
Palindrome Partitioning II → 1 if is_palindrome(s[j..i-1]) else ∞

Optimisation trigger drill: Given n = 10⁵ and the cost function — identify in 30 seconds whether CHT or D&C applies or neither

Phase 3 — Validate (unseen sources):
Stage 1/2: LeetCode weekly contest problems tagged "dynamic programming" from last 3 months not in this guide
Stage 3: USACO Gold "Landscaping" / "Painting the Barn" — partitioned resource allocation
Stage 4: Codeforces Div 2 D problems with 1D sequences and "assign to k groups" constraints
Stage 5: AtCoder DP Contest problems J (Sushi), L (Deque) for D&C DP variants
Graduation Test
Test 1 — CF 1603D: Critical Points (Stage 3 + Stage 4)
Primary stage: Stage 4
Combination: Stage 4 (recognise that "segments between critical points" form a partition; the "critical point" structure is the hidden sequence) + Stage 3 (two-dimension DP over critical points and segment count; cost = precomputed segment properties)
Why it certifies mastery: The sequence to partition is not given directly — critical points must be extracted first (Stage 4 reduction). The DP then requires a 2D partition recurrence (Stage 3). Neither stage alone solves the problem.

Test 2 — LC 1531: String Compression II (Stage 3 + Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Primary stage: Stage 3
Combination: Stage 4 (disguised as a compression problem; must recognise that each "run of characters" = one segment; deletions reduce run counts) + Stage 3 (three-dimensional state: position, deletions used, last character group) + Stage 5 (naïve O(n³) can be optimised; memoisation makes it feasible for n≤100 but larger n needs pruning)
Why it certifies mastery: The segment = "run of same character" identification is non-obvious (Stage 4). The three-dimension state expansion is required (Stage 3). The RLE cost function is neither a simple sum nor a max — it's a logarithmic step function — making it a unique cost variant not reducible to standard templates.

Test 3 — USACO Gold 2019 February: Landscaping (Stage 4 + Stage 5)
Primary stage: Stage 5
Combination: Stage 4 (partition rows of a field into groups to minimise cost; the "segment" = a group of consecutive rows assigned the same landscaping type; cost depends on transition costs between segment types) + Stage 5 (naïve O(n²·T) where T = types; opt monotonicity holds → D&C DP; O(n·T·log n))
Why it certifies mastery: The partition structure is hidden inside a 2D grid problem (Stage 4 — must reduce rows to a 1D sequence). The D&C DP is required for large n (Stage 5). Correctly identifying AND implementing both layers cold within 45 minutes certifies full mastery of the Partition DP staircase.
37
Game Theory & Minimax
T5 Dynamic Programming Game Theory Minimax DP Sprague-Grundy Nim & Variants
Why This Topic Matters
Game Theory & Minimax DP solves problems where two rational players alternate making decisions with opposing objectives, and the question is either who wins under perfect play, what score can the first player guarantee, or what is the optimal strategy. The core invariant is: every reachable game position is either a W-position (the player to move wins with perfect play) or an L-position (the player to move loses regardless of their moves), and this labelling propagates deterministically from terminal positions backwards through the game graph. This topic dominates competitive programming and top-tier interviews because it is a forcing structure — once you understand the W/L labelling invariant, every variant (score-difference games, Nim, Sprague-Grundy, bitmask games, graph games) becomes a coating on the same skeleton. The hard problems require identifying WHAT the game state is and HOW the game graph is structured, not inventing a new algorithm. Sprague-Grundy theory — which unifies all impartial games through XOR of Grundy values — is the single most powerful reduction in combinatorial game theory and converts multi-pile exponential problems into O(n) one-liners.
The Mental Model
Every game position is a node in a directed graph. Terminal nodes (no moves) are coloured LOSS for the player to move. Propagate backwards: colour a node WIN if it has at least one edge to a LOSS node; colour it LOSS if ALL its edges lead to WIN nodes. Your entire job is to compute this colouring for the starting position. Everything else — score differences, Grundy values, Nim equivalences — is a refinement of this single colouring rule.

Universal W/L recurrence:
dp[pos] = L if no moves exist; W if ∃ move to an L-position; L if all moves lead to W-positions.

How this mental model scales through the 5 stages:
Stage 1 — Simplest form: The game graph is a line (pile sizes 0, 1, 2, …n). Terminal = pile 0. Colour left to right. dp[n] = W or L.
Stage 2 — What changes: The winning/losing condition flips (misère), or the move options become asymmetric (divisors, perfect squares). The colouring rule is identical — only the terminal definition or the edge set changes.
Stage 3 — What expands: The game state requires two or more dimensions. dp[i][j] = score-difference for interval games (both ends available), or dp[i][M] when the move-budget M is itself a state variable.
Stage 4 — What is hidden: The game is disguised. The "game positions" are bitmasks of chosen numbers, node-pairs in a graph, or pile configurations after reductions. The Sprague-Grundy theorem maps any impartial game to an equivalent Nim pile via mex — the reduction IS the hard part.
Stage 5 — What is optimised: The Grundy sequence for a take-away game is eventually periodic. Instead of computing O(n·|S|) for n up to 10¹⁸, detect the period in O(P·|S|) and answer any query in O(1).
Core Invariant / Proof Sketch
Invariant (W/L labelling completeness): In any finite, acyclic, two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information game, every position receives exactly one label: W or L.

Proof sketch (strong induction on game-graph depth):
Base case (depth 0): Positions with no legal moves — the player to move cannot act and loses. Label = L. ✓
Inductive step: Assume all positions at depth < d are correctly labelled. For a position P at depth d:
If any successor S has label L: the current player moves to S, leaving the opponent in a losing state. Label P = W. ✓
If ALL successors have label W: every move leaves the opponent in a winning state. Label P = L. ✓
Uniqueness: P cannot be both W and L — the two cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive (some successor is L, or none is L). ✓
Finiteness guarantees termination: The game is acyclic (a finite DAG), so every branch reaches a terminal in finite steps. The induction bottoms out. □

Score-difference correctness sketch: Define dp[i][j] = (current player's score) − (opponent's score) for sub-game on interval [i,j]. The recurrence dp[i][j] = max(a[i] − dp[i+1][j], a[j] − dp[i][j-1]) is correct because: after the current player takes a[i] and the game state moves to [i+1,j], the NEW current player (the original opponent) achieves dp[i+1][j] advantage over the NEW opponent (the original player). So the original player's advantage is a[i] − dp[i+1][j]. The subtraction is the perspective flip.

Sprague-Grundy theorem sketch: Define G(P) = mex{G(P') : P' reachable from P}. Then P is an L-position iff G(P) = 0, and for a sum of independent games, G(combined) = XOR(G(components)). The first claim follows directly from the W/L induction: G(P) = 0 iff mex{...} = 0 iff 0 is NOT in the reachable Grundy values iff every successor has Grundy value ≥ 1 iff every successor is a W-position (G ≥ 1 → W) iff P is an L-position. The XOR composition follows from Bouton's theorem on multi-pile Nim, extended to all impartial games via Grundy equivalence.
Sub-Variants to Master
Sub-VariantStageTimeSpaceNew Idea
Single-pile take-away (fixed move set S)Stage 1O(n·|S|)boolean dp arrayTHE baseline — W/L colouring from terminal dp[0]=L; dp[n] = any(not dp[n-s] for s in S); sequence is eventually periodic
Take-away with divisor movesStage 2O(n log n)boolean dpMove set is STATE-DEPENDENT (divisors of n, not a fixed set); moves recomputed per state
Take-away with perfect-square movesStage 2O(n√n)boolean dpMove set grows with n; inner loop is O(√n) per state
Misère take-awayStage 2O(n·|S|)boolean dp with flipped terminaldp[0] = W (cannot move = WIN, because opponent took the last stone); same recurrence, ONE base-case flip
Score-difference (interval game, take from ends)Stage 2O(n²)2D interval DPTracks SCORE DIFFERENCE not just W/L; dp[i][j] = max(a[i]−dp[i+1][j], a[j]−dp[i][j-1]); fill by interval length
Score-difference (suffix game, take from front)Stage 2O(n)1D dp on suffixConsumption is always left-to-right (not both ends); 1D dp replaces 2D; O(n) with rolling window
Minimax guaranteed cost (Guess Number)Stage 2O(n³)2D interval DPAdversary maximises cost; we minimise worst-case; max inside min is the adversarial structure
Stone Game II (variable M parameter)Stage 3O(n²)dp[i][M] + suffix prefix sumsThe MOVE BUDGET M is itself a state dimension; M updates as max(M, X) after each move
Stone Game IV (perfect-square removal)Stage 2O(n√n)W/L dp with square movesCombines square-move set (S.No 3) with a W/L (not score-diff) query; foundational bridge
Can I Win (bitmask game state)Stage 3O(2ⁿ·n)bitmask dpGame state is a BITMASK of used integers; first time exponential state space appears
Nim (multi-pile, Sprague-Grundy)Stage 4O(n)XOR of pile sizesMulti-pile game; Grundy value of pile n = n; XOR = combined Grundy; L iff XOR = 0
Generalised Grundy computation (arbitrary move set)Stage 4O(n·|S|)grundy[n] = mex{grundy[n-s]}mex replaces any; Grundy value encodes the full game spectrum (not just W/L); reveals Nim-equivalence
Staircase NimStage 4O(n)XOR of ODD-indexed pile sizes onlyNot all piles contribute to XOR; odd-index reduction is the non-obvious insight
Chalkboard XOR GameStage 4O(n)XOR property + parity; no DP neededInitial XOR = 0 OR n is even → first player wins; proof via parity and XOR structure
Cat and Mouse (graph game with DRAW)Stage 3O(n³)reverse-BFS propagation; three-outcome DPCyclic game graph introduces DRAW as a third outcome; standard bottom-up DP fails on cycles
Flip Game II / string state gameStage 4O(2ⁿ) memoisedgame state = string hashGame state is an arbitrary string; exponential without memoisation; string hash as cache key
Sprague-Grundy sum of independent gamesStage 4O(Σ Gᵢ)XOR of component Grundy valuesMultiple INDEPENDENT subgames played simultaneously; combined Grundy = XOR; identifying independent components IS the insight
Period detection in Grundy sequencesStage 5O(P·|S|) + O(1) queriescycle detection on Grundy arrayGrundy sequences for fixed-move-set take-away games are eventually periodic; period detection enables O(1) queries for n up to 10¹⁸
Wythoff's Game (two-pile, can remove equal from both)Stage 4O(n)golden-ratio formula; no DP neededL-positions follow Beatty sequence: (⌊φ·k⌋, ⌊φ²·k⌋) for k = 0,1,2,…; closed-form bypasses DP entirely
Partisan game (asymmetric moves)Stage 4O(n²)dp[pos][turn] — turn dimension requiredLeft/Right players have DIFFERENT legal moves; Sprague-Grundy doesn't apply; turn is a required state dimension
Complexity Staircase
Sub-VariantTimeSpaceWhat Changed from Previous
Single-pile take-away (fixed S)O(n·|S|)O(n)Baseline: boolean dp on pile size
Stone Game III (take 1/2/3 from front)O(n)O(1)1D score-diff; window = 3; O(n) with rolling
Predict the Winner (take from ends)O(n²)O(n²) → O(n)2D interval DP; score-diff encoding
Stone Game II (M-parameter)O(n²)O(n²)Second dim M; suffix sums needed
Can I Win (bitmask)O(2ⁿ·n)O(2ⁿ)Exponential state via bitmask
Cat and Mouse (graph game)O(n³)O(n²)Cyclic graph; three outcomes; reverse BFS
Grundy mex computationO(n·|S|)O(n)mex replaces boolean; full Grundy spectrum
Multi-pile Nim (Sprague-Grundy)O(n)O(1)XOR composition; no inner loop
Period-detected GrundyO(P·|S|) + O(1)O(P)Period detection; O(1) per query
Mastery Problem Set
S.NoLayerStageSource# / NameDifficultyPremium?Sub-VariantNew Idea AddedKey Insight
111LeetCode292 – Nim GameMediumSingle-pile take-away; fixed moves {1,2,3}THE baseline: dp[0]=L; dp[n]=W if any(dp[n-1],dp[n-2],dp[n-3] = L); period = 4; answer = n%4 != 0Establishes the W/L colouring from terminal; periodic pattern means O(1) answer; first problem to solve cold — name the pattern in 10 seconds
211LeetCode1025 – Divisor GameMediumState-dependent move set (divisors of n)Move set changes per state: move set for n = all d where d|n, d<n; dp[n] = any(not dp[n-d] for d in divisors(n))Same W/L template; ONE change: recompute valid moves per state; answer = n%2==0 (provable by strong induction: even always wins, odd always loses)
311🔧 CUSTOMTake-Away Game with Set SMediumGeneralised single-pile; arbitrary fixed move setGiven any fixed set S, compute dp[0..N]; observe eventually-periodic Grundy sequenceSee full statement ↓; teaches that the W/L template is universal for any fixed move set; period ≤ LCM(S)+max(S) always finite
412LeetCode877 – Stone GameMediumScore-difference (take from ends; n even)dp[i][j] = max score-diff; dp[i][j]=max(piles[i]-dp[i+1][j], piles[j]-dp[i][j-1]); fill by lengthAdds SCORE TRACKING (not just W/L); 2D interval DP; base dp[i][i]=piles[i]; even-n structural guarantee: first player always wins
512LeetCode486 – Predict the WinnerMediumScore-difference (take from ends; arbitrary n)Identical DP to S.No 4 but n can be odd; answer = dp[0][n-1] >= 0 is no longer always TrueONE change from S.No 4: structural even-n guarantee removed; DP now needed to determine outcome; base cases identical
612LeetCode1510 – Stone Game IVMediumW/L with perfect-square movesdp[n]=W if any(dp[n-k²]=L for k²≤n); inner loop O(√n) per state; total O(n√n)Bridges S.No 2 (state-dependent moves) with a W/L (not score-diff) objective; introduces the O(√n) inner loop pattern
722LeetCode1406 – Stone Game IIIMedium1D score-diff; take 1/2/3 from FRONT onlydp[i] = max score-diff from suffix piles[i:]; dp[i]=max(piles[i]-dp[i+1], sum(piles[i:i+2])-dp[i+2], sum(piles[i:i+3])-dp[i+3])Changes consumption direction: left-to-right only (not both ends); 1D dp replaces 2D; rolling window of 3 → O(1) space
822LeetCode1563 – Stone Game VHardInterval split where score = min(left_sum, right_sum)dp[i][j]= max score splitting piles[i..j]; split at k; earn min(left_sum, right_sum) and recurse into the larger halfHard: the MOVE is a split (not take); score earned = min half; the "recurse into larger" creates asymmetric sub-problems; prefix sums needed
922LeetCode1690 – Stone Game VIMediumTwo-player pick; non-adversarial; sort by combined valueSort by a[i]+b[i] desc; alternate picks; greedy O(n log n) — NOT minimax DP⚠️ TRAP WARNING embedded in Layer 2: looks like score-diff DP but sort+greedy is optimal; teaches that "two players" ≠ "minimax DP"
1023LeetCode1140 – Stone Game IIMediumdp[i][M] — move budget M as second state dimensiondp[i][M] = max stones from piles[i:] with current M; can take X=1..2M piles; M→max(M,X) after each take; suffix sums for O(1) range sumAdds M as an explicit SECOND STATE DIMENSION; move set depends on current M; suffix prefix sums essential
1123LeetCode375 – Guess Number Higher or Lower IIMediumMinimax guaranteed cost; min(k + max(dp[i][k-1], dp[k+1][j]))dp[i][j]= min money to guarantee finding number in [i,j]; inner max models adversary choosing worst branchAdds the MINIMAX STRUCTURE: outer min (our choice of guess k) wraps inner max (adversary picks worst branch); covered in Interval DP but placed here as the canonical minimax template
1223LeetCode1872 – Stone Game VIIIHardPrefix-sum game; always take from position i onwardsdp[i] = max score-diff Alice achieves from index i; dp[i] = max(prefix[i] - dp[i+1], dp[i+1]); scan right to leftHard: the move is "take everything from i onwards at cost = prefix[i]"; 1D dp scanning right-to-left; the non-obvious reduction is that each move yields the full prefix sum
⚡ CLIFF WARNING — Layer 2 → Layer 3: Next problems require either (1) bitmask as the game state (exponential state space), (2) Grundy mex computation beyond simple W/L, or (3) a cyclic game graph requiring reverse-BFS instead of standard DP. Prepare: understand mex({0,1,2})=3, mex({0,2})=1, mex({})=0. Understand why standard DP fails on graphs with cycles.
1333LeetCode464 – Can I WinMediumBitmask game state: dp[mask]=W/LState = bitmask of used integers; dp[mask]=W if any(not dp[mask|(1<<i)] where adding i+1 ≥ desiredTotal or not dp[next]) for unused iAdds BITMASK STATE — first time the game state is a subset of available choices; 2ⁿ states; early termination if total sum < desiredTotal
1433LeetCode913 – Cat and MouseHardCyclic graph game; three outcomes (W/L/DRAW)State (mouse,cat,turn); terminal: mouse at 0 = MOUSE_WIN; cat=mouse = CAT_WIN; cycles = DRAW; reverse-BFS from terminalsHard: DRAW is the third outcome; standard bottom-up DP cannot handle cycles; reverse-BFS propagation from known terminals is required; uncoloured states after BFS = DRAW
1533LeetCode1728 – Cat and Mouse IIHardExtended graph game with JUMP DISTANCE and food limitdp[mouse][cat][turn] extended; cat/mouse jump at most catJump/mouseJump; food limit adds implicit bound on game length; all three terminal conditionsHard: three terminal conditions (mouse eats, cat catches, food exhausted); larger state space O(n²·n²·2); jump constraint creates denser edges; same reverse-BFS approach
1634🔧 CUSTOMCompute Grundy ValuesMediumgrundy[n] = mex{grundy[n-s] for s in S}; full Grundy spectrumIntroduces mex; Grundy=0 ↔ L-position; Grundy=k means position is Nim-equivalent to pile of size kSee full statement ↓; bridges W/L (S.No 1) to Sprague-Grundy (S.No 17); mex computation is the single new idea; O(n·|S|)
1734LeetCode1908 – Nim Game (multi-pile)Medium✅Multi-pile Nim; XOR of pile sizes = combined GrundyXOR of all pile sizes = 0 → second player wins; XOR ≠ 0 → first player wins; Sprague-Grundy: G(pile of size n) = nAdds MULTI-PILE GAME; combined Grundy = XOR of components; O(n) computation; no DP needed once Sprague-Grundy understood
1834LeetCode810 – Chalkboard XOR GameHardXOR game with removal; structural parity + XOR argumentInitial XOR=0 OR array length is even → first player wins; no DP needed; proof via parity mirror strategy and XOR invariant maintenanceHard: requires recognising the XOR invariant and PARITY argument simultaneously; the mirror strategy proof is non-trivial and tests deep understanding of XOR structure
1944LeetCode294 – Flip Game IIHard✅String game; dp[state]=W/L with string hash as keyState = string of '+'/'-'; move = flip "++" to "--"; memo by string hash; Sprague-Grundy on string state spaceHard: state is an arbitrary string → exponential without memoisation; hash the string for O(1) cache lookup; shows Sprague-Grundy theory on implicit game graphs
2044CodeforcesCF 768C – VotingHardStrategy-stealing + simulation; partisan gameTwo factions voting to eliminate each other; each player optimally delays own elimination; greedy queue simulationHard: partisan game (two different factions) where Sprague-Grundy doesn't apply; optimal strategy = process by round using two queues; the game structure dictates the algorithm
2144CodeforcesCF 850C – Arpa and a GameHardSprague-Grundy sum of independent subgamesPartition the game into INDEPENDENT subgames; compute Grundy value per subgame; XOR for combined gameHard: identifying which components are INDEPENDENT is the Stage 4 insight; once identified, Grundy XOR is automatic; incorrect independence assumptions give wrong XOR
2244CodeforcesCF 768E – Game of StonesHardStaircase Nim: XOR of ODD-indexed pile sizes onlyMoves transfer k stones from position i to i-1; only ODD-indexed positions contribute to XOR; even positions cancel via mirror strategyHard: the odd-index reduction — moves from EVEN positions can always be neutralised by opponent on the very next turn (mirror to corresponding odd position); non-obvious reduction
2345AtCoderAGC017B – Moderate DifferencesHardPeriod detection in Grundy sequence; O(1) query for n up to 10¹⁸Compute Grundy[0..3P] until period P detected; answer = grundy[n % P]; period found via cycle detectionHard: n up to 10¹⁸ makes O(n) dp impossible; period detection (Sprague-Grundy sequences are eventually periodic for fixed S) reduces to O(P) precompute + O(1) query
2445USACOGold – Mortal Kombat TowerHard2D game DP with health thresholds; optimal play on DAGdp[i][player] = can current player survive from level i; transitions depend on health remaining after fight; minimax on health valuesHard: the game state encodes health threshold constraints across turns; recognising that optimal play = minimax on health (not just W/L) is Stage 4; two-dimensional state is Stage 3
255 ⚠️—LeetCode877 – Stone Game (n even) ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — when n is even, structural proof gives O(1)n even → sum of even-indexed piles ≠ sum of odd-indexed piles in general; HOWEVER the first player can always choose to take ALL even-index or ALL odd-index elements → always winsNOT worth O(n²) DP: for n even, answer is always True; building the full DP wastes time; teach the structural argument first
265 ⚠️—LeetCode1686 – Stone Game VI ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — two players but GREEDY, not minimax DPSort by a[i]+b[i] desc; alternate picks; O(n log n) greedy — NOT adversarial DPNOT minimax DP: combined-value sort makes greedy globally optimal; applying minimax DP gives correct answer but is 100x more complex than needed
275 ⚠️—LeetCode2140 – Solving Questions with Brainpower ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — looks like adversarial game; is single-player optimisationSingle player choosing answer-or-skip; no opponent; standard 1D DP dp[i] = max(points[i]+dp[i+brainpower[i]+1], dp[i+1])NOT game theory: only one decision-maker; "skip" cost is a self-imposed constraint, not an adversary's move; minimax gives wrong answer by treating skip as opponent's choice
285 ⚠️—LeetCode1927 – Sum Game ⚠️Medium⚠️ TRAP — looks like W/L DP; is pure mathematical analysisAlice/Bob alternate replacing '?' with digit to equalise/prevent-equalising the sum; mathematical condition: remaining_count_left == remaining_count_right and sum balance determine winnerNOT minimax DP: the optimal strategy reduces to checking a single mathematical condition; DP over game states is correct but O(n) formula exists; pattern-match to "can the algebraic constraint be satisfied?"
🔧 Custom Problem 3 — Take-Away Game with Set S
Problem: Two players alternate taking stones from a pile of n stones. On each turn, the current player must take exactly s stones where s ∈ S (a given set of positive integers). The player who CANNOT move loses. Given n and S, output W if the first player wins, L otherwise.

Constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10⁵, 1 ≤ |S| ≤ 20, 1 ≤ s ≤ 100 for all s ∈ S.

Example: S = {2, 5}, n = 1. dp[0]=L, dp[1]=L (no move ≤ 1 in S), dp[2]=W, dp[3]=W (can go to dp=L), dp[4]=L, dp[5]=W, dp[6]=W, dp[7]=L. Period = 7. Answer for n=1: L.

Solution: dp[0] = False; for n ≥ 1: dp[n] = any(not dp[n-s] for s in S if s ≤ n). Answer = dp[n]. Time: O(n·|S|). The Grundy sequence is eventually periodic with period ≤ LCM(elements of S)+max(S).

Stage: 1 — establishes the universal take-away template. The ONE new idea over S.No 2 is that the move set is FIXED and arbitrary (not state-dependent).
🔧 Custom Problem 16 — Compute Grundy Values
Problem: Same setup as Custom Problem 3. Instead of outputting W/L, output the full Grundy sequence grundy[0], grundy[1], ..., grundy[N]. Then, given Q queries of multi-pile Nim where each query specifies k pile sizes, output W or L for each query.

Constraints: Same as Custom Problem 3; 1 ≤ Q ≤ 10⁵; k ≤ 100 piles per query.

Solution: grundy[0] = 0; for n ≥ 1: grundy[n] = mex({grundy[n-s] for s in S if s ≤ n}) where mex(T) = min non-negative integer not in T. For each query: XOR all pile Grundy values; L iff result = 0. Time: O(n·|S|) precompute + O(k) per query.

Why this exists: Bridges Stage 1 (boolean W/L) to Stage 4 (Sprague-Grundy XOR). The single new idea is the mex function, which generalises the any(not dp[...]) into a full Grundy value. Without understanding mex, all Stage 4 problems are inaccessible.
State Evolution Across Stages
Group 1: Single-Pile W/L Take-Away DP

Stage 1 — Fixed move set:
dp[n] = True (W) or False (L)
Recurrence: dp[n] = any(not dp[n-s] for s in S if s ≤ n)
Base: dp[0] = False (normal play: no moves = lose)
Fill: left to right, n = 0, 1, 2, ..., N
Preferred: Bottom-up — trivial left-to-right; no dependencies on future states
Space optimisation: if sequence is periodic with period P, only store dp[0..P-1] and answer = dp[n % P]

Stage 2 — Misère variant (last to move loses):
Same recurrence, ONE change: dp[0] = True (cannot move = WIN, because opponent took the last stone and loses)
Note: for Nim specifically, misère strategy = standard Nim EXCEPT when all piles ≤ 1; at that point, aim for ODD number of size-1 piles

Stage 4 — Grundy values:
grundy[n] = Grundy value (nimber) of position n
Recurrence: grundy[n] = mex({grundy[n-s] for s in S if s ≤ n})
Base: grundy[0] = 0 (mex of empty set = 0)
Fill: left to right; W/L determination: L iff grundy[n] == 0
Multi-pile query: L iff XOR(grundy[p] for p in piles) == 0

Group 2: Score-Difference Interval DP

Stage 2 — Take from either end:
dp[i][j] = (current player score) − (opponent score) for piles[i..j]
Recurrence: dp[i][j] = max(piles[i] − dp[i+1][j], piles[j] − dp[i][j-1])
Base: dp[i][i] = piles[i]; dp[i][i-1] = 0 (empty interval)
Fill: by increasing length L = j-i+1; ensures dp[i+1][j] and dp[i][j-1] computed before dp[i][j]
Answer: first player wins iff dp[0][n-1] ≥ 0; player 1 score = (total_sum + dp[0][n-1]) / 2
Preferred: Bottom-up by length — memoised top-down also works for interval DP; bottom-up avoids recursion for large n
Space: O(n) with two rolling rows if only final answer needed; O(n²) to reconstruct moves

Stage 2 — Take from front only (Stone Game III):
dp[i] = max score-diff from piles[i:]
Recurrence: dp[i] = max(piles[i] − dp[i+1], sum(p[i:i+2]) − dp[i+2], sum(p[i:i+3]) − dp[i+3])
Base: dp[n] = dp[n+1] = dp[n+2] = 0 (past end)
Fill: right to left; O(1) space with 3-variable rolling
Preferred: Bottom-up right-to-left — no 2D table needed; O(n) time, O(1) space

Stage 3 — Stone Game II (M-parameter):
dp[i][M] = max stones current player gets from piles[i:] when current M = M
Recurrence: dp[i][M] = max(suffix[i] − dp[i+X][max(M,X)] for X in 1..2M)
Taking X piles → opponent faces suffix[i+X:] with M' = max(M, X)
Since opponent maximises, dp[i][M] = suffix[i] − min(dp[i+X][max(M,X)] for X in 1..2M)
Base: dp[i][M] = suffix[i] when i + 2*M ≥ n (can take everything)
Fill: right to left on i; for each i, all M values; suffix prefix sums precomputed
Preferred: Top-down memoisation — the M dimension ranges irregularly (1 to n/2); top-down avoids over-computing states with large M that are never reached

Group 3: Bitmask Game DP

Stage 3 — Can I Win:
dp[mask] = True (W) or False (L) where mask = bitmask of integers already chosen
Running sum from mask = sum(i+1 for i in range(n) if mask & (1<<i))
Recurrence: dp[mask] = any(not dp[mask | (1<<i)] for i in range(n) if not (mask & (1<<i)) and running_sum + (i+1) ≥ desiredTotal) — if adding i reaches target, this is immediately a WIN move
More precisely: dp[mask] = any(running_sum + (i+1) ≥ desiredTotal OR not dp[mask|(1<<i)] for unused i)
Base: dp[full_mask] = False (no moves; but this state is reached after total ≥ target already resolved)
Early termination: if sum(1..maxChoosableInteger) < desiredTotal → always False
Preferred: Top-down memoisation with mask as dictionary key — fill order is complex (depends on which bits are set)

Group 4: Graph Game DP (Cat and Mouse)

Stage 3 — Cat and Mouse (cyclic game graph):
dp[mouse][cat][turn] ∈ {MOUSE_WIN, CAT_WIN, DRAW}
Terminal states: mouse == 0 → MOUSE_WIN (for any cat, any turn); mouse == cat → CAT_WIN (cat catches mouse)
Recurrence (NOT standard bottom-up — uses reverse BFS):
Algorithm: propagate from known terminals backwards.
1. Initialise all terminal positions as MOUSE_WIN or CAT_WIN.
2. For each terminal T, propagate to all predecessor positions P: if P is a MOUSE_WIN position reaching T (mouse's turn) and T = MOUSE_WIN → P = MOUSE_WIN immediately; if P is a CAT_WIN position reaching T (cat's turn) and T = CAT_WIN → P = CAT_WIN immediately; Count: decrement out-degree of P; if all moves lead to same outcome → P gets that label.
3. Any position never labelled = DRAW.
Preferred: Reverse BFS from terminal states — ONLY correct approach for cyclic game graphs; standard DP produces wrong answers.
Pattern Recognition Triggers
Linguistic Triggers:
"two players take turns; player who cannot move LOSES / WINS" → Stage 1 W/L take-away DP; dp[0]=L (normal play) or dp[0]=W (misère); identify move set immediately
"both players play OPTIMALLY; who wins / what is the maximum score the first player can guarantee" → Stage 2/3 score-difference DP; dp[i][j] = max(a[i]−dp[i+1][j], a[j]−dp[i][j-1])
"remove from piles; player wins if all piles empty / XOR" → Stage 4 Sprague-Grundy; check if multi-pile; XOR of Grundy values
"choose from a set of integers 1..N; first player to reach target or exceed it wins" → Stage 3 bitmask game DP; state = mask of used integers; O(2ⁿ·n)
"two entities move on a graph; one is predator, one is prey; who catches whom" → Stage 3 graph game DP; three-outcome (W/L/DRAW); reverse-BFS required if graph has cycles

Structural Triggers:
Two players, alternating, perfect information, no randomness, finite acyclic game → W/L DP or score-diff DP; classify as impartial (Sprague-Grundy applies) or partisan (score-diff or turn-dimension DP)
Game positions form an INTERVAL [i, j] that shrinks from both ends on each turn → Score-difference interval DP; dp[i][j]; fill by increasing length
The "move" is taking a number from a set and the chosen number is REMOVED permanently → Bitmask game state; dp[mask]; N ≤ 20

Constraint Triggers:
n ≤ 20 → Bitmask game DP; O(2ⁿ·n) feasible
n ≤ 10¹⁸ → O(n) DP impossible; look for periodic Grundy sequence or closed-form (Nim: XOR; Wythoff: golden ratio)

Anti-Triggers:
"two players cooperate to maximise TOTAL score" → NOT minimax → Standard maximisation DP; no adversarial structure
"maximise your score (single player)" → NOT game theory → 1D/2D optimisation DP or greedy
"who wins if both play optimally (n even)" → MIGHT NOT need DP → check for parity/symmetry shortcut first
Decision Framework
Q1: Are there EXACTLY TWO players making alternating decisions with OPPOSING objectives? NO → Not game theory. YES → Continue.

Q2: Is the game IMPARTIAL (both players have IDENTICAL legal moves from any position)? YES → Sprague-Grundy applies; Q3. NO (Partisan) → Score-diff or turn-dimension DP; Q5.

Q3: MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT PILES/subgames simultaneously? YES → XOR of Grundy values; L iff XOR=0. Standard Nim: G(pile n)=n. Stage 4. NO → Compute Grundy/W/L directly; Q4.

Q4: Is n so large that O(n·|S|) TLEs (n > 10⁶)? YES → Detect period P; O(P·|S|)+O(1) query. Stage 5. NO → Standard grundy[n] = mex{...} or dp[n] = any(not dp[n-s]). Stage 1. O(n·|S|).

Q5 (Partisan): Does score per move depend on BOTH players' positions (take from either end)? YES → Score-diff interval DP dp[i][j]. O(n²); Q6. NO → 1D dp[i]. O(n). Rolling window if window ≤ k.

Q6: Does state need MORE than two dimensions? YES → Add M (move budget) → dp[i][M]. O(n²). Or color → dp[i][j][c]. O(n²·C). NO → Standard 2D dp[i][j]. O(n²).

Q7: Is the game state EXPONENTIAL? Bitmask (N ≤ 20): dp[mask]. O(2ⁿ·n). Graph with cycles: REVERSE-BFS from terminals. Three outcomes: W, L, DRAW.
When This Technique BREAKS
[Stage 1] Standard W/L DP on a cyclic game graph: The bottom-up W/L recurrence assumes a DAG. Cycles make DRAW possible. Fix: use reverse BFS from terminals; uncoloured positions = DRAW (Cat and Mouse).

[Stage 2] Applying standard Nim strategy to Misère Nim throughout: Misère strategy = standard Nim EXCEPT when all piles ≤ 1. Then aim for ODD number of size-1 piles. Applying inversion mid-game gives wrong answers.

[Stage 4] Applying Sprague-Grundy to a PARTISAN game: SG works only for impartial games (identical moves). Partisan: track turn dp[pos][turn] or temperature theory.

[Stage 3] Period detection without full verification: Single-value match is coincidental. Fix: verify grundy[n] = grundy[n % P] for n = P..3P.

[Stage 2] Score-diff DP: adding instead of subtracting: dp[i][j] = max(a[i]+dp[i+1][j], ...) is WRONG. Must SUBTRACT: a[move] − dp[subgame] (perspective flip).

[Stage 4] Assuming Grundy periodicity for state-dependent move sets: Periodicity holds only for FIXED move sets (octal games). State-dependent moves (divisors) → no guarantee.
Proof of Correctness Skeleton
Stage 1 — W/L Take-Away DP: Invariant Maintenance
Claim: dp[n] = True iff the player to move from pile-size n wins under perfect play with move set S.
Terminal: dp[0] = False; no moves → current player loses. ✓
W-step: If ∃ s ∈ S, s ≤ n, with dp[n-s] = False: take s; opponent in L. dp[n] = True. ✓
L-step: If ∀ s ∈ S with s ≤ n: dp[n-s] = True. Every move leaves opponent in W. dp[n] = False. ✓
Induction: dp[n] only references dp[n-s] for s ≥ 1 (smaller). By strong induction, all correct. □

Stage 2 — Score-Difference DP: Perspective-Flip
Claim: dp[i][j] = max(a[i] − dp[i+1][j], a[j] − dp[i][j-1]) equals (current player's score) − (opponent's score) for piles[i..j].
Base: dp[i][i] = a[i]. ✓
Inductive step: Current takes a[i]; game → [i+1,j]. Opponent is now "current" and achieves dp[i+1][j] over original. So original advantage = a[i] − dp[i+1][j]. Symmetric for a[j]. ✓
Score recovery: S₁ + S₂ = total_sum and S₁ − S₂ = dp[0][n-1]. Wins iff dp[0][n-1] ≥ 0. ✓ □

Stage 4 — Sprague-Grundy: mex Correctness
Claim: G(P) = mex({G(P') : P' reachable from P}) correctly encodes W/L, and P is L iff G(P)=0.
G(P)=0 → L: 0 ∉ {G(P')} means all successors have G ≥ 1 (W). So P is L. ✓
G(P)>0 → W: mex=k>0 means 0 ∈ {G(P')}. Some successor is L → P is W. ✓
XOR composition: Bouton's theorem; move in component i changes XOR by replacing G(Gᵢ) with smaller, mirroring moves in single Nim pile. □
Implementation Pitfalls (Code-Level)
Stage 1–2 Bugs:

Bug 1 — Wrong terminal: dp = [True] * (n+1) — misère base applied to normal-play; FIX: dp[0] = False; for i in range(1, n+1): dp[i] = any(not dp[i-s] for s in S if s <= i).

Bug 2 — Adding instead of subtracting: dp[i][j] = max(piles[i] + dp[i+1][j], ...) — accumulates instead of difference; FIX: dp[i][j] = max(piles[i] - dp[i+1][j], piles[j] - dp[i][j-1]).

Bug 3 — Wrong fill order: Row-major iteration can reference uncomputed dp[i+1][j]; FIX: iterate by increasing length: for length in range(2, n+1): for i in range(n - length + 1): j = i + length - 1; dp[i][j] = max(piles[i] - dp[i+1][j], piles[j] - dp[i][j-1]).

Bug 4 — mex using max+1: mex({0,1,3}) = 2, NOT max+1=4; FIX: def mex(s): i = 0; while i in s: i += 1; return i.

Bug 5 — Predict the Winner strict inequality: return dp[0][n-1] > 0 misses ties; FIX: return dp[0][n-1] >= 0.

Stage 3 Bugs:

Bug 6 — Stone Game II wrong M update: using dp[i+X][M] instead of dp[i+X][max(M,X)]; FIX: val = suffix[i+X] - dp[i+X][max(M, X)].

Bug 7 — Can I Win no early termination: runs expensive bitmask DP even when sum(1..max) < desiredTotal (always False); FIX: early return False if impossible, True if desiredTotal <= 0.

Bug 8 — Cat and Mouse forward DP: forward fill on cyclic graph never settles DRAW; FIX: reverse-BFS from terminals; never-reached = DRAW.

Stage 4 Bugs:

Bug 9 — Multi-pile Nim XOR of indices: for i, pile in enumerate(piles): result ^= i is WRONG; FIX: XOR pile VALUES, not indices.

Stage 5 Bugs:

Bug 10 — Declaring period from single match: grundy[i] == grundy[0] once ≠ period; FIX: verify_period(grundy, P) checking all values in [P, 3P].
Red Flags / Common Mistakes
[Stage 1] Confusing normal play with misère — dp[0]: Normal play ("last to move wins"): dp[0]=False. Misère ("last to move loses"): dp[0]=True. OPPOSITE base cases, IDENTICAL recurrences. Read the losing condition first.

[Stage 2] Score-diff DP: using absolute scores with turn flag: dp[i][j][turn] is O(n²·2) and requires special-casing the turn. Score-difference encoding dp[i][j] is O(n²) with NO turn dimension — subtraction handles the flip automatically.

[Stage 3] Can I Win: forgetting current_sum check: Game ends when running sum ≥ desiredTotal, not just when all chosen. Must check current_sum + (i+1) ≥ desiredTotal in recurrence.

[Stage 4] Applying Sprague-Grundy to Cat and Mouse: Cat and Mouse is CYCLIC with DRAWS — NOT impartial acyclic. SG via mex on cyclic graphs → infinite loops or wrong values. Check acyclicity before applying SG.

[Stage 4] Assuming independence of subgames without verification: XOR composition is correct ONLY when components are truly independent. Moves affecting multiple components simultaneously → XOR gives wrong answers.

[Stage 2] Stone Game VI: applying minimax when greedy suffices: When each player values items INDEPENDENTLY (Alice values a[], Bob values b[]), joint-value sort a[i]+b[i] desc always dominates — O(n log n) greedy, not O(n²) DP.

[Stage 5] Period detection on non-fixed move sets: Only works for FIXED S. Divisor Game and similar have non-periodic Grundy sequences.
Interviewer Follow-Up Questions
#StageQuestionIntended Answer Direction
12"Nim Game: what if a player can take at most K stones (not just 1/2/3)?"Generalise to move set {1,...,K}; L-positions are multiples of K+1; n % (K+1) == 0 → L; period = K+1; pure formula
22"Stone Game: what if the array is circular (last adjacent to first)?"dp[i][j] on linear can't model circular; fix element 0: run two passes — one where element 0 goes to player 1 first, one where it doesn't; take max; O(n²) still
33"Predict the Winner: reconstruct the actual sequence of optimal moves."Parent pointer array choice[i][j] ∈ {'L','R'}; backtrack from dp[n-1]; O(n) reconstruction
43"Can I Win: n increases to 40. How does complexity change?"2⁴⁰ ≈ 10¹² infeasible; meet-in-the-middle: split into two halves of 20; enumerate (sum, parity) pairs per half; combine in O(2²⁰ · 20) ≈ 2×10⁷
54"Explain Staircase Nim and prove why only odd-indexed piles matter."Moves transfer k stones from i to i-1; moves from EVEN positions are mirrored by opponent (same k, i-1 to i-2); only ODD positions affect XOR; mirror strategy maintains invariant
64"How do you handle Cat and Mouse if the graph has self-loops?"Self-loops at non-terminals → player can "stay" indefinitely → DRAW; in reverse-BFS such nodes are never forced to a terminal and remain uncoloured (DRAW); no algorithm change
75"Grundy values for take-away where n can be 10¹⁸?"Detect period P; compute grundy[0..3P]; verify; answer = grundy[n % P]; period P ≤ LCM(S)+max(S) empirically; total O(P·|S|)
85"Stone Game V: optimise from O(n³) to O(n²)?"Inner k-scan is O(n); if optimal split k*[i][j] is monotone → D&C DP → O(n² log n); concave SMAWK holds → O(n²); or two-pointer on crossing point of left_sum < right_sum
9Beyond"Distribute Nim across 10 machines with 10⁹ piles. How?"XOR is associative/commutative; each machine XORs its piles (O(n/m)); coordinator XORs machine results (O(m)); total O(n/m + m); optimal m = √n for O(√n); L iff global XOR = 0

Covers: House Robber III (tree), Diameter of Binary Tree, Binary Tree Cameras, Maximum Path Sum, Count Nodes Equal to Average of Subtree, Longest Path with Same Values, Rerooting technique, Tree Knapsack & more.
Content for Digit DP is coming soon.

Covers: Count numbers with digit sum = k, Count numbers without repeated digits, Numbers with even number of digits, Digit DP template (tight/free bounds), Non-decreasing digits, At most N given digit set & more.

Covers: Longest Path in DAG, Count paths in DAG, Dice Roll simulation, Knight Probability in Chessboard, New 21 Game, Soup Servings, Probability of reaching target & more.

Covers: Number of Ways to Reorder Array, Unique BSTs (Catalan), Count Vowels Permutation, K Inverse Pairs Array, Count different palindromic subsequences, MOD arithmetic in DP & more.
Content for Data Structure Optimized DP is coming soon.

Covers: Segment Tree + DP, BIT/Fenwick Tree + DP (LIS in O(n log n)), Monotonic Deque + DP (Sliding Window DP), Divide & Conquer DP optimization, Convex Hull Trick, Knuth's optimization & more.
